elements.sent-2411-001: Parts I, II, III are included in this preview, while Part IV is not yet available.
elements.sent-2411-002: We welcome all feedback from readers---please contact the authors at tfcpbook@gmail.com with any typos, corrections, clarifications, or suggestions.
elements.sent-2411-003: Since this manuscript is a work-in-progress, if referencing any of the results in the book please be aware that later updates may lead to changes in the statements and the numbering of the results.
elements.sent-2411-004: These techniques are useful in a diverse array of tasks, including hypothesis testing and providing uncertainty quantification guarantees for machine learning systems.
elements.sent-2411-005: Much of the current interest in conformal prediction is due to its ability to integrate into complex machine learning workflows, solving the problem of forming prediction sets without any assumptions on the form of the data generating distribution.
elements.sent-2411-006: Since contemporary machine learning algorithms have generally proven difficult to analyze directly, conformal prediction's main appeal is its ability to provide formal, finite-sample guarantees when paired with such methods.
elements.sent-2411-007: The goal of this book is to teach the reader about the fundamental technical arguments that arise when researching conformal prediction and related questions in distribution-free inference.
elements.sent-2411-008: Many of these proof strategies, especially the more recent ones, are scattered among research papers, making it difficult for researchers to understand where to look, which results are important, and how exactly the proofs work.
elements.sent-2411-009: We hope to bridge this gap by curating what we believe to be some of the most important results in the literature and presenting their proofs in a unified language, with illustrations, and with an eye towards pedagogy.
elements.sent-2411-010: We note that this book does not focus on the question of how to apply conformal prediction in practice---for a more practical and application-oriented introduction to conformal prediction, the reader may instead prefer to read ``Conformal Prediction: A Gentle Introduction'' \citep{angelopoulos2022conformal .}
elements.sent-2411-011: This book is meant for those working on the development of statistical theory and methodology, broadly speaking.
elements.sent-2411-012: We envision our audience including everyone from classically trained statisticians interested in finite-sample model-agnostic bounds, to machine learning researchers looking for a modular theory that applies to the ever-changing landscape of machine learning algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-013: The background required is generally at the level of first-year graduate coursework in theoretical statistics; some measure theory will be used occasionally, but the vast majority of results in this book do not require it.
elements.sent-2411-014: We hope that this book will provide the reader with a deep understanding of the theoretical underpinnings of the field, so that they may themselves contribute to the ongoing theoretical development of conformal prediction and other areas within distribution-free inference.
elements.sent-2411-015: We would like to give our deep thanks to the many friends, collaborators, mentors, students, and reviewers who gave us feedback on this work, including Emmanuel Cand{\`e s, Michael Jordan, Elena Yutong Li, Peter McCullagh, Ethan Naegele, Theo Olausson, Andi Qu, Aaditya Ramdas, Ryan Tibshirani, Vladimir Vovk, Lekun (Bill) Wang, and Tijana Zrni\'c.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Lastly, we thank our families for their long-standing support in this effort---this book would not be possible without them.
elements.sent-2411-017: Conformal prediction is a statistical technique that quantifies uncertainty in predictive models.
elements.sent-2411-018: When we train a model using a machine learning algorithm, the resulting predictions are made based on the patterns observed in the training data---but these predictions can be prone to errors due to various sources of uncertainty, such as noisy data or inaccurate models.
elements.sent-2411-019: Uncertainty quantification enables us to make better decisions from model predictions by understanding the level of confidence and limitations of the model.
elements.sent-2411-020: It might be surprising to learn that, with conformal prediction, rigorous uncertainty quantification is possible without any assumptions at all on the model, and with minimal assumptions on the distribution of the data---no asymptotics, no limit theorems, and no Gaussian approximations.
elements.sent-2411-021: As such, conformal prediction and related methods are widely used in applications.
elements.sent-2411-022: These methods are also theoretically rich and interesting.
elements.sent-2411-023: This book discusses foundational statistical theory for conformal prediction, and points toward the many avenues for continued study.
elements.sent-2411-024: We now describe the prediction problem.
elements.sent-2411-025: Suppose we have a sequence of data points $(X_i, Y_i) \in \cX \times \cY$, for $i=1,\dots,n$.
elements.sent-2411-026: Here, $X_i$ is the feature vector and $Y_i$ is the response variable.
elements.sent-2411-027: We are then given a new feature vector $X_{n+1}$, with the task of predicting its corresponding response value $Y_{n+1}$ (which is unobserved).
elements.sent-2411-028: If we have trained a predictive model $\hf$ using the available data, we can return a prediction $ \hf(X_{n+1 )$---but this predicted value alone does not express our confidence or uncertainty in the trained model, and does not communicate information about whether the prediction can be trusted.}
elements.sent-2411-029: To do so, we could provide a margin of error around our prediction $\hf(X_{n+1 )$, or more generally, a prediction set $\cC(X_{n+1 )\subseteq\cY$.}}
elements.sent-2411-030: A common aim for this set is the property of marginal coverage, \begin{equation}\label{eq:marg_coverage} \P\Big(Y_{n+1} \in \cC(X_{n+1})\Big) \ge 1 - \alpha,\end{equation} where $\alpha$ in $(0,1)$ is a user-specified error level (e.g., $\alpha = 0.1$ for 90\% coverage).
elements.sent-2411-031: At a high level, by returning an interval or set to accompany any model prediction $\hf(X_{n+1 )$, we are able to communicate the degree of trustworthiness in any given model prediction---if the margin of error (or more generally, the size of the set $\cC(X_{n+1 )$) is large, this indicates high uncertainty in the prediction.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: Of course, it is not immediately clear whether the marginal coverage property~\eqref{eq:marg_coverage is attainable in a general setting.}
elements.sent-2411-033: We might suspect that this property can only be achieved in certain settings, for instance, if the model is known to be a good fit to the data distribution.
elements.sent-2411-034: This question is exactly what conformal prediction aims to address: conformal prediction is a statistical approach to uncertainty quantification wherein model predictions are accompanied by an interval or set, communicating the degree of trustworthiness in any given model prediction, without relying on any assumptions on the correctness of the model.
elements.sent-2411-035: In particular, if the underlying predictive model $\hf$ is a poor fit to the data, then the accompanying set $\cC(X_{n+1 )$ will be large---potentially even infinite.}
elements.sent-2411-036: On the other hand, accurate models will result in smaller sets, and (under additional conditions) can lead to notions of coverage over and above that in~\eqref{eq:marg_coverage .}
elements.sent-2411-037: Either way, the role of conformal prediction is to accurately quantify the level of uncertainty present when using the predictive model on the current data distribution.
elements.sent-2411-038: How does conformal prediction work? Let's start with a simple version of the method, called split conformal prediction.
elements.sent-2411-039: As before, suppose we have data points $(X_i,Y_i)$ for $i=1,\dots,n$, and a test point $(X_{n+1 , Y_{n+1 )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Take $n$ to be even for simplicity.
elements.sent-2411-041: We consider the setting $\cY=\R$---that is, a regression problem with a real-valued response.
elements.sent-2411-042: The following algorithm allows us to construct prediction intervals for $Y_{n+1}$: \begin{algbox [Split conformal prediction, special case].}
elements.sent-2411-043: Use data $(X_i,Y_i)$ for $i=1,\dots,n/2$ to fit a predictive model $\hf : \cX \to \R$.
elements.sent-2411-044: For $i=n/2 + 1,\dots,n$, compute the absolute residual $S_i = |Y_i - \hat f(X_i)|$.
elements.sent-2411-045: Sort $S_{n/2 + 1 ,\dots, S_n$ in increasing order, and let $\hat{q}$ be the $\lceil (1-\alpha)(\tfrac{n {2 + 1)\rceil$-th element in the sorted list.}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: For example, it might be a linear model fitted via least-squares regression.
elements.sent-2411-048: The final set is an interval centered at the model's prediction, $\hf(X_{n+1 )\pm \hat{q}$.}
elements.sent-2411-049: How should we interpret this choice of $\hat{q}$? Observe $\hat q$ is chosen such that the intervals $[\hf(X_i) - \hat{q , \hf(X_i) + \hat{q ]$ contain the response variable $Y_i$ for approximately a $(1-\alpha)$ fraction of the points $i=n/2+1,\dots,n$ (i.e., the data points that were not used for training the model $\hf$).}}
elements.sent-2411-050: If the data points are independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.), then no matter what predictive model $\hf$ was used, the prediction set above satisfies marginal coverage:.
elements.sent-2411-051: Then the marginal coverage property~\eqref{eq:marg_coverage holds.}
elements.sent-2411-052: In other words, if the data points are drawn i.i.d.\ from any distribution, then split conformal prediction offers marginal coverage---even if the fitted model $\hf$ is an extremely poor fit to the data, and even if the sample size $n$ is small.
elements.sent-2411-053: In fact the i.i.d.\ assumption is sufficient here, it is stronger than necessary---a weaker property known as exchangeability, which will be introduced in Chapter~\ref{chapter:exchangeability , is the fundamental property required for conformal prediction.}
elements.sent-2411-054: The example above builds intuition for valid coverage with any predictive model and dataset, but the exact form of the algorithm above is rather constrained: by construction, the prediction set will always be of the form $\hf(X_{n+1 )\pm \hat{q}$, i.e., a band of constant width around the fitted predictive model $\hf$; see Figure~\ref{fig:score-shape .}}
elements.sent-2411-055: Fortunately, the conformal framework is much more flexible than the above example, allowing for nearly unlimited choice in how $\cC(X_{n+1 )$ is constructed.}
elements.sent-2411-056: The key concept is the conformal score function, which is a function $s(x,y)$ such that larger values indicate that the data point $(x,y)$ does not agree with (does not ``conform'' to) the trends observed in the training data.
elements.sent-2411-057: For instance, given a fitted model $\hf$, a common choice for $s$ is the residual score, $s(x,y) = |y - \hf(x)|$, since a large value of the residual indicates that $(x,y)$ does not appear to agree with the model trained on the available data.
elements.sent-2411-058: We will give additional examples of score functions shortly.
elements.sent-2411-059: We now state a more general version of the split conformal prediction algorithm, using a generic conformal score function.
elements.sent-2411-060: Use data $(X_i,Y_i)$ for $i=1,\dots,n/2$ to construct a conformal score function $s : \cX\times \cY \to \R$.
elements.sent-2411-061: The idea to construct $s(x,y)$ to measure how unusual $(x,y)$ is based on a model fit on data from this split.
elements.sent-2411-062: For $i=n/2 + 1,\dots,n$, compute the score $S_i = s(X_i, Y_i)$.
elements.sent-2411-063: Sort $S_{n/2 + 1 ,\dots, S_n$ in increasing order, and let $\hat{q}$ be the $\lceil (1-\alpha)(\tfrac{n {2 +1)\rceil$-th element in the sorted list.}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: Return the prediction set $\cC(X_{n+1 ) = \{y\in\cY : s(X_{n+1 , y ) \le \hat{q \}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-065: However, in many cases, it simplifies to an interval that can be computed explicitly, just as in Algorithm~\ref{alg:split-cp-preview-residual ---we will see some examples below.}
elements.sent-2411-066: While we are referring to split conformal prediction as ``an algorithm'', the flexibility in choosing the score function $s$ means that we should actually think of this as a family of algorithms---any given choice of the conformal score function $s$ specifies a particular algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-067: For example, by choosing the residual score function $s(x,y) = |y - \hf(x)|$, we can obtain Algorithm~\ref{alg:split-cp-preview-residual as a special case of Algorithm~\ref{alg:split-cp-preview-general .}}
elements.sent-2411-068: The following result states that the marginal coverage guarantee holds with any score function.
elements.sent-2411-069: Although any score function results in marginal coverage, in practice, the choice of the conformal score function $s$ is the single most important decision when implementing conformal prediction: different choices can lead to very different procedures, and a poorly chosen conformal score function $s$ can lead to completely uninformative prediction sets.
elements.sent-2411-070: We next outline several of the most common conformal score functions, and give intuition for the properties of the resulting conformal prediction method.
elements.sent-2411-071: Figure~\ref{fig:score-shape gives an illustration of the sets resulting from these conformal score functions.}
elements.sent-2411-072: We first return to the residual score, $s(x,y) = |y - \hf(x)|$, as used in Algorithm~\ref{alg:split-cp-preview-residual above, where $\hf$ is fitted on the data points $(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_{n/2 ,Y_{n/2 )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-073: This construction will always return a prediction set $\cC(X_{n+1 )$ of the same form: a symmetric interval, centered around the point prediction $\hf(X_{n+1 )$ with the same width for all values of $X_{n+1}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-074: This is a simple and natural choice.
elements.sent-2411-075: However, in many settings, the form of the resulting prediction sets may be far from ideal---for instance, if the response variable $Y$ has higher or lower noise variance depending on the value of $X$.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/diagrams/score-shape.pdf} \caption{The conformal score function determines the shape of the sets. The shaded band is a visualization of the prediction set $\cC(X_{n+1})\subseteq\cY$ as a function of $X_{n+1}\in\cX$. On the left, the residual score gives a fixed-width band. In the middle, the scaled residual score gives a symmetric band that adapts to the non-constant noise variance. On the right, the CQR score gives an asymmetric band that follows the quantiles of the distribution. } \label{fig:score-shape}
elements.sent-2411-076: We can modify the residual score to result in intervals of different width (e.g., in settings where the variance of $Y$ is different at different values of $X$).
elements.sent-2411-077: This construction will again use a trained predictive model $\hf$, and will also require an estimate $\hat{\sigma (x)$ of the scale of the noise in $Y$ given $X = x$ (e.g., an estimate of the standard deviation), where $\hf$ and $\hat{\sigma}$ are both fitted using data points $(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_{n/2 ,Y_{n/2 )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: The scaled residual score is then defined as \begin{equation}s(x,y) = \frac{|y - \hf(x)|}{\hat{\sigma}(x)},\end{equation} and results in a prediction set \begin{equation}\cC(X_{n+1}) = \hf(X_{n+1}) \pm \hat q \cdot \hat \sigma (X_{n+1}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-079: This can lead to prediction intervals that are a better fit to the data (as compared to the residual score), since the function $\hat\sigma(x)$ can capture the nonconstant variance of the noise.
elements.sent-2411-080: Both the residual score and the scaled residual score will return symmetric intervals, which may be a poor fit for certain data distributions.
elements.sent-2411-081: This inspires a more nonparametric approach towards choosing the score.
elements.sent-2411-082: Suppose we use the data points $(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_{n/2 ,Y_{n/2 )$ to obtain an estimate $\hat{\tau (x; \alpha/2)$ of the $\alpha/2$ quantile of the distribution of $Y$ given $X = x$, and an estimate $\hat{\tau (x; 1-\alpha/2)$ of the $1-\alpha/2$ quantile---for instance, we might fit these models by running a quantile regression method.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: A straightforward way to use these estimates when confronted with a test point $X_{n+1}$ would be to output the interval $[\hat{\tau (X_{n+1 ; \alpha/2), \hat{\tau (X_{n+1 ; 1-\alpha/2)]$, using the estimated quantiles---but this may not give a coverage level of $1-\alpha$ if the estimates $\hat \tau$ are imperfect.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: Instead, the conformalized quantile regression (CQR) method uses these initial quantile estimates to construct a conformal score, so that the resulting prediction set is an adjusted version of the initial interval.
elements.sent-2411-085: Specifically, the CQR score is given by \begin{equation}s(x,y) = \max\left\{ \hat{\tau}(x; \alpha/2) - y , y - \hat{\tau}(x; 1-\alpha/2)\right\}\end{equation} which is the signed distance of $y$ to the interval $[\hat{\tau (x ; \alpha/2), \hat{\tau (x; 1-\alpha)]$.}}
elements.sent-2411-086: With this choice of $s$, the resulting conformal prediction set takes the form \begin{equation}\cC(X_{n+1}) = [\hat{\tau}(X_{n+1} ; \alpha/2) - \hat q, \hat{\tau}(X_{n+1}; 1-\alpha/2) + \hat q]\end{equation} for a number $\hat q$.
elements.sent-2411-087: That is, conformal prediction with this score function takes the prediction interval from the initial quantile estimates, and then either inflates it if $\hat{q}$ is positive or shrinks it if $\hat{q}$ is negative.
elements.sent-2411-088: The split conformal prediction algorithm can also be applied to classification problems, i.e., when the response variable takes values in a discrete set.
elements.sent-2411-089: Suppose that $\cY = \{1,\dots,K\}$ is the set of possible labels, and suppose we have an estimate $\hat{\pi (y\mid x)$ of the probability of label $Y=y$ given features $X=x$, which was trained on the data points $(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_{n/2 ,Y_{n/2 )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-090: The high-probability score is then given by \begin{equation}s(x,y) = -\hat{\pi}(y\mid x).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-091: It is important to note that the score is the negative of the estimated probability; this is because a conformal score is intended to return larger values when the data point $(x,y)$ appears more unlikely, i.e., when $\hat{\pi (y\mid x)$ is small.}
elements.sent-2411-092: If we use this score for split conformal prediction, the resulting prediction set is given by \begin{equation}\cC(X_{n+1}) = \{y : \hat{\pi}(y \mid X_{n+1}) \ge -\hat{q}\} \subseteq \{1,\dots, K\},\end{equation} which is the set of all labels $y\in\{1,\dots,K\}$ with a sufficiently high estimated probability, given the test point features $X_{n+1}$.
elements.sent-2411-093: Of course, this type of score function may also be used in the case of a continuous response $Y$, if we use an estimated conditional density in place of the estimated conditional probability.
elements.sent-2411-094: At this point, we have seen that conformal prediction can provide a marginal coverage guarantee with only weak assumptions, and can leverage the power of arbitrary predictive models---the better the predictive model, the more precise the prediction set will be.
elements.sent-2411-095: Nonetheless, there are many natural questions at this stage.
elements.sent-2411-096: Is it necessary to split the data into two parts, one for model fitting and one for the calibration of confidence intervals, as in split conformal prediction---or can the data splitting step be avoided? Which conformal score functions are optimal? Can the method be extended to cases where the data are not i.i.d.? We will address these questions throughout the book as we develop conformal prediction in full generality.
elements.section-2411-004: The conformal prediction framework in context
elements.sent-2411-097: This book discusses the statistical theory underlying conformal prediction and related techniques for providing uncertainty quantification for our predictive models---but of course, this question has long been studied in the statistics literature.
elements.sent-2411-098: What distinguishes the conformal prediction framework from other methods? As we have seen above, conformal prediction guarantees a marginal coverage property for data drawn i.i.d.\ from any distribution.
elements.sent-2411-099: We do not need to place conditions on the distribution (such as smoothness, or, a parametric model)---because of this, conformal prediction is often described as a distribution-free approach to inference.
elements.sent-2411-100: Moreover, we do not need to place conditions on the underlying model fitted to the data (such as assuming that $\hf$ is a consistent estimator of the true association between $Y$ and $X$), and the result is finite-sample, in the sense that it holds at any value of $n$ rather than offering only an asymptotic guarantee.
elements.sent-2411-101: Conformal prediction is closely related to the field of nonparametric statistics, which has also aimed to provide statistical methods that can flexibly handle data distributions that do not fall within some simple parametric model.
elements.sent-2411-102: However, there are some fundamental differences between these fields.
elements.sent-2411-103: In nonparametric and semiparametric statistics, most methods and results rely on regularity conditions that, while weaker than a parametric model assumption, are nonetheless much stronger than the minimal assumptions required by conformal.
elements.sent-2411-104: For example, we might assume smoothness conditions on the distribution of the data.
elements.sent-2411-105: With these types of assumptions, it is often possible to provide guarantees not only for predictive inference but also for estimation (for instance, estimating the mean of $Y$ given $X$).
elements.sent-2411-106: By contrast, exchangeability is a far weaker assumption than what is usually considered in nonparametric statistics.
elements.sent-2411-107: Without regularity conditions, it is still possible to provide useful and powerful methods for predictive inference, as we have seen with the marginal coverage guarantee for split conformal, above---but, as we will see in some of the hardness results in Part IV of the book, other types of inference questions become more challenging or even impossible.
elements.sent-2411-108: Conformal prediction is intimately connected with permutation testing---we will soon see that it can be formulated as the inversion of a particular permutation test.
elements.sent-2411-109: It is also closely connected to quantile estimation and distribution estimation.
elements.sent-2411-110: A natural use of quantile estimation is to give prediction intervals for test points, although such a procedure would again require assumptions on the regularity of the distribution in order to have guaranteed coverage.
elements.sent-2411-111: Farther afield are resampling approaches, such as the bootstrap and cross-validation.
elements.sent-2411-112: Unlike conformal prediction, these are most commonly applied for confidence intervals on functionals of the distribution, and require regularity conditions for validity.
elements.sent-2411-113: Having said that, we will explore distribution-free variants of cross-validation for the purpose of predictive inference later on in the book.
elements.sent-2411-114: What makes it unique is its ability to provide nonasymptotic guarantees on the quality of predictions that do not require smoothness or other regularity conditions.
elements.sent-2411-115: After this introductory chapter, the remainder of Part~\ref{part:background is an introduction to exchangeability from a mathematical point of view, with a glossary of facts and properties that will be useful for the statistical results developed later in the book.}
elements.sent-2411-116: We pay special attention to permutation tests, since conformal prediction can be reframed as inverting a permutation test.
elements.sent-2411-117: These tools will be critical to many of the proofs and intuitions in the remainder of the book.
elements.sent-2411-118: Part~\ref{part:conformal of the book then turns to the conformal prediction framework.}
elements.sent-2411-119: In particular, we discuss full conformal prediction, a generalization of the split conformal prediction method we have already introduced, which reveals the basic statistical logic at play.
elements.sent-2411-120: We then describe stronger properties than marginal coverage, with a mix of positive results for various methods, and hardness results that show the limits of what is possible without more assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-121: Part~\ref{part:extensions-conformal of the book focuses on a broad range of different extensions to the conformal prediction methodology, including cross-validation based methods within the conformal framework, weighted versions of conformal prediction that allow us to move beyond the i.i.d.\ setting, online versions of conformal methods that are designed for streaming data, and computational shortcuts for conformal prediction.}
elements.sent-2411-122: We also briefly cover additional topics such as variants of conformal prediction that can handle broader notions of risk, and connections with selective inference, multiple testing, and model aggregation---these topics are a sample of some recent work in the field, and are suggestive of the many directions for continued study.
elements.sent-2411-123: Finally, in Part~\ref{part:beyond-predictive-inference , we depart from our focus on predictive inference, and study the problem of distribution-free inference for a range of other questions: estimating a regression function, calibrating probability estimates, and testing conditional independence.}
elements.sent-2411-124: Here, we will briefly mention some other textbooks and tutorials on conformal prediction.
elements.sent-2411-125: The first such book was~Algorithmic Learning in a Random World by~\citet{vovk2005algorithmic , which introduced the mathematical framework behind conformal prediction.}
elements.sent-2411-127: Turning to the specific algorithms in this section, split conformal prediction was first described in~\citet{papadopoulos2002inductive , which also introduced the scaled residual score function; see also \citet{lei2018distribution .}}
elements.sent-2411-128: Lastly, the CQR score function is due to~\citet{romano2019conformalized , and the high-probability score is studied in~\citet{Sadinle2016LeastAS .}}
elements.sent-2411-129: In this chapter, we provide an introduction to the idea of exchangeability, which provides the core mathematical foundations of conformal prediction and many related methodologies.
elements.sent-2411-130: Exchangeability is a property of a sequence of random variables---informally, it expresses the idea that the sequence is equally likely to appear in any order.
elements.sent-2411-131: A formal definition is as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-132: We say that the random vector $(Z_1, \ldots, Z_n)$ is exchangeable if, for every permutation $\sigma\in\cS_n$, \begin{equation}(Z_1,\dots,Z_n)\eqd (Z_{\sigma(1)},\dots,Z_{\sigma(n)}),\end{equation} where $\eqd$ denotes equality in distribution, and $\cS_n$ is the set of all permutations on $[n]:=\{1,\dots,n\}$.
elements.sent-2411-133: Similarly, let $Z_1,Z_2,\dots\in\mathcal{Z}$ be an infinite sequence of random variables with a joint distribution.
elements.sent-2411-134: We say that this infinite sequence is exchangeable if $(Z_1,\dots,Z_n)$ is exchangeable for every $n\geq 1$.
elements.sent-2411-135: The elements of an exchangeable sequence are identically distributed, but not necessarily independent.
elements.sent-2411-136: Exchangeability constrains the dependence structure so that all permutations are equally likely.
elements.sent-2411-137: Throughout this book, we might interchangeably say that a random vector $(Z_1,\dots,Z_n)$ is exchangeable, or that the random variables $Z_1,\dots,Z_n$ are exchangeable.
elements.sent-2411-138: Exchangeability can arise in a broad range of scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-139: In particular, exchangeability of a sequence $Z_1,\dots,Z_n$ arises in the following important special cases:.
elements.sent-2411-140: If $Z_1,\dots,Z_n$ are sampled uniformly without replacement from a finite set $\{z_1,\dots,z_N\}\subseteq\mathcal{Z}$.
elements.sent-2411-141: If $Z_1,\dots,Z_n$ are drawn i.i.d.\ from a distribution $P$ on $\mathcal{Z}$.
elements.sent-2411-142: However, these common scenarios are far from exhaustive.
elements.sent-2411-143: As an intuitive example, consider a distribution on $Z_1,Z_2\in\{0,1\}$ defined by the probability mass function \begin{equation}p(0,0) = p(1,1) = \frac{1}{8}, \quad p(0,1) = p(1,0) = \frac{3}{8}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-144: This joint distribution is exchangeable, but cannot be expressed either via sampling without replacement or via i.i.d.\ sampling.
elements.sent-2411-145: It instead illustrates a different way that exchangeability can arise---any mixture of exchangeable distributions is itself an exchangeable distribution.
elements.sent-2411-146: Indeed, the above example can be derived as a mixture, where with probability $\frac{1 {2}$ we sample $Z_1,Z_2$ uniformly without replacement from the set $\{0,1\}$, and with probability $\frac{1 {2}$ we draw $Z_1,Z_2$ i.i.d.\ from the $\textnormal{Bernoulli (0.5)$ distribution.}}}
elements.sent-2411-147: Exchangeability can be formally described in a number of different ways.
elements.sent-2411-148: Here, we give several characterizations of exchangeability of a random vector $(Z_1,\dots,Z_n)$, to help build intuition.
elements.sent-2411-149: Exchangeability can be characterized in a simple way if the random vector $(Z_1,\dots,Z_n)$ is either discrete, or has a joint density.
elements.sent-2411-150: First, supposing $\mathcal{Z}$ is a countable space so that the $Z_i$'s are discrete, let $p:\mathcal{Z ^n\rightarrow[0,1]$ be the probability mass function for the joint distribution of $(Z_1,\dots,Z_n)$.}
elements.sent-2411-151: Then this joint distribution is exchangeable if and only if \begin{equation}p(z_1,\dots,z_n) = p(z_{\sigma(1)},\dots,z_{\sigma(n)})\textnormal{ for all $z_1,\dots,z_n\inZ$ and for all $\sigma\in\cS_n$}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-173: Permutation tests are used in statistics for a wide range of different inference tasks, most of which are not related to the problem of prediction.
elements.sent-2411-174: Often, a permutation test can be expressed as testing the null hypothesis of exchangeability.
elements.sent-2411-175: To understand this, we will first define permutation testing in general and will examine its properties as a test of exchangeability, and then will give two concrete examples of commonly used permutation tests to reinterpret them within the framework of exchangeability.
elements.sent-2411-176: Let $\mathcal{P}$ be the set of all distributions on $\mathcal{Z ^n$, and let $\mathcal{P _{\textnormal{exch }\subseteq\mathcal{P}$ be the subset of distributions for which exchangeability is satisfied.}}}
elements.sent-2411-177: Consider a random vector $(Z_1,\dots,Z_n)$ drawn from some joint distribution $P$.
elements.sent-2411-178: We would like to perform a hypothesis test of \begin{equation}H_0: \ P\inP_{\textnormal{exch}} \textnormal{\quad versus \quad} H_1: \ P\inP\backslashP_{\textnormal{exch}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-179: Before observing the data, we fix any function $T:\mathcal{Z ^n\rightarrow\R$, with the intuition that a large value of our test statistic $T(Z_1,\dots,Z_n)$ will indicate evidence against exchangeability.}
elements.sent-2411-180: Then we define the quantity \begin{equation}\label{eq:perm-pvalue} p = \frac{\sum_{\sigma\in\cS_n}\ind{T(Z_{\sigma(1)},\dots,Z_{\sigma(n)}) \geq T(Z_1,\dots,Z_n)}}{n!}\end{equation} which compares the observed value of the test statistic, $T(Z_1,\dots,Z_n)$, against all possible values obtained via permutations of the data.
elements.sent-2411-181: For any function $T:\mathcal{Z ^n\rightarrow\R$, the p-value $p$ defined in~\eqref{eq:perm-pvalue satisfies $\mathbb{P _P(p\leq \tau)\leq \tau$ for all $\tau\in[0,1]$ and all $P\in\mathcal{P _{\textnormal{exch }$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-182: In many settings, it is common to avoid the computational burden of computing all $n!$ permutations by instead sampling a smaller number $M$ of permutations uniformly at random, to obtain the p-value \begin{equation}\label{eq:perm-pval-random} p = \frac{1+ \sum_{m=1}^M\ind{T(Z_{\sigma_m(1)},\dots,Z_{\sigma_m(n)}) \geq T(Z_1,\dots,Z_n)}}{1+M}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-183: This p-value is again valid against the null hypothesis of exchangeability:.
elements.sent-2411-184: For any function $T:\mathcal{Z ^n\rightarrow\R$, the p-value $p$ defined in~\eqref{eq:perm-pval-random satisfies $\mathbb{P _P(p\leq \tau)\leq \tau$ for all $\tau\in[0,1]$ and all $P\in\mathcal{P _{\textnormal{exch }$, where the probability is now taken with respect to both the random draw of $(Z_1,\dots,Z_n)\sim P$, and the permutations $\sigma_1,\dots,\sigma_M$ sampled uniformly at random (with replacement) from $\cS_n$.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-186: To apply the permutation test, we need to specify a choice of the test statistic $T$.
elements.sent-2411-187: If the statistic captures the deviations from exchangeability that we expect may occur, then it will lead to a powerful test.
elements.sent-2411-188: We illustrate this with several common examples in the case of real-valued data, $\mathcal{Z = \R$---namely, testing for equality of distributions, and testing for outliers.}
elements.sent-2411-189: Other common applications of permutation tests include testing stationarity of a time series and testing independence between two random variables.
elements.sent-2411-190: Suppose that we have two independent samples from two potentially different distributions, with $n_0$ many draws from $P_0$ and $n_1=n-n_0$ many draws from $P_1$.
elements.sent-2411-191: Without loss of generality, we can take $Z_1,\dots,Z_{n_0}\iidsim P_0$ and $Z_{n_0+1 ,\dots,Z_n\iidsim P_1$.}
elements.sent-2411-192: If $P_0=P_1$, then the $Z_i$'s are i.i.d.\ from a single shared distribution $P_0=P_1$, and therefore exchangeability holds.
elements.sent-2411-193: If we conjecture that any potential difference between $P_0$ and $P_1$ would likely lead to a difference of means, we might choose the test statistic \begin{equation}T(z_1,\dots,z_n) = \left|\frac{1}{n_0}\sum_{i=1}^{n_0} z_i - \frac{1}{n_1}\sum_{i=n_0+1}^n z_i\right|,\end{equation} the difference in the sample means.
elements.sent-2411-194: Alternatively, we might make a choice that is more agnostic to the type of difference between the two distributions, \begin{equation}T(z_1,\dots,z_n) = \sup_{v\in\R} \left|\frac{1}{n_0}\sum_{i=1}^{n_0} \ind{z_i\leq v} - \frac{1}{n_1}\sum_{i=n_0+1}^n \ind{z_i\leq v}\right|,\end{equation} which measures the maximum difference between the two empirical cumulative distribution functions (CDFs).
elements.sent-2411-195: The latter is sometimes called the Kolmogorov--Smirnov statistic.
elements.paragraph-2411-009: Testing if a new data point is an outlier.
elements.sent-2411-196: Next, suppose that we would like to test whether a particular data point---say, the last data point $Z_n$---is an outlier relative to the rest of the sequence.
elements.sent-2411-197: In fact, as we will see later on, this use of the permutation test is central to the development of conformal prediction.
elements.sent-2411-198: For example, we might conjecture that $Z_n$ is more likely to be unusually large relative to the other $Z_i$'s.
elements.sent-2411-199: In this case, we could consider the test statistic \begin{equation}T(z_1,\dots,z_n) = \sum_{i=1}^n \ind{z_n > z_i},\end{equation} which is large when the rank of the last value is large among the rest of the list.

elements.sent-2411-202: Examining this quantity, we can then see that $T(Z_{\sigma(1) ,\dots,Z_{\sigma(n) ) \geq T(Z_1,\dots,Z_n)$ if and only if $Z_{\sigma(n)}\geq Z_n$, and therefore, the p-value can be simplified as \begin{equation}p = \frac{\sum_{\sigma\in\cS_n}\ind{T(Z_{\sigma(1)},\dots,Z_{\sigma(n)}) \geq T(Z_1,\dots,Z_n)}}{n!}= \frac{1}{n!}\sum_{\sigma\in\cS_n}\ind{Z_{\sigma(n)} \geq Z_n} = \frac{1}{n!}\sum_{i=1}^n\sum_{\sigma\in\cS_n,\sigma(n)=i}\ind{Z_i \geq Z_n} =\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \ind{Z_i \geq Z_n}.\end{equation}.}}

elements.sent-2411-209: We next turn to building a theoretical understanding of permutation tests, within the framework of exchangeability.

elements.sent-2411-222: The key tool in this proof is the CDF of the negative values of the test statistic, i.e., $-T(Z)$ (and its permuted version, $-T(Z_\sigma)$).

elements.section-2411-009: Appendix: order statistics, quantiles, and CDFs
elements.sent-2411-224: In this section, we will give some additional background on some technical details in order to build a strong foundation for theoretical results later in this work.
elements.sent-2411-225: This section will contain definitions and key facts about quantities such as quantiles and CDFs, which provide some of the basic ingredients for studying exchangeability and developing conformal prediction methods.
elements.sent-2411-226: The structure of the section is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-227: In Section~\ref{sec:deterministic-properties-quantiles-cdfs , we state properties of order statistics, quantiles, and CDFs of lists that hold deterministically---i.e., for any list of numbers.}
elements.sent-2411-228: In Section~\ref{sec:exchangeability-properties-quantiles-cdfs , we state implications of these properties under the assumption of exchangeability.}
elements.sent-2411-229: Finally, in Section~\ref{sec:distributional-properties-quantiles-cdfs , we look at the quantile and CDF function again from a distributional perspective, as opposed to operating over finite lists.}
elements.sent-2411-230: First, though we have referred to order statistics, CDFs, and quantiles informally throughout this chapter, we now define them rigorously for a list of real numbers, $z = (z_1, \ldots, z_n)$.
elements.sent-2411-231: We emphasize that, for the moment, we are treating the $z_i$'s as fixed values rather than random variables.
elements.sent-2411-232: Then the $k$th order statistic of $z\in\R^n$, written as $z_{(k)}$, is defined as \begin{equation}z_{(k)} = \inf\left\{ v: \sum\limits_{i=1}^n \ind{z_i \leq v } \geq k\right\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-233: The order statistics $z_{(1)}\leq \dots \leq z_{(n)}$ simply rearrange the values of $z$ into nondecreasing order.
elements.sent-2411-234: The $k$th order statistic of $z$ is always uniquely defined, although its value may occur more than once in the list in the case of ties.
elements.sent-2411-235: For example, in the vector $(3, 2, 1, 2)$, the order statistics are $z_{(1) =1$, $z_{(2) = z_{(3) = 2$, and $z_{(4) = 3$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-236: The CDF evaluated at $v$ is the fraction of data points $z_1,\dots,z_n$ that lie at or below the value $v$.
elements.sent-2411-238: In other words, the $\tau$-quantile is the smallest value $v$ such that a fraction $\tau$ of the data points lie at or below the value $v$.
elements.sent-2411-239: Order statistics, quantiles, and CDFs are intimately related.
elements.sent-2411-240: One can see them intuitively as different parameterizations of the exact same core concept: counting the number of data points falling below some value.
elements.sent-2411-241: As we will see below, the order statistics and quantiles of a finite list are essentially equivalent.
elements.sent-2411-242: Furthermore, the CDF is approximately the inverse of the quantile function when $z$ does not contain repeated values (and when it does, it provides a bound on the inverse).
elements.sent-2411-244: If all elements of $z$ are distinct, $\widehat{F _z\left( \quantile\left(z ; \tau \right) \right) = \frac{\lceil \tau n \rceil {n}$ for all $\tau\in[0,1]$.}}
elements.sent-2411-245: In contrast, in the presence of ties, the value of the CDF at the $\tau$-quantile may be substantially larger than $\tau$.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.4\textwidth]{figures/diagrams/quantiles-cdfs.pdf} \caption{An illustration of two quantiles chosen on the CDF. It can be seen that when there are ties, it results in a jump in the CDF, so $\widehat{F}_z(v_{\rm tie})$ is much larger than $ \tau_{\rm tie}$. However, when there are no ties, the gap is bounded; i.e., $\tau_{\rm no-tie}\leq \widehat{F}_z(v_{\rm no-tie}) \leq \tau_{\rm no-tie} + 1/n$.} \label{fig:cdf-ties}
elements.sent-2411-247: What happens to the order statistics, quantiles, and CDFs when we take transformations of $z$? Here we show what happens when we take monotone transformations, when we take the negative, and perhaps most importantly, when we permute.
elements.sent-2411-248: We begin with the invariance to monotone transformations.
elements.sent-2411-249: Furthermore, for $z\in\R^n$, let $f(z)\in\R^n$ denote the elementwise application of $f$ to $z$.. Then,.
elements.sent-2411-252: If additionally $f$ is a strictly increasing function, then for all $v\in\R$, $\widehat{F _{f(z) (v) = \widehat{F _z(f^{-1 (v))$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-253: From the perspective of this book, this is the most important property of quantiles and CDFs.

elements.sent-2411-268: It is more common to define quantiles of distributions, rather than of lists or sequences.
elements.sent-2411-269: For a distribution $P$ on $\mathbb{R}$, for any $\tau\in[0,1]$ the $\tau$-quantile of the distribution $P$ is defined as \begin{equation}\quantile(P;\tau) = \inf\{x\in\mathbb{R}: \mathbb{P}_P\left(X\leq x\right) \geq \tau\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-270: If $F$ is the CDF of the distribution $P$, we will sometimes equivalently write $\quantile(F;\tau)$.

elements.sent-2411-272: This distribution-based representation will be important throughout later chapters.
elements.sent-2411-273: It is well known that any mixture of exchangeable distributions is exchangeable; the most well-known result of de Finetti is the converse of this fact.
elements.sent-2411-274: In other words, any infinite exchangeable distribution can be represented as a mixture of i.i.d.\ distributions.
elements.sent-2411-275: This was first proved by de Finetti for the binary setting $\cZ=\{0,1\}$.
elements.sent-2411-276: Many authors have extended this result to broader settings (e.g., any standard Borel space $\cZ$), most notably by \cite{hewitt1955symmetric , but with many recent advances in the literature as well.}
elements.sent-2411-277: While the theorem does not hold in the finite setting, approximate versions of the result can be established, namely, \cite{diaconis1980finite 's well-known result showing that, if $Z_1,\dots,Z_n$ can be embedded into a longer exchangeable sequence $Z_1,\dots,Z_m$ for $m\gg n$, then its distribution can be approximated as a mixture of i.i.d.\ distributions.}
elements.sent-2411-278: Permutation tests have been core objects of study in statistics since at least the time of Fisher's randomized experiments, including the famous `Lady Tasting Tea'~\citep{fisher1956mathematics .}
elements.sent-2411-279: The permutation test as we know it today as a significance test for arbitrary distributions was formalized by \cite{pitman1937significance .}
elements.sent-2411-280: Since the advent of powerful computers, permutation tests have become a practical solution for testing nonparametric null hypotheses, and thus their popularity has grown~\citep{ernst2004permutation .}
elements.sent-2411-281: Critical to the practical use of permutation tests was the development of the randomized test by \cite{dwass1957modified .}
elements.sent-2411-282: In the work of Dwass and througout the late 20th century, the randomized permutation p-value was simply seen as an estimate of the quantity computed on all $n!$ permutations.
elements.sent-2411-283: Thus, it was common to calculate estimates of the p-value which can be appropriate asymptotically---essentially, this amounts to removing the `+1' in~\eqref{eq:perm-pval-random .}
elements.sent-2411-284: However, it was pointed out by~\cite{phipson2010permutation that this strategy can lead to extreme violations of the Type I error rate in finite samples, and the exact test (i.e., adding the `+1') has much better practical performance (this was likely known to some beforehand as well, as the `+1' does also appear in~\cite{lehmann1986testing ).}}
elements.sent-2411-285: The literature on permutation tests and other randomization tests has since developed significantly, and several generalizations have been proposed, e.g., to arbitrary groups~\citep{hemerik2021another, besag1989generalized , to dependent data generated by Markov chains~\citep{besag1989generalized , and to incorporate sampling weights~\citep{harrison2012conservative .}}}
elements.sent-2411-286: Other work in the area includes efficient subroutines for their computation~\citep{koning2022faster and the study of the optimality of the permutation test, e.g., in the minimax setting~\citep{kim2022minimax .}}
elements.sent-2411-287: There are several good reference books for learning about permutation tests, including~\cite{lehmann1986testing .}
elements.sent-2411-288: A proof of the sampled permutation p-value is available in~\cite{hemerik2018exact , and an earlier copy is not known to the authors of this book, although the result itself was well-known beforehand.}
elements.sent-2411-289: This chapter begins Part~\ref{part:conformal of the book, where we introduce conformal prediction, examine the ideas underlying its construction, and study its theoretical properties---its statistical guarantees, both under exchangeability and under stronger assumptions, and the hardness results that capture its limitations.}
elements.sent-2411-290: The goal of this first chapter is to provide the reader with a theoretical understanding of the conformal prediction method.
elements.sent-2411-291: We will make a connection to exchangeability, which is at the core of the conformal prediction framework.
elements.sent-2411-292: This chapter aims to provide both an understanding of how conformal prediction works and also why it works.
elements.section-2411-010: Setting: data points, datasets, and scores
elements.sent-2411-293: We begin with an exchangeable sequence of data points, $(X_1, Y_1), \ldots, (X_{n+1 , Y_{n+1 )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-294: As before, we will refer to $X_i\in\cX$ as the feature and $Y_i\in\cY$ as the response, for the $i$th data point $(X_i,Y_i)\in\cX\times\cY$.
elements.sent-2411-295: In the setting of a prediction problem, the final response value $Y_{n+1}$ is unobserved---this is the value that we are trying to predict, given the training data points $(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_n,Y_n)$ and the test feature $X_{n+1}$.
elements.sent-2411-296: Throughout the book, we generally refer to random variables with uppercase letters and fixed values with lowercase letters.
elements.sent-2411-297: Conformal prediction constructs prediction sets $\cC(X_{n+1 ) \subseteq \cY$ that satisfy marginal predictive coverage at level $1-\alpha$, that is, $\P(Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1 ))\geq 1-\alpha$.}}
elements.sent-2411-298: The set will be constructed using a score function $s$---this is a function that maps a data point $(x,y)\in\cX\times\cY$ and a dataset $\cD\in(\cX\times\cY)^k$ (for any number $k$ of data points) to a real value, $s((x,y);\cD)\in\R$.
elements.sent-2411-299: The reader should think of the score $s( (x,y) \, ; \, \cD)$ as a measure of a model's error on a single test point $(x,y)$ after training the model on the dataset $\cD$.
elements.sent-2411-300: A motivating example of a score function is the residual score, \begin{equation}\label{eq:absolute-residual} s( (x,y) \, ; \, \cD) = |y - \hf(x\, ; \, \cD)|,\end{equation} where $\hf(x\, ; \, \cD)$ is the prediction of a model trained on $\cD$ when given a feature $x$ as input.
elements.sent-2411-301: Note that this score is large when the model is badly wrong in its prediction.
elements.sent-2411-302: This will be true for all scores used in this book: a high value of the score $s((x,y);\cD)$ indicates that the value $y$ is far from what the model would have predicted given $x$, after training on the data contained in $\cD$.
elements.sent-2411-303: Throughout Part~\ref{part:conformal of the book, we require that the score function is symmetric, meaning it is invariant to permutations of the dataset $\cD$:.}
elements.sent-2411-304: Here, given a permutation $\sigma$, we use the notation $\cD_{\sigma}$ to refer to the dataset whose elements are permuted by $\sigma$, i.e., the $i$th element of $\cD_\sigma$ is given by the $\sigma(i)$th element of $\cD$.
elements.sent-2411-305: In the case of the residual score in~\eqref{eq:absolute-residual , symmetry implies that the fitted model $\hat{f (\cdot; \cD)$ is trained using some learning algorithm that is itself symmetric, i.e., it does not depend on the ordering of the data points in $\cD$.}}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{figures/diagrams/full-conformal-panel-stretched.pdf} \caption{Illustration of notation for a single hypothesized response $y$. This figure illustrates the definitions of Section~\ref{sec:define_conformal}. Each of the dark gray dots is a data point, $(X_i, Y_i)$. The larger yellow dot is the hypothesized test point $(X_{n+1}, y)$. The regression model $\hf(x ; \mathcal{D}^y_{n+1})$ is shown as a gray curve. Each score, $S_i^y$, is shown as a dotted line, representing the residual score as defined in~\eqref{eq:absolute-residual}---i.e., the absolute residual of the model $\hf(x ; \mathcal{D}^y)$ on the point $(X_i, Y_i)$ (or $(X_{n+1},y)$, for the case $i=n+1$). The quantile $\hat{q}^y$ is defined as in~\eqref{eq:full-cp-quantile}.} \label{fig:full-cp-panel}
elements.sent-2411-306: We next describe the full conformal prediction procedure, which generalizes the split conformal procedure from Chapter~\ref{sec:conformal-preview .}
elements.sent-2411-307: At a high level, the prediction sets are constructed by inverting the score function $s$ to identify possible values $y\in\cY$ for the response $Y_{n+1}$ that agree (or conform) with the trends observed in the available data.

elements.sent-2411-310: For each $y\in\cY$, we also define the augmented dataset $\cD^y_{n+1 = \left( (X_1,Y_1), \ldots, (X_n,Y_n), (X_{n+1 , y) \right)\in(\cX\times\cY)^{n+1}$, consisting of the $n$ training data points together with an additional point $(X_{n+1 ,y)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-311: This last element of $\cD^y_{n+1}$ is the hypothesized test point, where we substitute a hypothesized value $y$ in place of the unknown test response value $Y_{n+1}$.

elements.sent-2411-313: Next, as shorthand, we use the notation $S_{i ^y$ to refer to the score for the $i$th data point within the augmented dataset $\cD^y_{n+1}$, using our score function $s$---that is, for the $n$ training points $(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_n,Y_n)$, we have scores \begin{equation}S_i^y = s( (X_i, Y_i)\, ; \, \cD^y_{n+1}), \ i=1,\dots,n,\end{equation} while for the hypothesized test point $(X_{n+1 ,y)$ we have the score \begin{equation}S_{n+1}^y = s((X_{n+1},y)\,;\,\cD^y_{n+1}).\end{equation}.}}

elements.sent-2411-317: At a high level, when the conformal score $S^y_{n+1}$ is large compared to $S^y_{1 , \ldots, S^y_{n}$, then the hypothesized response $y$ is inconsistent with the data, and should therefore be excluded from the prediction set $\cC(X_{n+1 )$.}}

elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/diagrams/full-conformal.pdf} \caption{An illustration of full conformal prediction with the residual score function. Note that each center figure has a different fitted function $\hf(\cdot; \cD^y_{n+1})$, since changing the value $y$ has an effect on the regression function. elements.sent-2411-334: When implementing full conformal prediction, how do we choose a score function $s = s((x,y);\cD)$? The score function is a measure of the agreement between $y$ and $x$, typically based on a model that was fit using data $\cD$.
elements.sent-2411-335: There are many sensible ways to instantiate this broad intuition, and we outlined four examples in Section~\ref{sec:intro-scores .}
elements.sent-2411-336: The idea is that no matter how good or bad our models are, one can always make the sets wide enough to contain test points with at least the desired probability $1-\alpha$ (for example, by taking the set to be all of $\cY$).
elements.sent-2411-337: In other words, coverage will always hold, but the choice of score function affects the shape of the sets.
elements.sent-2411-338: The essential logic behind conformal prediction is that we include a point $y$ in the prediction set when $s((X_{n+1 ,y);\cD^y_{n+1 )$ is sufficiently small (given the test feature $X_{n+1}$).}}
elements.sent-2411-339: In other words, when choosing the conformal score function $s$, we are implicitly defining what types of $y$ values are sufficiently consistent with $X_{n+1}$, given the trends we observe in the training data.
elements.sent-2411-340: In this way, the choice of score function affects the shape of the resulting prediction sets.
elements.sent-2411-341: For example, we saw in Section~\ref{sec:intro-scores that the residual score, the scaled residual score, and the CQR score all resulted in prediction sets with different shapes.}
elements.sent-2411-342: The changing shape of the prediction set, which results from the choice of conformal score, certainly affects the quality of the prediction sets.
elements.sent-2411-343: A more accurate model will naturally result in smaller, and therefore more informative, prediction sets---but even beyond this point, different choices of $s$ can lead to vastly different shapes and behaviors in the resulting prediction sets.
elements.sent-2411-344: In Chapter~\ref{chapter:model-based , we will return to this point in more detail, examining the choice of the score function from a model-based theoretical perspective.}
elements.section-2411-012: Why does conformal prediction guarantee coverage?
elements.sent-2411-345: In this section, we will examine the marginal coverage guarantee of full conformal prediction (stated in Theorem~\ref{thm:full-conformal ).}
elements.sent-2411-346: To build an understanding of the idea behind the proof, let us first consider a naive procedure that does not guarantee coverage.
elements.sent-2411-347: Imagine that we have a model trained on data $\cD_n = ((X_1,Y_1), \ldots, (X_n,Y_n))$, and we want to predict a new response $Y_{n+1}$ based on the covariate $X_{n+1}$ for this test point.
elements.sent-2411-348: The scores $s((X_1,Y_1);\cD_n), \ldots, s((X_n,Y_n);\cD_n)$ on the training data points might not be directly comparable to the test point's score, $s((X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 );\cD_n)$, since our model will likely overfit to the training data---that is, the test point score $s((X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 );\cD_n)$ would likely be higher than the others.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-349: Thus, if we were to use the $1-\alpha$ quantile of the training scores, it would likely be too low to ensure the desired coverage probability for the test point.
elements.sent-2411-350: However, if we could somehow include $(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )$ into the training set, we could avoid this issue---the score for the test point would have the same distribution as the calibration scores.}}
elements.sent-2411-351: This is where full conformal prediction comes into play: it trains on all the data, including the test point.
elements.sent-2411-352: This means the test point is treated exactly the same as the training instances, ensuring exchangeability: that is, defining scores \begin{equation}\label{eqn:define_n+1_scores}S_i = s((X_i,Y_i);\cD_{n+1})\end{equation} for training points $i=1,\dots,n$ as well as for test point $i=n+1$, these scores are exchangeable (as we will verify formally below).
elements.sent-2411-353: Of course, computing these scores cannot be done in practice, since the test point's response value $Y_{n+1}$ is unknown, and so we cannot train our model on the larger dataset $\cD_{n+1}$.
elements.sent-2411-354: To get around this, conformal prediction's core idea is to consider including the data point $(X_{n+1 ,y)$ into the training set, for each possible value $y\in\cY$.}
elements.sent-2411-355: With this intuition in place, we are now ready to present our first proof of the marginal coverage property of full conformal prediction.
elements.sent-2411-356: We will give some alternative proofs and perspectives later on in Section~\ref{sec:reinterpret-conformal .}
elements.sent-2411-357: Our first step will derive an equivalent definition of the prediction set $\cC_n(X_{n+1 )$.}
elements.sent-2411-358: To do this, we will need a lemma:.

elements.formula-2411-044: \begin{equation}v_{n+1} \leq \quantile\left( v_1, \ldots, v_{n+1} \, ; \, t\right) \Longleftrightarrow v_{n+1} \leq \quantile\left( v_1, \ldots, v_n \, ; \, t (1+1/n) \right).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-360: Since by definition we have \begin{equation}\hat{q}^y = \quantile\left(S_1^y,\dots,S_n^y;(1-\alpha)(1+1/n)\right),\end{equation} for each $y\in\cY$, this lemma implies that \begin{equation}\label{eqn:standard-proof-coverage-equiv} y \in \cC(X_{n+1}) \Longleftrightarrow S_{n+1}^y \leq \hat{q}^y \Longleftrightarrow S_{n+1}^y\leq \quantile\left(S_1^y,\dots,S_{n+1}^y;1-\alpha\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-361: In other words, the prediction set $\cC(X_{n+1 ) $ can be constructed by comparing the test point score, $S_{n+1 ^y$, against the $(1-\alpha)$-quantile of the augmented list of scores, $S_1^y,\dots,S_{n+1 ^y$ (i.e., this list includes the test point score $S_{n+1 ^y$ itself).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-362: This therefore implies that the coverage event can equivalently be written as \begin{equation}\label{eqn:standard-proof-coverage-equiv_Yn+1}Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1})\Longleftrightarrow S_{n+1}\leq \quantile\left(S_1,\dots,S_{n+1};1-\alpha\right),\end{equation} where the scores $S_1,\dots,S_{n+1}$ are defined as in~\eqref{eqn:define_n+1_scores above.}
elements.sent-2411-363: From this point on, then, to establish the coverage guarantee, we only need to show that the event $S_{n+1}\leq \quantile\left(S_1,\dots,S_{n+1 ;1-\alpha\right)$ holds with $\geq 1-\alpha$ probability.}
