elements.section-2411-013: Split conformal prediction as a special case
elements.sent-2411-385: Split conformal prediction, which we previewed earlier in Chapter~\ref{chapter:introduction , is a variant of conformal prediction that uses data splitting to avoid the need to retrain the conformal score function $s$ for each possible response value $y\in\cY$.}
elements.sent-2411-386: It differs from full conformal prediction in two important ways, as summarized in Table~\ref{tab:conformal .}
elements.figure-2411-005: \begin{tabular}{c|c} Full Conformal Split Conformal All data used for both training and calibration Disjoint datasets for training and calibration Retrains the model for each value $y\in\cY$ No model retraining (requires only one model fit) Requires a symmetric score function $s$ Works for any (pretrained) score function $s$ \end{tabular} \caption{Comparison of full and split conformal methods} \label{tab:conformal}
elements.sent-2411-387: Despite the large operational differences in the algorithms, split conformal prediction is a special case of the full conformal method presented in Algorithm~\ref{alg:full-cp .}
elements.sent-2411-388: We formally describe the specialization now.
elements.sent-2411-389: Let us consider a pretraining set $\cD_{\rm pre}$, which is disjoint from $\cD_n=((X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_n,Y_n))$.
elements.sent-2411-390: For example, in the context of a residual score~\eqref{eq:absolute-residual , we can imagine a scenario where the predictive model $\hf$ was pretrained on earlier data, $\cD_{\rm pre}$, and we now have a calibration (or holdout) set $\cD_n$ consisting of $n$ many new data points that we may use to quantify our uncertainty around $\hf$.}
elements.sent-2411-391: In particular, since $s((x,y);\cD) = s(x,y)$ does not depend on $\cD$, trivially this is a symmetric score function.
elements.sent-2411-392: Henceforth, split conformal prediction refers to the case where the score function $s((x,y); \cD)$ does not depend on $\cD$.
elements.sent-2411-393: In this case, we write $s(x,y)$ as shorthand for $s( (x,y) ;\, \cD )$.
elements.sent-2411-394: With all this in mind, we can then construct the split conformal prediction set as \begin{equation}\label{eq:split-conformal-sets} \cC(X_{n+1}) = \{ y : s(X_{n+1},y) \leq \hat{q} \},\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\label{eq:split-conformal-quantile} \hat{q} = \quantile\left( S_1, \ldots, S_n; \; (1-\alpha)(1+1/n) \right),\end{equation} and where we use the notation $S_i$ as shorthand for $s(X_i, Y_i)$.
elements.sent-2411-395: Note that $\hat{q}$ is equivalent to the threshold $\hat{q ^y$ defined for full conformal in~\eqref{eq:full-cp-quantile (since the score function does not depend on the dataset, there is no dependence on $y$---i.e., $S_i = S_i^y$ for all $y$, and consequently, $\hat{q = \hat{q ^y$ for all $y$).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-396: An explicit algorithm for split conformal prediction is given in Algorithm~\ref{alg:split-cp .}
elements.sent-2411-397: Note that it is simpler and more computationally efficient than Algorithm~\ref{alg:full-cp , and therefore used much more in practice.}
elements.sent-2411-398: Input pretraining dataset $\cD_{\rm pre}$, calibration data $(X_1, Y_1), ..., (X_n, Y_n)$, test point $X_{n+1}$, target coverage level $1-\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-399: Using the pretraining dataset $\cD_{\rm pre}$, construct a conformal score function $s:\cX\times\cY\to\R$.
elements.sent-2411-400: Compute the conformal scores on the calibration set, $S_i = s(X_i, Y_i)$ for $i\in[n]$.
elements.subsection-2411-006: Exchangeability and split conformal: a closer look
elements.sent-2411-403: There is a subtle difference in the interpretation of the conformal score in split versus full conformal prediction.
elements.sent-2411-404: In full conformal prediction, the scores $S_1^y,\dots,S_{n+1 ^y$ are computed on the augmented dataset, $\cD^y_{n+1}$, using a score function trained on this same augmented dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-405: Thus, in full conformal, verifying the exchangeability of $(S_1,\dots,S_{n+1 ) = (S_1^{Y_{n+1 ,\dots,S_{n+1 ^{Y_{n+1 )$ only relies on the exchangeability of $\cD_{n+1 = D^{Y_{n+1 _{n+1}$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-406: In contrast, for split conformal prediction, there are two datasets at play: the calibration set $\cD_n$ and the pretraining set $\cD_{\rm pre}$.
elements.sent-2411-407: The vector of scores $(S_1,\dots,S_{n+1 )$ depends on both datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-408: So, to establish exchangeability of the scores, we need to consider the additional randomness coming from $\cD_{\rm pre}$.
elements.sent-2411-409: The most straightforward formal argument to justify split conformal prediction is to assume that $\cD_{\rm pre}$ is independent of the calibration data and test point, i.e., \begin{equation}((X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_{n+1},Y_{n+1})) \indep \cD_{\rm pre}\textnormal{ (and $(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_{n+1},Y_{n+1})$ are exchangeable)}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-410: In that case, by conditioning on $\cD_{\rm pre}$, we can think of the score function $s:\cX\times\cY\to\R$ (which was constructed based on $\cD_{\rm pre}$) as a fixed function---and therefore, exchangeability of $(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )$ directly implies exchangeability of the scores $s(X_1,Y_1),\dots,s(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-411: More generally, however, to achieve marginal coverage it is sufficient to assume a strictly weaker condition: \begin{equation}\label{eq:split-training-conditional-exchangeability} ((X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_{n+1},Y_{n+1}))\mid \cD_{\rm pre}\textnormal{ is exchangeable,}\end{equation} i.e., $((X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 ))$ has an exchangeable conditional distribution (when conditioning on $\cD_{\rm pre}$).}}
elements.sent-2411-412: For instance, this assumption holds whenever the pretraining, calibration, and test data---i.e., the entire dataset ($\cD_{\rm pre , \cD_n , (X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 ))$, containing $n_{\rm pre +n+1$ many data points---satisfies exchangeability.}}}}
elements.subsection-2411-007: Statistical versus computational efficiency for split and full conformal
elements.sent-2411-413: In general, split conformal is far more computationally efficient than full conformal---for example, for the residual score~\eqref{sec:score-preview , split conformal only needs to train one model $\hat{f (\cdot;\cD_{\rm pre )$ (i.e., train on $\cD_{\rm pre}$), but full conformal needs to train $\hat{f (\cdot;\cD^y_{n+1 )$ for every possible value $y\in\cY$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-414: On the other hand, as we will now explain, full conformal is more efficient in a statistical sense.
elements.sent-2411-415: In practical implementations of these methods, it is common to use $n$ to denote the total sample size of available data---that is, for split conformal, the pretraining set and the calibration set are taken to be disjoint subsets of the available dataset, and thus have a combined size of $n$.
elements.sent-2411-416: This means that the greatly improved computational efficiency of Algorithm~\ref{alg:split-cp (split conformal) comes with a statistical cost: the pretraining set and the calibration set each have sample size smaller than $n$ (say, $n/2$ data points each---as in our earlier notation, in Chapter~\ref{chapter:introduction ), potentially leading to wider prediction intervals.}}
elements.sent-2411-417: In contrast, in Algorithm~\ref{alg:full-cp , full conformal prediction uses all $n$ data points both for training (i.e., fitting a score function) and for calibration.}
elements.sent-2411-418: In this book, for consistency of notation across both split and full conformal prediction, we will continue to use $n$ to denote the sample size of the calibration set in the case of split conformal, as in Algorithm~\ref{alg:split-cp .}
elements.sent-2411-419: This notation reveals the way in which split conformal prediction can be viewed as a special case of full conformal prediction, and enables a more unified presentation of the theoretical results across these different variants of the method.
elements.sent-2411-420: In this section, we give some different interpretations of conformal prediction to give intuition about the procedure and connect it to standard ideas in statistics, such as permutation tests.
elements.subsection-2411-008: Conformal prediction as a permutation test
elements.sent-2411-421: In this section, we formulate conformal prediction as a permutation test.
elements.sent-2411-422: In particular, it can be viewed as a test of whether $(X_{n+1 ,y)$ is an outlier relative to the other data points, $(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_n,Y_n)$, as in the last example from Section~\ref{sec:permtest-examples .}}
elements.sent-2411-423: We will do this by defining the conformal p-value, which lets us build an explicit connection between constructing prediction sets and testing hypotheses.
elements.sent-2411-424: This p-value is asking whether the hypothesized test point $(X_{n+1 ,y)$ appears to follow the same distribution as the training data $(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_n,Y_n)$---if not, its score $s((X_{n+1 ,y);\cD^y_{n+1 )$ might be substantially larger than the other scores, and consequently its p-value $p^y$ will likely be small.}}}
elements.sent-2411-425: We can interpret $p^y$ as a p-value testing the hypothesis of exchangeability of the data points $(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_n,Y_n),(X_{n+1 ,y)$.}
elements.sent-2411-426: We can then repeat this reasoning for every possible value $y \in \mathcal{Y}$, and collect all the plausible values (i.e., values $y$ for which $p^y$ is not too small) into a prediction set.
elements.sent-2411-427: It turns out that conformal prediction is exactly equivalent to this procedure:.
elements.sent-2411-428: The full conformal prediction set $\cC(X_{n+1 )$ defined in~\eqref{eq:full-cp-set-construction satisfies \begin{equation}\cC(X_{n+1}) = \left\{y\in\cY: p^y > \alpha\right\},\end{equation} where $p^y$ is the conformal p-value (Definition~\ref{def:conformal-pvalue ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-429: Next, by definition of the quantile of a finite list, for any $\tau\in[0,1]$ and any $t\in\R$ it holds that \begin{equation}\quantile\left(S^y_1,\dots,S^y_n;\tau\right)< t \Longleftrightarrow \sum_{i=1}^n \ind{S^y_i < t} \geq n\tau.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-431: Finally, we return to the p-value: we calculate \begin{equation}p^y = \frac{1 + \sum_{i=1}^n \ind{S^y_i\geq S^y_{n+1}}}{n+1} = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^n \ind{S^y_i<S^y_{n+1}}}{n+1},\end{equation} i.e., $p^y\leq \alpha$ if and only if $y\not\in\cC(X_{n+1 )$, as desired.}
elements.sent-2411-432: So far, we have only established a deterministic result: for any dataset, the full conformal set can equivalently be constructed via the conformal p-value.
elements.sent-2411-433: Now we are ready to turn to the question of coverage.
elements.sent-2411-434: We will see that $p^y$ can be reinterpreted as the p-value for a permutation test, and then validity of permutation tests will imply the marginal coverage property of full conformal prediction.
elements.sent-2411-435: First we recall the permutation test p-value, developed in Section~\ref{sec:perm_test : given any test function $T:\cZ^{n+1}\to\R$, we define \begin{equation}p_{\rm perm} = \frac{\sum_{\sigma\in\cS_{n+1}}\ind{T(Z_{\sigma(1)},\dots,Z_{\sigma(n+1)})\geq T(Z_1,\dots,Z_{n+1})}}{(n+1)!},\end{equation} where, as before, $\cS_{n+1}$ denotes the set of permutations on $[n+1]=\{1,\dots,n+1\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-436: To complete the proof, then, we will verify that \begin{equation}Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1})\Longleftrightarrow p_{\rm perm}> \alpha,\end{equation} when the test function $T$ is chosen appropriately.
elements.sent-2411-437: In particular, in light of Proposition~\ref{prop:conformal-via-pvalues , it suffices to show that \begin{equation}\label{eqn:full-conformal-perm-pvalue}p_{\rm perm} = p^{Y_{n+1}}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-438: We will choose the test function $T$ as \begin{equation}T(z_1,\dots,z_{n+1}) = s\left(z_{n+1};(z_1,\dots,z_{n+1})\right),\end{equation} i.e., the score for the last data point $z_{n+1}$ when trained on a dataset $(z_1,\dots,z_{n+1 )$.}
elements.sent-2411-439: In particular, we can calculate the value of this test function on the permuted dataset $(Z_{\sigma(1) ,\dots,Z_{\sigma(n+1) )$ as \begin{equation}T(Z_{\sigma(1)},\dots,Z_{\sigma(n+1)}) = s(Z_{\sigma(n+1)}\,;\, (\cD_{n+1})_\sigma) = s(Z_{\sigma(n+1)}\,;\,\cD_{n+1}) = S_{\sigma(n+1)},\end{equation} where the first step holds since $(\cD_{n+1 )_{\sigma =(Z_{\sigma(1) ,\dots,Z_{\sigma(n+1) )$ by definition, and the second step holds since $s$ is assumed to be symmetric.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-440: Therefore, \begin{equation}p_{\rm perm} = \frac{\sum_{\sigma\in\cS_{n+1}} \ind{S_{\sigma(n+1)} \geq S_{n+1}}}{(n+1)!} = \sum_{i=1}^{n+1} n! \cdot \frac{ \ind{S_i \geq S_{n+1}}}{(n+1)!},\end{equation} where the last step holds since, for each $i\in[n+1]$, there are exactly $n!$ many permutations $\sigma\in\cS_{n+1}$ for which $\sigma(n+1)=i$.
elements.sent-2411-441: After simplifying, we then have \begin{equation}p_{\rm perm} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n+1} \ind{S_i \geq S_{n+1}}}{n+1} = \frac{1 + \sum_{i=1}^n \ind{S_i \geq S_{n+1}}}{n+1} = p^{Y_{n+1}},\end{equation} with the last step holding since $S_i = S_i^{Y_{n+1 }$ for all $i$ by definition.}
elements.subsection-2411-009: Conditioning on the empirical distribution of the data
elements.sent-2411-443: Another interpretation of conformal prediction is given by treating the dataset as an unordered collection of data points, and then using exchangeability to argue that the test point $(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )$ is equally likely to be any one of these points.}}
elements.sent-2411-444: In particular, if each data point $(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )$ is distinct, then if we observe the unordered set of $n+1$ values (i.e., we observe the unordered set of data points $\{(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )\}$, but do not know which data value corresponds to which index $i\in[n+1]$), then the test point $(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )$ is equally likely to be any one of these $n+1$ values.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-445: To formalize this type of conditional statement, given the dataset $\cD_{n+1 = ((X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 ))$ containing the training data and test point, recalling our notation from Chapter~\ref{chapter:exchangeability , we define its empirical distribution as \begin{equation}\widehat{P}_{n+1} = \frac{1}{n+1}\sum_{i=1}^{n+1}\delta_{(X_i,Y_i)}.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-446: This is a discrete distribution on $\cX\times\cY$, and in the case that the $n+1$ data points are distinct it can be simply defined by placing mass $\frac{1 {n+1}$ on each value that appears in the dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-447: More generally, values that appear multiple times in the dataset will be given higher probability under this empirical distribution.
elements.sent-2411-448: The following proof makes critical use of Proposition~\ref{prop:empirical-distrib-exch , which tells us that due to the exchangeability of the dataset $\cD_{n+1}$, after conditioning on the empirical distribution $\widehat{P _{n+1}$ the distribution of the test point $(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )$ is equal to $\widehat{P _{n+1}$ itself.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-449: To emphasize this point, we will use the notation $s(\cdot ;\widehat{P _{n+1 )$ in place of $s(z;\cD_{n+1 )$ throughout the remainder of this proof.}}}
elements.sent-2411-450: We will also write $Z_i = (X_i,Y_i)$ for the $i$th data point.
elements.sent-2411-452: Conditioning on $\widehat{P _{n+1}$, by Proposition~\ref{prop:empirical-distrib-exch , the test point $Z_{n+1}$ has distribution $\widehat{P _{n+1}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-453: This implies that, again conditioning on $\widehat{P _{n+1}$, the random variable $h(Z_{n+1 )$ has distribution $\frac{1 {n+1}\sum_{i=1 ^{n+1}\delta_{h(Z_i)}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-454: In particular, \begin{equation}\P\left(h(Z_{n+1})\leq \quantile\big((h(Z_i))_{i\in[n+1]};1-\alpha\big)\,\middle|\,\widehat{P}_{n+1}\right) \geq 1-\alpha,\end{equation} by definition of the quantile.
elements.sent-2411-455: Next, observe that since this calculation is carried out conditionally on $\widehat{P _{n+1}$, the same argument holds for a function $h$ that depends on the empirical distribution $\widehat{P _{n+1}$ as well as on a data point $z\in\cZ$---in particular, we can take $h(z) = s(z;\widehat{P _{n+1 )$, to obtain \begin{equation}\P\left(s(Z_{n+1};\widehat{P}_{n+1}) \leq \quantile\Big((s(Z_i;\widehat{P}_{n+1}))_{i\in[n+1]};1-\alpha\Big)\,\middle|\,\widehat{P}_{n+1}\right) \geq 1-\alpha.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-456: Since $S_i = s(Z_i;\cD_{n+1 ) = s(Z_i;\widehat{P _{n+1 )$ for each $i\in[n+1]$, by definition, we then have \begin{equation}\P(Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1})\mid \widehat{P}_{n+1}) = \P\left(S_{n+1}\leq \quantile(S_1,\dots,S_{n+1};1-\alpha)\,\middle|\,\widehat{P}_{n+1}\right) \geq 1-\alpha,\end{equation} where the first step applies~\eqref{eqn:standard-proof-coverage-equiv_Yn+1 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-457: Marginalizing over $\widehat{P _{n+1}$, we have proved the claim.}
elements.subsection-2411-010: Tuning based on a plug-in estimate of the error rate
elements.sent-2411-458: We next consider an especially simple and intuitive interpretation of split conformal prediction.
elements.sent-2411-459: Recall that in split conformal prediction, we work with a pretrained score function $s(x,y)$ that does not depend on the calibration set.
elements.sent-2411-460: With this in hand, consider prediction sets of the form \begin{equation}\label{eq:conformal_sets_with_lambda} \cC(X_{n+1}; \lambda) = \{y : s(X_{n+1}, y) \le \lambda\},\end{equation} where $\lambda \in \mathbb{R $ is a parameter controlling the size of the set.}
elements.sent-2411-461: Split conformal prediction can be understood as selecting $\lambda = \hat{q = \quantile\left(S_1, \ldots, S_n ; (1-\alpha)(1+1/n)\right)$.}
elements.sent-2411-462: Why choose this value? We next offer an explanation.
elements.sent-2411-463: A natural way to choose the parameter $\lambda$ is by choosing it to control the coverage on an available dataset.
elements.sent-2411-464: More formally, let \begin{equation}\hat{R}(\lambda) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n \ind{Y_i \notin \cC(X_i; \lambda)} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n \ind{s(X_i, Y_i) > \lambda} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n\ind{S_i>\lambda}\end{equation} be the empirical miscoverage on the calibration data.
elements.sent-2411-465: If the data points $(X_i,Y_i)$ are i.i.d.\ draws from some distribution $P$ on $\cX\times\cY$, then $\hat{R (\lambda)$ is a noisy estimate of the population miscoverage, \begin{equation}R(\lambda) = \mathbb{E}_P[\ind{Y \notin \cC(X; \lambda)}] = 1 - \P_P(Y \in \cC(X;\lambda)).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-466: If we had knowledge of $R(\lambda)$, we would simply set $\lambda$ to be the smallest value such that $R(\lambda) \le \alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-467: In words, we take the smallest sets that have the desired coverage level.
elements.sent-2411-468: Since we do not know $R(\lambda)$, one reasonable approach is a plug-in method: we could choose $\lambda$ to be the smallest value such that $\hat{R (\lambda)\leq \alpha$.}
elements.sent-2411-469: This plug-in method is appealing, but it does not quite guarantee a bound on the true risk $R(\hat\lambda)$, since $\hat{R (\hat\lambda)$ is a noisy estimate of this true risk.}
elements.sent-2411-470: To correct for this issue, we will now see that we should instead find a value $\lambda$ such the coverage is at least $(1-\alpha)(1+1/n)$ on the calibration data.
elements.sent-2411-471: That is, we should choose $\hat\lambda$ as \begin{equation}\label{eq:conformal-plugin-estimate-lambdahat} \hat\lambda = \inf\left\{\lambda \in \R : \hat{R}(\lambda) \leq \alpha'\right\},\end{equation} where $\alpha' = \alpha - \frac{1-\alpha {n}$.}
elements.sent-2411-472: See Figure~\ref{fig:calibration_viz for a visualization of this definition.}
elements.sent-2411-473: The risk threshold $\alpha'$ is slightly lower than the naive value of $\alpha$, indicating that we must be slightly more conservative than the naive plug-in approach to guarantee coverage.
elements.sent-2411-474: Though it may not be obvious, this choice of $\hat\lambda$ is exactly equivalent to the split conformal prediction algorithm; the next proposition formalizes this connection.
elements.sent-2411-475: The split conformal prediction set $\cC(X_{n+1 )$ defined in~\eqref{eq:split-conformal-sets satisfies \begin{equation}\cC(X_{n+1}) = \left\{y\in\cY: s(X_{n+1}, y) \leq \hat\lambda \right\},\end{equation} where $\hat\lambda$ is defined in~\eqref{eq:conformal-plugin-estimate-lambdahat .}}}
elements.sent-2411-476: By definition of $\hat{R}$ and of $\alpha'$, we calculate \begin{equation}\hat{R}(\lambda) \leq \alpha' \Longleftrightarrow \frac{1}{n}\sum\limits_{i=1}^n \ind{S_i > \lambda} \leq \alpha-\frac{1-\alpha}{n} \Longleftrightarrow \frac{1}{n}\sum\limits_{i=1}^n \ind{s(X_i, Y_i) \leq \lambda} \geq 1 - \left(\alpha-\frac{1-\alpha}{n}\right) = (1-\alpha)(1+1/n).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-477: Plugging this into the definition of $\hat\lambda$, we have \begin{equation}\hat\lambda = \inf\left\{ \lambda \in \R : \frac{1}{n}\sum\limits_{i=1}^n \ind{s(X_i, Y_i) \leq \lambda} \geq (1-\alpha)(1+1/n) \right\},\end{equation} which is exactly the definition of the conformal quantile $\hat{q}$ for split conformal.
elements.sent-2411-478: Finally, we give a proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:full-conformal (for the special case of split conformal) from the perspective of plug-in estimates of error rates.}
elements.sent-2411-479: The quantity $\tilde{\lambda}$ is an oracle version of $\hat\lambda$ that depends on the full dataset $\cD_{n+1}$, including both the calibration and test data (while, in contrast, $\hat\lambda$ depends only on the calibration data $\cD_n$).
elements.sent-2411-480: Since $\tilde\lambda$ is a symmetric function of the $n+1$ data points, exchangeability of the data implies that \begin{equation}\label{eqn:lambda_tilde_exchangeability} \P(S_{n+1} > \tilde\lambda) = \P(S_i > \tilde\lambda),\end{equation} for all $i\in[n+1]$.
elements.sent-2411-481: Below, we will show that $\tilde{\lambda \leq \hat\lambda$ must always hold.}
elements.sent-2411-482: This is enough to prove the result, since we then have \begin{equation}\P(Y_{n+1}\not\in\cC(X_{n+1})) =\P(S_{n+1})>\hat\lambda)\textnormal{ \ by Proposition~\ref{prop:conformal-equivalent-plugin}} \leq \P(S_{n+1} > \tilde{\lambda}) \textnormal{ \ since $\tilde{\lambda} \leq \hat\lambda$} = \E\left[ \ind{S_{n+1} > \tilde{\lambda}} \right] = \E\left[\frac{1}{n+1}\sum\limits_{i=1}^{n+1} \ind{S_i > \tilde{\lambda}} \right] \leq \alpha,\end{equation} where the next-to-last step holds by~\eqref{eqn:lambda_tilde_exchangeability , while the last step applies the definition of $\tilde\lambda$.}
elements.sent-2411-485: This means the infimum in the definition of $\tilde{\lambda}$ is taken over a smaller set than the infimum in the definition of $\hat\lambda$, proving the desired claim.
elements.section-2411-015: Can conformal prediction be overly conservative?
elements.sent-2411-486: So far, we have discussed lower bounds on coverage, which say the prediction sets will cover the ground truth with at least probability $1-\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-487: But this leaves open the possibility that the sets are unnecessarily large.
elements.sent-2411-488: This section gives a distribution-free bound on exactly how conservative conformal prediction can get---i.e., how much the coverage probability could potentially exceed the target level $1-\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-489: Under the conditions of Theorem~\ref{thm:full-conformal , we have that \begin{equation}\P(Y_{n+1} \in \cC(X_{n+1})) \leq \frac{\lceil (1-\alpha)(n+1)\rceil}{n+1} + \epsilon_{\rm tie} \leq 1-\alpha+ \frac{1}{n+1}+\epsilon_{\rm tie},\end{equation} where $\epsilon_{\rm tie}$ captures the likelihood of the score of the $(n+1)$st data point being tied with any another data point, \begin{equation}\epsilon_{\rm tie} = \P\left(\exists j\in[n], \ S_{n+1} = S_j\right).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-490: This theorem says that the coverage of conformal prediction is not too far from $1-\alpha$ as long as the distribution of the scores is unlikely to produce ties.
elements.sent-2411-491: Without making further assumptions on the model, this does not directly translate to a bound on the size of the prediction set $\cC(X_{n+1 )$.}
elements.sent-2411-492: Instead, the bound says that this set is not conservative on the scale of coverage.
elements.sent-2411-493: For example, for a residual score $s$ of the form $s((x,y);\cD) = |y - \hf(x;\cD)|$, a model $\hf(\cdot;\cD)$ that is a very poor fit to the data distribution will necessarily lead to wide prediction intervals.
elements.sent-2411-494: However, this result is telling us that the prediction intervals are no wider than is needed to compensate for the errors in $\hf(\cdot;\cD)$.
elements.sent-2411-495: Writing $S_{(1)}\leq \dots\leq S_{(n+1)}$ as the order statistics of the scores $S_1,\dots,S_{n+1}$, we can equivalently write \begin{equation}Y_{n+1} \in \cC(X_{n+1}) \Longleftrightarrow S_{n+1}\leq S_{(k)} \textnormal{ where $k=\lceil (1-\alpha)(n+1)\rceil$,}\end{equation} by the equivalence between quantiles and order statistics (Fact~\ref{fact:conversion-order-stats-quantiles ).}
elements.sent-2411-496: We can assume $k \in[n]$ to avoid the trivial case.
elements.sent-2411-497: Next, by definition of the order statistics, \begin{equation}S_{n+1}\leq S_{(k)} \Longleftrightarrow\textnormal{ either $S_{n+1} < S_{(k+1)}$ or $S_{n+1} = S_{(k)} = S_{(k+1)}$}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-090: \begin{equation}S_{n+1}\leq S_{(k)} \Longleftrightarrow\textnormal{ either $S_{n+1} < S_{(k+1)}$ or $S_{n+1} = S_{(k)} = S_{(k+1)}$}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-498: If $S_{n+1 = S_{(k) = S_{(k+1)}$ then we must have $S_{n+1 =S_j$ for some $j\in[n]$, and therefore, \begin{equation}\P(Y_{n+1}\in\cC_{n+1}) = \P(S_{n+1} < S_{(k+1)}) + \P(S_{n+1} = S_{(k)} = S_{(k+1)}) \leq \P(S_{n+1} < S_{(k+1)}) +\epsilon_{\rm tie}.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-499: Finally, to complete the proof, we have \begin{equation}\P(S_{n+1} < S_{(k+1)}) \leq \frac{(k+1)-1}{n+1} = \frac{\lceil (1-\alpha)(n+1)\rceil}{n+1}\end{equation} by Fact~\ref{fact:exchangeable-properties .}
elements.sent-2411-500: An important corollary of Theorem~\ref{thm:upper-bound is that, when the joint distribution of the scores is continuous, then the bound holds with $\epsilon_{\rm tie = 0$.}}
elements.sent-2411-501: This is the most familiar version of the bound, stated below.
elements.sent-2411-502: Under the conditions of Theorem~\ref{thm:full-conformal , further assume that the scores $S_1,\dots,S_{n+1}$, have a continuous joint distribution.. Then \begin{equation}\P\left( Y_{n+1} \in \cC(X_{n+1}) \right) \leq 1-\alpha + \frac{1}{n+1}.\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-519: Conformal prediction guarantees marginal coverage---on average over all the data---but in truth we often want to ensure stronger guarantees.
elements.sent-2411-520: Marginal coverage does not rule out the following problems:.
elements.sent-2411-521: We could see a misleading batch of training data that causes us to miscover on future data points.
elements.sent-2411-522: There could be multiple subgroups in our data---say, group A and group B---and the intervals could badly miscover for tests points in group A while overcovering for test points in group B.
elements.sent-2411-523: If we want to ensure that these issues are unlikely to arise, we need to examine the conditional coverage properties of conformal prediction.
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics{figures/diagrams/conditional-coverage.pdf} \caption{An illustration of marginal and conditional coverage. The interval on the left satisfies marginal coverage, but does not satisfy coverage conditional on the value of the test point $X_{n+1}$---there is undercoverage for larger values of $X_{n+1}$. In contrast, the interval on the right satisfies both properties.} \label{fig:conditional-coverage}
elements.sent-2411-524: In the simplest cases---for example, achieving coverage conditional on the event $X_{n+1 \in \cX_0$, for some large region $\cX_0\subseteq\cX$---conditional coverage is relatively easy to achieve, but in other contexts it can be hard or even impossible.}
elements.sent-2411-525: In this chapter, we explore these questions of conditional coverage.
elements.sent-2411-526: In some settings we will provide procedures that achieve conditional guarantees, while in other settings, we will establish hardness results---that is, results showing that achieving some types of conditional coverage is impossible without further assumptions.
elements.section-2411-016: Training-conditional coverage for split conformal prediction
elements.sent-2411-527: We first examine the coverage of conformal prediction conditional on the training set: \begin{equation}\P(Y_{n+1} \in \cC(X_{n+1}) \mid \cD_n).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-528: This is the empirical coverage that we would obtain if we used the points $\cD_n = ((X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_n,Y_n))$ for training (or, in the case of split conformal, for calibration), and then evaluated our coverage over an infinite test set.
elements.sent-2411-529: This quantity is a random variable, since it depends on the training set $\cD_n$.
elements.sent-2411-530: If the conformal prediction set satisfies marginal coverage at level $1-\alpha$, then the training-conditional coverage must be $\geq 1-\alpha$ in expectation: \begin{equation}1-\alpha \leq \P(Y_{n+1} \in \cC(X_{n+1})) = \E\left[\P(Y_{n+1} \in \cC(X_{n+1}) \mid \cD_n)\right].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-531: However, a marginal coverage guarantee does not ensure that training-conditional coverage will be close to $1-\alpha$ with high probability; for this, we need a stronger concentration-type statement.
elements.sent-2411-532: In this section, we will consider split conformal prediction, where $s((x,y); \cD)$ has no dependence on $\cD$ (see Section~\ref{sec:split-conformal ).}
elements.sent-2411-533: Throughout this section, we will assume the data points $(X_i,Y_i)$ are i.i.d., rather than the more general assumption of exchangeability.
elements.sent-2411-534: We will see that, for i.i.d.\ data, the training-conditional coverage will indeed concentrate near (or above) the nominal level $1-\alpha$, for the split conformal method.
elements.sent-2411-535: In fact, there is a closed-form expression that characterizes the training-conditional coverage in this setting.
elements.sent-2411-536: For intuition, let us first consider a simplified setting where the score distribution is continuous (so that there are no ties, almost surely), and where $(1-\alpha)(n+1)$ is an integer.
elements.sent-2411-537: In that case, training-conditional coverage is exactly distributed as a Beta distribution: \begin{equation}\label{eq:beta-training-conditional} \P\left(Y_{n+1} \in \cC\left(X_{n+1}\right) \big| \: \cD_n\right) \sim Beta\left((1-\alpha)(n+1),\alpha(n+1)\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-538: The Beta distribution arises from the order statistics of the uniform distribution---conformal prediction relies only on the ranks of the scores, so if there are no ties one can without loss of generality analyze conformal scores that are uniformly distributed.
elements.sent-2411-539: More generally, without these assumptions, the Beta distribution provides a lower bound on the training-conditional coverage, as we see in the following result:.
elements.sent-2411-540: Suppose the data points $(X_i,Y_i)$ are i.i.d., and let $\cC(X_{n+1 )$ be constructed via split conformal prediction (Algorithm~\ref{alg:split-cp ) using any pretrained score function $s$.}}
elements.sent-2411-541: Then the training-conditional coverage $\P\left(Y_{n+1 \in \cC\left(X_{n+1}\right) \big| \: \cD_n\right)$ stochastically dominates the $Beta\left((1-\alpha)(n+1),\alpha(n+1)\right)$ distribution, and in particular, for any $\Delta$, \begin{equation}\P\Bigg( \P\left(Y_{n+1} \in \cC\left(X_{n+1}\right) \big| \: \cD_n\right) \, \leq \, 1-\alpha-\Delta \Bigg) \leq F_{Beta\left((1-\alpha)(n+1),\alpha(n+1)\right)}(1-\alpha-\Delta) \, \leq \, e^{-2n\Delta^2},\end{equation} where $F_{Beta(a,b)}$ denotes the CDF of the $Beta(a,b)$ distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-542: One consequence of this result is an answer to the question, how large should the calibration set be for split conformal prediction? We know that the marginal coverage will be at least $1-\alpha$ for any $n$, but the above theorem tells us that a larger calibration set is still better because it leads to coverage that lies closer to $1-\alpha$ conditionally.
elements.sent-2411-543: This is because the distribution of coverage is $Beta\left((1-\alpha)(n+1),\alpha(n+1)\right)$ (or larger, in the case of ties).
elements.sent-2411-544: This Beta distribution has expected value $1-\alpha$ and variance $\leq \frac{\alpha(1-\alpha) {n+2}$; for example, with $\alpha = 0.1$, a calibration set size of $n=500$ leads to training-conditional coverage with standard deviation $0.013$, meaning that training-conditional coverage will almost certainly lie near (or above) $1-\alpha$ whenever the calibration set size is $\geq 500$.}
elements.sent-2411-545: More generally, the theorem implies that training-conditional coverage for split conformal can be lower-bounded as \begin{equation}\P(Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1})\mid \cD_n) \geq 1- \alpha - \epsilon_n,\end{equation} for an error term $\epsilon_n$ has standard deviation scaling as $n^{-1/2}$.
elements.sent-2411-546: See Figure~\ref{fig:beta for a visualization of the distribution of conditional coverage for different values of $n$.}
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{figures/diagrams/training-conditional-coverage-distribution.pdf} \caption{{The distribution of coverage of split conformal prediction conditional on the training data, when the scores have no ties. The distribution concentrates around $1-\alpha$ with rate $n^{-1/2}$.}} \label{fig:beta}
elements.sent-2411-547: Let $F$ be the CDF of the distribution of $s(X,Y)$, when the data point is sampled as $(X,Y)\sim P$.
elements.sent-2411-548: Define $S_i = s(X_i,Y_i)$, which are i.i.d.\ draws from the distribution with CDF $F$.
elements.sent-2411-549: Next, let $S_{(1)}\leq \dots \leq S_{(n)}$ be the order statistics of $S_1,\dots,S_n$.
elements.sent-2411-550: By definition of split conformal, we have $Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1 )$ if and only if $S_{n+1}\leq S_{(k)}$ for $k = \lceil (1-\alpha)(n+1)\rceil$, and so we have \begin{equation}\P(Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1})\mid \cD_n) = \P(S_{n+1}\leq S_{(k)}\mid \cD_n) = F(S_{(k)}),\end{equation} where the last step holds since $S_{n+1}$ is independent of $\cD_n$ and has CDF $F$, while $S_{(k)}$ is a function of $\cD_n$.. Therefore, \begin{equation}\P\big(\P(Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1})\mid \cD_n) \leq 1-\alpha-\Delta\big) = \P\big(F(S_{(k)}) \leq1-\alpha-\Delta\big).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-552: Then the $U_i$'s are i.i.d., and by the basic property of CDFs~\eqref{eq:CDF_basic_fact , their shared distribution is superuniform---that is, $\P(U_i\leq\tau)\leq\tau$ for all $\tau\in[0,1]$.}
elements.sent-2411-553: Note also that, by monotonicity of $F$, the order statistics $U_{(1)}\leq\dots\leq U_{(n)}$ of $U_1,\dots,U_n$ satisfy $U_{(k) =F(S_{(k) )$ (see Fact~\ref{fact:monotone-invariance-quantiles ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-554: Then \begin{equation}\P\big(F(S_{(k)})\leq 1-\alpha-\Delta\big) = \P\big(U_{(k)} \leq 1-\alpha-\Delta\big) \leq \P\big(U^*_{(k)}\leq 1-\alpha-\Delta\big),\end{equation} where $U^*_1,\dots,U^*_n$ are i.i.d.\ $\textnormal{Unif [0,1]$ random variables, with order statistics $U^*_{(1)}\leq \dots\leq U^*_{(n)}$.}
elements.sent-2411-555: Finally, by definition of the Beta distribution, the $k$th order statistic from $n$ i.i.d.\ uniform random variables has distribution \begin{equation}U^*_{(k)}\sim\textnormal{Beta}(k,n+1-k).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-557: This proves the first inequality claimed in the theorem.
elements.sent-2411-558: The second inequality (i.e., the bound on the CDF of the Beta distribution) is due to the fact that the $Beta\left((1-\alpha)(n+1),\alpha(n+1)\right)$ distribution has mean $1-\alpha$, and is $\frac{1 {4n}$-subgaussian.}
elements.sent-2411-559: The notion of training-conditional coverage also leads to an important variant of the marginal coverage property in~\eqref{eq:marg_coverage , known as high-probability coverage control or ``probably approximately correct'' (PAC) coverage.}
elements.sent-2411-560: As described above, the marginal coverage property in~\eqref{eq:marg_coverage is equivalent to a guarantee on the expected training-conditional coverage, \begin{equation}\P\Big(Y_{n+1} \in \cC(X_{n+1})\Big) \ge 1 - \alpha \iff \E\left[\P\left(Y_{n+1} \not\in \cC\left(X_{n+1}\right) \big| \: \cD_n\right)\right] \leq \alpha,\end{equation} where the expectation is over the calibration data $\cD_n$.}
elements.sent-2411-561: That is, we can think of marginal coverage as a bound on the average error (where in this case, error given by training-conditional miscoverage, $\P\left(Y_{n+1 \not\in \cC\left(X_{n+1}\right) \big| \: \cD_n \right)$).}
elements.sent-2411-562: We can also consider a different approach: instead of bounding the expected error, we might wish to bound the error with some desired probability---that is, in this setting, we enforce a bound on the chance that we end up with poor training-conditional coverage.
elements.sent-2411-563: Concretely, for some $\delta \in (0,1)$, we could ask for prediction sets $\cC(X_{n+1 )$ that satisfy \begin{equation}\label{eq:high-prob-control} \P\left( \ \P\left(Y_{n+1} \not\in \cC\left(X_{n+1}\right) \big| \: \cD_n\right)\le \alpha \ \right) \ge 1- \delta.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-564: In words, there is only a $\delta$ chance that we end up with an unlucky calibration set that results in miscoverage on future data being higher than $\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-565: In the special case of i.i.d.
elements.sent-2411-566: Taking as a starting point the distribution of training-conditional coverage given in Theorem~\ref{thm:training-conditional-split-CP , we see that by defining \begin{equation}\cC(X_{n+1}) = \left\{y\in\cY : s(X_{n+1},y)\leq \quantile\left(S_1,\dots,S_n;(1-\alpha')(1+1/n)\right)\right\}\end{equation} (i.e., split conformal run at a nominal level $1-\alpha'$), then we will achieve the desired high probability bound~\eqref{eq:high-prob-control if we choose the appropriate nominal level $1-\alpha'$.}}
elements.sent-2411-567: In particular, we can take $\alpha'$ to be the unique value that satisfies \begin{equation}F_{Beta((1-\alpha')(n+1),\alpha'(n+1))}(1-\alpha) = \delta.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-568: By our calculations on the mean and variance of the Beta distribution, above, we can see that $\alpha' = \alpha - \bigo(n^{-1/2 )$---that is, this is simply a slightly more conservative version of split conformal prediction.}
elements.sent-2411-569: It is important to note that for this chapter, our positive results will primarily be in the setting of i.i.d.\ data.
elements.sent-2411-570: In contrast, for the marginal results of Chapter~\ref{chapter:conformal-exchangeability (and for related results later on in the book as well), an assumption of exchangeability is sufficient.}
elements.sent-2411-571: The reason for the distinction, in the case of training-conditional coverage for split conformal, is that we need the sample quantiles of the empirical distribution of scores in the calibration set, $(S_i)_{i\in[n]}$, to concentrate around their corresponding true quantiles.
elements.sent-2411-572: If we only assume exchangeability rather an i.i.d.\ assumption, then this concentration phenomenon is no longer guaranteed to hold, making training-conditional coverage impossible to guarantee.
elements.section-2411-017: A hardness result for training-conditional coverage
elements.sent-2411-573: We have seen in Theorem~\ref{thm:training-conditional-split-CP above that split conformal prediction automatically satisfies a training-conditional coverage guarantee (as long as the data points are assumed to be i.i.d., rather than merely exchangeable).}
elements.sent-2411-574: The following result shows that, without further assumptions, training-conditional coverage is not guaranteed for full conformal prediction.
elements.sent-2411-575: Essentially, the reason is that, for split conformal in the i.i.d.\ data setting, the scores $S_i=s(X_i,Y_i)$ are i.i.d.\ (when we treat the pretrained conformal score function $s$ as fixed); for full conformal, on the other hand, the scores $S_i=s((X_i,Y_i);\cD_{n+1 )$ are not i.i.d.\ and thus may not exhibit the same favorable concentration type properties.}
elements.sent-2411-576: To make this more precise, we first introduce the terminology of a nonatomic distribution.
elements.sent-2411-577: For a distribution $P$ on $\cZ$, the atoms of $P$ are all points $z\in\cZ$ with positive probability, \begin{equation}\textnormal{atom}(P) = \{z\in\cZ : \P_P(Z=z)>0\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-578: The distribution $P$ is called nonatomic if it has no atoms, i.e., $\textnormal{atom (P)=\varnothing$ (the empty set).}
elements.sent-2411-579: As a concrete example, a distribution on $\R$ is nonatomic if and only if it is continuous (i.e., has a density).
elements.sent-2411-580: In $\R^d$ when $d>1$, only one direction holds: a distribution that has a density must be nonatomic, but a nonatomic distribution need not have a density (for example, the uniform distribution on the sphere is nonatomic).
elements.sent-2411-581: We are now ready to state the theorem, which establishes the impossibility of guaranteeing training-conditional coverage for full conformal prediction in the setting of a nonatomic feature distribution.
elements.sent-2411-582: Let $P$ be any distribution on $\cX\times\cY$ such that the marginal $P_X$ is nonatomic.
elements.sent-2411-583: Then there exists a symmetric conformal score function $s$ such that, when running full conformal prediction with this choice of $s$, the training-conditional coverage probability satisfies \begin{equation}\P\bigg( \P(Y_{n+1} \in \cC(X_{n+1}) \mid \cD_n) = 0\bigg)\geq \alpha - \bigo\left(\sqrt{\frac{\log n}{n}}\right),\end{equation} where the probability is taken with respect to the training set $\cD_n=((X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_n,Y_n))$ drawn i.i.d.\ from $P$.
elements.sent-2411-584: As we will see below, this theorem is proved by constructing an explicit counterexample, whose score function $s((x,y);\cD)$ is highly degenerate and is very different from any score function we might use in practice.
elements.sent-2411-585: We might ask whether placing mild assumptions on the score function could restore a training-conditional coverage guarantee.
elements.sent-2411-586: In fact, the hardness result of Theorem~\ref{thm:training-conditional-full-CP holds (in a very slightly weaker form) even if we restrict to residual score functions, i.e., score functions of the form \begin{equation}s((x,y);\cD) = |y - \hat{f}(x;\cD)|,\end{equation} where $\hat{f (\cdot;\cD)$ is a regression model fitted on training data $\cD$; in this version of the result, it is the regression algorithm itself (i.e., the map $\cD\mapsto \hat{f (\cdot;\cD)$) whose degenerate construction enables the counterexample.}}}
elements.sent-2411-587: In other words, without placing assumptions on the regression algorithm, full conformal does not achieve a training-conditional coverage guarantee even if we restrict ourselves to using the residual score.
elements.sent-2411-588: Of course, it will often be the case that, for the types of score functions we might encounter in practice, the prediction sets returned by full conformal may exhibit training-conditional coverage---this result is simply telling us that this type of property cannot be guaranteed to hold universally across all possible score functions.
elements.sent-2411-589: Likewise, if $X$ is instead discrete, then it may be possible to ensure training-conditional coverage.

Each plot shows a different random draw of the histogram of the values $a(X_i)\in\{0,\dots,n-1\}$, for training points $i\in[n]$, where $n=50$. However, setting $N=20$, a sliding window of length $n-N=30$ always contains approximately $n-N$ many data points. The outlined rectangles highlight this sliding window for the several shifts (corresponding to different values of $k$ in the notation of~\eqref{eqn:Ecal_unif}). The event $\mathcal{E}_{\rm unif}$ fails only if there exists a value of $k$ for which the window contains too few of the $a(X_i)$, as in the lower plot.} \label{fig:E_unif_illustration}

elements.sent-2411-609: First, we observe that, by definition of the mod function, $\cE_{\textnormal{unif }$ holds if and only if $\sum_{i=1 ^n\ind{\textnormal{mod (-a(X_i)+k,n) \geq N \geq (1-\alpha)(n+1)$ for all $k=0,\dots,n-1$, and so \begin{equation}\P\left(\cE_{\textnormal{unif}}^c\right) \leq \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \P\left( \sum_{i=1}^n\ind{\textnormal{mod}(-a(X_i)+k,n) \geq N} < (1-\alpha)(n+1)\right).\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-610: Moreover, since $a(X_i)\iidsim \textnormal{Unif}\{0,\dots,n-1\}$, we see that \begin{equation}\sum_{i=1}^n\ind{\textnormal{mod}(-a(X_i)+k,n) \geq N}\sim \textnormal{Binomial}\left(n,1-\frac{N}{n}\right)\end{equation} for each fixed $k$, and so \begin{equation}\P\left(\cE_{\textnormal{unif}}^c\right) \leq n \cdot \P\left(\textnormal{Binomial}\left(n,1-\frac{N}{n}\right) < (1-\alpha)(n+1)\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-611: Taking $N = \alpha n - \bigo(\sqrt{n \log n )$, and applying standard Binomial tail bounds, we can bound this probability as $\bigo(1/n)$, which completes the proof.}
elements.section-2411-018: Conditioning on the test point features
elements.sent-2411-612: Next we turn to the problem of conditioning on the test features $X_{n+1}$, rather than the training data.
elements.sent-2411-613: We call this ``test-conditional coverage'', meaning coverage conditional on the test feature $X_{n+1}$ only, as is common in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-614: In its strongest possible version, achieving test-conditional coverage would require that our construction of the prediction sets satisfies the following property: for any distribution $P$ on $\cX\times\cY$, \begin{equation}\P\big(Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1})\mid X_{n+1}\big)\geq 1-\alpha\end{equation} holds almost surely.
elements.sent-2411-615: In this section, we will explore this version of the test-conditional coverage property, and will study its challenges.
elements.sent-2411-616: Afterward, in later sections, we will examine relaxations of the property.
elements.sent-2411-617: First, we will consider a setting where test-conditional coverage can indeed be achieved---the discrete setting.
elements.sent-2411-618: To take a simple case, suppose that $\cX = \{x_1,\dots,x_K\}$ is a finite set---effectively, the feature $X_i$ for data point $i$ simply identifies which of $K$ many groups this data point belongs to.
elements.sent-2411-619: If the sample size $n$ is far larger than the number of feature groups $K$, we might expect that test-conditional coverage is easy to achieve.
elements.sent-2411-620: Indeed, we will now see that while conformal prediction may not itself achieve test-conditional coverage, a simple modification will enable this property.
elements.sent-2411-621: First, why doesn't conformal prediction already satisfy this property? Conformal prediction is guaranteed to satisfy a marginal coverage guarantee, which we can rewrite for this setting as \begin{equation}1-\alpha \leq \P\big(Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1})\big) = \sum_{k=1}^K \P(X_{n+1}=x_k)\cdot \P\big(Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1})\mid X_{n+1}=x_k\big).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-622: This means that the test-conditional coverage values, $\P\big(Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1 )\mid X_{n+1 =x_k\big)$ for $k\in[K]$, are guaranteed to be $\geq 1-\alpha$ in the (weighted) average, but some may be lower than this threshold.}}
elements.sent-2411-623: Now we will see how to modify conformal prediction to achieve test-conditional coverage in this setting.
elements.sent-2411-624: For simplicity, let us consider split conformal prediction, as defined earlier in Algorithm~\ref{alg:split-cp .}
elements.sent-2411-625: We recall that the split conformal prediction interval is given by the construction \begin{equation}\cC(x) = \{y : s(x,y) \leq \hat{q}\},\end{equation} where $s:\cX\times\cY\to\R$ is the pretrained conformal score function, and where \begin{equation}\hat{q} = \quantile\left(S_1, \ldots, S_n ; (1-\alpha)(1+1/n)\right)\end{equation} computes the quantile of the conformal scores on the calibration set.
elements.sent-2411-626: To achieve test-conditional coverage in the finite setting $\cX = \{x_1,\dots,x_K\}$, we will instead define \begin{equation}\label{eqn:test_conditional_coverage_discrete_X}\cC(x_k) = \{y : s(x_k,y) \leq \hat{q}_k\},\end{equation} for each $k\in[K]$, where \begin{equation}\hat{q}_k = \quantile\left((S_i)_{i\in [n], X_i = x_k} ; (1-\alpha)(1+1/n_k)\right)\end{equation} instead computes the quantile of scores among only those data points $i$ for which $X_i = x_k$, and where $n_k = \sum_{i\in [n] \indsub{X_i = x_k}$ counts the number of such points.}
elements.sent-2411-627: This modified procedure will now satisfy \begin{equation}\P(Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1})\mid X_{n+1} = x_k) \geq 1-\alpha\end{equation} for all $k\in[K]$, or equivalently, \begin{equation}\P(Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1})\mid X_{n+1}) \geq 1-\alpha\end{equation} almost surely.
elements.sent-2411-628: We defer a proof of this claim to Section~\ref{sec:test-conditional-binning , where we will see that this is a special case of a more general result.}
elements.sent-2411-629: We note that, if the $K$ many score distributions (i.e., for each $k$, the distribution of $s(X,Y)$ for $(X,Y)\sim P$, conditional on $X=x_k$) are fairly similar, then the group-wise quantiles $\hat{q _k$ will be fairly similar as well; in this setting, the original split conformal method may already be nearly achieving test-conditional coverage, without this modification.}
elements.sent-2411-630: On the other hand, if these $K$ distributions are widely different, then as discussed above, marginal coverage can be expressed as a weighted average of the $K$ many test-conditional coverage levels; these $K$ values can be quite different, with many falling far below the target level $1-\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-631: The modified method defined here fixes this issue by performing calibration within each group separately.
elements.sent-2411-632: In the previous section, we considered the discrete setting, where the feature $X$ takes only a small number of possible values.
elements.sent-2411-633: In this section, we will instead consider the other extreme: what if $X$ is continuously distributed? Can test-conditional coverage be guaranteed via any method, whether conformal prediction or some other procedure? To answer this question, we will now write $\cC$ to denote any procedure that uses a training dataset of size $n$ to construct prediction sets.
elements.sent-2411-634: Formally, $\cC$ is a map from $(\cX\times\cY)^n\times \cX$ to the set of subsets of $\cY$, but we will continue to write $\cC(X_{n+1 )$ for the resulting prediction set, i.e., we suppress the dependence on the training data $(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_n,Y_n)$ in the notation.}
elements.sent-2411-635: We will now consider the setting where, under the joint distribution $P$ of the (feature,response) pair $(X,Y)$, the marginal distribution $P_X$ of $X$ is nonatomic (recall Definition~\ref{def:nonatomic ).}
elements.sent-2411-636: In this setting, we will see that test-conditional inference is impossible.
elements.sent-2411-637: What does ``impossible'' mean in this context? For example, if we simply return $\cC(x) = \cY$ for any test feature $x$ (e.g., returning $(-\infty,\infty)$ as our prediction interval in the case of a real-valued response), then any desired coverage guarantee will hold.
elements.sent-2411-638: We can even be slightly less conservative by producing a randomized answer: for any $x$, return \begin{equation}\label{eqn:trivial_test_conditional}\cC(x) = \begin{cases}\cY, \textnormal{ with probability $1-\alpha$}, \varnothing, \textnormal{ with probability $\alpha$}.\end{cases}\end{equation} This solution is clearly still unsatisfactory, however, since the resulting prediction interval is completely uninformative---it does not even depend on the training data.
elements.sent-2411-639: However, the following result will demonstrate that this trivial (and useless) solution is the best that we can do.
elements.sent-2411-640: Suppose $\cC$ is any procedure that satisfies distribution-free conditional coverage, i.e., for any distribution $P$ on $\cX\times\cY$, \begin{equation}\P\big(Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1})\mid X_{n+1}\big)\geq 1-\alpha\end{equation} holds almost surely, where the probability is taken with respect to $(X_1,Y_1),\cdots,(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )\iidsim P$.}}
elements.sent-2411-641: Then, for any distribution $P$ on $\cX\times\cY$ for which the marginal $P_X$ is nonatomic, \begin{equation}\P\big(y\in \cC(x)\big)\geq 1-\alpha\textnormal{ for every $(x,y)\in\cX\times\cY$}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-642: To see more concretely how this result establishes the impossibility of nontrivial test-conditional inference, in the case that $\cY = \R$, we can consider its implications for the expected length (or more formally, the expected Lebesgue measure) of the prediction interval:.
elements.sent-2411-643: Under the assumptions of Theorem~\ref{thm:conditional_infinite , suppose also that $\cY=\R$.}
elements.sent-2411-644: Then, for any distribution $P$ on $\cX\times\cY$ for which the marginal $P_X$ is nonatomic, and for every $x\in\cX$, it holds that \begin{equation}\P\big(\textnormal{Leb}(\cC(x)) = \infty\big)\geq 1-\alpha,\end{equation} where $\textnormal{Leb (\cdot)$ denotes the Lebesgue measure.}
elements.sent-2411-645: In particular, this implies that the prediction set constructed at the test point $X_{n+1}$ must have infinite expected length, $\E[\textnormal{Leb (\cC(X_{n+1 ))] = \infty$.}}
elements.sent-2411-646: Now let's compare this to the trivial solution described above in~\eqref{eqn:trivial_test_conditional , where, regardless of the training data, we return $\cC(x) = \cY$ with probability $1-\alpha$, and $\cC(x)=\varnothing$ otherwise.}
elements.sent-2411-647: In that case, we have $\P(y\in\cC(x)) = 1-\alpha$ for all $(x,y)$.
elements.sent-2411-648: The result of Theorem~\ref{thm:conditional_infinite can therefore be interpreted as saying that, in the nonatomic setting, any procedure with distribution-free test-conditional coverage will return an output that is just as uninformative as this trivial construction.}
elements.sent-2411-649: Before proceeding, we pause to point out an important aspect of Theorem~\ref{thm:conditional_infinite (and its corollary): the bounds apply to every distribution $P$ (with nonatomic $P_X$), and we are not simply proving that nontrivial test-conditional coverage is impossible for some distribution $P$.}
elements.sent-2411-650: That is, while we might have expected that we could construct some valid $\cC$ that returns reasonably narrow intervals for ``nice'' distributions $P$, and is excessively wide only for distributions $P$ that fail to satisfy some needed conditions (e.g., smoothness), instead we see from this theorem that, if $\cC$ achieves test-conditional coverage for every $P$, then it also returns a completely uninformative solution for every (nonatomic) $P$.
elements.sent-2411-651: We are now ready to prove these results.
elements.sent-2411-652: For Theorem~\ref{thm:conditional_infinite , the key idea of the proof is to construct a perturbation of the distribution $P$: we consider the mixture distribution \begin{equation}P' = (1-\epsilon)\cdot P + \epsilon \cdot \delta_{(x,y)},\end{equation} where as before, $\delta_{(x,y)}$ denotes the point mass at $(x,y)$.}
elements.sent-2411-653: That is, to sample a point from the distribution $P'$, we either draw from $P$ (with probability $1-\epsilon$), or we simply return the point $(x,y)$ (with probability $\epsilon$).
elements.sent-2411-654: The proof will use the fact that $\cC$ is assumed to satisfy test-conditional coverage with respect to any distribution of the data---and in particular, must therefore offer test-conditional coverage relative to the perturbed distribution $P'$.
elements.sent-2411-656: Since $\cC$ offers distribution-free conditional coverage, this means that $\cC$ must satisfy the coverage guarantee with respect to data drawn from the distribution $P'$---that is, \begin{equation}\P_{P'}\big(Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1})\mid X_{n+1}\big)\geq 1-\alpha\end{equation} holds almost surely, where the notation $\P_{P' (\dots)$ indicates that we are calculating probability with respect to training and test data $(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )\iidsim P'$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-657: Since the event $X_{n+1 =x$ has positive probability under $P'$, in particular this means that this bound must hold on the event $X_{n+1 =x$: \begin{equation}\P_{P'}(Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1})\mid X_{n+1}=x)\geq 1-\alpha.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-658: Moreover, by definition of $P'$ together with the assumption that $P_X$ is nonatomic, we can see that $\P_{P'}\big(Y_{n+1 =y\mid X_{n+1 =x\big) = 1$, and so combining these facts, we must have \begin{equation}\P_{P'}\big(y\in\cC(X_{n+1})\mid X_{n+1}=x\big)\geq 1-\alpha,\end{equation} or equivalently, \begin{equation}\P_{P'}\big(y\in\cC(x)\big)\geq 1-\alpha.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-659: Note that the event no longer depends on the test point, and so we are calculating probability with respect to the draw of the training data only, i.e., with respect to $(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_n,Y_n)\iidsim P'$.
elements.sent-2411-660: Next, observe that the result above holds for an arbitrarily small $\epsilon>0$.
elements.sent-2411-661: By taking $\epsilon\rightarrow0$, we have shown that the same bound must hold for data drawn from $P$, rather than from $P'$.
elements.sent-2411-662: To formalize this argument, we can compute \begin{equation}\P_{P}\big(y\in\cC(x)\big) \geq \P_{P'}\big(y\in\cC(x)\big) - \dtv(P^n,P'{}^n)\geq 1-\alpha - \dtv(P^n,P'{}^n),\end{equation} where $\dtv$ denotes the total variation distance, and where $P^n$ and $P'{ ^n$ denote the corresponding product distributions, i.e., the distribution of an i.i.d.\ sample of size $n$ drawn from $P$ or from $P'$, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-663: Since $\dtv(P^n,P'{ ^n) \leq n\dtv(P,P') = n\epsilon$, we therefore have \begin{equation}\P_{P}\big(y\in\cC(x)\big) \geq 1-\alpha - n\epsilon.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-664: Since this holds for any $\epsilon>0$, this completes the proof.
elements.sent-2411-665: Finally, to complete this section, we present a proof of the corollary, which verifies that in this setting, the interval $\cC(X_{n+1 )$ must often have infinite length.}
elements.sent-2411-666: Then, deterministically, it holds that \begin{equation}\textnormal{Leb}(\cC(x)) =\int_{y\in\R}\ind{y\in\cC(x)}\;dy \geq \int_{y=0}^{A+B}\ind{y\in\cC(x)}\;dy ,\end{equation} and so \begin{equation}\textnormal{Leb}(\cC(x))\leq A \Longrightarrow \int_{y=0}^{A+B}\ind{y\in\cC(x)}\;dy\leq A \Longleftrightarrow \int_{y=0}^{A+B}\ind{y\not\in\cC(x)}\;dy\geq B.\end{equation}.

elements.section-2411-019: Relaxations of test-conditional coverage: a binning-based approach
elements.sent-2411-670: So far, we have seen that it is impossible to achieve pointwise test-conditional coverage, i.e., conditional on $X_{n+1 =x$, in the nonatomic setting, but easy to achieve in the discrete setting.}
elements.sent-2411-671: This discrepancy arises from the fact that the event $X=x$ might be observed many times in the training set in the discrete case, but (for any prespecified value $x$) will never occur in the training set for the nonatomic case.
elements.sent-2411-672: This suggests a way to relax the goal of conditional coverage in order to avoid the hardness result: we should condition on events that will be observed many times in a sample of size $n$.
elements.sent-2411-673: Specifically, we can consider partitioning the feature space into $K$ many bins, $\cX = \cX_1 \cup \dots \cup \cX_K$, and then requiring a bin-wise notion of conditional coverage: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:test_cond_coverage_by_bin}\P(Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1})\mid X_{n+1}\in\cX_k)\geq 1-\alpha \textnormal{ for all $k\in[K]$ with $\P(X_{n+1}\in\cX_k)>0$}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-674: Just like in the discrete case, the split conformal procedure can be modified to achieve this guarantee, by simply choosing a threshold for the score separately within each bin $k$: defining $k(x)\in[K]$ as the index of the bin containing $x$, we construct the prediction set \begin{equation}\cC(X_{n+1}) = \{y : s(X_{n+1},y) \leq \hat{q}_{k(X_{n+1})}\},\end{equation} where the score function $s$ is pretrained (i.e., this method is a variant of split conformal prediction), and where for each $k$, \begin{equation}\hat{q}_k = \quantile\left((S_i)_{i\in [n], X_i \in\cX_k} ; (1-\alpha)(1+1/n_k)\right)\end{equation} for $n_k = \sum_{i\in [n] \ind{X_i \in \cX_k}$ is the sample size for the $k$th bin.}
elements.sent-2411-675: This type of construction can be extended to full conformal prediction as well: define \begin{equation}\label{eqn:test_cond_coverage_by_bin_C} \cC(X_{n+1}) = \{y : S_{n+1}^y \leq \hat{q}_{k(X_{n+1})}^y\},\end{equation} where now the quantile is given by \begin{equation}\hat{q}^y_k = \quantile\left((S_i^y)_{i\in [n], X_i \in\cX_k} ; (1-\alpha)(1+1/n_k)\right),\end{equation} for scores $S_i^y$ defined exactly as in Chapter~\ref{chapter:conformal-exchangeability .}
elements.sent-2411-676: Of course, as usual, the bin-wise split conformal method defined above is simply a special case of this procedure.
elements.sent-2411-677: The following result verifies that this modified full conformal method achieves bin-conditional coverage:.
elements.sent-2411-679: Then the bin-wise prediction interval $\cC(X_{n+1 )$ defined in~\eqref{eqn:test_cond_coverage_by_bin_C satisfies\begin{equation}\P(Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1})\mid X_{n+1}\in\cX_k)\geq 1-\alpha\end{equation} for all $k\in[K]$ with $\P(X_{n+1}\in\cX_k)>0$.}}
elements.sent-2411-680: Pointwise coverage in the discrete case (i.e., the guarantee that $\P(Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1 )\mid X_{n+1 =x_k)\geq 1-\alpha$, for each $k$, which we covered in Section~\ref{sec:test_conditional_discrete above) is actually a special case of this result: in the setting where $|\cX|=K$, we can simply take each bin $\cX_k$ to be a singleton set.}}}
elements.sent-2411-681: The key idea for the proof of this theorem lies in the following lemma:.
elements.sent-2411-683: Fix any subset $\cZ_0\subseteq\cX\times\cY$, and for any fixed nonempty subset $I\subseteq[n+1]$, let $\cE_I$ be the event that $\{i \in[n+1]: (X_i,Y_i)\in\cZ_0\} = I$.
elements.sent-2411-684: If $\cE_I$ has positive probability, then $((X_i,Y_i))_{i\in I}$ is exchangeable conditional on $\cE_I$.
elements.sent-2411-685: Furthermore, if $(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )\in\cZ_0$ has positive probability, then for any symmetric score function $s$ the quantity \begin{equation}p = \frac{1 + \sum_{i\in[n]}\ind{(X_i,Y_i)\in\cZ_0,\ S_i\geq S_{n+1}}}{1 + \sum_{i\in[n]}\ind{(X_i,Y_i)\in\cZ_0}}\end{equation} satisfies $\P(p\leq\tau\mid (X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )\in\cZ_0)\leq \tau$ for any $\tau\in[0,1]$, where $S_i = s((X_i,Y_i);\cD_{n+1 )$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-686: We will use this lemma to prove the bin-wise conditional coverage guarantee here, but will also use it again for several related results later on in the chapter.
elements.sent-2411-687: Next, fix any bin $k$ with $\P(X_{n+1}\in \cX_k)>0$, and let $\cZ_0 = \cX_k \times\cY \subseteq \cX\times \cY$.
elements.sent-2411-688: By Lemma~\ref{lem:exch_conditional_subvector , the quantity \begin{equation}p = \frac{1+\sum_{i\in[n]}\ind{(X_i,Y_i)\in\cZ_0, \ S_i \geq S_{n+1}}}{1+\sum_{i\in[n]}\ind{(X_i,Y_i)\in\cZ_0}}\end{equation} satisfies $\P(p\leq \alpha\mid X_{n+1}\in\cX_k) = \P(p\leq \alpha\mid (X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )\in\cZ_0)\leq \alpha$.}}}

elements.section-2411-022: Relaxations of test-conditional coverage: beyond binning
elements.sent-2411-734: In Section~\ref{sec:test-conditional-continuous-impossible , we learned that the strongest form of the test-conditional coverage property, which requires that \begin{equation}\label{eqn:test-conditional-at-Xn+1}\P\big(Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1})\mid X_{n+1}\big)\geq 1-\alpha\textnormal{ holds almost surely over $X_{n+1}$},\end{equation} is in general too strong---for a nonatomic feature distribution $P_X$, it is essentially impossible to improve upon the trivial algorithm: outputting $\cY$ with probability $1-\alpha$ and $\varnothing$ otherwise.}
elements.sent-2411-735: Given this impossibility result, one might wonder if test-conditional coverage is intrinsically impossible, or if there are some mild relaxations of the problem which admit practical solutions.
elements.sent-2411-736: To explore this idea, we will now consider several such relaxations.
elements.sent-2411-737: To quantify this, we consider the following relaxation of the goal of test-conditional coverage: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:test_conditional_relax_alpha_delta}\P\big(Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1})\mid X_{n+1}\in\cX_0\big)\geq 1-\alpha\textnormal{ for all $P$, and all $\cX_0\subseteq\cX$ with $P_X(\cX_0)\geq \delta$}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-171: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:test_conditional_relax_alpha_delta}\P\big(Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1})\mid X_{n+1}\in\cX_0\big)\geq 1-\alpha\textnormal{ for all $P$, and all $\cX_0\subseteq\cX$ with $P_X(\cX_0)\geq \delta$}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-738: Here probability is taken with respect to data $(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )$ sampled i.i.d.\ from $P$.}}
elements.sent-2411-739: We can observe that if this relaxed condition holds for all values $\delta>0$, then we would simply recover the original condition~\eqref{eqn:test-conditional-at-Xn+1 , which we know to be impossible in the nonatomic setting.}
elements.sent-2411-740: Instead, we will ask whether it is possible to ensure that this relaxed condition holds for some fixed and positive $\delta$.
elements.sent-2411-741: Of course, even the original definition~\eqref{eqn:test-conditional-at-Xn+1 of test-conditional coverage can be satisfied if we are willing to accept the trivial algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-742: The impossibility result derived above in Theorem~\ref{thm:conditional_infinite and Corollary~\ref{cor:infinite-lebesgue can be interpreted as telling us that this trivial solution is a baseline that cannot be improved upon.}}
elements.sent-2411-743: When we instead aim for the relaxed condition~\eqref{eqn:test_conditional_relax_alpha_delta , a different trivial solution gives us a baseline for this setting: we can simply run any distribution-free method (e.g., split or full conformal) with target coverage level $1-\alpha\delta$ in place of $1-\alpha$.}
elements.sent-2411-744: This strategy is trivial because in no way is it required to adapt to the difficulty of the prediction task as a function of $X_{n+1}$---it simply achieves the guarantee by being more conservative overall.
elements.sent-2411-746: In fact, if we allow randomization in our construction, this trivial solution can be generalized to a family of solutions, parameterized by $c\in[0,1]$: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:alpha_delta_trivial_solution}\begin{cases} \textnormal{Construct $\cC'(X_{n+1})$, using any method that guarantees marginal coverage at level $1-c\alpha\delta$.} \textnormal{With probability $\frac{1-\alpha}{1-c\alpha}$, return $\cC(X_{n+1}) = \cC'(X_{n+1})$; otherwise, return $\cC(X_{n+1}) = \varnothing$.}\end{cases}\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-753: It turns out that, while we cannot prove this exact bound, the lower bound established in the following theorem tells a very similar story---we will explain more once we have stated the result.
elements.sent-2411-754: In order to present the theorem we first need to define a new quantity: for $t\in[0,1]$, let $L_P(1-t)$ be the minimum length of any oracle prediction interval $\cC^P_{1-t}$, which is constructed given knowledge of the distribution $P$ (i.e., not a distribution-free method), and has coverage level $1-t$: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:L_P_def}L_P(1-t) = \inf\left\{ \E_P[\textnormal{Leb}(\cC^P_{1-t}(X))] \ : \textnormal{ $\cC^P_{1-t}$ satisfies }\P_P(Y\in \cC^P_{1-t}(X))\geq 1-t\right\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-755: For example, if under the true distribution $P$ it holds that $Y=f(X) + \mathcal{N (0,1)$ for some function $f$, then the minimum-length interval is given by $\cC^P_{1-t (X) = f(X) \pm z^*_{1-t/2}$, where $z^*_{1-t/2}$ is the $(1-t/2)$-quantile of the standard normal distribution, and so $L_P(1-t) = 2z^*_{1-t/2}$.}}

elements.sent-2411-760: We can see that this theorem establishes a lower bound that, at first glance, appears similar to the goal laid out in~\eqref{eqn:hardness-test-conditional-coverage-relaxed-ideal-lower-bound , with a key difference: instead of the length of a split conformal interval, $\E[\textnormal{Leb (\cC_{\textnormal{split ; 1-c\alpha\delta (X_{n+1 ))]$, we instead have the length of an oracle interval, $L_P(1-c\alpha\delta)$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-761: Of course, it may be the case that this oracle length $L_P(1-c\alpha\delta)$ is much shorter than the expected length of the split conformal interval---naturally we expect to pay a price for distribution-free validity.
elements.sent-2411-762: Why do we say, then, that the result of the theorem can be viewed as telling us something similar to the original goal in~\eqref{eqn:hardness-test-conditional-coverage-relaxed-ideal-lower-bound ? This is because, as we will discuss in Chapter~\ref{chapter:model-based , in certain settings where we are able to find a score function that captures the true distribution of the data, the split conformal interval is asymptotically equivalent to the oracle prediction interval---that is, in ``nice'' settings, if the sample size $n$ is sufficiently large then we can assume the length of the split conformal interval, $\E[\textnormal{Leb (\cC_{\textnormal{split ,1-c\alpha\delta (X_{n+1 ))]$, is approximately equal to the best possible length that can be achieved with oracle knowledge of the distribution $P$, which is $L_P(1-c\alpha\delta)$.}}}}}}
