elements.chapter-2411-006: A Model-Based Perspective on Conformal Prediction
elements.sent-2411-860: The preceding chapters of this book give distribution-free coverage guarantees for conformal prediction.
elements.sent-2411-861: This makes conformal prediction an attractive tool for statistical inference in settings where we cannot rely on prior knowledge of our data's distribution---but if we do assume a certain model for our data, would using conformal prediction lead to worse performance than using a model-based method? We will see the answer is no: since conformal prediction offers the flexibility of choosing any score function, this means that we can incorporate any distributional assumptions or modeling tools into $s$.
elements.sent-2411-862: Thus, conformal prediction can inherit the strong performance of a model-based method.
elements.sent-2411-863: More generally, conformal prediction should be understood as a tool that we use in conjunction with a model, rather than as an alternative to a model.
elements.sent-2411-864: At a high level, then, the practical appeal of conformal prediction is that we can augment any given model with additional distribution-free guarantees---if our model is correct, the resulting performance will be approximately the same as if we had not used conformal, often including strong guarantees such as conditional coverage; if instead our model assumptions are wrong, conformal still ensures marginal coverage.
elements.sent-2411-865: In summary: \begin{equation}stronger modeling assumptions hold \implies stronger guarantees for conformal; modeling assumptions fail but data are exchangeable \implies marginal coverage guarantee for conformal.\end{equation} In other words, a well-designed conformal procedure has the potential to achieve strong guarantees far beyond marginal coverage---such as optimal set size and conditional coverage---while retaining marginal coverage in the worst case.
elements.formula-2411-205: \begin{equation}stronger modeling assumptions hold \implies stronger guarantees for conformal; modeling assumptions fail but data are exchangeable \implies marginal coverage guarantee for conformal.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-866: In this chapter, in contrast to the rest of the book, we will focus on the non-distribution-free properties of conformal.
elements.sent-2411-867: We will study a range of settings (``case studies'') where, given a correctly specified model that can be consistently estimated with the observed data, running conformal with a wisely chosen score function $s$ will inherit the same optimality properties as the model-based oracle method.
elements.sent-2411-868: In particular, for each setting, these analyses will reveal (approximately) optimal choices for the conformal score function $s$, which (as we saw in Chapter~\ref{chapter:introduction ) is the core ingredient determining the shape of the resulting prediction sets.}
elements.sent-2411-869: In practice, designing good conformal scores---ones with good conditional coverage, small size, or other favorable statistical properties---is the single most important practical choice when running conformal prediction; the theoretical results of this chapter offer insight into how this choice interacts with our modeling assumptions to shape the outcome of conformal prediction.
elements.sent-2411-870: The forthcoming chapter gives a template for analyzing the performance of different conformal scores under assumptions on the predictive model and data distribution.
elements.sent-2411-871: We will study this framework through most of this chapter in Sections~\ref{sec:oracle_highlevel --\ref{sec:unified_framework_splitCP_asymp .}}
elements.sent-2411-872: In Section~\ref{sec:models_without_exch we then move to a different but related question---settings where conformal prediction performs well even without exchangeability of the data, under certain distributional assumptions.}
elements.sent-2411-873: We begin with a general design principle: to build a good conformal score, we should first define an ``oracle'' model that is designed to target the desired aims, and then design a conformal score to approximate this oracle.
elements.sent-2411-874: We will then make this approximation precise with asymptotic results.
elements.sent-2411-875: There are many possible goals that we might aim for, depending on the particular setting.
elements.sent-2411-876: For example, we might be interested in optimizing the length of the prediction interval, or, in achieving (an approximation of) test-conditional coverage.
elements.sent-2411-877: These aims, and several others that we will consider in this chapter, are quite different from each other, but can be studied in a unified way.
elements.sent-2411-878: We will examine a number of different ``case studies'', each in a different setting with various aims, but each following the same high-level framework.
elements.sent-2411-879: First, we will define the aim for the particular setting (e.g., minimize prediction interval length).
elements.sent-2411-880: We then identify the oracle prediction interval construction that would optimize this aim, if we had access to knowledge of the data distribution $P$, and choose a score that attempts to mimic this oracle.
elements.sent-2411-881: We then state a model assumption (for instance, consistent estimation of some true regression function), which enables an asymptotic guarantee establishing that we are able to learn an approximation to the oracle interval via split conformal, and thus achieve the stated aim in an asymptotic sense.
elements.sent-2411-882: The key tool for our convergence guarantees will be the following result, which we state informally here and develop more formally later on in the chapter:.
elements.sent-2411-883: Define the oracle set \begin{equation}\label{eqn:oracle_set} \cC^*(x) = \left\{y : s^*(x,y) \leq q^* \right\},\end{equation} where $s^*$ is an oracle score function and $q^*$ is the $(1-\alpha)$-quantile of the distribution of $s^*(X,Y)$ under $(X,Y)\sim P$.
elements.sent-2411-884: Let $\cC_n(x)$ be the usual split conformal set, \begin{equation}\cC_n(x) = \left\{y : s_n(x,y ) \leq \hat{q}_n \right\},\end{equation} where $s_n$ is the pretrained conformal score function.
elements.sent-2411-885: Then, under appropriate regularity conditions, if $s_n\to s^*$ then \begin{equation}\label{eq:convergence-setdifference-as} \hat{q}_n \to q^*, \textnormal{ and } \cC_n \to \cC^*.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-886: In words, this result says that if our base model is consistent for the true model, in that $s_n$ converges to an oracle score $s^*$, then conformal prediction will converge to the oracle prediction set given by $\cC^*$.
elements.sent-2411-887: Of course, we will need to formalize all these notions of convergence---what does it mean for the score functions $s_n$ to converge to $s^*$, and what does it mean for the sets $\cC_n$ to converge to the oracle set $\cC^*$? With the appropriate definitions and formalities in place, we will see that these convergence guarantees allow us to show that the split conformal interval $\cC_n$ asymptotically reaches the stated aim of the oracle score (e.g., minimizing the interval length).
elements.sent-2411-888: In Section~\ref{sec:case_studies_classification , we will consider several case studies in a classification setting, examining the aims, oracles, assumptions, and convergence guarantees for each one.}
elements.sent-2411-889: Section~\ref{sec:case_studies_regression will then do the same for case studies in a regression setting.}
elements.sent-2411-890: The underlying technical tools---Theorems~\ref{thm:splitCP_asymp_formal_qn and \ref{thm:splitCP_asymp_formal_random_x , which are summarized in the informal statement, Theorem~\ref{thm:splitCP_asymp_informal , above---will then be developed in Section~\ref{sec:unified_framework_splitCP_asymp .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-891: Before proceeding, we pause to comment on a subtle point regarding how we should interpret these convergence results.
elements.sent-2411-892: In each specific case study that we will develop below, we will assume that the score function $s^*$ is capturing the true model for the data---that is, $s^*$ is truly an ``oracle'' and therefore leads to a prediction set $\cC^*$ that is optimally designed for the distribution $P$ of the data.
elements.sent-2411-893: However, the general theoretical framework of Theorem~\ref{thm:splitCP_asymp_informal (and its formal versions, Theorems~\ref{thm:splitCP_asymp_formal_qn and \ref{thm:splitCP_asymp_formal_random_x ) can be interpreted more broadly: the so-called oracle score function $s^*$ can technically be any fixed function, and does not necessarily need to reflect the true model for the data.}}}
elements.sent-2411-894: For instance, if the true model for $Y\mid X$ is not actually linear, but we are using linear quantile regression to construct the CQR score $s_n$, then we might expect the split conformal prediction set to converge to the best possible linear prediction set---that is, the best possible interval $\cC^*(x)$ whose endpoints are linear functions of $x$.
elements.sent-2411-895: Since we will be developing asymptotic guarantees for the split conformal intervals $\cC_n$, formally we are considering a sequence of problems, indexed by $n\geq 1$, while the underlying distribution $P$ of the data remains fixed.

elements.sent-2411-899: For instance, if our convention is that we use half of the available data for training $s_n$ and the other half for computing $\hat{q _n$, then both datasets $\cD_{{\rm pre ,n}$ and $\cD_n$ would have $n$ data points.}}
elements.sent-2411-900: In the case studies below, we will not make any explicit assumptions about the sample size of the pretraining set $\cD_{{\rm pre ,n}$.}

elements.sent-2411-902: This section considers the classification setting, where the response $Y$ is categorical and takes values in a finite set $\cY$.
elements.sent-2411-903: For each of our two case studies, we will define $\pi^*$ as the true label probability function, \begin{equation}\pi^*(y\mid x) = \P_{(X,Y)\sim P}(Y=y\mid X=x),\end{equation} and $\hat{\pi _n$ will denote a pretrained estimate of $\pi^*$ (that is, in the language of Section~\ref{sec:split-conformal , $\hat{\pi _n$ is an estimate of $\pi^*$ that is fitted using the pretraining set $\cD_{{\rm pre ,n}$).}}}}
elements.subsection-2411-017: Case study: classification with minimal set size
elements.sent-2411-904: In this first case study, our goal is to provide the sets with the smallest average size among those that have $1-\alpha$ marginal coverage---we would like to construct $\cC$ to solve the following optimization problem: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \minimize \quad \E_{X\sim P_X}[|\cC(X)|] \st \quad \P_{(X,Y)\sim P}(Y \in \cC(X)) \geq 1-\alpha. \end{aligned}\end{equation} Here in the categorical setting, $|\cC(X)|$ refers to the cardinality of $\cC(X)$, which is a subset of the finite set of labels $\cY$.
elements.sent-2411-905: Suppose for simplicity that we assume $\pi^*(Y\mid X)$ has a continuous distribution (under $(X,Y)\sim P$).
elements.sent-2411-906: It is not hard to prove that the solution to the above optimization problem then has the form $\cC^*(x) = \{ y : \pi^*(y \mid x) \geq t \}$, for some appropriate value $t$.
elements.sent-2411-907: Rewriting this into score function notation, we can define the oracle score \begin{equation}\label{eqn:case_study_1_classification} s^*(x,y) = -\pi^*(y\mid x),\end{equation} where we take the negative of the conditional label probability $\pi^*$ because our prediction set should include labels $y$ with high probability (which therefore should correspond to low values of the score).
elements.sent-2411-908: We can then construct the oracle prediction interval, \begin{equation}\cC^*(x) = \{ y : s^*(x,y) \leq q^* \},\end{equation} where \begin{equation}q^* = \inf\left\{q \in\R: \P_{(X,Y)\sim P}(s^*(X,Y)\leq q) \geq 1-\alpha\right\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-909: To approximate the oracle sets, we can instead use plug-in estimate $\hat \pi_n$ of the probability $\pi^*$.
elements.sent-2411-910: This leads to the score function \begin{equation}s_n(x,y) = -\hat \pi_n(y \mid x),\end{equation} which is the high-probability score defined previously in Chapter~\ref{chapter:introduction .}
elements.sent-2411-911: This results in the prediction set \begin{equation}\cC_n(x) = \{y\in\cY: s_n(x,y) \leq \hat{q}_n\} = \{y\in\cY : \hat{\pi}_n(y\mid x) \geq -\hat{q}_n\},\end{equation} i.e., all possible labels $y\in\cY$ whose estimated conditional probability is sufficiently large.
elements.sent-2411-912: We will assume that the estimated conditional distribution of $Y\mid X$, given by $\hat{\pi _n$ as defined above, is consistent as an estimator of the true $\pi^*$.}
elements.sent-2411-913: Specifically, at any $x\in\cX$, consider the quantity \begin{equation}\dtv\big(\pi^*(\cdot\mid x), \hat\pi_n(\cdot \mid x)\big) = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{y\in\cY} \big|\pi^*(y\mid x) - \hat\pi_n(y\mid x)\big|,\end{equation} which is the total variation distance between the true and estimated conditional distributions of $Y\mid X=x$.
elements.sent-2411-914: We will assume that this estimation error is small on average over the test feature $X$---that is, we will measure consistency via the quantity \begin{equation}\E_{X\sim P_X}\left[\dtv\big(\pi^*(\cdot\mid X), \hat\pi_n(\cdot \mid X)\big)\right]= \E_{X\sim P_X}\left[\frac{1}{2}\sum_{y\in\cY} \big|\pi^*(y\mid X) - \hat\pi_n(y\mid X)\big|\right].\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-217: \begin{equation}\E_{X\sim P_X}\left[\dtv\big(\pi^*(\cdot\mid X), \hat\pi_n(\cdot \mid X)\big)\right]= \E_{X\sim P_X}\left[\frac{1}{2}\sum_{y\in\cY} \big|\pi^*(y\mid X) - \hat\pi_n(y\mid X)\big|\right].\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-915: Informally, assuming that \begin{equation}\E_{X\sim P_X}\left[\dtv\big(\pi^*(\cdot\mid X), \hat\pi_n(\cdot \mid X)\big)\right]\to 0\end{equation} can be thought of as effectively claiming that, for large $n$, the conditional density of $Y\mid X=x$ is estimated accurately at most values $x$.
elements.sent-2411-916: For this consistency condition, and hereafter throughout the chapter, the notation $\E_{X\sim P_X}$ and $\P_{X\sim P_X}$ (or, $\E_{(X,Y)\sim P}$ and $\P_{(X,Y)\sim P}$) denotes that we are taking expected value or probability only with respect to the randomness of a random test point $X$ sampled according to $P_X$ (or $(X,Y)\sim P$).
elements.sent-2411-917: In particular, we are conditioning on the pretraining and calibration datasets---that is, $\hat\pi_n$, $\hat{q _n$, and other quantities that are constructed when running split conformal prediction, are all being treated as fixed.}
elements.sent-2411-918: We now arrive at our formal result.
elements.sent-2411-919: Under the above model assumption, the split conformal prediction set $\cC_n$ constructed with the score $s_n$ above is asymptotically a solution to the optimization problem~\eqref{eqn:case_study_1_classification .}
elements.sent-2411-920: Assume that the conditional probability $\pi^*(Y\mid X)$ is continuously distributed under $(X,Y)\sim P$.
elements.sent-2411-921: Then the following claim holds almost surely: if \begin{equation}\E_{X\sim P_X}\left[\dtv\big(\pi^*(\cdot\mid X), \hat\pi_n(\cdot \mid X)\big)\right]\to 0,\end{equation} then \begin{equation}\limsup_{n\to\infty}\E_{X\sim P_X}[|\cC_n(X)|] \leq \E_{X\sim P_X}[|\cC^*(X)|] \textnormal{ \ and \ }\liminf_{n\to\infty}\P_{(X,Y)\sim P}(Y\in\cC_n(X)) \geq 1-\alpha,\end{equation} i.e., $\cC_n$ is asymptotically optimal for the aim~\eqref{eqn:case_study_1_classification .}
elements.sent-2411-922: In words, conformal prediction with the high-probability score yields optimal set sizes as the fitted model probabilities $\hat \pi_n$ approach the true model $\pi^*$.
elements.sent-2411-923: This suggests that the high-probability score will generally be a good choice if one is seeking small average set size.
elements.sent-2411-924: Note, however, that the aim of having small average set length is often in tension with having good conditional coverage.
elements.subsection-2411-018: Case study: classification with minimal set size and conditional coverage
elements.sent-2411-926: Next, we provide sets that have the smallest size among those that have $1-\alpha$ conditional coverage.
elements.sent-2411-927: Note that optimizing set size while only enforcing marginal coverage can result in poor behavior, as we saw in Chapter~\ref{chapter:conditional (see Figure~\ref{fig:conditional-coverage ).}}
elements.sent-2411-928: Consequently, it is often better to target conditional coverage, as we do next.
elements.sent-2411-929: The corresponding optimization problem is as follows: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \label{eqn:case_study_2_classification} \text{minimize} \quad \E_{X \sim P_X}[|\cC(X)|] subject to \quad \P_{Y\sim P_{Y\mid X}}(Y \in \cC(X) \mid X) \geq 1-\alpha \text{ almost surely.} \end{aligned}\end{equation} The sets $\cC(X)$ that solve the above optimization problem also have the smallest size conditionally on $X$, almost surely.
elements.formula-2411-221: \begin{equation} \label{eqn:case_study_2_classification} minimize \quad \E_{X \sim P_X}[|\cC(X)|] subject to \quad \P_{Y\sim P_{Y\mid X}}(Y \in \cC(X) \mid X) \geq 1-\alpha  almost surely. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-930: In other words, for almost every $x\in\cX$, there cannot be no smaller subset of $\cY$ that would still maintain $1-\alpha$ coverage, since this would contradict the optimality of our set.
elements.sent-2411-931: A short calculation will show that the following set is the solution to our aim: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:case_study_2_classification_preliminary}\cC^*(x) = \{y: \pi^*(y\mid x)\geq t^*(x)\},\end{equation} where, at each $x\in\cX$, $t^*(x)$ is chosen so that $\P_{Y\sim P_{Y\mid X (\pi^*(Y\mid X)\geq t^*(x)\mid X=x) \geq 1-\alpha$; see Figure~\ref{fig:case_study_2_classification for an illustration of this set.}}}
elements.sent-2411-932: The reason for the optimality of this score is relatively straightforward to see: for any $x$, the oracle prediction set $\cC^*(x)$ includes only the most likely classes until the total mass reaches (at least) $1-\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-933: Any other rule would have to include at least as many classes to reach the $1-\alpha$ threshold.
elements.sent-2411-934: However, the oracle prediction set given in~\eqref{eqn:case_study_2_classification_preliminary cannot be immediately converted construction of the form $\{y:s^*(x,y)\leq q^*\}$, as in Theorem~\ref{thm:splitCP_asymp_informal ---this is because the threshold is dependent on $x$, rather than shared over all $x\in\cX$.}}
elements.sent-2411-935: Thus, we will need to reformulate this set.
elements.sent-2411-936: Define \begin{equation}s^*(x,y) = \sum_{y'\in\cY} \pi^*(y'\mid x) \cdot \ind{\pi^*(y'\mid x)> \pi^*(y\mid x)},\end{equation} which is the (conditional) probability captured by the set $\{y' : \pi^*(y'\mid x)> \pi^*(y\mid x)\}$, i.e., all labels that are strictly more likely than the label $y$ (given features $x$).
elements.sent-2411-937: With this reformulation, we can verify that the set defined in~\eqref{eqn:case_study_2_classification_preliminary can equivalently be written as \begin{equation}\cC^*(x) = \{y : s^*(x,y) < 1-\alpha \}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-938: If we again assume a continuity condition for simplicity---namely, that $\P_{(X,Y)\sim P (s^*(X,Y)=1-\alpha)=0$---then the set \begin{equation}\label{eqn:case_study_2_classification_reformulated} \cC^*(x) = \{y : s^*(x,y) \leq 1-\alpha \}\end{equation} is also a solution to~\eqref{eqn:case_study_2_classification , since it differs from the first definition only on an event of measure zero.}}
elements.sent-2411-939: In particular, this means that the oracle prediction set can be defined by a criterion of the form $s^*(x,y)\leq q^*$ (where $q^*=1-\alpha$), which thus fits into the notation of Theorem~\ref{thm:splitCP_asymp_informal .}
elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[width=0.55\textwidth]{figures/diagrams/oracle-classification-conditional-coverage.pdf} \caption{An illustration of the oracle prediction set for classification with conditional coverage. This figure illustrates the oracle prediction set $\cC^*(x)$constructed in~\eqref{eqn:case_study_2_classification_preliminary}, in yellow. The prediction sets all contain a $1-\alpha$ proportion of the probability mass.} \label{fig:case_study_2_classification}
elements.sent-2411-940: We again use a plug-in approach to approximate the oracle score distribution: \begin{equation}s_n(x,y) = \sum_{y'\in\cY} \hat \pi_n(y'\mid x) \cdot \ind{\hat \pi_n(y'\mid x)> \hat \pi_n(y\mid x)}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-941: To contrast this with the high-probability score of Section~\ref{sec:case_study_1_classification , we will refer to this as the cumulative-probability score.}
elements.sent-2411-942: We will require the same convergence condition on $\hat\pi_n$ as in the previous case study (in Section~\ref{sec:case_study_1_classification ): we will again assume that \begin{equation}\E_{X\sim P_X}\left[\dtv\big(\pi^*(\cdot\mid X), \hat\pi_n(\cdot \mid X)\big)\right] \to 0.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-943: We now want to show that, under the above model assumption, the split conformal prediction set $\cC_n$ constructed with $s_n$ above is asymptotically a solution to the optimization problem~\eqref{eqn:case_study_2_classification .}
elements.sent-2411-944: To this end, we first need to formalize what we mean by an ``asymptotic'' solution---what does it mean to achieve conditional coverage in an asymptotic sense? There are multiple valid answers to this question; here we will choose a particular one.
elements.sent-2411-945: Recall that (exact) conditional coverage means that \begin{equation}\P_{Y\sim P_{Y\mid X}}(Y\in\cC_n(X)\mid X) \geq 1-\alpha\end{equation} holds almost surely, with respect to $X\sim P_X$.
elements.sent-2411-946: We will take the convention that achieving conditional coverage in an approximate sense means that this inequality holds, at least approximately, for most $X$---i.e., with probability $\approx$ 1 with respect to $X\sim P_X$.
elements.sent-2411-947: Assume that the oracle score $s^*(X,Y)$ satisfies the following condition: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:continuity_for_case_study_2_classification}\P_{(X,Y)\sim P}(s^*(X,Y) = 0) < 1-\alpha, \textnormal{ and } \P_{(X,Y)\sim P}(s^*(X,Y) = t) = 0\textnormal{ for all $t>0$}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-229: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:continuity_for_case_study_2_classification}\P_{(X,Y)\sim P}(s^*(X,Y) = 0) < 1-\alpha, \textnormal{ and } \P_{(X,Y)\sim P}(s^*(X,Y) = t) = 0\textnormal{ for all $t>0$}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-948: Assume also that, for any $y\neq y'\in\cY$, we have $\pi^*(y\mid X)\neq\pi^*(y'\mid X)$ almost surely under $X\sim P_X$.
elements.sent-2411-949: Then the following claim holds almost surely: if \begin{equation}\E_{X\sim P_X}\left[\dtv\big(\pi^*(\cdot\mid X), \hat\pi_n(\cdot \mid X)\big)\right]\to 0\end{equation} then \begin{equation}\limsup_{n\to\infty}\E_{X\sim P_X}[|\cC_n(X)| ] \leq \E_{X\sim P_X}[|\cC^*(X)|]\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\lim_{n\to\infty}\P_{X\sim P_X}\Big( \,\P_{Y\sim P_{Y\mid X}}(Y\in\cC_n(X)\mid X) \geq 1-\alpha - \epsilon\,\Big) = 1\textnormal{ for all $\epsilon>0$},\end{equation} i.e., $\cC_n$ is asymptotically optimal for the aim~\eqref{eqn:case_study_2_classification .}
elements.formula-2411-231: \begin{equation}\lim_{n\to\infty}\P_{X\sim P_X}\Big( \,\P_{Y\sim P_{Y\mid X}}(Y\in\cC_n(X)\mid X) \geq 1-\alpha - \epsilon\,\Big) = 1\textnormal{ for all $\epsilon>0$},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-950: In words, conformal prediction with the cumulative-probability score gives the smallest sets with conditional coverage as the fitted model probabilities $\hat \pi_n$ approach the true probabilities $\pi$.
elements.sent-2411-951: This suggests the cumulative-probability score is generally a good choice when one is seeking to approximately achieve conditional coverage.
elements.sent-2411-952: This section considers the regression setting, where the response $Y$ is real-valued, i.e., $\cY = \R$.
elements.sent-2411-953: We will assume that the conditional distribution of $Y\mid X=x$, under the joint distribution $(X,Y)\sim P$, has a conditional density \begin{equation}f^*(y \mid x)\end{equation} with respect to Lebesgue measure.
elements.sent-2411-954: We also assume access to an estimate of this conditional density, given by $\hat{f _n(y \mid x)$.}
elements.sent-2411-955: We also make use of the conditional quantile function, $\tau^*(x; \beta)$ (that is, the $\beta$-quantile of the conditional distribution of $Y$ given $X=x$), and its corresponding estimator, $\hat{\tau _n(x ; \beta)$.}
elements.subsection-2411-019: Case study: regression with minimal set size
elements.sent-2411-956: Our next goal is to provide the sets with the smallest average size among those that have $1-\alpha$ marginal coverage in the regression setting.
elements.sent-2411-957: This goal is entirely analogous to that in Section~\ref{sec:case_study_1_classification , and the score solves the same optimization problem, \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \text{minimize} \quad \E_{X\sim P_X}[|\cC(X)|] subject to \quad \P_{(X,Y)\sim P}(Y \in \cC(X)) \geq 1-\alpha, \end{aligned} \label{eq:case_study_1_regression}\end{equation} the only difference being that $|\cC(X)|$ now refers to the size of $\cC(X)$ according to the Lebesgue measure.}
elements.sent-2411-958: By a standard argument reminiscent of the Neyman--Pearson lemma, the set construction with the smallest average size has the form $\cC^*(x) = \{ y : f^*(y \mid x) \geq t^* \}$, where $t^*$ is chosen as the largest possible value that still yields coverage at level $\geq 1-\alpha$: \begin{equation}t^* = \sup \{t \in \R : \P_{(X,Y)\sim P}(f^*(Y \mid X) \geq t) \geq 1-\alpha \}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-959: As earlier, this can equivalently be written as the set $\cC^*(x) = \{ y : s^*(x,y) \leq q^* \}$, where $s^*(x,y) = -f^*(y \mid x)$ and $q^* = -t^*$.
elements.sent-2411-960: All these definitions are entirely analogous to those used in Section~\ref{sec:case_study_1_classification , only now we are working in the context of regression.}
elements.sent-2411-961: To approximate the oracle sets, we use an estimate of the density $\hat{f}$ in place of the unknown $f^*$: \begin{equation}s_n(x,y) = -\hat{f}_n(y \mid x).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-962: We will refer to this as the high-density score---it is exactly analogous to the high-probability score of Section~\ref{sec:case_study_1_classification but with conditional density in place of conditional probability.}
elements.sent-2411-963: This leads to a prediction set of the form \begin{equation}\cC_n(x) = \{y\in\R: s_n(x,y) \leq \hat{q}_n\} = \{y\in\R : \hat{f}_n(y\mid x) \geq -\hat{q}_n\},\end{equation} i.e., all values $y$ with a sufficiently high estimated conditional density.
elements.sent-2411-964: We will assume that the estimated conditional density of $Y\mid X$, given by $\hat{f _n$ as defined above, is almost surely consistent as an estimator of the true $f^*$ with respect to TV distance.}
elements.sent-2411-965: In the continuous setting, for all $x\in\cX$, we calculate the TV distance between the true and estimated conditional distributions of $Y\mid X=x$ as \begin{equation}\dtv\big(f^*(\cdot\mid x), \hat f_n(\cdot \mid x)\big) = \frac{1}{2}\int_{y\in\R} \big|f^*(y\mid x) - \hat f_n(y\mid x)\big|.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-237: \begin{equation}\dtv\big(f^*(\cdot\mid x), \hat f_n(\cdot \mid x)\big) = \frac{1}{2}\int_{y\in\R} \big|f^*(y\mid x) - \hat f_n(y\mid x)\big|.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-966: We will measure TV distance on average over $X$: \begin{equation}\E_{X\sim P_X}\left[\dtv\big(f^*(\cdot\mid X), \hat f_n(\cdot \mid X)\big)\right]= \E_{X\sim P_X}\left[\frac{1}{2}\int_{y\in\cY} \big|f^*(y\mid X) - \hat f_n(y\mid X)\big|\right].\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-238: \begin{equation}\E_{X\sim P_X}\left[\dtv\big(f^*(\cdot\mid X), \hat f_n(\cdot \mid X)\big)\right]= \E_{X\sim P_X}\left[\frac{1}{2}\int_{y\in\cY} \big|f^*(y\mid X) - \hat f_n(y\mid X)\big|\right].\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-967: The core assumption will be that the estimation error vanishes in TV distance, \begin{equation}\E_{X\sim P_X}\left[\dtv\big(f^*(\cdot\mid X), \hat f_n(\cdot \mid X)\big)\right]\to 0,\end{equation} which means that for large $n$, the conditional density of $Y\mid X=x$ is estimated accurately at almost all $x$.
elements.sent-2411-968: We now show that, under the above model assumption, the split conformal prediction set $\cC_n$ constructed with $s_n$ above is asymptotically a solution to the optimization problem~\eqref{eq:case_study_1_regression .}
elements.sent-2411-969: Assume that the conditional density $f^*(Y\mid X)$ has a continuous distribution under $(X,Y)\sim P$.
elements.sent-2411-971: Then the following claim holds almost surely: if \begin{equation}\E_{X\sim P_X}\left[\dtv\big(f^*(\cdot\mid X), \hat f_n(\cdot \mid X)\big)\right]\to 0\end{equation} then \begin{equation}\limsup_{n\to\infty}\E_{X\sim P_X}[|\cC_n(X)|] \leq \E_{X\sim P_X}[|\cC^*(X)|] \textnormal{ \ and \ }\liminf_{n\to\infty}\P_{(X,Y)\sim P}(Y\in\cC_n(X)) \geq 1-\alpha,\end{equation} i.e., $\cC_n$ is asymptotically optimal for the aim~\eqref{eq:case_study_1_regression .}
elements.sent-2411-972: This result suggests that the high-density score is a good choice when one wants prediction intervals that have the smallest average length.
elements.sent-2411-973: However, as mentioned previously in Section~\ref{sec:case_study_1_classification , aiming for small average length may conflict with achieving conditional coverage.}
elements.sent-2411-974: We consider the question of conditional coverage next.
elements.subsection-2411-020: Case study: regression with minimal-length equal-tailed intervals
elements.sent-2411-975: Our next goal is to provide the prediction intervals with the smallest average size among those with $1-\alpha$ equal-tailed conditional coverage.
elements.sent-2411-976: Equal-tailed conditional coverage means that, conditionally on $X=x$ for all $x$, the true value of $Y$ will lie below the lower endpoint of the interval with probability $\alpha/2$, and similarly, the true value of $Y$ will lie above the upper endpoint of the interval with probability $\alpha/2$.
elements.sent-2411-977: Thus, errors happen equally frequently in the upper tail and lower tail.
elements.sent-2411-978: Setting this up as an optimization problem, we want to solve \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \text{minimize} \quad \E_{X\sim P_X}[|\cC(X)|] subject to \quad \P_{Y\sim P_{Y\mid X}}(Y > \sup\cC(X) \mid X ) \leq \alpha/2, \quad \P_{Y\sim P_{Y\mid X}}(Y < \inf\cC(X) \mid X ) \leq \alpha/2, \textnormal{$\cC(x)$ is an interval for all $x$.} \end{aligned} \label{eq:case_study_2_regression}\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-241: \begin{equation} minimize \quad \E_{X\sim P_X}[|\cC(X)|] subject to \quad \P_{Y\sim P_{Y\mid X}}(Y > \sup\cC(X) \mid X ) \leq \alpha/2, \quad \P_{Y\sim P_{Y\mid X}}(Y < \inf\cC(X) \mid X ) \leq \alpha/2, \textnormal{$\cC(x)$ is an interval for all $x$.}  \label{eq:case_study_2_regression}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-979: With access to the true conditional quantile function, we can simply take the oracle set to be \begin{equation}\cC^*(x) = [\tau^*(x ; \alpha/2), \tau^*(x ; 1-\alpha/2)],\end{equation} where we recall that $\tau^*(x;\beta)$ is the $\beta$-quantile of the conditional distribution of $Y\mid X=x$.
elements.sent-2411-980: Since this conditional distribution is continuous, this prediction interval, whose endpoints are given by the $(\alpha/2)$- and $(1-\alpha/2)$-quantiles of $Y\mid X=x$, solves the optimization problem in~\eqref{eq:case_study_2_regression .}
elements.sent-2411-981: We now want to define a split conformal prediction set $\cC_n$ that is asymptotically a solution to the optimization problem~\eqref{eq:case_study_1_regression .}
elements.sent-2411-982: For this purpose, we will construct $\cC_n$ using the score function \begin{equation}s_n(x,y) = \max\{\hat{\tau}_n(x; \alpha/2) - y, y - \hat{\tau}_n(x; 1-\alpha/2)\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-983: As in Chapter~\ref{chapter:introduction , since this is the score used in the conformalized quantile regression (CQR) method, we refer to this as the CQR score.}
elements.sent-2411-984: This score leads to a prediction interval of the form \begin{equation}\cC_n(x) = \left[ \hat{\tau}_n(x; \alpha/2) - \hat{q}_n , \hat{\tau}_n(x; 1-\alpha/2) + \hat{q}_n\right],\end{equation} where we take the convention that $[a,b]$ denotes the empty set if $a>b$, to handle degenerate cases.
elements.sent-2411-985: To see how this corresponds to the oracle interval, consider an oracle score, \begin{equation}s^*(x,y) = \max\{\tau^*(x; \alpha/2) - y, y - \tau^*(x; 1-\alpha/2)\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-986: Then, by construction, we have $\cC^*(x) = \{y\in\R: s^*(x,y)\leq q^*\}$, when we choose $q^*=0$.
elements.sent-2411-987: We will assume that the estimated $\alpha/2$ and $1-\alpha/2$ conditional quantiles of $Y \mid X$, given by $\hat{\tau _n(x ; \alpha/2)$ and $\hat{\tau _n(x ; 1-\alpha/2)$ respectively, converge to their oracle values: \begin{equation}\E_{X \sim P_X}[|\hat{\tau}(X ; \alpha/2) - \tau^*(X ; \alpha/2)|] \to 0 \text{ and } \E_{X \sim P_X}[|\hat{\tau}(X ; 1-\alpha/2) - \tau^*(X ; 1-\alpha/2)|] \to 0.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-988: This means that, for large $n$, the $\alpha/2$ and $1-\alpha/2$ quantiles converge on average over $X$.
elements.sent-2411-989: In this case, the equal-tailed CQR intervals converge to the oracle interval, as stated next.
elements.sent-2411-990: Assume that the conditional density $f^*$ satisfies $f^*(y\mid x)>0$ for all $(x,y)$, and that \begin{equation}\label{eqn:case_study_2_regression_finite_quantile_gap} \sup_{x\in\cX}\left(\tau^*(x;1-\alpha/2) - \tau^*(x;\alpha/2)\right) < \infty.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-991: Then the following claim holds almost surely: if \begin{equation}\E_{X \sim P_X}[|\hat{\tau}(X ; \alpha/2) - \tau^*(X ; \alpha/2)|] \to 0 \text{ and } \E_{X \sim P_X}[|\hat{\tau}(X ; 1-\alpha/2) - \tau^*(X ; 1-\alpha/2)|] \to 0,\end{equation} then \begin{equation}\limsup_{n\to\infty}\E_{X\sim P_X}[|\cC_n(X)| ] \leq \E_{X\sim P_X}[|\cC^*(X)|],\end{equation} and, for all $\epsilon>0$, \begin{equation}\lim_{n\to\infty}\P_{X\sim P_X}\Big( \,\P_{Y\sim P_{Y\mid X}}(Y > \sup \cC_n(X)\mid X) \geq \alpha/2+\epsilon \,\Big) = 0\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\lim_{n\to\infty}\P_{X\sim P_X}\Big( \,\P_{Y\sim P_{Y\mid X}}(Y < \inf \cC_n(X)\mid X) \geq \alpha/2+\epsilon \,\Big) = 0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-992: That is, $\cC_n$ is asymptotically optimal for the aim~\eqref{eq:case_study_2_regression .}
elements.sent-2411-993: This result suggests that the CQR score is a good choice for approximate conditional coverage with regression problems.

elements.section-2411-026: A unified framework for asymptotic guarantees
elements.sent-2411-995: This section gives the formal framework underlying the asymptotic optimality guarantees in the previous sections.
elements.sent-2411-996: The key is a formal version of Theorem~\ref{thm:splitCP_asymp_informal .}
elements.sent-2411-997: We will then give the proofs of the asymptotic optimality guarantees for the four case studies---Propositions~\ref{prop:case_study_1_classification ,~\ref{prop:case_study_2_classification ,~\ref{prop:case_study_1_regression , and~\ref{prop:case_study_2_regression ---using a unified, step-by-step approach.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-998: The theory in this chapter will be presented in a way that emphasizes its generality: this framework is a foundational approach that can be used to prove many asymptotic guarantees about conformal prediction, including but not limited to the specific propositions in the previous chapters.
elements.sent-2411-999: To begin, we need to lay out a precise asymptotic framework for the question.
elements.sent-2411-1000: We will define a sequence of split conformal prediction problems, indexed by $n$, the size of the calibration set.
elements.sent-2411-1001: In other words, for each $n$, we will define a pretraining set of size $m_n$ and a calibration set of size $n$: \begin{equation}\underbrace{(X'_{n,1},Y'_{n,1}),\dots,(X'_{n,m_n},Y'_{n,m_n})}_{\textnormal{pretraining set $\cD_{{\rm pre},n}$}}\, ,\ \underbrace{(X_{n,1},Y_{n,1}),\dots,(X_{n,n},Y_{n,n})}_{\textnormal{calibration set $\cD_n$}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1002: The data points $((X'_{n,i ,Y'_{n,i ))_{i\in[m_n]}$ form a pretraining set of size $m_n$, used to train a conformal score function: \begin{equation}\textnormal{$s_n:\cX\times\cY\to\R$ is constructed as a function of }\cD_{{\rm pre},n} = ((X'_{n,i},Y'_{n,i}))_{i\in[m_n]}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-253: \begin{equation}\textnormal{$s_n:\cX\times\cY\to\R$ is constructed as a function of }\cD_{{\rm pre},n} = ((X'_{n,i},Y'_{n,i}))_{i\in[m_n]}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-1003: The data points $((X_{n,i ,Y_{n,i ))_{i\in[n]}$ form the calibration set, so that the quantile $\hat{q _n$ is then computed as \begin{equation}\hat{q}_n = \quantile\left(s_n(X_{n,1},Y_{n,1}),\dots,s_n(X_{n,n},Y_{n,n}); 1-\alpha_n\right)\end{equation} (where $1-\alpha_n = (1-\alpha)(1+1/n)$, as in our usual definition of the split conformal method).}}}
elements.sent-2411-1004: Finally, at any test feature value $x$, the split conformal prediction interval is given by \begin{equation}\cC_n(x) = \left\{y\in\cY : s_n(x,y) \leq \hat{q}_n\right\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1005: In this chapter, we have focused on the i.i.d.\ data setting.
elements.sent-2411-1006: Under our new notation, this means that we assume \begin{equation}\label{eqn:iid_asymp}(X'_{n,1},Y'_{n,1}),\dots,(X'_{n,m_n},Y'_{n,m_n}),(X_{n,1},Y_{n,1}),\dots,(X_{n,n},Y_{n,n})\iidsim P,\end{equation} for each $n\geq1$, where $P$ is some fixed distribution on $\cX\times\cY$.
elements.sent-2411-1007: In particular, this implies that the calibration data points $(X_{n,i ,Y_{n,i )$ are independent of the pretrained score function $s_n$, since $s_n$ is fitted to the pretraining set $\cD_{{\rm pre ,n}$ (which is disjoint from the calibration set).}}}
elements.sent-2411-1008: Note that this condition places no assumptions on the dependence structure of the sequence of pretraining and calibration sets; for example, $\cD_{{\rm pre ,n}$ and $\cD_{{\rm pre ,n+1}$ could be dependent or independent.}}
elements.sent-2411-1009: As a preliminary result, we will study the convergence properties of the values $\hat{q _n$ that define the thresholds for the split conformal prediction intervals.}
elements.sent-2411-1010: For this part of the analysis, we will need to assume the sequence of score functions $s_n$ converges to the oracle score $s^*$, in a particular sense.
elements.sent-2411-1011: To be precise, we will write $s_n\stackrel{\rm CDF {\longrightarrow s^*$ to mean that \begin{equation}F_{P,s_n}(t)\to F_{P,s^*}(t),\textnormal{ for any $t\in\R$ such that $F_{P,s^*}$ is continuous at $t$},\end{equation} where, for any $s:\cX\times\cY\to\R$, we define $F_{P,s}$ to denote the CDF of $s(X,Y)$ under $(X,Y)\sim P$.}}
elements.formula-2411-257: \begin{equation}F_{P,s_n}(t)\to F_{P,s^*}(t),\textnormal{ for any $t\in\R$ such that $F_{P,s^*}$ is continuous at $t$},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-1012: Even though $s_n$ is a function of the randomly drawn pretraining set $\cD_{\rm pre ,n}$, the CDF $F_{P,s_n}$ is treating $s_n$ as fixed---that is, $F_{P,s_n}$ is the CDF of the score $s_n(X,Y)$ when we treat $s_n$ as fixed and only consider randomness coming from drawing an independent new data point $(X,Y)\sim P$.
elements.sent-2411-1013: In other words, $s_n \stackrel{\rm CDF {\longrightarrow s^*$ is a (random) event---it is the event that $s_n(X,Y)\to s^*(X,Y)$ in distribution, when $(X,Y)\sim P$ is treated as random while the score functions $s_1,s_2,\dots$ are treated as fixed.}}
elements.sent-2411-1014: For each $n\geq1$, assume the data follows assumption~\eqref{eqn:iid_asymp for some distribution $P$ on $\cX\times\cY$, and define the split conformal prediction interval $\cC_n(x) = \{y\in\cY: s_n(x,y) \leq \hat{q _n\}$ where \begin{equation}s_n(x,y) = s\left((x,y); \cD_{{\rm pre},n}\right)\end{equation} for $\cD_{{\rm pre ,n = ((X'_{n,i ,Y'_{n,i ))_{i\in[m_n]}$, and \begin{equation}\hat{q}_n = \quantile\left(s_n(X_{n,1},Y_{n,1}),\dots,s_n(X_{n,n},Y_{n,n}); 1-\alpha_n\right),\end{equation} for a sequence $\alpha_n\to\alpha\in(0,1)$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1015: Let $s^*:\cX\times\cY\to\R$ be any fixed score function, and define \begin{equation}q^* = \inf\{t: F_{P,s^*}(t)\geq 1-\alpha\}\textnormal{ \ and \ } q^*_+ = \sup\{t: F_{P,s^*}(t)\leq 1-\alpha\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1016: Then the following statement holds almost surely: \begin{equation}\textnormal{If $s_n\stackrel{\rm CDF}{\longrightarrow} s^*$, \ then \ $q^* \leq \liminf_{n\to\infty}\hat{q}_n \leq \limsup_{n\to\infty}\hat{q}_n \leq q^*_+$}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1017: To interpret the result of this theorem, we need to understand the quantities $q^*$ and $q^*_+$.

elements.sent-2411-1055: In the results above, we have studied convergence of the thresholds, $\hat{q _n$.}
elements.sent-2411-1056: In this section, we will now translate these results into a statement about convergence of the split conformal prediction intervals: we will aim to bound the difference between $\cC_n$, the split conformal prediction interval, and the ``oracle'' prediction interval $\cC^*$ given by \begin{equation}\cC^*(x) = \{y\in\cY : s^*(x,y)\leq q^*\},\end{equation} where $s^*$ is some fixed score function (e.g., reflecting the true model of the data), and where $q^*$ is the $(1-\alpha)$-quantile of $s^*(X,Y)$ under the data distribution $(X,Y)\sim P$.
elements.sent-2411-1057: At a high level, we are going to verify that \begin{equation}\label{eqn:convergence_of_sets_intuition}\textnormal{If $s_n$ converges to $s^*$, and $s^*$ satisfies some regularity conditions, then $\cC_n$ converges to $\cC^*$,}\end{equation} where $\cC_n(x) = \{y\in\cY:s_n(x,y)\leq \hat{q _n\}$ is the split conformal prediction set.}
elements.formula-2411-271: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:convergence_of_sets_intuition}\textnormal{If $s_n$ converges to $s^*$, and $s^*$ satisfies some regularity conditions, then $\cC_n$ converges to $\cC^*$,}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-1058: The remainder of this section will develop a formal version of the statement~\eqref{eqn:convergence_of_sets_intuition .}
elements.sent-2411-1059: In order to be able to ask whether the prediction set construction $\cC_n$ converges to the oracle $\cC^*$, we will study the symmetric set difference between the sets, $\cC_n(X)\triangle\cC^*(X)$---specifically, we will establish a bound on $\P_{(X,Y)\sim P (Y\in \cC_n(X)\triangle\cC^*(X))$, the mass of the symmetric set difference under a draw of a new data point $(X,Y)\sim P$.}
elements.sent-2411-1060: To be able to achieve this type of bound, we will need to define a stronger notion of convergence for the score functions $s_n$.
elements.sent-2411-1061: While in the previous section, it was sufficient to assume $s_n\stackrel{\rm CDF {\longrightarrow s^*$ in order to establish results on the $\hat{q _n$'s, this will no longer be sufficient: when we draw a test point $(X,Y)\sim P$, we need the evaluated scores $s_n(X,Y)$ and $s^*(X,Y)$ to return similar values, not just to be similar in distribution.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1062: To make this concrete, we will define a stronger notion of convergence: we will write $s_n\stackrel{P {\to s^*$ to mean that \begin{equation}\lim_{n\to\infty}\P_{(X,Y)\sim P}\Big(|s_n(X,Y)-s^*(X,Y)|>\epsilon\Big) = 0 \textnormal{ for all $\epsilon>0$}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-1063: Note that, treating the functions $s_n$ as fixed (as before), this simply means that the random variables $s_n(X,Y)$ converge in probability to $s^*(X,Y)$.
elements.sent-2411-1064: To compare the two notions of convergence, we have \begin{equation}\label{eqn:compare_convergence_sn}s_n\stackrel{P}{\to}s^* \ \Longrightarrow \ s_n\stackrel{\rm CDF}{\longrightarrow} s^*.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1065: Under the setting and notation of Theorem~\ref{thm:splitCP_asymp_formal_qn , assume also that \begin{equation}\label{eqn:asm_thm:splitCP_asymp_formal_random_x}\P_{(X,Y)\sim P}(s^*(X,Y)= q^*_+) = 0.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-1066: Then the following statement holds almost surely: \begin{equation}\textnormal{If $s_n\stackrel{P}{\to} s^*$ \ then \ $\P_{(X,Y)\sim P}\left(Y\in\cC_n(X)\triangle\cC^*(X)\right)\to 0$.}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1067: We remark also that the assumption~\eqref{eqn:asm_thm:splitCP_asymp_formal_random_x will immediately hold if we assume that $s^*(X,Y)$ has a continuous distribution under $(X,Y)\sim P$, but (as we will see below in the proofs for one of our case studies) it can hold more generally as well.}
elements.sent-2411-1068: Returning to our original goal~\eqref{eqn:convergence_of_sets_intuition for this section, we can interpret this theorem as follows: \begin{equation}\textnormal{If }\underbrace{\textnormal{$s_n$ converges to $s^*$,}}_{\textnormal{i.e., $s_n\stackrel{P}{\to}s^*$}}\textnormal{ \ and \ }\underbrace{\textnormal{$s^*$ satisfies some regularity conditions,}}_{\textnormal{i.e., condition~\eqref{eqn:asm_thm:splitCP_asymp_formal_random_x} holds}}\textnormal{ \ then \ }\underbrace{\textnormal{$\cC_n$ converges to $\cC^*$.}}_{\textnormal{i.e., bound $ \cC_n(X)\triangle\cC^*(X)$}}\end{equation} Of course, while this result measures the difference between $\cC_n$ and $\cC^*$ in terms of probability (by bounding the mass placed by $P$ on the event $Y\in \cC_n(X)\triangle\cC^*(X)$), similar techniques can be used to bound the difference of the sets in other ways---for instance, in the setting of a real-valued response ($\cY=\R$), we might instead be interested in bounding the Lebesgue measure of $\cC_n(X)\triangle\cC^*(X)$.}

elements.section-2411-027: Model-based robustness to violations of exchangeability
elements.sent-2411-1140: In this final section of Chapter~\ref{chapter:model-based , we will turn to a question of a very different flavor: instead of asking about guarantees that can be obtained by placing model-based assumptions on the data in addition to the assumption of exchangeability, we will now ask whether model-based assumptions allow for conformal type methods to perform well even if exchangeability does not hold.}
elements.sent-2411-1141: For simplicity, let us again consider only split conformal prediction throughout this section, so that we have a pretrained score function $s_n:\cX\times\cY\to \R$.
elements.sent-2411-1142: It may seem initially that exchangeability is a critical assumption: if the data points are not exchangeable, how can split conformal prediction still achieve marginal coverage? The key point to consider, however, is that the marginal coverage guarantee relies only on finding a good approximation for the $(1-\alpha)$-quantile of the test point's score, $s_n(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-1143: Exchangeability of the data points $(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )$ is certainly sufficient to ensure this (i.e., with the split conformal quantile $\hat{q _n$), but it is not necessary: it is possible to find a good estimate via model-based assumptions, without requiring exchangeability.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1144: We will now see that conformal prediction offers a double-robustness type guarantee: conformal prediction offers coverage guarantees as long as either exchangeability holds (as in the theory we have seen in Chapter~\ref{chapter:conformal-exchangeability ), or if instead we can rely on model-based assumptions.}
elements.sent-2411-1145: We now give an example of such a case: a stationary time-series, where exchangeability can fail drastically due to dependence over time.
elements.sent-2411-1146: We will see that, under model-based assumptions, applying conformalized quantile regression (CQR) method (recall Section~\ref{sec:case_study_2_classification ) can still ensure coverage.}
elements.sent-2411-1147: In particular, if our estimated conditional quantiles $\hat\tau_n(x;\beta)$ are a good approximation to the true $\tau^*(x;\beta)$, we can expect to achieve coverage even without exchangeability.
elements.sent-2411-1149: Consider a time series of data points, \begin{equation}(X_1,Y_1), \, (X_2,Y_2),\, \dots\end{equation} where the response is real-valued, $\cY=\R$.

elements.sent-2411-1184: This chapter begins Part~\ref{part:extensions-conformal of the book.}
elements.sent-2411-1185: The previous part has focused on conformal prediction for batches of exchangeable data points.
elements.sent-2411-1186: In this next part of the book, we present extensions to the conformal prediction procedure that modify it to have a better computational/statistical tradeoff, to handle non-exchangeable data, and to operate in an online setting with streaming data, and more.
elements.sent-2411-1187: In this chapter, we will focus on cross-validation (CV) style approaches to conformal prediction, covering the main methodologies in this area along with their corresponding theoretical guarantees.
elements.sent-2411-1188: Recall that in Part~\ref{part:conformal , we introduced full conformal prediction and split conformal prediction, which can be viewed as lying at two extremes of a computational/statistical tradeoff.}
elements.sent-2411-1189: Split conformal only requires fitting the model once, but may lead to wider intervals due to the statistical cost of data splitting.
elements.sent-2411-1190: In contrast, full conformal may provide narrower intervals as it uses the entire available training set for model fitting, but in principle may require a large number (or even infinitely many) calls to the model fitting algorithm and therefore is very expensive to compute even approximately.
elements.sent-2411-1191: A natural question is therefore whether we can use a strategy that is more statistically efficient than data splitting, without paying the steep computational price of full conformal prediction: namely, can we use cross-validation? Interestingly, the theoretical analysis of CV-type methods proves to be surprisingly different from the analysis of full and split conformal, and this chapter will go through these arguments in detail.
elements.section-2411-028: Preliminaries: split conformal as a tournament
elements.sent-2411-1192: Before presenting the cross-conformal prediction method, it will help to first return to split conformal, and reformulate the construction of that method.
elements.sent-2411-1193: Let $I\subseteq[n]$ be a subset of the indices of the available labeled data, and suppose we will use this subset $\cD_I=((X_i,Y_i):i\in I)$ as our calibration set, after training our model on the remaining data $\cD_{[n]\setminus I = ((X_i,Y_i):i\in[n]\setminus I)$.}
elements.sent-2411-1194: Note that here we depart from the notation of the earlier chapters: in this section, we assume a total of $n$ labeled data points, and that $\cD_n$ must be partitioned to provide both data for model training and for calibration.
elements.sent-2411-1195: For example, for a data point $i\in I$ used for calibration in the split conformal procedure, its score will be given by $s((X_i,Y_i);\cD_{[n]\setminus I )$.}
elements.sent-2411-1196: The split conformal method operates by comparing the score of a hypothesized test point, $s((X_{n+1 ,y);\cD_{[n]\setminus I )$ against the scores for calibration data points, $s((X_i,Y_i);\cD_{[n]\setminus I )$, for all $i\in I$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1197: Concretely, in this setting where we have $|I|$ (rather than $n$) as the number of calibration points, the split conformal interval is defined as \begin{equation}\cC(X_{n+1}) = \left\{y : s((X_{n+1},y);\cD_{[n]\setminus I}) \leq \quantile\left(\left(s((X_i,Y_i);\cD_{[n]\setminus I})\right)_{i\in I} ; (1-\alpha)\left( 1 + 1/|I| \right)\right)\right\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1198: To help us later on when defining cross-conformal, we observe that, by definition of the quantile, this prediction interval can equivalently be defined as \begin{equation}\label{eq:compare_split_to_CC}\cC(X_{n+1}) = \left\{y : \sum_{i\in I} \ind{ s((X_{n+1},y);\cD_{[n]\setminus I}) > s((X_i,Y_i);\cD_{[n]\setminus I})} < (1-\alpha)(|I|+1)\right\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1199: With this reformulation of the prediction interval, we can now see that split conformal can be interpreted as a tournament between teams---that is, between data points $i\in I\cup\{n+1\}$.
elements.sent-2411-1200: Each team's strength is determined by its score, namely, $s((X_i,Y_i);\cD_{[n]\setminus I )$ for each team $i\in I$, and $s((X_{n+1 ,y);\cD_{[n]\setminus I )$ for team $i=n+1$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1201: When team $i$ plays against team $j$, it wins if its score is strictly higher; if the scores are equal, neither team wins.
elements.sent-2411-1202: We can therefore see that, to determine whether a value $y$ is included in the prediction interval $\cC(X_{n+1 )$, we are equivalently asking:.}
elements.sent-2411-1203: Did team $n+1$ win fewer than $(1-\alpha)(|I|+1)$ many games, when playing against all teams $i\in I$?.
elements.sent-2411-1204: This tournament interpretation of conformal will help us to understand the construction of the cross-validation methods that we will study next, as well as the proofs of their corresponding theoretical guarantees.
elements.sent-2411-1205: Next, we will extend this reformulation of split conformal to a cross-validation style approach.
elements.sent-2411-1206: To begin, we partition the available data into $K$ disjoint folds, given by \begin{equation}[n] = I_1 \cup \dots \cup I_K.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1207: That is, for each $k$, \begin{equation}\textnormal{Compare $s((X_{n+1},y);\cD_{[n]\setminus I_k})$ against $\left(s((X_i,Y_i);\cD_{[n]\setminus I_k})\right)_{i\in I_k}$}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1208: To pool these $K$ comparisons together, and construct a single prediction interval, we define \begin{equation}\label{eq:cross-conf}\cC(X_{n+1}) = \left\{y : \sum_{k=1}^K\sum_{i\in I_k} \ind{s((X_{n+1},y);\cD_{[n]\setminus I_k}) > s((X_i,Y_i);\cD_{[n]\setminus I_k})} < (1-\alpha)(n+1)\right\},\end{equation} which is known as the cross-conformal prediction set.
elements.sent-2411-1209: In constructing this set, the innermost sum calculates how many games were won by player $n+1$ within fold $k$, and the outermost sum is over the $K$ folds.
elements.sent-2411-1210: Thus, the left-hand side of the inequality simply counts the total number of games won by player $n+1$.

elements.sent-2411-1285: We can see that, for each $i=1,\dots,2m+1$, we have \begin{equation}\sum_{j=1}^{n+1} A_{ij} = \sum_{j=1}^{2m+1} A_{ij} + \sum_{j=(2m+1)+1}^{n+1} A_{ij} = m + (n+1 - (2m+1)) = n - m = (1-\alpha)(n+1).\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-1286: In this section, we will first step away from the framework of conformal and cross-conformal and consider a more classical approach: the predictive interval constructed via cross-validation (CV), for the problem of predicting a real-valued response (i.e., $\cY=\R$).
elements.sent-2411-1287: Specifically, given a fitted model $\hf:\cX\rightarrow\R$ trained on the full training set $\cD_n=((X_i,Y_i))_{i\in[n]}$, a popular approach for constructing a prediction interval around $\hf(X_{n+1 )$ is to use cross-validation to estimate the margin of error (i.e., the width of the interval).}
elements.sent-2411-1288: CV has similar benefits to cross-conformal prediction when compared to full and split conformal prediction: it allows one to traverse the tradeoff between computational and statistical efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-1289: However, we will see that prediction intervals formed via CV can fail in the distribution-free setting, but a small modification (the CV+ method) resolves the issue.
elements.sent-2411-1290: We will then see how CV+ is actually closely connected to cross-conformal.
elements.sent-2411-1291: Before proceeding, we introduce a new piece of notation.
elements.sent-2411-1292: For the remainder of this chapter, we will write $\cA$ to denote the regression algorithm that is run in order to produce the fitted model $\hf$---for instance, the least-squares algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-1293: That is, $\cA$ is a function that inputs a dataset $\cD$ (a collection of points in $\cX\times\R$), and returns a fitted model---the function $\hf:\cX\rightarrow\R$.
elements.sent-2411-1294: Cross-validation refers to a genre of classical methods for estimating predictive error.
elements.sent-2411-1295: Given a training set of $n$ data points, $\cD_n = ((X_i,Y_i))_{i\in[n]}$, the cross-validation procedure for constructing a prediction interval can be implemented as follows.
elements.sent-2411-1296: Split the training dataset $\cD_n$ into $K$ disjoint folds, via a partition $[n] = I_1\cup\ldots\cup I_K$.
elements.sent-2411-1297: For each fold $k \in [K]$, fit the model $\hf_{-I_k = \cA(\cD_{[n]\setminus I_k )$, where $\cD_{[n]\setminus I_k = ((X_i,Y_i))_{i\in[n]\setminus I_k}$ denotes the training set with $k$th fold $I_k$ held out.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1298: For each data point $i\in[n]$, compute $S_i = |Y_i - \hf_{-I_{k(i) (X_i)|$, where $k(i)$ is the fold of the $i$th data point (i.e., the value $k$ such that $i\in I_k$).}}
elements.sent-2411-1299: Compute the margin of error, $\hat{q _{\rm CV = \quantile\left(S_1,\dots,S_n;1-\alpha\right)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-1300: Refit the model on the full dataset, $\hf=\cA(\cD_n)$, and output the prediction interval \begin{equation}\label{eq:classical_CV}\cC(X_{n+1}) = \hf(X_{n+1}) \pm \hat{q}_{\rm CV}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1301: The reason cross-validation works well in practice is that the residuals $S_i$ are approximately unbiased estimates of the error of the fully trained model $\hf$, because the data point $(X_i,Y_i)$, was not used in training the model $\hf_{-I_{k(i) }$.}
elements.sent-2411-1302: When $K=n$, this algorithm is sometimes called the jackknife, or, leave-one-out cross-validation.
elements.sent-2411-1303: Although the CV algorithm above is well-motivated, it does not have an assumption-free guarantee of coverage.
elements.sent-2411-1304: To see why, consider the following failure case:.
elements.sent-2411-1305: Suppose that our regression algorithm $\cA$ is the following: given a dataset $\cD$, to fit the model $\hf=\cA(\cD)$, if $\cD$ has an even number of data points then we return the constant model $\hf(x)\equiv 0$, while if $\cD$ has an odd number of data points then we instead return $\hf(x)\equiv 1$.
elements.sent-2411-1307: Suppose that $n$ and $n/K$ are both odd numbers.
elements.sent-2411-1308: Then, for each fold $k$, $\hf_{-I_k (x)\equiv 0$, because the dataset $\cD_{[n]\setminus I_k}$ contains an even number of data points.}
elements.sent-2411-1309: Consequently, the $i$th training data point has residual $S_i=0$, for all $i\in[n]$.
elements.sent-2411-1310: On the other hand, $\cD_n$ has an odd number of data points, and so we will have $\hf(X_{n+1 )=1$.}
elements.sent-2411-1311: The $K$-fold CV interval will then be equal to $\cC(X_{n+1 ) =1 \pm 0 = \{1\}$ almost surely, which will have zero probability of coverage under the distribution $P$.}
elements.sent-2411-1312: To avoid such degenerate failure cases, below we will derive a slight modification to the CV algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-1313: This modification (the CV+ method, and its special case, jackknife+) enjoys an assumption-free coverage guarantee.
elements.sent-2411-1314: First, however, we will present an alternative definition for the traditional jackknife and CV prediction intervals, which will help build intuition for their modified versions.
elements.sent-2411-1315: Before proceeding, we pause to give an equivalent definition of the CV prediction interval, which will be useful for extending to the CV+ method below.
elements.sent-2411-1316: The $K$-fold CV prediction interval~\eqref{eq:classical_CV can equivalently be defined as \begin{equation}\cC(X_{n+1}) = \left[\hf(X_{n+1}) - \quantile\left( (S_i)_{i\in[n]} ; 1-\alpha \right),\right. \left. \hf(X_{n+1}) + \quantile\left( (S_i)_{i\in[n]} ; 1-\alpha \right)\right],\end{equation} where, for each $i\in[n]$, the score is given by $S_i = |Y_i - \hf_{-I_{k(i) (X_i)|$, as before.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1317: Rewriting this one more time, we have \begin{equation}\label{eq:classical_CV_reinterpret}\cC(X_{n+1}) = \left[ -\quantile\left( \left( -(\hf(X_{n+1}) - S_i)\right)_{i\in[n]} ; 1-\alpha \right), \right. \left.\quantile\left( \left( \hf(X_{n+1}) + S_i\right)_{i\in[n]} ; 1-\alpha\right)\right].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1318: Why does the left endpoint of this interval use a ``double negative'' type construction---the negative quantile of the negative values---instead of simply taking $\quantile(( \hf(X_{n+1 ) - S_i)_{i\in[n] ; 1-\alpha)$? The reason is that the definition of the quantile, given in Definition~\ref{def:quantile , is not symmetric: in general we may have \begin{equation}\quantile\big((-z_1,\dots,-z_n);\tau\big) \neq -\quantile\big((z_1,\dots,z_n);1-\tau\big).\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1319: Using the ``double negative'' to define the left endpoint in~\eqref{eq:classical_CV_reinterpret makes this interval equivalent to the $K$-fold CV prediction interval.}
elements.sent-2411-1320: We are now ready to define the modified versions of the CV and jackknife prediction intervals.
elements.sent-2411-1321: First, for $K$-fold CV+, we define \begin{equation}\label{eq:CV+}\cC(X_{n+1}) = \left[ -\quantile\left( \left( -( \hf_{-I_{k(i)}}(X_{n+1}) - S_i)\right)_{i\in[n]} ; (1-\alpha)(1+1/n) \right), \right. \left.\quantile\left( \left( \hf_{-I_{k(i)}}(X_{n+1}) + S_i\right)_{i\in[n]} ; (1-\alpha)(1+1/n) \right)\right],\end{equation} where as before we define $S_i = |Y_i - \hf_{-I_{k(i) (X_i)|$, and $k(i)\in[K]$ denotes the fold to which data point $i$ belongs.}}
elements.sent-2411-1322: Comparing to~\eqref{eq:classical_CV , we can see that the difference lies in using the leave-one-fold-out predictions, $\hf_{-I_k (X_{n+1 )$, in place of the prediction $\hf(X_{n+1 )$ obtained by retraining on the full dataset.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1323: The quantile level $1-\alpha$ has also been replaced by $(1-\alpha)(1+1/n)$, which is useful for obtaining finite-sample guarantees, just as for conformal prediction in earlier chapters.
elements.sent-2411-1324: For the special case $K=n$, the jackknife+ prediction interval is defined as \begin{equation}\label{eq:jackknife+}\cC(X_{n+1}) = \left[ -\quantile\left( \left(-( \hf_{-i}(X_{n+1}) - S_i)\right)_{i\in[n]} ; 1 - (1-\alpha)(1+1/n) \right), \right. \left.\quantile\left( \left(\hf_{-i}(X_{n+1}) + S_i\right)_{i\in[n]} ; (1-\alpha)(1+1/n) \right)\right].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1325: Section~\ref{sec:CC_CV below will relate the CV+ and jackknife+ methods to the cross-conformal framework defined earlier in this chapter, and will present distribution-free theoretical guarantees for these methods.}
elements.sent-2411-1326: For the moment, we pause to give some brief intuition about why the CV+ and jackknife+ methods avoid the worst-case scenario that arose for CV and jackknife in Example~\ref{example:jackknife_fails .}
elements.sent-2411-1327: In this degenerate example, the issue arose from the fact that CV (i.e., traditional CV, rather than CV+) uses residuals $S_i = |Y_i - \hf_{-I_{k(i) (X_i)|$ to try to estimate the value of the error on the test point, $|Y_{n+1 - \hf(X_{n+1 )|$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1328: For a fixed training point $i$, these two quantities are not drawn from the same distribution: $S_i$ is an out-of-sample error for a model fitted on $n -n/K$ data points, while the test error $|Y_{n+1 - \hf(X_{n+1 )|$ is an out-of-sample error for a model fitted on $n$ data points.}}
elements.sent-2411-1329: In contrast, for CV+, by using predictions $\hf_{-I_k (X_{n+1 )$ (in place of the original prediction $\hf(X_{n+1 )$), we are implicitly comparing $S_i$ with a test point error $|Y_{n+1 - \hf_{-I_k (X_{n+1 )|$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1330: These two errors are now drawn from the same distribution (and are in fact exchangeable), since both are out-of-sample errors for the same fitted model $\hf_{-I_k}$.
elements.sent-2411-1331: Unlike the CV (or jackknife), this new CV+ (or jackknife+) interval is no longer a symmetric interval around the prediction $\hf(X_{n+1 )$ fitted on the entire training dataset---and indeed in extreme cases it can even be the case that the prediction $\hf(X_{n+1 )$ is not even contained in the CV+ interval $\cC(X_{n+1 )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1332: Therefore, we can't interpret this new construction as giving us an interval around $\hf(X_{n+1 )$.}
elements.sent-2411-1333: However, an alternative interpretation does hold:.
elements.sent-2411-1335: The CV+ interval~\eqref{eq:CV+ (run with $K$ folds of equal size $n/K$) must contain the median prediction \begin{equation}\hf^{\textnormal{med}}(X_{n+1}) = \textnormal{Median}\left(\left(\hf_{-I_k}(X_{n+1})\right)_{k\in [K]}\right).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-1336: In particular for the case $K=n$, the jackknife+ prediction interval contains the median leave-one-out prediction, \begin{equation}\hf^{\textnormal{med}}(X_{n+1}) = \textnormal{Median}\left(\left(\hf_{-i}(X_{n+1})\right)_{i\in [n]}\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1337: We can view this median value as an ensembled prediction, obtained by retraining $\hf$ on different subsets of the training data, and can therefore interpret CV+ as providing an interval around $\hf^{\textnormal{med (X_{n+1 )$ to quantify our uncertainty about this ensembled predictor.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1338: A similar argument shows that the lower bound of the CV+ interval satisfies \begin{equation}\quantile\left( \left( -( \hf_{-I_{k(i)}}(X_{n+1}) - S_i)\right)_{i\in[n]} ; (1-\alpha)(1+1/n) \right) \leq \hat{f}^{\textnormal{med}}(X_{n+1}),\end{equation} and therefore, it must hold that $\hat{f ^{\textnormal{med (X_{n+1 )\in \cC(X_{n+1 )$.}}}}}
elements.section-2411-031: Coverage guarantees for CV+ and jackknife+
elements.sent-2411-1339: On the surface, the CV+ method (with jackknife+ as a special case) appears to be quite different from cross-conformal.
elements.sent-2411-1340: The CV+ method is constructed to form intervals around a predicted value of $Y_{n+1}$ based on computing residuals $S_i$ for the leave-one-out or leave-one-fold-out models, while cross-conformal prediction can return a prediction set that is not an interval, and does not even need to use a score that is based on a regression model.
elements.sent-2411-1341: However, we will now see that CV+ is closely related to the cross-conformal prediction method: the cross-conformal set is always contained in the CV+ interval, if cross-conformal is implemented with the residual score, $s((x,y);\cD) = |y - [\cA(\cD)](x)|$.
elements.sent-2411-1342: In practice, however, the output of the two methods is often identical.
elements.sent-2411-1343: Let $[n] = I_1\cup \dots \cup I_K$ be any partition.
elements.sent-2411-1344: Let $\cC^{\textnormal{CV+ (X_{n+1 )$ be the $K$-fold CV+ interval constructed with some regression algorithm $\cA$, as in~\eqref{eq:CV+ .}}}}

elements.sent-2411-1347: We note that in the special case $K=n$, this result implies that the jackknife+ interval contains the $n$-fold (i.e., leave-one-out) cross-conformal prediction set.
elements.sent-2411-1348: Since, by Proposition~\ref{prop:CC_CV+ , the prediction interval constructed by jackknife+ or CV+ is guaranteed to contain the cross-conformal prediction set, the coverage guarantees in Section~\ref{sec:theory-CV apply immediately to jackknife+ and to CV+.}}
elements.sent-2411-1349: For completeness, these are stated in the following corollary.
elements.sent-2411-1350: Assume $\cA$ is a symmetric algorithm (i.e., $\cA(\cD) = \cA(\cD_\sigma)$ for any dataset $\cD$ and any permutation $\sigma$).
elements.sent-2411-1351: If the data points $(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )$ are exchangeable, then the jackknife+ interval~\eqref{eq:jackknife+ satisfies \begin{equation}\P\left(Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1})\right) \geq 1-2\alpha.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1352: Moreover, for any $K$, if the exchangeability condition~\eqref{eqn:longer_exch_sequence_cross_conf holds, then the CV+ interval~\eqref{eq:CV+ (run with $K$ folds of equal size $n/K$) satisfies \begin{equation}\P\left(Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1})\right) \geq 1-2\alpha - 2(1-\alpha)\cdot\min\left\{\frac{1-1/K}{n/K+1},\frac{1-K/n}{K+1}\right\} \geq 1-2\alpha -2/\sqrt{n}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-1353: Then, equivalently, we have \begin{equation}\sum_{i=1}^n \ind{y > \hf_{-I_{k(i)}} + S_i} = \sum_{k=1}^K \sum_{i\in I_k} \ind{y > \hf_{-I_k} + S_i} \geq (1-\alpha)(n+1).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1354: For any $i\in I_k$, \begin{equation}y > \hf_{-I_k}(X_{n+1}) + S_i \ \Rightarrow \ |y - \hf_{-I_k}(X_{n+1})| > S_i \ \Leftrightarrow \ s((X_{n+1},y);\cD_{[n]\setminus I_k}) > s((X_i,Y_i);\cD_{[n]\setminus I_k}),\end{equation} where the last step holds by definition of the score function, and so \begin{equation}\sum_{k=1}^K \sum_{i\in I_k} \ind{s((X_{n+1},y);\cD_{[n]\setminus I_k}) > s((X_i,Y_i);\cD_{[n]\setminus I_k})} \geq (1-\alpha)(n+1).\end{equation}.

elements.section-2411-032: Training-conditional coverage for cross-validation type methods
elements.sent-2411-1356: In Chapter~\ref{chapter:conditional , for the full and split conformal methods, we considered the question of training-conditional coverage---do the methods offer reliable predictive coverage, conditional on the dataset $\cD_n$? In this section, we will now ask this question for the cross-validation based methods that are the focus of this chapter.}
elements.sent-2411-1357: First, we will see that for $K$-fold cross-conformal, we can establish a training-conditional coverage guarantee as long as the size $n/K$ of each fold is sufficiently large.
elements.sent-2411-1358: At a high level, this result holds because the scores $(S_i)_{i\in I_k}$, where $S_i = s\big((X_i,Y_i);\cD_{[n]\setminus I_k}\big)$, are i.i.d.\ conditional on the data $\cD_{[n]\setminus I_k}$ used for training for the $k$th fold, and therefore concentration results can be applied.
elements.sent-2411-1359: In this sense, the result below can be viewed as an extension of the training-conditional coverage guarantee for split conformal, which was given in Theorem~\ref{thm:training-conditional-split-CP .}
elements.sent-2411-1360: Before stating the result, we first recall a definition from Chapter~\ref{chapter:conditional : we define $\alpha_P(\cD_n) =\P(Y_{n+1 \not\in \cC(X_{n+1 ) \mid \cD_n)$, the probability of coverage failing when we condition on $\cD_n$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1361: As before, the goal of training-conditional coverage is to establish a high-probability upper bound on $\alpha_P(\cD_n)$.
elements.sent-2411-1362: Let $P$ be any distribution on $\cX\times\cY$, and let $s$ be any score function.
elements.sent-2411-1363: Then cross-conformal (with $K$ folds of equal size $n/K$) satisfies the training-conditional coverage guarantee \begin{equation}\P\left(\alpha_P(\cD_n) \leq 2\alpha + \sqrt{\frac{2\log(K/\delta)}{n/K}}\right)\geq 1-\delta,\end{equation} where the probability is taken with respect to the training set $\cD_n=((X_i,Y_i))_{i\in[n]}$ drawn i.i.d.\ from $P$.
elements.sent-2411-1364: Since $K$-fold CV+ is strictly more conservative than $K$-fold cross-conformal (by Proposition~\ref{prop:CC_CV+ ), the same bound holds as well for $K$-fold CV+ implemented with any regression algorithm $\cA$.}
elements.sent-2411-1365: Before turning to the proof, we first briefly comment on the nature of this result.
elements.sent-2411-1366: Note that we have not proved a bound showing that $\alpha_P(\cD_n) \lessapprox \alpha$ with high probability---rather, we instead have $\alpha_P(\cD_n) \lessapprox 2\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-1367: This is because the marginal coverage guarantee for cross-conformal only establishes coverage at level (approximately) $1-2\alpha$, rather than $1-\alpha$ (see Theorems~\ref{thm:CC_smallK and~\ref{thm:CC_bigK ), or in other words, $\E[\alpha_P(\cD_n)]$ is bounded (approximately) by $2\alpha$ rather than by $\alpha$.}}
elements.sent-2411-1368: Therefore, we cannot hope to remove this factor of 2 from the conditional result without further assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-1369: Then, conditional on $\cD_{[n]\setminus I_k}$, we have \begin{equation}(S_i)_{i\in I_k}\iidsim P_k,\end{equation} where as before, for each $k\in[K]$ and each $i\in I_k$, we define $S_i = s((X_i,Y_i);\cD_{[n]\setminus I_k )$.}
elements.sent-2411-1370: A standard concentration result (namely, the Dvoretzky--Kiefer--Wolfowitz inequality) implies that \begin{equation}\frac{1}{n/K}\sum_{i\in I_k}\ind{S_i \geq t} \geq \P_{S\sim P_k}(S \geq t) - \sqrt{\frac{\log(K/\delta)}{2n/K}}\end{equation} holds simultaneously for all $k$ and for all $t\in\R$, with probability at least $1-\delta$.
elements.sent-2411-1371: Next, define a p-value \begin{equation}p^*_k(x,y) = \P_{S\sim P_k}\big(S \geq s((x,y);\cD_{[n]\setminus I_k}) \mid \cD_n\big).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1372: Comparing to the p-value $p_k(x,y)$ defined in the proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:CC_smallK , here we are comparing the score $s((x,y);\cD_{[n]\setminus I_k )$ for the data point $(x,y)$ against the true score distribution, $P_k$, while in the proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:CC_smallK , we were comparing against the empirical distribution of scores obtained from data points in the $k$th fold $I_k$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1373: Note that, for $(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )\sim P$, the value $p^*_k(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )$ is indeed a valid p-value conditional on the training data, i.e., $\P(p^*_k(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )\leq \tau\mid \cD_n)\leq \tau$ for all $\tau\in[0,1]$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1374: This is true since, conditional on $\cD_n$, $s((X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 );\cD_{[n]\setminus I_k )$ is a draw from the (random) distribution $P_k$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1375: Since averaging p-values provides a quantity that is a p-value up to a factor of 2, then, \begin{equation}\P\left(\frac{\sum_{k=1}^K p^*_k(X_{n+1},Y_{n+1})}{K}\leq \tau\ \Bigg\vert\ \cD_n\right)\leq 2\tau\end{equation} holds almost surely for any $\tau\in[0,1]$.
elements.sent-2411-1376: Combining this with the concentration result above, then, with probability at least $1-\delta$, \begin{equation}\P\left(\frac{\sum_{k=1}^K \frac{1}{n/K}\sum_{i\in I_k}\ind{S_i \geq s((X_{n+1},Y_{n+1});\cD_{[n]\setminus I_k})}}{K}\leq \tau - \sqrt{\frac{\log(K/\delta)}{2n/K}} \ \Bigg\vert\ \cD_n\right)\leq 2\tau.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1377: Choosing $\tau = \alpha +\sqrt{\frac{\log(K/\delta) {2n/K }$, and simplifying, we have shown that \begin{equation}\P\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n\ind{S_i \geq s((X_{n+1},Y_{n+1});\cD_{[n]\setminus I_k})}\leq \alpha \ \Bigg\vert\ \cD_n\right)\leq 2\alpha + \sqrt{\frac{2\log(K/\delta)}{n/K}}\end{equation} holds with probability at least $1-\delta$.}}
elements.sent-2411-1378: Finally, it follows directly from the definition of the $K$-fold cross-conformal method that \begin{equation}Y_{n+1}\not\in\cC(X_{n+1}) \ \Rightarrow \ \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n\ind{S_i \geq s((X_{n+1},Y_{n+1});\cD_{[n]\setminus I_k})}\leq \alpha,\end{equation} and therefore, \begin{equation}\alpha_P(\cD_n) = \P\big(Y_{n+1}\not\in\cC(X_{n+1})\mid \cD_n\big) \leq \P\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n\ind{S_i \geq s((X_{n+1},Y_{n+1});\cD_{[n]\setminus I_k})}\leq \alpha \ \Bigg\vert\ \cD_n\right),\end{equation} which completes the proof.
elements.sent-2411-1379: In the proof of the result above, the key idea is that, when the fold $I_k$ has a large size $n/K$, the empirical distribution of scores $(S_i)_{i\in I_k}$ within this fold exhibits concentration.
elements.sent-2411-1380: This is the reason that the term $n/K$ appears in the denominator, in the upper bound.
elements.sent-2411-1381: If instead $n/K$ is small---in particular, with jackknife+ or leave-one-out cross-conformal we have $K=n$---then this bound no longer gives a meaningful result.
elements.sent-2411-1382: The next theorem shows that, for jackknife+, training-conditional coverage can in fact fail to hold if we do not place any assumptions on the regression algorithm $\cA$.
elements.sent-2411-1383: By Proposition~\ref{prop:CC_CV+ , this result therefore applies to leave-one-out cross-conformal as well---that is, without placing assumptions on the score function $s$, we cannot guarantee training-conditional coverage for leave-one-out cross-conformal.}
elements.sent-2411-1384: Let $P$ be any distribution on $\cX\times\cY$ such that the marginal $P_X$ is nonatomic while $P_Y$ has bounded support.
elements.sent-2411-1385: Then there exists a symmetric regression algorithm $\cA$ such that, for jackknife+, the training-conditional noncoverage probability $\alpha_P(\cD_n)$ satisfies \begin{equation}\P\left(\alpha_P(\cD_n) = 1\right)\geq \alpha - \bigo\left(\sqrt{\frac{\log n}{n}}\right),\end{equation} where the probability is taken with respect to the training set $\cD_n=((X_i,Y_i))_{i\in[n]}$ drawn i.i.d.\ from $P$.
elements.sent-2411-1386: The proof of this result is very similar to that of Theorem~\ref{thm:training-conditional-full-CP , which is the analogous result proving that training-conditional coverage can fail for full conformal prediction.}
