elements.section-2411-033: Algorithmic stability and prediction with the jackknife
elements.sent-2411-1397: The results of Corollary~\ref{cor:CV_jack show that CV+ and jackknife+ can offer coverage at level $\geq 1-2\alpha$, but this does not match the target level $1-\alpha$---and in practice, we generally see coverage around level $1-\alpha$ empirically.}
elements.sent-2411-1398: However, Section~\ref{sec:why-factor-of-2 explains why the factor of 2 in the theory cannot be removed.}
elements.sent-2411-1399: Strikingly, we saw in Example~\ref{example:jackknife_fails that CV and jackknife (rather than their modified versions, CV+ and jackknife+) can even reach zero coverage.}
elements.sent-2411-1400: The counterexample works by proposing a model $\hf=\cA(\cD_n)$ that when fitted to a training set $\cD_n$ of size $n$ behaves very differently from the model $\hf_{-i = \cA(\cD_{[n]\setminus \{i\} )$, which was fitted to the same training set with a single data point removed.}}
elements.sent-2411-1401: This can be described as an issue of instability of the training algorithm: a small change to the training set creates a large change in the resulting fitted model.
elements.sent-2411-1402: We will now study algorithmic stability in more detail, developing a formal definition of this property and analyzing how it can improve our theoretical guarantees for the cross-validation-based predictive inference methods.
elements.sent-2411-1403: At a high level, algorithmic stability simply means that small perturbations to the training dataset (e.g., adding or removing a single data point) do not lead to large changes in the fitted model.
elements.sent-2411-1404: However, there are a multitude of different ways to formalize this intuition into a precise definition, and various choices can have extremely different implications for downstream results.
elements.sent-2411-1405: Here we will focus on a version of the definition that is most relevant for analyzing the jackknife.
elements.sent-2411-1406: An algorithm $\cA$ and distribution $P$ satisfy algorithmic stability, with parameters $\epsilon,\delta\geq 0$ at sample size $n$, if for all $i\in[n]$ it holds that \begin{equation}\P\left( \big|\hf(X_{n+1}) - \hf_{-i}(X_{n+1})\big|\leq \epsilon \right) \geq 1- \delta,\end{equation} with respect to data drawn i.i.d.\ from $P$, where $\hf = \cA(((X_j,Y_j))_{j\in[n] )$ and $\hf_{-i = \cA(((X_j,Y_j))_{j\in[n]\setminus\{i\} )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1407: Importantly, this condition requires the change in the prediction to be small when $\hf$ and $\hf_{-i}$ are evaluated on an independent test point $X_{n+1}$---a data point that does not appear in the training dataset for either $\hf$ or $\hf_{-i}$.
elements.sent-2411-1408: In contrast, requiring $|\hf(X_i)-\hf_{-i (X_i)|$ to be small would be a much stronger assumption---it would imply that the prediction at $X_i$ is essentially the same regardless of whether $(X_i,Y_i)$ was used for training, which cannot hold for algorithms $\cA$ that exhibit substantial overfitting.}
elements.sent-2411-1409: A number of common regression algorithms are known to satisfy this type of stability property.
elements.sent-2411-1410: One example is $K$-nearest-neighbors regression, which satisfies Definition~\ref{def:alg_stab_eps_delta with $\epsilon=0$ and $\delta = K/n$ (since the predictions $\hf(X_{n+1 )$ and $\hf_{-i (X_{n+1 )$ are actually equal, unless $X_i$ is one of the $K$ nearest neighbors of the test point).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1411: Other examples include algorithms based on strongly convex optimization problems such as ridge regression and algorithms based on ensembled or bootstrapped methods.
elements.sent-2411-1412: See the bibliography for more details.
elements.sent-2411-1413: We will now see that the algorithmic stability assumption ensures predictive coverage for the jackknife method.
elements.sent-2411-1414: Similar results can also be established for jackknife+, or for the $K$-fold versions of these methods, but we do not consider these extensions here.
elements.sent-2411-1415: Recall the jackknife (i.e., leave-one-out cross-validation) prediction interval, defined as \begin{equation}\cC(X_{n+1}) = \hf(X_{n+1}) \pm \hat{q}_{\textnormal{CV}} ,\end{equation} where the margin of error is given by the quantile of the leave-one-out residuals, \begin{equation}\hat{q}_{\textnormal{CV}} = \quantile((S_1,\dots,S_n);1-\alpha)\textnormal{ for }S_i = |Y_i - \hf_{-i}(X_i)|.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1416: Here $\hf$ is the model trained on the full training set, $\hf = \cA(((X_j,Y_j))_{j\in[n] )$, while for each $i\in[n]$, $\hf_{-i}$ is the leave-one-out model, $\hf_{-i = \cA(((X_j,Y_j))_{j\in[n]\setminus\{i\} )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1417: We will now see that, while it is not possible to prove $1-\alpha$ coverage for the jackknife interval (without adding even more assumptions), algorithmic stability enables us to establish coverage at a level that is nearly the target level $1-\alpha$, for a slightly inflated version of the jackknife interval.
elements.sent-2411-1418: Suppose $(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )$ are drawn i.i.d.\ from some distribution $P$, and assume that $\cA$ satisfies algorithmic stability as in Definition~\ref{def:alg_stab_eps_delta with parameters $\epsilon, \delta\geq 0$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1419: Assume also that $\cA$ is symmetric in the input data.
elements.sent-2411-1420: Then the inflated jackknife prediction interval \begin{equation}\cC(X_{n+1}) = \hf(X_{n+1}) \pm (\hat{q}_{\textnormal{CV}} + \epsilon)\end{equation} satisfies \begin{equation}\P(Y_{n+1}\in \cC(X_{n+1})) \geq 1- \alpha - 2\sqrt{\delta} - \frac{1}{n+1}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1421: Analogous results can also be established for jackknife+, which was shown in Corollary~\ref{cor:CV_jack to satisfy coverage at level $1-2\alpha$; a stability assumption allows us to remove this factor of 2.}
elements.sent-2411-1422: One interpretation of the reason that jackknife can fail in the absence of algorithmic stability is that since $\hf$ and $\hf_{-i}$ might be fitted using completely different procedures, the test error $|Y_{n+1 -\hf(X_{n+1 )|$ and the leave-one-out residuals $S_i = |Y_i - \hf_{-i (X_i)|$ may have very different distributions---and in particular, the test error $|Y_{n+1 -\hf(X_{n+1 )|$ fails to be exchangeable with the $S_i$'s.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1423: The idea of the proof is to instead construct a different vector of scores $\tilde{S _1,\dots,\tilde{S _n$, which are exchangeable with the test error $|Y_{n+1 -\hf(X_{n+1 )|$, and then show that the algorithmic stability assumption is sufficient to ensure $S_i\approx \tilde{S _i$ for most training points $i\in[n]$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1424: Note that for $i=n+1$, we simply have $\hf_{n+1 =\hf$, while for $i\in[n]$, this model is trained on the dataset consisting of the $n-1$ training points when data point $i$ is held out, together with the test point $(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1425: Define also a modified leave-one-out residual \begin{equation}\tilde{S}_i = |Y_i - \tilde{f}_{-i}(X_i)|,\end{equation} for each $i\in[n+1]$.
elements.sent-2411-1426: Since $\cA$ is a symmetric algorithm, by symmetry of the construction we see that $\tilde{S _1,\dots,\tilde{S _{n+1}$ are exchangeable.}}
elements.sent-2411-1427: We therefore have \begin{equation}\P\left(\tilde{S}_{n+1} \leq \quantile(\tilde{S}_1,\dots,\tilde{S}_{n+1};1-\alpha')\right)\geq 1-\alpha',\end{equation} by Fact~\ref{fact:exchangeable-properties (ii), for any $\alpha'\in[0,1]$.}

elements.sent-2411-1429: On the other hand, by definition of the inflated jackknife prediction interval $\cC(X_{n+1 )$, we have \begin{equation}Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1}) \ \Longleftrightarrow \ |Y_{n+1} - \hf(X_{n+1})| \leq \hat{q}_{\textnormal{CV}} + \epsilon.\end{equation}. Therefore, \begin{equation}\P(Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1})) \geq 1-\alpha' - \P\left(\hat{q}_{\textnormal{CV}}+\epsilon < \quantile\left((\tilde{S}_i)_{i\in[n]}; (1-\alpha')(1+1/n)\right) \right).\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-1432: We now need to use the assumption of algorithmic stability: since $\cA$ is stable, we expect to obtain similar predictions $\hf_{-i (X_i)\approx \tilde{f _{-i (X_i)$ (i.e., the fitted model is only slightly altered by including an additional data point $(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )$ in the training process), and so we will have $S_i\approx \tilde{S _i$ for most indices $i$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1433: To formalize this, we calculate \begin{equation}\P(S_{(k)} + \epsilon < \tilde{S}_{(k')}) \leq\P\left(\sum_{i\in[n]} \ind{\tilde{S}_i > S_i + \epsilon} \geq k - k'+1\right) \leq \P\left(\sum_{i\in[n]} \ind{|\tilde{f}_{-i}(X_i) - \hf_{-i}(X_i)|>\epsilon} \geq k - k'+1\right) \leq \frac{\E\left[\sum_{i\in[n]} \ind{|\tilde{f}_{-i}(X_i) - \hf_{-i}(X_i)|>\epsilon}\right]}{k-k'+1}\textnormal{\quad by Markov's inequality} = \frac{\sum_{i\in[n]} \P(|\tilde{f}_{-i}(X_i) - \hf_{-i}(X_i)|>\epsilon)}{k-k'+1} \leq \frac{n\delta}{k-k'+1},\end{equation} where the last step uses the algorithmic stability assumption (Definition~\ref{def:alg_stab_eps_delta ).}
elements.formula-2411-423: \begin{equation}\P(S_{(k)} + \epsilon < \tilde{S}_{(k')}) \leq\P\left(\sum_{i\in[n]} \ind{\tilde{S}_i > S_i + \epsilon} \geq k - k'+1\right) \leq \P\left(\sum_{i\in[n]} \ind{|\tilde{f}_{-i}(X_i) - \hf_{-i}(X_i)|>\epsilon} \geq k - k'+1\right) \leq \frac{\E\left[\sum_{i\in[n]} \ind{|\tilde{f}_{-i}(X_i) - \hf_{-i}(X_i)|>\epsilon}\right]}{k-k'+1}\textnormal{\quad by Markov's inequality} = \frac{\sum_{i\in[n]} \P(|\tilde{f}_{-i}(X_i) - \hf_{-i}(X_i)|>\epsilon)}{k-k'+1} \leq \frac{n\delta}{k-k'+1},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-1434: Finally, by construction, $k-k'+1 \geq (1-\alpha)n - (1-\alpha')(n+1)$, and so combining everything, we obtain \begin{equation}\P(Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1})) \geq 1-\alpha' - \frac{n\delta}{(1-\alpha)n - (1-\alpha')(n+1)}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1436: We have seen above that algorithmic stability leads to important downstream guarantees: in particular, it implies that the jackknife offers guaranteed predictive coverage (as well as a refined result for jackknife+), and provides confidence intervals for the risk of a trained model, without further distributional assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-1437: To make use of this in practice, however, we would need to know that jackknife is being applied to a stable algorithm---that is, if we cannot be sure that $\cA$ is stable, then we do not know whether the jackknife leads to valid predictive inference and/or valid inference on risk.
elements.sent-2411-1438: Earlier, we gave several examples of regression algorithms $\cA$ that are stable by construction---algorithms such as $K$-nearest neighbors, for which we can theoretically establish that the stability property given in Definition~\ref{def:alg_stab_eps_delta must hold, without any need for empirical validation---but these examples are quite limited and may not be practically useful in most modern settings.}
elements.sent-2411-1439: We are therefore motivated to turn to the second option: is it possible to certify that an algorithm $\cA$ is stable by testing it empirically, without needing to analyze $\cA$ theoretically? Interestingly, it turns out that in general this is not possible (when the sample size of the available data is limited).
elements.sent-2411-1440: While formalizing this type of hardness result is beyond the scope of this book (we refer the reader to the bibliography for references on results in this area), the implications for distribution-free inference are important to mention: these hardness results imply that a method such as jackknife, whose validity relies on a stability condition, cannot in general be viewed as an assumption-free method, because aside from special cases (i.e., simple algorithms such as $K$-nearest neighbors), the method's guarantees require untestable assumptions in order to hold.

elements.sent-2411-1449: Algorithmic stability, as introduced in Section~\ref{sec:alg_stab_framework , has been broadly studied in the fields of statistics and learning theory.}

elements.sent-2411-1461: In the previous chapters, our conformal algorithms have treated all data points equally.
elements.sent-2411-1462: Often, however, some data points are more relevant than others.
elements.sent-2411-1463: For example, data collected more recently is generally expected to be more closely related to the test point, and data points closer in feature space to the test point are expected to be more informative about the test point.
elements.sent-2411-1464: This chapter concerns weighted variants of conformal prediction, which allow us to give different data points a different level of influence.
elements.sent-2411-1465: We begin by defining a generic algorithm for weighted conformal prediction in Section~\ref{sec:weighted-algorithm .}
elements.sent-2411-1466: After that, in the remaining sections, we show how to instantiate weighted conformal prediction for various settings:.
elements.sent-2411-1467: In Section~\ref{sec:covariate_and_label_shift , we consider known distribution shifts relating the training data distribution and the test point, such as covariate shifts and label shifts.}
elements.sent-2411-1468: For this setting, we use data-dependent weights to correct for this distribution shift.
elements.sent-2411-1469: In Section~\ref{sec:localized , we present the localized conformal prediction method, which aims to improve conditional coverage by placing higher weights on data points that are close to the test point $X_{n+1}$.}
elements.sent-2411-1470: In Section~\ref{sec:fixed-weights , we consider arbitrary (and unknown) distribution shifts.}
elements.sent-2411-1471: We use fixed weights to prioritize more trusted data points, which leads to coverage closer to $1-\alpha$ when exchangeability is violated.
elements.sent-2411-1472: In Section~\ref{sec:general-outlook-permutations , we consider a more general and unified view of conformal prediction, providing a framework for deriving conformal algorithms that generalize beyond the assumptions of exchangeability or of a symmetric score function.}
elements.sent-2411-1473: These setups all use variants of the same algorithm, weighted conformal prediction, but with different choices of the weights, to achieve different effects.
elements.section-2411-034: Weighted quantiles and the weighted conformal algorithm
elements.sent-2411-1474: In this section, we will define a weighted version of the split and full conformal prediction algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-1475: While conformal prediction computes a threshold $\hat{q}$ (or $\hat{q ^y$) as a quantile of the data points' scores, thus implicitly placing equal weight on each data point, we will now introduce the ability to place different weights on the various data points.}
elements.sent-2411-1476: We will see in later sections that this modification will allow us to extend conformal prediction for a variety of different aims---we will analyze the behavior of this procedure and its extensions to handle distribution shifts and approximate conditional coverage.
elements.sent-2411-1477: For now, though, we restrict our attention to the mechanics of the procedure: how can we incorporate weights into the conformal prediction procedure? Taking a step back, recall that in unweighted full conformal prediction, we include the value $y$ in the prediction set whenever $S^y_{n+1 \leq \hat{q ^y$, where $\hat{q ^y$ is calculated as a quantile of the data points' scores.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1478: In weighted full conformal prediction, there is just one difference: we take a weighted $1-\alpha$ quantile, \begin{equation}\hat{q}^y_w = \quantile\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n+1} w_i \delta_{S_i^y} ; 1-\alpha\right),\end{equation} for an $(n+1)$-length vector $w$ of nonnegative, unit-sum weights.
elements.sent-2411-1479: This is the quantile of a weighted empirical distribution---that is, we are calculating the $(1-\alpha)$-quantile of the distribution that places weight $w_i$ on the value $S_i^y$, for each $i$.
elements.sent-2411-1480: When $w_i = \frac{1 {n+1}$ for all $i$, this calculation simply reduces to $\quantile(S_1^y,\dots,S_{n+1 ^y;1-\alpha)$, the quantile of the list of scores.}}
elements.sent-2411-1481: In general, though, if the weights are not constant across the data points, then points with higher weights will have more of an influence in the chosen quantile.
elements.sent-2411-1482: See Figure~\ref{fig:weighted-quantile for an illustration of the weighted quantile.}
elements.figure-2411-014: \includegraphics[width=0.7\linewidth]{figures/diagrams/weighted-quantile.pdf} \caption{An illustration of the weighted conformal quantile. The horizontal axis shows the scores. The vertical axis shows the CDF of $\cD_{n+1}^y$ on the left, and its weighted CDF on the right. The quantile level $1-\alpha$ is shown as a light gray line on both plots. The standard choice of quantile $\hat{q}^y$ is shown on the left, and its weighted version $\hat{q}_w^y$ is shown on the right.} \label{fig:weighted-quantile}
elements.sent-2411-1483: Depending on the situation, the weights can be fixed a-priori, or set as a function of the data---we will discuss the choice of weights later on, as we encounter several different scenarios over the course of this chapter.
elements.sent-2411-1484: We now state the weighted full conformal algorithm for a generic (and potentially data-dependent) vector of weights.
elements.sent-2411-1485: Input training data $(X_1, Y_1), ..., (X_n, Y_n)$, test point $X_{n+1}$, weights $w_1,\dots,w_{n+1}\geq 0$ with $\sum_i w_i =1$, target coverage level $1-\alpha$, conformal score function $s$.
elements.sent-2411-1486: Compute $S_i^y = s((X_i, Y_i); \cD^y_{n+1 )$ for all $i\in[n]$, and $S_{n+1 ^y = s((X_{n+1 , y);\cD^y_{n+1 )$.}}}}

elements.section-2411-035: Conformal prediction under covariate and label shifts
elements.sent-2411-1497: In this section, we see how the weighted conformal algorithm can be applied to handle covariate shift and label shift---that is, differences between the distribution of the training data and the test data, so that $(X_1,Y_1), \ldots, (X_n,Y_n)$ are drawn from a covariate- or label-shifted distribution as compared to $(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-1498: The strategy for constructing weights in these two settings will be a common one: setting the weights $w_i$ proportionally to the likelihood ratio relating these two distributions.
elements.sent-2411-1499: In the final subsection, we connect back to the underlying principles of conformal prediction, introducing a new notion of exchangeability---weighted exchangeability---that is fundamental to this data-dependent reweighting strategy.
elements.sent-2411-1500: We will begin by using weighted conformal prediction to address covariate shift: the setting where the distribution of $X$ differs between the training and test data, but the distribution of $Y \mid X$ remains the same.
elements.sent-2411-1501: For example, covariate shift is a common model for capturing demographic shifts, where certain demographics might be more common in the training population than in the test population.
elements.sent-2411-1502: We assume that $(X_1,Y_1), \ldots, (X_n,Y_n) \iidsim P_X \times P_{Y \mid X}$ and that $(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )\sim Q_X \times P_{Y \mid X}$ independently, for a different covariate distribution $Q_X$.}}
elements.sent-2411-1503: Here, the notation $P_X \times P_{Y \mid X}$ means that $X$ follows distribution $P_X$ and then, conditionally on $X$, $Y \mid X$ follows distribution $P_{Y \mid X}$ (and, $Q_X \times P_{Y \mid X}$ is defined similarly).
elements.sent-2411-1504: The key insight is that if we know the likelihood ratio $\frac{\mathsf{d Q_X {\mathsf{d P_X : \cX \to \R_{\geq 0}$---the ratio of likelihoods for the test versus train data distributions---we can construct weights that compensate for the distribution shift to give us coverage.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1505: Formally, $\frac{\mathsf{d Q_X {\mathsf{d P_X}$ denotes the Radon--Nikodym derivative relating the distribution $Q_X$ of the test data point's feature vector, to the distribution $P_X$ of the training features.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1506: For example, if $P_X$ and $Q_X$ both have densities, then $\frac{\mathsf{d Q_X {\mathsf{d P_X}$ can simply be taken to be the ratio of the densities.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1507: The strategy for setting the weights is straightforward: we set $w_i \propto \frac{\mathsf{d Q_X {\mathsf{d P_X (X_i)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1508: To ensure that the weights sum to $1$, we set \begin{equation}\label{eq:weights-covariate-shift} w_i = \frac{\frac{\mathsf{d}Q_X}{\mathsf{d}P_X}(X_i)}{\sum\limits_{j=1}^{n+1}\frac{\mathsf{d}Q_X}{\mathsf{d}P_X}(X_j)}\end{equation} for all $i \in [n+1]$.
elements.sent-2411-1509: We will now see that this choice of weights gives us a coverage guarantee.
elements.sent-2411-1510: Let $(X_1,Y_1), \ldots, (X_n,Y_n) \iidsim P_X \times P_{Y \mid X}$ and $(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )\sim Q_X \times P_{Y \mid X}$ independently.}}
elements.sent-2411-1511: Furthermore, assume that $Q_X$ is absolutely continuous with respect to $P_X$, and so $\frac{\mathsf{d Q_X {\mathsf{d P_X (x) < \infty$ for all $x \in \cX$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1512: Fix any symmetric score function $s$, and define the prediction set \begin{equation}\cC(X_{n+1}) = \left\{y : S^y_{n+1} \leq \hat{q}^y_w\right\}\textnormal{ where }\hat{q}^y_w = \quantile\left(\sum_{i=1}^n w_i \delta_{S_i^y};1-\alpha\right),\end{equation} where the weights $w_i$ are defined as in~\eqref{eq:weights-covariate-shift .. Then, \begin{equation}\P(Y_{n+1} \in \cC(X_{n+1})) \geq 1-\alpha.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-1513: In other words, coverage for the covariate shift setting is obtained by running Algorithm~\ref{alg:wtd-full-cp with weights $w_i$ defined as in~\eqref{eq:weights-covariate-shift .}}
elements.sent-2411-1514: We will defer the proof of this guarantee to Section~\ref{sec:train-test-shifts-weighted-lr .}
elements.figure-2411-015: \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{figures/diagrams/covariate-shift-example.pdf} \caption{Visualization of a covariate shift in two groups on the left and the resulting unweighted and weighted quantiles on the right. The unweighted quantile sits close to the yellow curve, representing the CDF of the score when $X=1$, since $P$ places high weight on $X=1$. The weighted quantile sits close to the gray curve, representing the CDF of the score when $X=2$, since $Q$ places high weight on $X=2$.} \label{fig:covariate-shift}
elements.sent-2411-1515: Why does reweighting give coverage? First, it is worth considering why unweighted conformal prediction does not provide coverage under covariate shift.
elements.sent-2411-1516: Imagine that we have just two groups, i.e., $\cX = \{1, 2\}$, and that the scores in the subpopulation where $X=1$ tend to be much smaller than in the subpopulation where $X=2$.
elements.sent-2411-1517: If $\P_{P_X (X=1) > \P_{Q_X (X=1)$, then $X=1$ is over-represented in the training data (relative to the test point's distribution), and the conformal quantile will be biased downwards; see Figure~\ref{fig:covariate-shift for a visualization.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1518: The fundamental problem is that the training and test scores $S_1,\dots,S_n,S_{n+1}$ are no longer exchangeable---the test point's score $S_{n+1}$ no longer behaves like a draw from the empirical distribution of $S_1, \ldots, S_{n+1}$, and so calculating an unweighted quantile cannot be expected to achieve the right coverage level.
elements.sent-2411-1519: Weighted conformal prediction works because the distribution of $S_{n+1}$ can be explicitly represented as a weighted empirical distribution instead.
elements.sent-2411-1520: In other words, conditionally on the values of the data points, the test point behaves like a draw from the reweighted empirical distribution: \begin{equation}(X_{n+1},Y_{n+1}) \; \Big\vert \; \widehat{P}_{n+1} \sim \sum\limits_{i=1}^{n+1} w_i \delta_{(X_i,Y_i)},\end{equation} where $\widehat{P _{n+1 = \frac{1 {n+1}\sum_{i=1 ^{n+1}\delta_{(X_i,Y_i)}$ is the empirical distribution of the dataset $\cD_{n+1}$, and consequently, an analogous statement holds for the scores: \begin{equation}S_{n+1} \; \Big\vert \; \widehat{P}_{n+1} \sim \sum\limits_{i=1}^{n+1} w_i \delta_{S_i}.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1521: This is a property called weighted exchangeability.
elements.sent-2411-1522: It gives us an explicit distribution for $(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )$, and thus $S_{n+1}$, allowing us to prove the coverage guarantee for weighted conformal prediction.}}
elements.sent-2411-1523: We go into more detail on this argument in Section~\ref{sec:general-outlook-permutations .}
elements.sent-2411-1524: In label shift, the distribution of $Y$ changes and the distribution of $X \mid Y$ is held fixed.
elements.sent-2411-1525: Label shift is common in many classification problems.
elements.sent-2411-1526: Our strategies for handling covariate shift and label shift are almost identical, with one key difference: the weights are now a function of $Y$ as opposed to $X$.
elements.sent-2411-1527: The setup is entirely analogous to the covariate shift procedure.
elements.sent-2411-1528: We assume that $(X_1,Y_1), \ldots, (X_n,Y_n) \iidsim P_{X \mid Y \times P_Y$ and that $(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )\sim P_{X \mid Y \times Q_Y$ independently, for a different label distribution $Q_Y$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1529: Here, the notation $P_{X \mid Y \times P_Y$ means that $Y$ follows distribution $P_Y$ and then $X \mid Y$ follows distribution $P_{X \mid Y}$, and similarly, for $P_{X \mid Y \times Q_Y$.}}
elements.sent-2411-1530: Then, we define the likelihood ratio $\frac{\mathsf{d Q_Y {\mathsf{d P_Y : \cY \to \R$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1531: To run Algorithm~\ref{alg:wtd-full-cp , we need to make a slight modification in the calculation of the weighted quantile, since the weights are now a function of $Y$ (which, for the test point, is unknown), rather than of $X$ (where the test point value $X_{n+1}$ is known).}

elements.subsection-2411-037: General shifts between the training distribution and test distribution
elements.sent-2411-1539: When we apply the weighted conformal algorithm to the covariate shift setting and to the label shift setting, the intuition for the two is essentially the same.
elements.sent-2411-1540: Since the training and test scores $S_1,\dots,S_n,S_{n+1}$ are not exchangeable, we cannot think of the test score $S_{n+1}$ as a uniform draw from the empirical distribution of the training and test scores---but instead, the distribution of the test score $S_{n+1}$ can be described as a reweighted empirical distribution.
elements.sent-2411-1541: In fact, the same argument works for any distribution shift between the train and test sets with a known likelihood ratio, with covariate shift and label shift as two special cases.
elements.sent-2411-1542: In this section, we will develop the method and theory for the general case.
elements.sent-2411-1543: For the general setting, let $(X_1,Y_1), \ldots, (X_n,Y_n) \iidsim P$ and that $(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )\sim Q$ independently, for a different distribution $Q$, where we assume that $Q$ is absolutely continuous with respect to $P$ so that the likelihood ratio (i.e., the Radon--Nikodym derivative) $\frac{\mathsf{d Q {\mathsf{d P : \cX\times\cY \to \R$ is well-defined.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1544: Paralleling the label shift section, we will set the weights as \begin{equation}\label{eq:weights-train-test-shift} w_i^y = \frac{\frac{\mathsf{d}Q}{\mathsf{d}P}(X_i, Y_i)}{\sum\limits_{j=1}^{n}\frac{\mathsf{d}Q}{\mathsf{d}P}(X_j, Y_j) + \frac{\mathsf{d}Q}{\mathsf{d}P}(X_{n+1}, y)} \text{ for } i \in [n] \quad \text{ and } \quad w_{n+1}^y = \frac{\frac{\mathsf{d}Q}{\mathsf{d}P}(X_{n+1}, y)}{\sum\limits_{j=1}^{n}\frac{\mathsf{d}Q}{\mathsf{d}P}(X_j, Y_j) + \frac{\mathsf{d}Q}{\mathsf{d}P}(X_{n+1}, y)}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-1545: As a side note, because these weights are self-normalized, we only need to know $\frac{\mathsf{d Q {\mathsf{d P}$ up to a constant of proportionality---that is, if $\frac{\mathsf{d Q {\mathsf{d P (x,y) \propto w(x,y)$ for some function $w:\cX\times\cY\to\R$, it suffices to know the function $w$, without calculating its normalizing constant.}}}}}}}

Then, \begin{equation}\P(Y_{n+1} \in \cC(X_{n+1})) \geq 1-\alpha.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-1549: Our proof of this result will generalize the ideas developed in Section~\ref{sec:conformal-conditioning-bag , where we proved the coverage guarantee for unweighted full conformal prediction (in a setting without distribution shift) by considering the empirical distribution $\widehat{P _{n+1 = \frac{1 {n+1}\sum_{i=1 ^{n+1}\delta_{(X_i,Y_i)}$ of the training and test data.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1550: The argument in Section~\ref{sec:conformal-conditioning-bag showed that, in the setting of exchangeability, the conditional distribution of the test point $(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )$, if we condition on $\widehat{P _{n+1}$, is equal to the empirical distribution $\widehat{P _{n+1}$ itself.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1551: This is no longer true in the distribution shift setting---if $P\neq Q$, then the test point is not equally likely to be equal to each one of the points in the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-1552: The following proposition shows that the conditional distribution is instead given by a reweighted version of the empirical distribution.
elements.sent-2411-1553: Let $Z_1, \ldots, Z_n \iidsim P$ and $Z_{n+1}\sim Q$ independently, for some distributions $P,Q$ on $\cZ$.
elements.sent-2411-1554: Furthermore, assume that $Q$ is absolutely continuous with respect to $P$, and so $\frac{\mathsf{d Q {\mathsf{d P (z) < \infty$ for all $z \in \cZ$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1555: Let \begin{equation}\widehat{P}_{n+1} = \frac{1}{n+1}\sum_{i=1}^{n+1}\delta_{Z_i}\end{equation} denote the empirical distribution of the $n+1$ data points.
elements.sent-2411-1556: Then the conditional distribution of $Z_{n+1}$, given the empirical distribution $\widehat{P _{n+1}$, is given by \begin{equation}Z_{n+1} \mid \widehat{P}_{n+1} \sim \sum\limits_{i=1}^{n+1} w_i\cdot \delta_{Z_i},\end{equation} where \begin{equation}w_i = \frac{\frac{dQ}{dP}(Z_i) }{\sum_{j=1}^{n+1} \frac{dQ}{dP}(Z_j) }, \ i\in[n+1].\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-1566: Using the conditional distribution of $(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )$ as derived in Proposition~\ref{prop:weighted-exchangeability , then, we have \begin{equation}\label{eqn:weighted_quantile_eqn_for_citing_ref}\P\left(s(Z_{n+1};\widehat{P}_{n+1})\leq \quantile\left(\sum_{i=1}^n w_i \delta_{s(Z_i;\widehat{P}_{n+1})};1-\alpha\right) \,\middle|\, \widehat{P}_{n+1}\right) = \sum_{j=1}^{n+1} w_j \cdot \ind{s(Z_j;\widehat{P}_{n+1})\leq \quantile\left(\sum_{i=1}^n w_i \delta_{s(Z_i;\widehat{P}_{n+1})};1-\alpha\right)}.\end{equation}.}}}

elements.subsection-2411-038: Comparing distribution shift and conditional coverage
elements.sent-2411-1579: Robustness to distribution shift is closely related to conditional coverage.
elements.sent-2411-1580: In fact, requiring test-conditional coverage (i.e., coverage conditional on the test feature $X_{n+1}$) is strictly stronger than requiring coverage relative to a covariate shift: test-conditional coverage implies coverage with respect to any covariate shifts (i.e., for any $Q_X$).
elements.sent-2411-1581: Similarly, label-conditional coverage implies coverage with respect to any label shift (i.e., for any $Q_Y$).
elements.sent-2411-1582: However, conditional coverage guarantees are harder to obtain.
elements.sent-2411-1583: For example, if label-conditional coverage holds (that is, $\P(Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1 )\mid Y_{n+1 =y)\geq 1-\alpha$ for each possible label $y$) then it implies coverage for any label-shifted distribution---but these prediction sets will be very large unless we have a large number of observations in each category (i.e., $\sum_{i\in[n] \ind{Y_i=y}$ is large, for each $y\in\cY$).}}}
elements.sent-2411-1584: In contrast, the method studied in Proposition~\ref{prop:label-shift gives a marginal guarantee relative to a particular label-shifted distribution $P_{X \mid Y \times Q_Y$ and could therefore produce more informative prediction sets.}}
elements.sent-2411-1585: We next turn to a different use case of weighting: the goal of improving conditional coverage.
elements.sent-2411-1586: That is, suppose we wish to give confidence sets that approximately give conditional coverage for a test point $X_{n+1}$, as we have discussed in earlier chapters---in particular, in Chapter~\ref{chapter:conditional we established that it is impossible in general to ensure test-conditional coverage, but as in Chapter~\ref{chapter:model-based , certain score functions (such as the CQR score) lead to approximate conditional coverage under additional assumptions.}}
elements.sent-2411-1587: In this section, we pursue a complementary strategy based on weighting.
elements.sent-2411-1588: To see why weighting may be useful, notice that conformal prediction chooses the cutoff $\hat{q}$ (or $\hat{q ^y)$ by looking at the conformal score for all training points---even data points that are far away from the test point $X_{n+1}$ are nonetheless included when choosing the cutoff $\hat{q}$.}
elements.sent-2411-1589: This naturally can lead to a failure of test-conditional coverage, if the distribution of scores is very different across different regions of $\cX$.
elements.sent-2411-1590: If we instead wish to improve test-conditional coverage, we might choose to give more weight to data points that lie near to the test point $X_{n+1}$.
elements.sent-2411-1591: This is the key idea behind localized conformal prediction.
elements.sent-2411-1592: To make this concrete, suppose we have a function $H : \mathcal X \times \mathcal X \to \mathbb{R _{\ge 0}$ that is capturing the distance between two feature vectors---we will refer to $H$ as the localization kernel.}
elements.sent-2411-1593: For example, when $\mathcal X = \mathbb{R ^d$, we could take $H(x, x') = \exp\{-\lVert x - x' \rVert / 2h^2\}$, where $h>0$ is a bandwidth parameter.}
elements.sent-2411-1594: An initial idea is to run Algorithm~\ref{alg:wtd-full-cp with the weight $w_i$ on data point $(X_i,Y_i)$ chosen to be proportional to $H(X_i,X_{n+1 )$: that is, we define \begin{equation}\label{eqn:baseLCP}\cC(X_{n+1}) = \left\{y\in\cY : S_{n+1}^y\leq \quantile\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n+1}w_i \delta_{S_i^y};1-\alpha\right)\right\} \textnormal{ where }w_i = \frac{H(X_i,X_{n+1})}{\sum_{j=1}^{n+1}H(X_j,X_{n+1})}\end{equation} That is, when running conformal prediction, we weight data points according to their distance from the test point.}}
elements.sent-2411-1595: This initial approach is intuitively appealing and in practice would likely improve test-conditional coverage, but interestingly, in theory even the marginal coverage guarantee no longer holds for this weighted algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-1597: To endow the localized conformal approach with marginal coverage guarantees, it can be modified with a recalibration step, as stated next.
elements.sent-2411-1598: Input training data $(X_1, Y_1), ..., (X_n, Y_n)$, test point $X_{n+1}$, localization kernel $H$, target coverage level $1-\alpha$, conformal score function $s$.
elements.sent-2411-1599: Compute $w_{i,j = H(X_j, X_i) / \sum_{j'=1 ^{n+1 H(X_{j' , X_i)$ for each $i,j\in[n+1]$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1602: When running Algorithm~\ref{alg:lcp , therefore, the prediction set $\cC(X_{n+1 )$ contains all values $y$ for which $\tilde{S _{n+1 ^y\leq \tilde{q ^y$, or equivalently, \begin{equation}\sum_{i=1}^{n+1} w_i \ind{S^y_i < S_{n+1}^y} \leq \tilde{q}^y,\end{equation} while the set defined in~\eqref{eqn:baseLCP instead contains $y$ whenever \begin{equation}S_{n+1}^y \leq \quantile\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n+1}w_i \delta_{S_i^y};1-\alpha\right) \Longleftrightarrow \sum_{i=1}^{n+1} w_i\ind{S_i^y < S_{n+1}^y} < 1-\alpha.\end{equation}.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1603: In other words, the localized conformal prediction method defined in Algorithm~\ref{alg:lcp is exactly the same as the intuitive approach outlined in~\eqref{eqn:baseLCP , except with a data-dependent quantile threshold $\tilde{q ^y$ replacing the usual threshold $1-\alpha$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1604: In other words, the localized conformal prediction method can essentially be interpreted as running the intuitive weighted conformal method suggested in~\eqref{eqn:baseLCP , but with a modified value of $\alpha$ (which varies with $y$).}

elements.sent-2411-1609: Consequently, by Theorem~\ref{thm:full-conformal , marginal coverage must hold for localized conformal prediction as long as we assume that the data is exchangeable.}

elements.sent-2411-1630: Next, we focus on conformal prediction with fixed weights---ones that don't depend on $X$ or $Y$---in order to be more robust to deviations from exchangeability.
elements.sent-2411-1631: For example, in a time series, we may want to give recent data higher weights relative to data from long ago, since we expect that the distribution of recent points is closer to that of the test point.
elements.sent-2411-1632: In this section, we will, a-priori, assign each of our data points a weight, $w_i \geq 0$ for all $i \in [n+1]$, with larger weights indicating greater relevance.
elements.sent-2411-1633: When we run Algorithm~\ref{alg:wtd-full-cp with this non-data-dependent vector of weights, we call it fixed-weight conformal prediction.}
elements.sent-2411-1634: Incorporating weights allows us to achieve a coverage guarantee for exchangeable datasets and bound the loss of coverage for datasets that violate the exchangeability assumption.
elements.sent-2411-1635: The point is that if choose the weights well, the weighted procedure will be more robust to certain violations of exchangeability.
elements.sent-2411-1637: Throughout this section, we will use the notation $Z_i = (X_i,Y_i)$ for the data points.
elements.sent-2411-1638: Let weights $w_1,\dots,w_{n+1}\geq 0$ be fixed, with $\sum_i w_i = 1$ and $w_{n+1}\geq w_i$ for all $i$.
elements.sent-2411-1639: Let $Z_1,\dots,Z_{n+1}$ be random variables with any joint distribution.
elements.sent-2411-1640: Fix any symmetric score function $s$, and define the prediction set \begin{equation}\cC(X_{n+1}) = \left\{y : S^y_{n+1} \leq \hat{q}^y_w\right\}\textnormal{ where }\hat{q}^y_w = \quantile\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n+1} w_i \delta_{S_i^y};1-\alpha\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1641: Then, \begin{equation}\P(Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1})) \geq 1 - \alpha - \sum_{i=1}^n w_i \cdot {\rm d}_{\rm TV}\left((Z_1,\dots,Z_{n+1}), (Z_1,\dots,Z_{i-1},Z_{n+1},Z_{i+1},\dots,Z_n,Z_i)\right),\end{equation} where ${\rm d _{\rm TV}$ denotes the total variation distance between distributions.}
elements.sent-2411-1642: This guarantee gives a bound on the loss of coverage for conformal prediction run on a general dataset, regardless of whether it satisfies the exchangeability assumption.
elements.sent-2411-1643: The bound depends on two quantities: the TV distance of the swapped versions of the dataset (which we can view as a measure of the degree of non-exchangeability), and the weights.
elements.sent-2411-1644: When data points $Z_i$ that are differently distributed from the test point $Z_{n+1}$ are given high weight, this can hurt the coverage.
elements.sent-2411-1645: On the other hand, if they have a low weight, then coverage is (approximately) preserved.
elements.sent-2411-1646: Two important implications of the theorem are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-1647: If the dataset is exchangeable, the fixed-weight procedure has coverage at least $1-\alpha$ (since the total variation distance appearing in the bound is equal to zero in that case).
elements.sent-2411-1648: If the dataset is not exchangeable, the loss of coverage can be mitigated by choosing the weights strategically---e.g., placing higher weights on more recently gathered data points.
elements.sent-2411-1649: Recall from Chapter~\ref{chapter:conformal-exchangeability that we write $S_i = s((X_i,Y_i);\cD_{n+1 )$ to denote the score of data point $Z_i=(X_i,Y_i)$ when the algorithm is trained on the entire dataset $\cD_{n+1 = (Z_1,\dots,Z_{n+1 )$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1650: We will write $S=(S_1,\dots,S_{n+1 )$ to denote this vector of scores, and will write \begin{equation}S^i = (S_1,\dots,S_{i-1},S_{n+1},S_{i+1},\dots,S_n,S_i)\end{equation} to denote the vector $S$ with $i$th and $(n+1)$st entries swapped, for each $i\in[n]$.}
elements.sent-2411-1651: Our first step is to observe that the total variation distance between the vectors $S$ and $S^i$, is bounded by the total variation distance on the data.
elements.sent-2411-1652: Specifically, define a function $h: (\cX\times\cY)^{n+1}\to\R^{n+1}$ as \begin{equation}h(z_1,\dots,z_{n+1}) = \left( s(z_1;(z_1,\dots,z_{n+1})), \dots, s(z_{n+1} ; (z_1,\dots,z_{n+1})\right),\end{equation} i.e., the function that computes a score for each $z_i$ (relative to the dataset comprised of $z_1,\dots,z_{n+1}$).
elements.sent-2411-1653: Then by definition we have $S = h(Z_1,\dots,Z_{n+1 )$.}

elements.sent-2411-1657: Our last task is to simplify the remaining expected value.
elements.sent-2411-1658: Below we will prove that the following claim holds deterministically: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:claim_for_S_vs_Si}S_i \leq \quantile\left(\sum_{j=1}^{n+1} w_j \cdot \delta_{S_j};1-\alpha\right)\Longrightarrow (S^i)_{n+1} \leq \quantile\left(\sum_{j=1}^{n+1} w_j \cdot \delta_{(S^i)_j};1-\alpha\right) .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1659: Assuming that this holds, we then have \begin{equation}\P(Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1})) \geq\E\left[ \sum_{i=1}^{n+1} w_i\cdot \ind{S_i \leq \quantile\left(\sum_{j=1}^{n+1} w_j \cdot \delta_{S_j};1-\alpha\right)}\right] - \sum_{i=1}^n w_i \cdot {\rm d}_{\rm TV}(S,S^i) \geq 1 - \alpha- \sum_{i=1}^n w_i \cdot {\rm d}_{\rm TV}(S,S^i),\end{equation} where the last step follows since, by definition of the weighted quantile, it must hold that \begin{equation}\sum_{i=1}^{n+1} w_i\cdot \ind{S_i \leq \quantile\left(\sum_{j=1}^{n+1} w_j \cdot \delta_{S_j};1-\alpha\right)}\geq 1-\alpha,\end{equation} which we can view as a weighted version of Fact~\ref{fact:conversion-quantiles-cdfs (iii).}
elements.formula-2411-473: \begin{equation}\P(Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1})) \geq\E\left[ \sum_{i=1}^{n+1} w_i\cdot \ind{S_i \leq \quantile\left(\sum_{j=1}^{n+1} w_j \cdot \delta_{S_j};1-\alpha\right)}\right] - \sum_{i=1}^n w_i \cdot {\rm d}_{\rm TV}(S,S^i) \geq 1 - \alpha- \sum_{i=1}^n w_i \cdot {\rm d}_{\rm TV}(S,S^i),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-1660: To complete the proof, we now need to verify~\eqref{eqn:claim_for_S_vs_Si .}
elements.sent-2411-1661: For $i=n+1$ the claim holds trivially (since $S=S^{n+1}$), so we restrict our attention to the case $i\in[n]$.
elements.sent-2411-1662: We will need the following result, which state without proof---we can view this result as a generalized version of the Replacement Lemma (recall Lemma~\ref{lem:n+1-to-n-reduction ):.}
elements.sent-2411-1663: Let $P_0,P_1$ be any distributions on $\R$, and let \begin{equation}P = (1 - \epsilon)\cdot P_0 + \epsilon \cdot P_1\end{equation} be their mixture, for some $\epsilon\in [0,1]$.
elements.sent-2411-1664: Then for any $x\in\R$, and any $\tau\in[0,1]$, \begin{equation}x \leq \quantile(P;\tau) \Longrightarrow x \leq \quantile\left( (1-\epsilon)\cdot P_0 + \epsilon\cdot \delta_x ; \tau\right) .\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-1667: By construction, we can verify that \begin{equation}(1-\epsilon)P_0 + \epsilon P_1 = \sum_{j=1}^{n+1} w_j \cdot \delta_{S_j}\end{equation} and similarly \begin{equation}(1-\epsilon)P_0 + \epsilon \delta_{S_i} = \sum_{j\in[n],j\neq i} w_j \cdot \delta_{S_j} + w_{n+1}\cdot\delta_{S_i} + w_i\cdot\delta_{S_{n+1}}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-1669: It is worth noting that this proof does not directly use the TV distance between the swapped datasets, but rather, only uses the TV distance between swapped score vectors, as in~\eqref{eqn:dtv_scores_or_data .}

elements.section-2411-038: A general outlook through weighted permutations
elements.sent-2411-1671: As a rejoinder, let us zoom out and reinterpret conformal prediction through weighted distributions over permutations.
elements.sent-2411-1672: With this lens, we will see that it is possible to generalize conformal prediction to work with any joint distribution of the data.
elements.sent-2411-1673: In particular, we now give an extension that applies even when the joint distribution and test point are not exchangeable, and the algorithm is not symmetric.
elements.sent-2411-1674: The fundamental idea is that if we know the probability of observing every ordering of the data points conditionally on their values, this is enough to run conformal prediction.
elements.sent-2411-1675: To make this more concrete, we will again write $Z_i =(X_i,Y_i)$, and define $f:(\cX\times\cY)^{n+1}\to\R$ to be the joint density of $(Z_1,\dots,Z_{n+1 )$, with respect to some base measure on $\cX\times\cY$ (in particular, if the data has a continuous joint distribution, then $f$ is simply the joint density in the usual sense, while if the data is discrete, then $f$ is the joint probability mass function).}
elements.sent-2411-1676: If we assume exchangeability, then $f$ must be a symmetric function---but in this section, we are working in a general setting where $f$ may be arbitrary.
elements.sent-2411-1677: Now recall that $\widehat{P _{n+1}$ denotes the empirical distribution of the $n+1$ data points---informally, observing $\widehat{P _{n+1}$ means that we have observed the collection of data points $Z_1,\dots,Z_{n+1}$, but not their order, and in particular we do not know which one of these $n+1$ data points is equal to the test point, $Z_{n+1}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-1678: Then we can verify that the conditional distribution of the test point $Z_{n+1}$, given the empirical distribution $\widehat{P _{n+1}$, is equal to \begin{equation}\label{eqn:empirical_distribution_general_perm} Z_{n+1} \mid \widehat{P}_{n+1} \sim \frac{1}{\sum\limits_{\sigma \in \cS_{n+1}} f(Z_{\sigma(1)}, \ldots, Z_{\sigma(n+1)}) }\sum\limits_{\sigma \in \cS_{n+1}} f(Z_{\sigma(1)}, \ldots, Z_{\sigma(n+1)}) \cdot \delta_{Z_{\sigma(n+1)}}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-1679: That is, once we fix the values of the data points, then we can explicitly describe the distribution of the test point in terms of their ordering, if we have knowledge of the joint density $f$.
elements.sent-2411-1680: This is just a generalized version of weighted exchangeability, as in Proposition~\ref{prop:weighted-exchangeability .}
elements.sent-2411-1681: With this conditional distribution in hand, we can now construct prediction sets using a generalized form of weighted conformal prediction.
elements.sent-2411-1682: First, we need some notation: let $Z^y_1,\dots,Z^y_{n+1}$ index the dataset comprised of the training points and the hypothesized test point $(X_{n+1 ,y)$, i.e., \begin{equation}Z^y_i = Z_i, \ i\in[n], \quad Z^y_{n+1} = (X_{n+1},y),\end{equation} so that the dataset $\cD^y_{n+1}$ is equal to $(Z^y_1,\dots,Z^y_{n+1 )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-1683: Then for every $\sigma \in \cS_{n+1}$, given a score function $s$, we define \begin{equation}\label{eq:weights-general-permutation} w_\sigma^y = \frac{f\left(Z^y_{\sigma(1)}, \ldots, Z^y_{\sigma(n+1)}\right)}{\sum\limits_{\sigma' \in \cS_{n+1}}f\left(Z^y_{\sigma'(1)}, \ldots, Z^y_{\sigma'(n+1)}\right)}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1684: An important point is that we often do not need complete knowledge of $f$ in order to compute these weights: for instance, if the data points are i.i.d.\ from an unknown distribution, we immediately have $w_\sigma^y\equiv \frac{1 {n+1}$.}
elements.sent-2411-1685: Note that here the weight depends on the entire permutation $\sigma$, which includes the index $\sigma(n+1)$ assigned to the test point position but also the ordering of the training data points as well.
elements.sent-2411-1686: In contrast, for covariate shift and label shift (studied in Section~\ref{sec:covariate_and_label_shift ), the weights $w^y_i$ depend only on a single index $i$, corresponding to the question of which data value is placed into the test point position.}
elements.sent-2411-1687: With these weights defined, we now present a coverage guarantee for this general formulation.
elements.sent-2411-1688: Let $(Z_1, \ldots, Z_n, Z_{n+1 ) \sim P$ for an arbitrary joint distribution $P$ on $(\cX\times\cY)^{n+1}$, and let $f$ be the joint density of this distribution with respect to any base measure on $\cX\times\cY$.}
elements.sent-2411-1689: Fix any score function $s$ (not necessarily symmetric), and define the prediction set \begin{equation}\cC(X_{n+1}) = \left\{y : s((X_{n+1},y);\cD_{n+1}) \leq \hat{q}^y_w\right\}\textnormal{ where }\hat{q}^y_w = \quantile\left(\sum_{\sigma \in S_{n+1}} w_\sigma^y \delta_{s\left(Z_{\sigma(n+1)}^y ; (\cD_{n+1}^y)_\sigma\right)} ; 1-\alpha\right),\end{equation} where the weights $w_\sigma^y$ are defined as in~\eqref{eq:weights-general-permutation , and where $(\cD_{n+1 ^y)_\sigma$ denotes the permuted dataset, $(Z^y_{\sigma(1) ,\dots,Z^y_{\sigma(n+1) )$.. Then, \begin{equation}\P(Y_{n+1} \in \cC(X_{n+1})) \geq 1-\alpha.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1690: This result can be proved as a consequence of the calculation~\eqref{eqn:empirical_distribution_general_perm above.}
elements.sent-2411-1691: The consequence of this theorem is that if we are able to compute the weights in~\eqref{eq:weights-general-permutation , then we can carry out conformal prediction, even though we may have nonexchangeable data and/or a nonsymmetric algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-1692: However, this general version of the algorithm is not practically implementable without further structure, for both statistical reasons (we are assuming prior knowledge of the joint density $f$, so that the weights in~\eqref{eq:weights-general-permutation can be computed) and computational reasons (since computing the weights, and the quantile $\hat{q ^y_w$, involves performing calculations over $(n+1)!$ many permutations $\sigma$).}}
elements.sent-2411-1693: Nonetheless, it points toward an underlying structure that facilitates conformal prediction.
elements.sent-2411-1694: In particular, many our preceding results can be viewed as special cases of this general formulation, where the statistical and computational challenges are simplified due to the structure of the specific setting.
elements.sent-2411-1695: For example, in the case of covariate shift (Section~\ref{sec:covariate-shift ), we have assumed that $Z_1,\dots,Z_n$ are i.i.d.\ from some distribution $P$, and that the test distribution is a known covariate shift of $P$.}
elements.sent-2411-1696: In this case, the weights $w_\sigma^y$ depend only on the known covariate shift but not the whole distribution $f$, so they can in principle be computed without full knowledge of $f$.
elements.sent-2411-1697: Moreover, they can be computed efficiently---the weights $w_\sigma^y$ in~\eqref{eq:weights-general-permutation depend on $\sigma$ only through $\sigma(n+1)$.}
elements.sent-2411-1698: That is, for any $\sigma\in\cS_{n+1}$ with $\sigma(n+1)=i$, we have $w^y_\sigma = w_i$, where the weight $w_i$ is defined for the covariate shift setting in~\eqref{eq:weights-covariate-shift .}
elements.sent-2411-1699: Thus, covariate shift is a tractable special case of the general view.
elements.sent-2411-1700: In summary, this abstract viewpoint unifies existing cases of the conformal algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-1701: In particular, exchangeable conformal prediction, and conformal prediction with covariate shift, label shift, or general train-test split, are all special cases.
elements.sent-2411-1702: This unified framework can also be viewed as a roadmap for developing extensions to additional settings as well: it suggests that we can aim to identify more complex settings (e.g., if the data exhibits dependence over time) where the structure of the problem enables computation of the weights $w^y_\sigma$ and the weighted quantiles $\hat{q ^y_w$, in order to be able to apply conformal methodology.}
elements.sent-2411-1703: In this chapter, we focus on weighted versions of the full and split conformal algorithms; relatedly, \citet{prinster2022jaws develop a weighted version of jackknife+.}
elements.sent-2411-1704: In the setting of a distribution shift between the training and test data (discussed in Section~\ref{sec:covariate_and_label_shift ), the weighted conformal method for covariate shift and for label shift was studied by \citet{tibshirani2019conformal and by \citet{podkopaev2021distribution , respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1705: These results have been applied to a range of statistical problems and settings, including causal inference (specifically, estimation of individualized treatment effects) \citep{lei2020conformal,jin2023sensitivity , censored data and survival analysis \citep{candes2021conformalized,gui2024conformalized , and adaptive learning \citep{fannjiang2022conformal .}}}
elements.sent-2411-1706: Results guaranteeing approximate coverage, in the setting where the distribution shift is only known approximately when we define the weights in the WCP algorithm, appear in many works in the literature, e.g., \citet{lei2020conformal,yang2022doubly,jin2023sensitivity,yin2021conformal,gui2024conformalized .}
elements.sent-2411-1707: A different approach is taken by \citet{cauchois2024robust , where they establish robustness of (unweighted) conformal prediction to bounded distribution shifts.}
elements.sent-2411-1708: As a technical note, the step~\eqref{eqn:weighted_quantile_eqn_for_citing_ref in our proofs (which we can view as a weighted version of Fact~\ref{fact:conversion-quantiles-cdfs (ii)) is due to \cite{harrison2012conservative .}}}
elements.sent-2411-1709: Section~\ref{sec:localized presents the localized conformal prediction method, which was developed by~\citet{guan2023localized , and the randomly-localized conformal prediction method, due to~\citet{hore2023conformal .}}}
elements.sent-2411-1710: In the case where $s$ is a pretrained score function, \citet{guan2023localized provides an efficient implementation of localized conformal prediction (i.e., for the conformalization procedure to determine the thresholds $\tilde{q ^y$ in Algorithm~\ref{alg:lcp ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-1711: Both of these works also establish approximate test-conditional coverage guarantees for (randomly-)localized conformal prediction, namely, coverage when the test point is drawn from a small neighborhood.
elements.sent-2411-1712: The results of Proposition~\ref{prop:LCP_is_fullCP , establishing that localized conformal prediction can be viewed as an instance of full conformal prediction, appears in \citet{hore2023conformal .}}
elements.sent-2411-1713: The fixed-weight conformal prediction approach described in Section~\ref{sec:fixed-weights is due to \citet{barber2022conformal .}}
elements.sent-2411-1714: In this book, we have presented a special case of that result, by focusing on the setting of a symmetric score function $s$; the work of \citet{barber2022conformal also extends to the case of non-symmetric $s$, as well.}
elements.sent-2411-1715: Finally, in Section~\ref{sec:general-outlook-permutations , we considered a more general viewpoint that unifies some of the results presented earlier in the chapter.}
elements.sent-2411-1716: Our exposition in this section follows ideas developed by \citet{prinster2024conformal , which explains the viewpoint of conformal prediction through weighted permutations; our results in this section extend further to allow nonsymmetric algorithms, drawing from the work of \citet{tibshirani2019conformal,barber2022conformal .}}
elements.sent-2411-1717: A related viewpoint is developed by \cite{dobriban2023symmpi , which extends the idea of distributional invariance under permutations to the more general setting of invariance under an arbitrary group action.}
elements.sent-2411-1718: So far, we have been discussing conformal prediction in the batch setting: that is, after obtaining a training dataset $\cD_n$ comprising $n$ data points, we then train conformal prediction on this dataset to provide predictive inference on future test points.
elements.sent-2411-1719: On the other hand, there are many applications where we observe data points online (i.e., sequentially), and conformal prediction can be extended to this setting.
elements.sent-2411-1720: More precisely, imagine at time $t$, we have observed data points, $(X_1, Y_1), \ldots, (X_{t-1 , Y_{t-1 )$, and the covariate $X_t$ for the next data point in the series, and need to construct a prediction set $\cC_t(X_t)$ for $Y_t$.}}
elements.sent-2411-1721: Then at time $t+1$, we add the newly observed data point $(X_t, Y_t)$ into our dataset, and the process repeats.
elements.sent-2411-1722: In this new setting, unique problems arise relating to constructing multiple prediction sets, while reusing data, all under potentially shifting distributions.
elements.sent-2411-1723: This chapter describes some solutions to these problems.
elements.section-2411-039: Online conformal prediction with exchangeable data
elements.sent-2411-1724: We first consider the case where we have a sequence of data points $(X_1, Y_1), (X_2, Y_2), \dots, (X_T, Y_T)$ that are exchangeable.
elements.sent-2411-1725: At each time step $t$, we have seen $(X_1, Y_1), \dots, (X_{t-1 , Y_{t-1 )$ and the next feature vector $X_t$, and we would like to construct a prediction set $\cC_t(X_t)$ for the as-yet-unseen response $Y_t$ with correct coverage: \begin{equation}\label{eq:online_timet_cvg} \P(Y_t \in \cC_t(X_t)) \ge 1-\alpha.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-1726: To this end, suppose we generate prediction sets at each step with full conformal prediction in the usual way.
elements.sent-2411-1727: That is, $\cC_t(X_t)$ is the output of Algorithm~\ref{alg:full-cp on training data $(X_1, Y_1), \dots, (X_{t-1 , Y_{t-1 )$ with test point $X_t$, implemented with some choice of the score function $s$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1728: Interestingly, we will now see that this algorithm has desirable properties beyond this basic marginal coverage result.
elements.sent-2411-1729: We will use the notation \begin{equation}\label{eq:err-def} \err_t = \begin{cases} 1 \text{ if } Y_t \notin \cC_t(X_t) 0 \text{ if } Y_t \in \cC_t(X_t) \end{cases}\end{equation} to denote the event that we make a miscoverage at time step $t$.
elements.sent-2411-1730: Our marginal coverage property in~\eqref{eq:online_timet_cvg then equivalent to the statement that $\E[\err_t] \le \alpha$ for all $t$.}
elements.sent-2411-1731: This by itself is not enough to imply that the average coverage of this procedure for a large sequence of data points (i.e., averaging $\err_t$ over a long range of times $t$) would concentrate around $1-\alpha$; since each data point $t$ is reused in constructing $\cC_{t'}$ for every $t'>t$, the coverage events would appear to be dependent in a complex way.
elements.sent-2411-1732: A surprising and deep result about conformal prediction is that the events $\err_t$ are in fact independent across different time steps.
elements.sent-2411-1733: Then the miscoverage events $\err_t$ as defined above are mutually independent.
elements.sent-2411-1734: Here $\cD_t$ denotes the dataset comprised of the first $t$ data points, $\cD_t=((X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_t,Y_t))$, for each $t\in[T]$.
elements.sent-2411-1735: This is a consequence of the slightly more general result that the conformal p-values at each step are independent.
elements.sent-2411-1736: To be explicit, the conformal p-value at time step $t$ is defined as \begin{equation}\label{eq:online-conformal-pvalue} p_t = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{t} \ind{s\left((X_i, Y_i); \cD_t \right) \geq s\left((X_{t}, Y_t); \cD_t \right))}}{t}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-1739: For any permutation $\sigma\in\cS_t$, define the corresponding permutation $\tilde\sigma\in\cS_T$ as \begin{equation}\tilde\sigma(i) =  \sigma(i), i \leq t, i, i>t.\end{equation} By exchangeability of the data, it holds that \begin{equation}(X_1, Y_1),\dots, (X_t, Y_t), (X_{t+1}, Y_{t+1}), \dots,(X_T, Y_T) \eqd (X_{\sigma(1)}, Y_{\sigma(1)}),\dots, (X_{\sigma(t)}, Y_{\sigma(t)}), (X_{t+1}, Y_{t+1}), \dots,(X_T, Y_T)\end{equation} for any $\sigma\in\cS_t$.

elements.sent-2411-1765: We next consider the ability to test the exchangeability hypothesis online---an important task when monitoring the online deployment of learning algorithms to identify the presence of distribution shift, especially those harmful shifts that affect the errors of an algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-1766: In this section, we show how to test this hypothesis using the conformal p-values from~\eqref{eq:online-conformal-pvalue .}
elements.sent-2411-1767: More concretely, given a stream of data $(X_1,Y_1),(X_2,Y_2),\dots$ (with finite or infinite length), we would like to detect any failure of exchangeability in this data stream---for instance, due to a sudden changepoint.
elements.sent-2411-1768: The main message is that the conformal p-values can be combined in such a way that, under exchangeability, the resulting statistic is a supermartingale.
elements.sent-2411-1769: Any supermartingale will generally take only small values, so large values of this statistic constitute evidence against the null hypothesis of exchangeability.
elements.sent-2411-1770: In this section, we show how to define such supermartingales and how to use them to test the hypothesis of exchangeability online.
elements.sent-2411-1771: Essentially, a supermartingale is a sequence of random variables whose average is getting no larger over time.
elements.sent-2411-1772: For the problem of testing the hypothesis of exchangeability, we need to define a sequence $M_t$ which is large if the hypothesis is violated, but is a supermartingale if the hypothesis holds.

elements.figure-2411-016: \includegraphics[width=0.85\textwidth]{figures/diagrams/ville-test.pdf} \caption{The online test for exchangeability. We apply the online test for exchangeability to two example draws from the same supermartingale. On the left-hand side is a draw for which the test does not reject the null hypothesis of exchangeability, because the realized value of the supermartingale never goes above $1/\alpha$. On the right, the hypothesis of exchangeability is rejected, since the realized value of the supermartingale goes above $1/\alpha$ at some point in its history. elements.sent-2411-1808: The previous sections have dealt with online conformal prediction for exchangeable sequences.

elements.sent-2411-1810: Because we do not want to make any distributional assumptions in this setting, the best one can generally hope for is a form of long-run coverage, i.e., that a large fraction of the prediction sets $\cC_t(X_t)$ contain $Y_t$.
elements.sent-2411-1811: A recently developed line of work has leveraged conformal prediction to achieve guarantees of the form \begin{equation}\left| \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^T \err_t - \alpha \right|\rightarrow 0,\end{equation} meaning that, on average, the miscoverage rate is essentially equal to $\alpha$---without any assumption of exchangeability or, indeed, even assuming that the data is randomly generated at all.
elements.sent-2411-1812: How is this setup related to conformal prediction? The prediction sets $\cC_t$ studied in this section will be of the form \begin{equation}\label{eqn:Ct_st_qt} \cC_t(X_t) = \{ y : s_t(X_t, y) \leq q_t \},\end{equation} where the subscript $t$ indicates a dependence on all previous time steps $1, 2, \ldots, t-1$.
elements.sent-2411-1813: That is, the score function $s_t$, and the threshold $q_t$, may be designed in a way that depends on the previous data.
elements.sent-2411-1814: Conformal prediction uses exactly this set construction---specifically, by taking $q_t = \quantile(s_t(X_1,Y_1),\dots,s_t(X_{t-1 ,Y_{t-1 );(1-\alpha)(1+1/(t-1)))$, if we define $s_t(x,y)=s(x,y)$ then we recover split conformal prediction (for a pretrained score function $s$), while defining $s_t(x,y) = s\big((x,y);((X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_{t-1 ,Y_{t-1 ),(x,y))\big)$ recovers the full conformal prediction set (for an arbitrary conformal score function $s$).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1815: In the adversarial sequence setting, where the data may no longer be exchangeable (e.g., if the distribution changes over time), we will deviate from this specific construction of $s_t$ and $q_t$: we will allow the score functions $s_t$ to be chosen arbitrarily, and the thresholds $q_t$ will then be defined in an online way.
elements.sent-2411-1816: Specifically, after initializing at any value $q_1\in[0,B]$, for each $t\geq 2$ the threshold $q_t$ will be defined as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:quantile-tracking} q_{t+1} = q_t + \eta_t(\err_t - \alpha),\end{equation} with $\err_t$ defined as in~\eqref{eq:err-def .}
elements.sent-2411-1817: This quantile tracking algorithm does something very simple: when we fail to cover (i.e., $\err_t = 1$), it increases the conformal quantile in the next time step, to be slightly more conservative.
elements.sent-2411-1818: Alternatively, when we cover (i.e., $\err_t=0$), we decrease the next quantile, to be slightly less conservative.
elements.sent-2411-1819: This construction leads to the following guarantee on average coverage.
elements.sent-2411-1820: Consider an arbitrary sequence of data points $(X_1, Y_1), (X_2, Y_2), \ldots$, and sequence of score functions $s_1, s_2, \ldots$ satisfying $s_t(x,y) \in [0, B]$ for all $t$ and $(x,y)$.
elements.sent-2411-1821: Furthermore, let the sequence of step sizes $\eta_t$ be any nonincreasing positive sequence.
elements.sent-2411-1822: Then, for all $T\geq 1$, it holds that \begin{equation}\frac{1}{T}\sum\limits_{t=1}^T \err_t \in \left[ \alpha \pm \frac{B + \eta_1}{\eta_T T}\right].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1823: In other words, for a constant step size, our historical error concentrates to $\alpha$ at a fast rate of $\bigo(1/T)$.
elements.sent-2411-1824: We emphasize that this result holds deterministically, for any sequence of data points and any choice of score functions $s_t$.
elements.sent-2411-1825: In particular, we have not assumed exchangeability: by adjusting the threshold $q_t$ based on the method's coverage over past data points, the procedure is able to maintain approximately the desired coverage level over time, even under distribution shift or other non-exchangeable settings.

elements.figure-2411-017: \includegraphics[width=0.7\textwidth]{figures/diagrams/qt-coverage-plot.pdf} \caption{The long-run coverage gap. The yellow line displays the long-run coverage gap $\frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^T \err_t - \alpha$ over time $T$. The procedure is initialized with $q_1 = 0$ and run with a fixed step size $\eta_t = \eta$. By~\eqref{eq:qT_equals_sum}, the coverage gap at time $T$ is equal to $\frac{q_{T+1}}{\eta T}$. The thick gray lines at $\frac{B + \eta}{\eta T}$ define a deterministic envelope for the long-term coverage gap; the proof says that the yellow line can never leave the envelope. The thin gray envelope defines the region where $q_{T+1} > B$ (top) or $q_{T+1} < 0$ (bottom). One way to understand the proof of coverage is that, once the yellow line leaves the thin gray envelope, it is forced to return---because the next prediction set $\cC_{T+1}$ will be $\cY$ (if $q_{T+1}>B$) or $\varnothing$ (if $q_{T+1}<0$), forcing the miscoverage to move back towards the target value $\alpha$ in the next step. } \label{fig:quantile-tracker-coverage-plot}

elements.sent-2411-1844: When tracking a highly varying score sequence, such as one generated by a dynamical system, it can make sense to think of the quantile tracker as a control algorithm and pick a non-decaying $\eta_t = \eta$ for some constant $\eta$, or even one that adapts to the score sequence.
elements.sent-2411-1845: However, this can lead to quantiles $q_t$ that fluctuate wildly, yielding many infinite-size sets and empty sets.

elements.sent-2411-1847: This choice of $\eta_t$ allows the quantile to adapt to distribution drift (since Theorem~\ref{thm:quantile-tracking guarantees average error $\lesssim T^{-(1/2-\epsilon)}$ at each time $T$), while stabilizing the quantile and even converging when possible.}

elements.sent-2411-1868: In this chapter, we return to the core conformal prediction framework of Chapter~\ref{chapter:conformal-exchangeability , taking a closer look at some interesting details of the algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-1869: We discuss the role of randomization, addressing how to use conformal prediction with randomized algorithms such as stochastic gradient descent.
elements.sent-2411-1870: We next turn to computational results for full conformal prediction, explaining cases where the set can be computed exactly and the correct way to employ discretization to maintain coverage.
elements.sent-2411-1871: Lastly, we turn to the universality of conformal prediction---any method with distribution-free validity that is permutation invariant is equivalent to a conformal method.
elements.sent-2411-1872: Each section in this chapter stands alone, but together they supply many new insights about conformal prediction.
elements.sent-2411-1873: Thus far, we have presented full conformal prediction as a deterministic algorithm---the output $\cC(X_{n+1 )$ is a deterministic function of the training data $\big((X_i,Y_i)\big)_{i\in[n]}$ and test point $X_{n+1}$.}
elements.sent-2411-1874: In practice, however, we may want to allow for the score $s$ to be determined with a randomized algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-1875: For example, for a residual score of the form $s((x,y);\cD)=|y-\hf(x;\cD)|$, in some settings we may want to fit the function $\hf(\cdot;\cD)$ with a randomized regression algorithm, such as stochastic gradient descent.
elements.sent-2411-1876: Section~\ref{sec:cp-random-1 will extend our framework to allow for the possibility of pairing conformal prediction with a randomized algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-1877: Randomization can also enter into the conformal prediction framework in a different form.
elements.sent-2411-1878: Specifically, we recall from Chapter~\ref{chapter:conformal-exchangeability that the (full or split) conformal prediction intervals are guaranteed to have at least $1-\alpha$ level coverage; as explored in Theorem~\ref{thm:upper-bound , overcoverage can arise from the possibility of ties among scores and/or from the fact that $(1-\alpha)(n+1)$ is not an integer.}}
elements.sent-2411-1879: Introducing randomization into the construction of the conformal prediction set to break ties at random removes this overcoverage, resulting in exactly $1-\alpha$ coverage, as we will see in Section~\ref{sec:cp-random-2 .}
elements.subsection-2411-041: Allowing for randomization in the score function
elements.sent-2411-1880: We first consider the case where our score functions may contain auxiliary randomness, such as a model fit using stochastic gradient descent.
elements.sent-2411-1881: In this case, the requirement of a symmetric score function, as in Definition~\ref{def:symmetric_score needs to be modified.}
elements.sent-2411-1882: To be more concrete, let $\xi\in[0,1]$ denote a stochastic noise term that we use for randomization---we will refer to this as the random seed.
elements.sent-2411-1883: The score function is now randomized: given a data point $(x,y)$, a dataset $\cD$, and a random seed value $\xi$, we can now compute a score \begin{equation}s((x,y);\cD,\xi).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1884: Returning to our example of stochastic gradient descent, we might write $\hf(\cdot;\cD,\xi)$ to denote the fitted model when stochastic gradient descent is run on dataset $\cD$ with random seed $\xi$---in practice, this means that $\xi$ determines the order in which we cycle through points in the dataset $\cD$, when taking gradient descent steps for our optimization problem.
elements.sent-2411-1885: Then we can define a score such as $s((x,y);\cD,\xi) = |y - \hf(x;\cD,\xi)|$.
elements.sent-2411-1886: What does it mean for the score to be symmetric in this randomized definition? Here, Definition~\ref{def:symmetric_score is replaced by a requirement that the score function is symmetric in a distributional sense:.}
elements.sent-2411-1887: Note that the property in~\eqref{eq:symmetric_randomized_score is weaker than requiring that $s\big((x'_m,y'_m);\cD,\xi\big) = s\big((x'_m,y'_m);\cD_\sigma,\xi\big)$ for all test points $(x_m',y_m')$, and this distinction is important in practice.}
elements.sent-2411-1888: With stochastic gradient descent, for instance, for a fixed random seed $\xi$ the fitted function will in general be changed if we permute the data---if the random seed $\xi$ determines that the first gradient descent step is taken with respect to the $i$th data point for some particular $i$, this data point is equal to $(X_i,Y_i)$ in the dataset $\cD$, but is instead equal to $(X_{\sigma(i) ,Y_{\sigma(i) )$ for the permuted dataset $\cD_\sigma$.}}
elements.sent-2411-1889: Thus, we would generally have different fitted models, $\hf(\cdot;\cD,\xi)\neq \hf(\cdot;\cD_\sigma,\xi)$, leading to different score functions, $s(\cdot;\cD,\xi)\neq s(\cdot;\cD_\sigma,\xi)$---but equality in distribution (as in~\eqref{eq:symmetric_randomized_score ) does hold.}
elements.sent-2411-1890: Now we will see how this new symmetry assumption allows us to again establish exchangeability of the vector of scores $S_1,\dots,S_{n+1}$.
elements.sent-2411-1891: For $\cD_{n+1 = ((X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 ))$, we have \begin{equation}(S_{\sigma(1)},\dots,S_{\sigma(n+1)}) = (s((X_{\sigma(i)},Y_{\sigma(i)});\cD,\xi))_{i\in[n+1]} \eqd (s((X_{\sigma(i)},Y_{\sigma(i)});\cD_\sigma,\xi))_{i\in[n+1]},\end{equation} where the equality in distribution in the last step holds by applying Definition~\ref{def:symmetric_randomized_score with the data points $(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )$ playing the role of $(x_1',y_1'),\dots,(x_M',y_M')$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1892: But we also have \begin{equation}(s((X_{\sigma(i)},Y_{\sigma(i)});\cD_\sigma,\xi))_{i\in[n+1]}\eqd (s((X_i,Y_i);\cD,\xi))_{i\in[n+1]}=(S_1,\dots,S_{n+1}),\end{equation} where this next equality in distribution holds by exchangeability of the data points $(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-1893: We have thus verified that the scores $S_1,\dots,S_{n+1}$ are exchangeable even in this randomized setting; this is analogous to the key step~\eqref{eqn:scores_exchangeable_n+1 in the proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:full-conformal (in Section~\ref{sec:full_conformal_first_proof ), for the nonrandomized case, and the remainder of the proof proceeds exactly as before.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1894: Thus, if we run conformal prediction with a symmetric randomized score function, we are again guaranteed to have marginal coverage at level $\geq 1-\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-1895: From this point on, throughout the book, for clarity of the presentation we will generally only consider deterministic score functions, but in general, all results can easily be generalized to encompass randomized score functions.
elements.sent-2411-1896: Next, we will consider a different type of randomization in the construction of the conformal prediction set for the purpose of achieving prediction sets with exact coverage, rather than conservative coverage.
elements.sent-2411-1897: While in Section~\ref{sec:cp-random-1 we considered randomization in the construction of the scores, here we instead consider randomization in comparing the values of the scores, which will help handle ties.}
elements.sent-2411-1898: Recall from Proposition~\ref{prop:conformal-via-pvalues that the conformal prediction set can equivalently be defined as \begin{equation}\cC(X_{n+1}) = \{y\in\cY : p^y>\alpha\},\end{equation} where the conformal p-value is given by \begin{equation}p^y = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n+1} \ind{S^y_i \geq S^y_{n+1}}}{n+1}\end{equation} (as in Definition~\ref{def:conformal-pvalue ).}}
elements.sent-2411-1899: If the scores are distinct, then exchangeability ensures that the p-value $p^{Y_{n+1 }$, corresponding to the true test response value $y=Y_{n+1}$, is uniformly distributed on the grid $\{\frac{1 {n+1 ,\dots,\frac{n {n+1 ,1\}$, reflecting the fact that the scores $S_1,\dots,S_{n+1}$ are equally likely to be ranked in any order (see Theorem~\ref{thm:online-indep-pvals ).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1900: This means that the coverage event, $p^{Y_{n+1 >\alpha$, holds with probability exactly $\frac{k {n+1}$, where $k = \lceil(1-\alpha)(n+1)\rceil$---which leads to overcoverage if $(1-\alpha)(n+1)$ is not an integer.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1901: Moreover, this calculation has not accounted for ties between the scores---another potential source of overcoverage.
elements.sent-2411-1902: To avoid this issue, we can define a randomized version of the conformal prediction set for which coverage will hold at exactly level $1-\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-1903: This modification is sometimes referred to as smoothing---the distribution of $p^{Y_{n+1 }$, which is supported on the grid $\{\frac{1 {n+1 ,\dots,\frac{n {n+1 ,1\}$, is smoothed to achieve a $\textnormal{Unif [0,1]$ distribution.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1904: The smoothed version of the prediction set is easiest to define using the p-value-based construction.
elements.sent-2411-1905: Given a hypothesized value $y\in\cY$ for the test response, we redefine the conformal p-value \begin{equation}\label{eqn:cp-p-value-randomized}p^y(\xi) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n+1} \ind{S^y_i > S^y_{n+1}} + \xi \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n+1} \ind{S^y_i = S^y_{n+1}}}{n+1},\end{equation} which now depends additionally on a randomization term $\xi\sim \textnormal{Unif [0,1]\in[0,1]$.}
elements.sent-2411-1906: The conformal prediction set is then given by \begin{equation}\label{eqn:define-cp-smoothed}\cC(X_{n+1}) = \left\{y\in\cY : p^y(\xi) > \alpha\right\},\end{equation} where the dependence on the random $\xi$ is implicit in the notation.
elements.sent-2411-1907: Operationally, it is common to use a single draw of $\xi$ that is shared across all values $y\in\cY$; that is, p-values $p^y(\xi)$ and $p^{y' (\xi)$ are computed using the same value of $\xi$.}
elements.sent-2411-1908: Suppose that $(X_1,Y_1),...,(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )$ are exchangeable and that $s$ is a symmetric score function.}}

elements.sent-2411-1910: Then the smoothed conformal prediction set $\cC$ defined in~\eqref{eqn:define-cp-smoothed has marginal coverage level exactly $1-\alpha$, \begin{equation}\P\left( Y_{n + 1} \in \cC(X_{n + 1}) \right) = 1-\alpha.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-1911: We also remark that, in the streaming data setting studied in Chapter~\ref{chapter:online-conformal , it can again be shown that the smoothed p-values at each time $t$ are independent (analogous to the result of Theorem~\ref{thm:online-indep-pvals , but now we no longer need to assume that scores are distinct almost surely, since the randomized smoothing step removes the issue of ties).}}
elements.sent-2411-1912: We have \begin{equation}Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1}) \ \Longleftrightarrow \ p^{Y_{n+1}}(\xi) >\alpha,\end{equation} where by definition, we have \begin{equation}p^{Y_{n+1}}(\xi) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n+1} \ind{S_i > S_{n+1}} + \xi \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n+1} \ind{S_i = S_{n+1}}}{n+1}.\end{equation}.
