elements.sent-2411-1914: Therefore, our last remaining step is to prove the validity of a randomized version of permutation testing, which is established with the following lemma:.
elements.sent-2411-1915: Let $S_1,\dots,S_{n+1}$ be exchangeable, and let $\xi\sim\textnormal{Unif [0,1]$ be drawn independently from $S_1,\dots,S_{n+1}$.. Define \begin{equation}p(\xi) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n+1} \ind{S_i > S_{n+1}} + \xi \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n+1} \ind{S_i = S_{n+1}}}{n+1}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-1917: The lemma immediately implies that $\P(p^{Y_{n+1 (\xi) \leq \alpha)= \alpha$, which completes the proof of the theorem.}}
elements.sent-2411-1918: To complete this section, we prove the lemma.
elements.sent-2411-1919: First, by exchangeability, we have $p(\xi)\eqd p_j(\xi)$ for each $j$.. Therefore, \begin{equation}\P(p(\xi)\leq \alpha) = \frac{1}{n+1}\sum_{j\in[n+1]}\P(p_j(\xi)\leq\alpha) = \E\left[\frac{1}{n+1} \sum_{j\in[n+1]} \P(p_j(\xi)\leq \alpha \mid S_1,\dots,S_{n+1}),\right].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1920: Next, define \begin{equation}q = \quantile(S_1,\dots,S_{n+1};1-\tau),\end{equation} and let \begin{equation}N_+ =\sum_{j\in[n+1]}\ind{S_j > q}, \quad N_= =\sum_{j\in[n+1]}\ind{S_j = q}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1921: By definition of the quantile for a finite list (recall Fact~\ref{fact:conversion-order-stats-quantiles ), we have \begin{equation}N_+ \leq \tau(n+1), \quad N_= + N_+ \geq 1 + \tau(n+1).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-1922: By definition of $p_j(\xi)$, for any $j$ with $S_j<q$, we have \begin{equation}p_j(\xi) \geq \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n+1} \ind{S_i>S_j}}{n+1} \geq \frac{N_=+N_+}{n+1} > \tau,\end{equation} while for any $j$ with $S_j>q$, we have \begin{equation}p_j(\xi) \leq\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n+1} \ind{S_i\geq S_j}}{n+1} \leq \frac{N_+}{n+1} \leq \tau.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1923: And, if $S_j=q$, then \begin{equation}p_j(\xi) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n+1} \ind{S_i>q} + \xi \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n+1} \ind{S_i = q}}{n+1} = \frac{N_+ + \xi N_=}{n+1},\end{equation} so that on the event $S_j=q$, \begin{equation}p_j(\xi)\leq \tau \ \Longleftrightarrow \ \xi\leq \frac{\tau(n+1) - N_+}{N_=}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1924: These calculations combine to show that \begin{equation}\P(p_j(\xi)\leq\tau \mid S_1,\dots,S_{n+1}) = \ind{S_j > q} + \ind{S_j = q}\cdot \frac{\tau(n+1) - N_+}{N_=}.\end{equation}.

elements.section-2411-043: Computational shortcuts for full conformal prediction
elements.sent-2411-1926: As we have seen, full conformal prediction is computationally expensive---for each possible value $y\in\cY$ of the test response $Y_{n+1}$, full conformal requires refitting the score function $s(\cdot;\cD^y_{n+1 )$ (e.g., re-running a regression algorithm, in the case that our score function is of the form $s((x,y);\cD) = |y - \hf(x;\cD)|$).}
elements.sent-2411-1927: While this might be easy in the case that $\cY$ is a small finite set of possible labels, this becomes prohibitively expensive if $Y$ is categorical but has a large number of possible values, or even impossible if $Y$ is continuous, say, if $\cY=\R$.
elements.sent-2411-1928: In this section, we will focus on the case that $Y$ is continuous, so that $\cY=\R$.
elements.sent-2411-1929: First we will examine some special cases where, due to the particular construction of the score, the full conformal prediction interval can be computed exactly.
elements.sent-2411-1930: We will then turn to a general strategy that uses discretization to allow for efficient computation regardless of the choice of score function.
elements.figure-2411-018: \includegraphics[width=0.55\linewidth]{figures/diagrams/ols-full-conformal.pdf} \caption{Visualization of the conformal p-value resulting from the least-squares predictor. The top plot shows the residuals on the augmented dataset as a function of the putative label $y$. The residuals are all linear in $y$. The yellow line corresponds to the test residual. In the middle plot, we show the absolute residuals on the augmented dataset. The bottom plot shows the conformal p-values resulting from the residual score. Since the conformal p-value only changes when the lines cross, one does not have to check all $y \in \R$ to compute the prediction set $\{y \in \cY : p^y > \alpha\}$; it suffices to consider only the $y$ values at the crossing points of the absolute residuals.} \label{fig:ols-full-conformal}
elements.sent-2411-1931: First, we examine linear regression, in the low-dimensional case, where $\cX = \R^d$ for some $d\leq n$.
elements.sent-2411-1932: This is an important case and is the first step toward more flexible models such as ridge regression and lasso.
elements.sent-2411-1933: We restrict attention to the score function given by the residual for the least-squares regression model: we have \begin{equation}s((x,y);\cD) = |y-\hf(x;\cD)|,\end{equation} where the fitted model is given by \begin{equation}\hf(x;\cD) = x^\top\hat\beta \textnormal{ for }\hat\beta= \argmin_{\beta\in\R^d}\left\{\sum_{i=1}^m(y_i-x_i\top\beta)^2\right\}\textnormal{ where }\cD = ((x_1,y_1),\dots,(x_m,y_m)).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1935: Fix a training dataset $\cD_n=((X_i,Y_i))_{i\in[n]}$ and test point $X_{n+1}$, and let $X_{[n+1]}\in\R^{(n+1)\times d}$ denote the matrix with $i$th row given by $X_i$, for each $i\in[n+1]$.
elements.sent-2411-1936: Assume $X_{[n+1]}$ has full column rank, i.e., rank $d$.
elements.sent-2411-1937: Suppose the score function is given by the residual score for least-squares regression, as defined above.
elements.sent-2411-1938: Then the full conformal prediction set is equal to \begin{equation}\label{eqn:CP_set_lin_reg}\cC(X_{n+1}) = \left\{y \in\R :\frac{ 1+ \sum_{i=1}^n \ind{|y \cdot (-b_i) - (a_i - Y_i)| \geq |y\cdot (1-b_{n+1}) - a_{n+1}|}}{n+1} > \alpha\right\},\end{equation} where we define $a,b\in\R^{n+1}$ with entries \begin{equation}a_i = \sum_{j\in[n+1]} H_{ij}Y_j, \quad b_i = H_{i,n+1}\end{equation} for matrix $H\in\R^{(n+1)\times(n+1)}$ defined as \begin{equation}H = X_{[n+1]}\left(X_{[n+1]}^\top X_{[n+1]}\right)^{-1}X_{[n+1]}^\top .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1939: To see why this reformulation of the prediction $\cC(X_{n+1 )$ leads to an efficient calculation, we note that \begin{equation}y\mapsto \sum_{i=1}^n \ind{|y \cdot (-b_i) - (a_i - Y_i)| \geq |y\cdot (1-b_{n+1}) - a_{n+1}|}\end{equation} is a piecewise constant function, and its changepoints can only occur at values $y$ that satisfy \begin{equation}y \cdot (-b_i) - (a_i - Y_i) = \pm\big(y\cdot (1-b_{n+1}) - a_{n+1}\big)\end{equation} for some $i=1,\dots,n$.}
elements.sent-2411-1940: Therefore, after solving $2n$ many univariate linear equations to identify the candidate values $y$ that might be changepoints of the above function, we can derive the exact set $\cC(X_{n+1 )$.}
elements.sent-2411-1942: We can rewrite this as \begin{equation}\hat\beta^y = (X_{[n+1]}^\top X_{[n+1]})^{-1} X_{[n+1]}^\top \left({c}Y_1 \vdots Y_n y\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1943: For each $i\in[n+1]$, then, \begin{equation}X_i^\top \hat\beta^y = X_i^\top (X_{[n+1]}^\top X_{[n+1]})^{-1} X_{[n+1]}^\top \left({c}Y_1 \vdots Y_n y\right) = \sum_{j\in[n]} H_{ij}\cdot Y_j + H_{i,n+1} \cdot y = a_i + b_iy,\end{equation} by plugging in the definitions of $H$, $a$, and $b$ from the statement of the proposition.
elements.formula-2411-544: \begin{equation}X_i^\top \hat\beta^y = X_i^\top (X_{[n+1]}^\top X_{[n+1]})^{-1} X_{[n+1]}^\top \left({c}Y_1 \vdots Y_n y\right) = \sum_{j\in[n]} H_{ij}\cdot Y_j + H_{i,n+1} \cdot y = a_i + b_iy,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-1944: We can then calculate the scores of the training and test data points as \begin{equation}S^y_i = s((X_i,Y_i);\cD^y_{n+1}) = |Y_i - X_i^\top\hat\beta^y| = |y \cdot (-b_i) - (a_i - Y_i)|,\end{equation} for $i=1,\dots,n$, and\begin{equation}S^y_{n+1} = s((X_{n+1},y);\cD^y_{n+1}) = |y - X_{n+1}^\top\hat\beta^y| = |y\cdot (1-b_{n+1}) - a_{n+1}|.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1945: Next, following the reinterpretation of full conformal given in Section~\ref{sec:conformal_as_perm , we have \begin{equation}\cC(X_{n+1}) = \{y\in\R: p^y>\alpha\},\end{equation} where we compute the conformal p-value as \begin{equation}p^y = \frac{1 + \sum_{i\in[n]}\ind{S^y_i \geq S^y_{n+1}}}{n+1} = \frac{1 + \sum_{i\in[n]}\ind{|y \cdot (-b_i) - (a_i - Y_i)| \geq |y\cdot (1-b_{n+1}) - a_{n+1}|}}{n+1}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-1946: The example of linear regression can be generalized to a broader class of regression algorithms (while still using the residual score); here we mention some of these special cases briefly, without going into details.
elements.sent-2411-1947: Suppose that, for all $x'\in\cX$, the prediction $\hf(x';\cD^y_{n+1 )$ depends linearly on $y$---that is, \begin{equation}\label{eqn:hat_f_is_linear_in_y}\hf(x';\cD^y_{n+1}) = a(x';\cD_n,X_{n+1}) + b(x';\cD_n,X_{n+1})\cdot y,\end{equation} where $a(x';\cD_n,X_{n+1 )$ and $b(x';\cD_n,X_{n+1 )$ can depend arbitrarily on their arguments.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1948: In addition to linear regression, methods in this class include ridge regression, nearest neighbor type methods, and various kernel regression methods.
elements.sent-2411-1949: In this type of setting, after calculating $a_i=a(X_i;\cD_n,X_{n+1 )$ and $b_i=b(X_i;\cD_n,X_{n+1 )$ for each $i=1,\dots,n+1$ (which we only need to compute once, as these values do not depend on $y$), we then have $\cC(X_{n+1 )$ given by the calculation~\eqref{eqn:CP_set_lin_reg , exactly as for linear regression, and so the same calculation strategy can be applied to compute this set.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1950: Another special case arises if the regression algorithm is given by the Lasso, i.e., $\ell_1$-penalized linear regression, where the fitted model is now given by \begin{equation}\hf(x;\cD) = x^\top\hat\beta \textnormal{ for }\hat\beta= \argmin_{\beta\in\R^d}\left\{\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^m(y_i-x_i^\top\beta)^2 + \lambda\|\beta\|_1\right\}\textnormal{ where }\cD = ((x_1,y_1),\dots,(x_m,y_m)).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1951: The addition of the $\ell_1$ penalty, $\lambda\|\beta\|_1$ (for some penalty parameter $\lambda>0$), allows for accurate estimation even in the high-dimensional setting where $d\gg n$, under the assumption that there is (approximate) sparse structure in the true dependence of $Y$ on $X$.
elements.sent-2411-1952: In this setting, our score function is again given by the residual score, \begin{equation}s((x,y);\cD) = |y-\hf(x;\cD)|,\end{equation} but the improved accuracy of the fitted model will likely lead to improved predictions (and thus, narrower prediction intervals) relative to non-sparse methods such as ridge regression.
elements.sent-2411-1953: This choice of the base algorithm is more computationally complex than the special case of linear regression---the Lasso does not satisfy a linearity property along the lines of~\eqref{eqn:hat_f_is_linear_in_y .}
elements.sent-2411-1954: Nonetheless, we will now see that efficient computation of $\cC(X_{n+1 )$ can be achieved by exploiting the observation that the map $y\mapsto \hf(x;\cD^y_{n+1 )$ is now piecewise linear.}}
elements.sent-2411-1955: We will give an informal derivation of the prediction interval, stating facts about the Lasso optimization problem without proof (and assuming without comment any necessary regularity conditions).
elements.sent-2411-1956: Write \begin{equation}\hat\beta^y = \argmin_{\beta\in\R^d}\left\{\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^n (Y_i - X_i^\top\beta)^2 + \frac{1}{2}(y - X_{n+1}^\top\beta)^2 + \lambda\|\beta\|_1\right\},\end{equation} the coefficient vector returned by running Lasso on the dataset $\cD^y_{n+1}$.
elements.sent-2411-1957: Define also the support, \begin{equation}\hat{I}^y = \{j\in[d] : \hat\beta^y_j\neq 0\}\subseteq [d],\end{equation} and the signs, \begin{equation}\hat\gamma^y = (\textnormal{sign}(\hat\beta^y_j))_{j\in \hat{I}^y}\in \{\pm 1\}^{|\hat{I}^y|}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1958: We will now see how the fitted values of the Lasso model, $\hf(X_i;\cD^y_{n+1 ) = X_i^\top\hat\beta^y$, relate to the support and the signs.}
elements.sent-2411-1959: The nature of the Lasso optimization problem ensures that the support and signs are piecewise constant---that is, the map $y\mapsto (\hat{I ^y,\hat\gamma^y)$ is piecewise constant over $y\in\R$.}
elements.sent-2411-1960: In particular, it can be shown that we can partition $\R$ into a finite number of intervals, \begin{equation}\R = I_1 \cup\dots\cup I_R,\end{equation} with each interval $I_r$ of the form $(a,b)$ or $(a,b]$ or $[a,b)$ or $[a,b]$ for some $a<b\in\R$, such that $(\hat{I ^y,\hat\gamma^y)$ is constant over $y\in I_r$ for each $r$.}
elements.sent-2411-1961: Let $(I_r,\gamma_r)$ denote this constant value in the $r$th interval, i.e., $(\hat{I ^y,\hat\gamma^y) = (I_r,\gamma_r)$ for all $y\in I_r$.}
elements.paragraph-2411-022: Preliminaries: relating the predictions to the support and signs.
elements.sent-2411-1962: For any $y\in\R$, the first-order optimality conditions for the solution $\hat\beta^y$ to the Lasso optimization problem yield the identity \begin{equation}(\hat\beta^y)_{\hat{I}^y} = \left(X_{[n+1],\hat{I}^y}^\top X_{[n+1],\hat{I}^y}\right)^{-1}\left(X_{[n+1],\hat{I}^y} ^\top \left({c}Y_1 \vdots Y_n y\right) - \lambda \hat\gamma^y\right),\end{equation} i.e., if we know the support and signs of the solution $\hat\beta^y$ then this solution can be expressed as a linear function of the vector $(Y_1,\dots,Y_n,y)^\top$ of response values.
elements.sent-2411-1963: Here $X_{[n+1]}\in\R^{(n+1)\times d}$ is the matrix with $i$th row given by $X_i$, as in Proposition~\ref{prop:full_CP_leastsq , while the notation $X_{[n+1],\hat{I ^y}$ indicates that we take the submatrix given by columns $\hat{I ^y\subseteq[d]$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1964: Defining \begin{equation}a_i(I;\gamma) = (X_i)_I^\top\left(X_{[n+1],I}^\top X_{[n+1],I}\right)^{-1}\left(X_{[n+1],I} ^\top \left({c}Y_1 \vdots Y_n 0\right) - \lambda \gamma\right)\end{equation} and \begin{equation}b_i(I;\gamma) = (X_i)_I^\top \left(X_{[n+1],I}^\top X_{[n+1],I}\right)^{-1}\left(X_{[n+1],I} ^\top \left({c}0 \vdots 0 1\right) \right)\end{equation} for any (nonempty) $I\subseteq[d]$ and $\gamma\in\{\pm1\}^{|I|}$, we can therefore calculate that the prediction for the $i$th data point is given by \begin{equation}\hf(X_i;\cD^y_{n+1}) = X_i^\top\hat\beta^y = a_i(\hat{I}^y,\hat\gamma^y) + b_i(\hat{I}^y,\hat\gamma^y)\cdot y,\end{equation} for each $i\in[n+1]$.
elements.sent-2411-1965: Relating this to the observation above, where we explained that $y\mapsto (\hat{I ^y,\hat\gamma^y)$ is piecewise constant with $(\hat{I ^y,\hat\gamma^y)= (I_r,\gamma_r)$ for all $y\in I_r$, for each interval $r\in[R]$ we therefore have \begin{equation}\hf(X_i;\cD^y_{n+1}) = a_i(I_r,\gamma_r) + b_i(I_r,\gamma_r)\cdot y\textnormal{ for all $y\in I_r$}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-559: \begin{equation}\hf(X_i;\cD^y_{n+1}) = a_i(I_r,\gamma_r) + b_i(I_r,\gamma_r)\cdot y\textnormal{ for all $y\in I_r$}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-1966: With all these preliminaries in place, we are now ready to describe the algorithm for computing the full conformal prediction interval with the Lasso.
elements.sent-2411-1967: Begin by choosing an arbitrary value $y_1\in\R$ as a starting point, and run the Lasso to compute $\hat\beta^{y_1}$.
elements.sent-2411-1969: Certain properties of the Lasso allow us to solve a system of linear inequalities to find interval endpoints $y_{\textnormal{lo \leq y_1 \leq y_{\textnormal{hi }$, such that the support and signs are constant inside this range, i.e., $(\hat{I ^y,\hat\gamma^y) = (I_1,\gamma_1)$ for all $y\in I_1$, where we define the interval $I_1$ as $(y_{\textnormal{lo ,y_{\textnormal{hi )$ or $(y_{\textnormal{lo ,y_{\textnormal{hi ]$ or $[y_{\textnormal{lo ,y_{\textnormal{hi )$ or $[y_{\textnormal{lo ,y_{\textnormal{hi ]$, as appropriate.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1970: From the work above, then, we can compute predictions \begin{equation}\hf(X_i;\cD^y_{n+1}) = a_i(I_1,\gamma_1) + b_i(I_1,\gamma_1)\cdot y\textnormal{ for all $y\in I_1$}\end{equation} for each $i\in[n+1]$, where $a_i(I_1,\gamma_1)$ and $b_i(I_1,\gamma_1)$ are calculated as above.
elements.formula-2411-560: \begin{equation}\hf(X_i;\cD^y_{n+1}) = a_i(I_1,\gamma_1) + b_i(I_1,\gamma_1)\cdot y\textnormal{ for all $y\in I_1$}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-1971: We are now ready to calculate a portion of the conformal prediction interval: we have \begin{equation}\cC(X_{n+1}) \cap I_1 = \bigg\{y \in I_1 : \frac{ 1+ \sum_{i=1}^n \ind{|y \cdot (-b_i(I_1,\gamma_1)) - (a_i(I_1,\gamma_1) - Y_i)| \geq |y\cdot (1-b_{n+1}(I_1,\gamma_1)) - a_{n+1}(I_1,\gamma_1)|}}{n+1} > 1-\alpha\bigg\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1972: Note that this is similar to the calculation~\eqref{eqn:CP_set_lin_reg for the case of linear regression.}
elements.sent-2411-1973: The difference is that for linear regression, the predictions $\hf(X_i;\cD^y_{n+1 )$ are linear in $y$, and so a single set of this form suffices to compute the entire prediction interval $\cC(X_{n+1 )$; in the Lasso case, the predictions $\hf(X_i;\cD^y_{n+1 )$ are instead piecewise linear, and so the calculation above is only sufficient to calculate one piece of $\cC(X_{n+1 )$, namely, its intersection with the interval $I_1$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1974: From this point on, we iterate this procedure.
elements.sent-2411-1975: We choose another value $y_2\in\R\setminus I_1$, and run the Lasso to compute $\hat\beta^{y_2}$ and thus discover a new interval $I_2\subseteq\R$ within which $y\mapsto (\hat{I ^y,\hat\gamma^y)$ is constant.}
elements.sent-2411-1976: We then follow the same steps as above to compute $\cC(X_{n+1 )\cap I_2$.}
elements.sent-2411-1977: We repeat this procedure as many times as necessary until the entire real line is covered.
elements.sent-2411-1978: That is, we repeat the procedure $R$ times, for some unknown but finite integer $R$, such that the intervals $I_1,\dots,I_R$ collectively form a partition of the real line.
elements.sent-2411-1979: The final prediction interval is then given by \begin{equation}\cC(X_{n+1}) = \cup_{r=1}^R \big(\cC(X_{n+1})\cap I_r\big),\end{equation} where each piece $\cC(X_{n+1 )\cap I_r$ has been calculated via the procedure above.}
elements.sent-2411-1980: Moving beyond simple cases like linear regression and the Lasso, in general settings where the regression method and/or score function may be arbitrarily complex, we may no longer be able to derive computational shortcuts for computing $\cC(X_{n+1 )$ efficiently.}
elements.sent-2411-1981: Since $\cC(X_{n+1 )$ is impossible to compute exactly for $\cY=\R$ aside from special cases like the above, in practice it is common to use an informal discretization strategy, approximating $\R$ with a fine grid.}
elements.sent-2411-1982: The strategy is typically implemented as follows.
elements.sent-2411-1983: Let $y^{(1) <\dots<y^{(M)}$ be a prespecified list of values---typically these values are chosen to have equal spacing, $y^{(m+1) -y^{(m) =\Delta$ for each $m=1,\dots,M-1$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1984: For each grid point, fit the score function by augmenting the dataset with a test point $(X_{n+1 ,y^{(m) )$, \begin{equation}s_m(x,y) = s((x,y);\cD^{y^{(m)}}_{n+1}),\end{equation} and determine whether $y^{(m)}$ should be included into the conformal prediction interval: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:full_CP_grid_informal}y^{(m)}\in\cC(X_{n+1})\textnormal{ \ if \ } s_m(X_{n+1},y^{(m)}) \leq \quantile\left((s_m(X_i,Y_i))_{i\in[n]};(1-\alpha)(1+1/n)\right).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-1985: Then we compute an approximation to the full conformal prediction set by constructing the set \begin{equation}\cC(X_{n+1}) = \bigcup_{\substack{m\in\{1,\dots,M\} y^{(m)}\in\cC(X_{n+1})} }\big(y^{(m-1)}, y^{(m+1)}\big).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1986: In other words, if we observe that $y^{(m)}$ needs to be included in the prediction interval $\cC(X_{n+1 )$, then we also include any value $y$ that lies in between grid points $y^{(m-1)}$ and $y^{(m)}$, and any value $y$ that lies in between grid points $y^{(m)}$ and $y^{(m+1)}$, since we are not certain of where the true boundary of the set lies.}
elements.sent-2411-1987: The reason is that the training data points $i=1,\dots,n$ and the test point $n+1$ are no longer being treated exchangeably: when computing our score functions, we use data points $(X_i,Y_i)$ for each training point $i=1,\dots,n$, but the test point $(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )$ is never used, unless by coincidence we happen to have $Y_{n+1 =y^{(m)}$ for one of the preselected grid points.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1988: That is, we do not compute scores in a way that treats all the (training and test) data points symmetrically.
elements.sent-2411-1989: The following result addresses this issue, by modifying the method to restore symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-1990: At a high level, it works by discretizing all the points, not just the test point, thus yielding a symmetric algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-1991: Let $y_1,\dots,y_M\in\cY$ be a prespecified collection of values and let $k:\cY\rightarrow[M]$ be any function.
elements.sent-2411-1992: Then this prediction set satisfies distribution-free marginal coverage, i.e., if $(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )$ are exchangeable then \begin{equation}\P(Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1}))\geq 1-\alpha.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-1993: As for the informal grid-based approximation defined in~\eqref{eqn:full_CP_grid_informal , the cost of constructing this new discretized full conformal prediction interval is simply the cost of $M$ many runs of the modeling algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-1994: For example, if we use a residual score function, then writing $\hf_m$ to denote the fitted model when we run our regression algorithm on data points $(X_1,y_{k(Y_1) ),\dots,(X_n,y_{k(Y_n) ), (X_{n+1 ,y_m)$, the $m$th trained score function $s_m$ is given by $s_m(x,y) = |y-\hf_m(x)|$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1995: This means that, to compute $\cC(X_{n+1 )$, we need to run our regression algorithm $M$ many times (as compared to infinitely many times---at least in theory---for the original full conformal method).}
elements.sent-2411-1996: The choice of $M$ will affect both the statistical performance and the computational cost of this procedure.
elements.sent-2411-1997: If $M$ is chosen to be very small, then the procedure is computationally much more efficient, but the trained models $\hf_m$ will be much less accurate since all the response values are rounded---that is, each $Y_i$ is replaced with a value on the grid, $y_{k(Y_i)}$.
elements.sent-2411-1998: We can therefore expect to see a tradeoff between the computation time and the width of the resulting prediction intervals, with higher values of $M$ leading to more expensive computation but more precise prediction intervals.
elements.sent-2411-1999: To make this precise, we define a new score function $\tilde{s}$ as follows.
elements.sent-2411-2000: For any dataset $\cD = ((x'_1,y'_1),\dots,(x'_\ell,y'_\ell))$ and data point $(x',y')$, let \begin{equation}\tilde{s}\big((x',y');\cD\big) = s\big((x',y'); (x'_1,y_{k(y'_1)}),\dots,(x'_\ell,y_{k(y'_\ell)})\big).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-2001: In other words, the score function $\tilde{s}$ is obtained by implementing the original score function $s$, but on a modified version of the dataset $\cD$, obtained by rounding all $Y$ values to the grid $\{y_1,\dots,y_M\}$ via the map $k:\cY\rightarrow[M]$.
elements.sent-2411-2002: Now suppose that we run full conformal prediction with $\tilde{s}$ as our score function.
elements.sent-2411-2003: Let $\cC(X_{n+1 )$ be the resulting prediction set, so that we have \begin{equation}\P(Y_{n+1}\in \cC(X_{n+1}))\geq 1-\alpha\end{equation} by exchangeability of the data (together with the fact that $\tilde{s}$ is a symmetric score function, because it inherits this property from $s$).}

elements.sent-2411-2008: By construction of $\tilde{s}$, we can verify that, for any $y\in\cY$ with $k(y) = m$, we have \begin{equation}\tilde{s}(\cdot ; \cD^y_{n+1}) = s(\cdot; (X_1,y_{k(Y_1)}),\dots,(X_n,y_{k(Y_n)}), (X_{n+1}, y_{k(y)})) = s(\cdot; (X_1,y_{k(Y_1)}),\dots,(X_n,y_{k(Y_n)}), (X_{n+1}, y_m)) = s_m(\cdot).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-2009: Therefore, for any $y\in\cY$ with $k(y)=m$, \begin{equation}\tilde{S}^y_i =  s_m(X_i,Y_i), i\in[n], s_m(X_{n+1},y), i = n+1.\end{equation} Therefore, we can see that the set defined in the statement of the proposition is exactly equal to the set $\cC(X_{n+1 )$ given in~\eqref{eqn:cC_for_stilde_discretizedCP .}}
elements.sent-2411-2010: Since this set is equal to the full conformal prediction set (constructed with score function $\tilde{s}$), marginal coverage is therefore guaranteed by Theorem~\ref{thm:full-conformal .}
elements.sent-2411-2011: Throughout this book, we have seen that conformal prediction provides a strategy for ensuring distribution-free marginal predictive coverage under only an assumption of exchangeability.
elements.sent-2411-2012: But are there alternative methods that might achieve the same goal---and, perhaps, offer more informative prediction intervals? The following result proves a universality property of full conformal prediction.
elements.sent-2411-2013: It demonstrates that any method achieving distribution-free marginal coverage must actually be equivalent to running full conformal prediction (with some choice of score function), as long as we assume that the method is symmetric in the training data.
elements.sent-2411-2014: Let $\cC$ be any predictive inference procedure, which maps any training dataset and test point to a prediction interval (or prediction set), \begin{equation}(\cD, x) \mapsto \cC(x;\cD)\subseteq \cY.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-2015: Assume $\cC$ is symmetric in the training data, i.e., $\cC(x;\cD)=\cC(x;\cD_\sigma)$ for any $x\in\cX$, any dataset $\cD$, and any permutation $\sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-2016: Assume also that $\cC$ satisfies distribution-free predictive coverage at level $1-\alpha$: \begin{equation}\textnormal{If $(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_{n+1},Y_{n+1})$ are exchangeable then $\P(Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1};\cD_n))\geq 1-\alpha$}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-2017: Then there exists a symmetric conformal score function $s$ such that $\cC$ is equal to the full conformal prediction interval constructed with this score function.
elements.sent-2411-2018: How should we interpret this result? Essentially, it is telling us that the conformal prediction methodology is so broad---due to the flexibility in choosing the score function $s$---that any method with distribution-free validity can be expressed as a special case.
elements.sent-2411-2019: Therefore, if we are restricting ourselves to symmetric methods, it is not fruitful to ask whether we can find a distribution-free method that will perform better than conformal prediction; instead, we should simply search for a better score function if we are not satisfied with the performance of conformal prediction on a particular application.
elements.sent-2411-2020: However, the symmetry assumption is important.
elements.sent-2411-2021: As an example of a distribution-free method that does not satisfy symmetry, we can recall the weighted version of conformal prediction (with fixed weights), covered in Chapter~\ref{chapter:weighted-conformal (see Section~\ref{sec:fixed-weights ); this method achieves marginal coverage under exchangeability, but cannot be expressed as an instance of (unweighted) conformal prediction.}}
elements.sent-2411-2022: Given a dataset $\cD$ and a data point $(x,y)$, we define $\cD_{\setminus(x,y)}$ as the dataset obtained by removing one copy of $(x,y)$, if $(x,y)$ appears (one or more times) in $\cD$, or otherwise simply returning $\cD$.
elements.sent-2411-2023: Now define the score function $s$ as follows: for any dataset $\cD'$ and any point $(x,y)$, let \begin{equation}s((x,y);\cD') = \ind{y\not\in\cC(x; \cD'_{\setminus (x,y)})}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-2024: The assumption of symmetry on $\cC$ implies that $s$ is a symmetric score function.
elements.sent-2411-2025: Given training data $\cD_n = ((X_i,Y_i))_{i\in[n]}$ and test point $X_{n+1}$, we now claim that $\cC(X_{n+1 ;\cD_n) = \cC_{\textnormal{CP (X_{n+1 )$, where $\cC_{\textnormal{CP (X_{n+1 )$ denotes the output of full conformal prediction (Algorithm~\ref{alg:full-cp ) run with the score function $s$ defined above, and with $\cD_n$ as the training data.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-2026: As usual for full conformal, for any $y\in\cY$, let $\cD^y_{n+1}$ denote the augmented dataset, \begin{equation}\cD^y_{n+1} = ((X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_n,Y_n),(X_{n+1},y)),\end{equation} and note that $(\cD^y_{n+1 )_{\setminus (X_{n+1 ,y) = \cD_n$ (or, we may have that $(\cD^y_{n+1 )_{\setminus (X_{n+1 ,y)}$ is equal to some permutation of $\cD_n$, in the case that the data point $(X_{n+1 ,y)$ happens to be equal to one of the training points $(X_i,Y_i)$).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-2027: By definition of full conformal prediction, we have \begin{equation}\cC_{\textnormal{CP}}(X_{n+1}) = \{y\in\cY : S^y_{n+1} \leq \quantile(S^y_1,\dots,S^y_n; (1-\alpha)(1+1/n))\},\end{equation} where \begin{equation}S^y_i = s((X_i,Y_i);\cD^y_{n+1}), i\in[n], \ S^y_{n+1} = s((X_{n+1},y);\cD^y_{n+1}).\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-2115: Thus, this discussion sheds light on the essential mathematical structure necessary for split conformal prediction: we have a nested family of prediction sets that we tune to control miscoverage, which is a monotone loss.
elements.sent-2411-2116: Conformal prediction is typically presented as a method for constructing a prediction interval for a single test point $X_{n+1}$, given a training set of size $n$.
elements.sent-2411-2117: However, in practice, we often need to provide prediction intervals to multiple test points in a batch.
elements.sent-2411-2118: Alternatively, we might want to screen a large collection of data points for potential outliers, via conformal p-values (as developed in Chapter~\ref{chapter:online-conformal ).}
elements.sent-2411-2119: These uses of conformal prediction raise an important question: how can we use conformal prediction on multiple data points if it only possesses validity guarantees for each data point individually? This is the central question of multiplicity.
elements.sent-2411-2120: In this section, we show how to prove guarantees of joint validity when running split conformal prediction across multiple test points.
elements.sent-2411-2121: Specifically, we will study family-wise error rate (FWER) control for the problem of joint validity of a collection of prediction intervals.
elements.sent-2411-2122: We will also study the setting where conformal methodology is applied to the problem of outlier detection, and will examine FWER control as well as false discovery rate (FDR) control in this setting.
elements.subsection-2411-046: Controlling the family-wise error rate (FWER) for predictive coverage
elements.sent-2411-2123: Throughout this section, we will use our previous notation for the split conformal prediction setting: we write $\cD_n=((X_i,Y_i))_{i\in[n]}$ to denote the calibration set, while $s:\cX\times\cY\to\R$ is the pretrained score function.
elements.sent-2411-2124: Given a set of test data points $X'_1, \ldots, X'_m$, we might be interested in obtaining a guarantee of the form \begin{equation}\label{eqn:FWER_goal} \P\big( Y'_1 \in \cC(X'_1) \textnormal{ and } \ldots \textnormal{ and } Y'_m \in \cC(X'_m) \big) \ge 1-\alpha_{\textnormal{FWER}},\end{equation} meaning all of the $m$ prediction sets cover (with probability at least $1-\alpha_{\textnormal{FWER }$).}
elements.sent-2411-2125: Here $\alpha_{\textnormal{FWER }$ represents the family-wise error rate: that is, the probability of at least one miscoverage among our $m$ prediction sets.}
elements.sent-2411-2126: Let us now consider how the split conformal prediction method would perform relative to this goal.
elements.sent-2411-2127: Since the prediction set is given by $\cC(x) = \{y\in\cY: s(x,y)\leq \hat{q}\}$, where $\hat{q = \quantile(S_1,\dots,S_n;(1-\alpha)(1+1/n))$, we can rephrase the goal~\eqref{eqn:FWER_goal above as \begin{equation}\label{eq:multiplicity-scores} \P\left( S'_1 \leq \hat{q} \textnormal{ and } \ldots \textnormal{ and } S'_m \leq \hat{q} \right) \ge 1-\alpha_{\textnormal{FWER}},\end{equation} where the test point scores are given by $S'_i = s(X'_i,Y'_i)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-2128: The key challenge is that the events $ \{S'_i \leq \hat{q \}_{i \in [m]}$ are not independent, since the threshold $\hat{q}$ (which is random) is shared across all $m$ events.}
elements.sent-2411-2129: Let us first consider the asymptotic case, where the number of calibration points is $n\rightarrow\infty$ (while the number of test points $m$ remains fixed).
elements.sent-2411-2130: For this case, we will write $\hat{q _n$ (rather than $\hat{q}$) to emphasize that $n$ is increasing.}
elements.sent-2411-2131: Let $(X'_1,Y'_1),\ldots,(X'_m,Y'_m),(X_1,Y_1),(X_2,Y_2),\dots$ be sampled i.i.d.\ from some distribution $P$.
elements.sent-2411-2132: Let $s:\cX\times\cY\to\R$ be a pretrained score function, and for each calibration set size $n\geq 1$, define the split conformal prediction set as \begin{equation}\cC_{\hat{q}_n}(x) = \{y:s(x,y)\leq\hat{q}_n\}\end{equation} where the threshold is given by \begin{equation}\hat{q}_n = \quantile\big(S_1,\dots,S_n;(1-\alpha)(1+1/n)\big)\end{equation} for scores $S_i = s(X_i,Y_i)$, $i\in[n]$.
elements.sent-2411-2133: Define $q^* = \quantile(F,1-\alpha)$, where $F$ is the CDF of $s(X,Y)$ when $(X,Y)\sim P$.
elements.sent-2411-2134: Assume that $F(q)$ is continuous at $q=q^*$, and that $q^*$ is the unique $(1-\alpha)$-quantile in the sense that $F(q)>1-\alpha$ for all $q>q^*$.. elements.sent-2411-2151: We will next turn to the setting of the outlier detection problem.
elements.sent-2411-2152: As before, i.i.d.~calibration points $(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_n,Y_n)$ from a distribution $P$, and an independent set of test points $(X_1',Y'_1),\dots,(X_m',Y_m')$.
elements.sent-2411-2153: In contrast to the prediction problem, our goal now is to detect outliers (i.e., test points $(X'_i,Y'_i)$ that were not drawn from $P$), rather than to construct prediction intervals.
elements.sent-2411-2154: In particular, $Y'_1,\dots,Y_m'$ are observed, which was not previously the case.
elements.sent-2411-2155: To formalize the question, for each test point $i\in[m]$ define the null hypothesis \begin{equation}H_{0,i}: (X'_i,Y'_i) \sim P.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-2156: Rejecting $H_{0,i}$ means that we claim to have detected that test point $(X'_i,Y'_i)$ is an outlier.
elements.sent-2411-2157: For the outlier detection problem, when we are simultaneously testing $m$ many data points, we generally wish to detect outliers without incurring too many false positives.
elements.sent-2411-2158: This may be formalized by seeking to control notions of error such as the family-wise error rate (FWER) or the false discovery rate (FDR).
elements.sent-2411-2159: In particular, consider a procedure that sees both the calibration points and the test points and then returns a subset $\cR \subseteq \{1,\dots,m\}$ of the test points as outliers---this is the set of rejections among the null hypotheses $H_{0,1 ,\dots,H_{0,m}$ being tested.}
elements.sent-2411-2160: In this setting, the FWER is defined as \begin{equation}\label{eq:fwer_def_outlier_detection} \mathsf{FWER} = \P\left(|\{i\in\cR : H_{0,i}\textnormal{ is true}\}|>0\right),\end{equation} the probability of making at least one false rejection (i.e., falsely labeling at least one $(X'_i,Y'_i)$ as an outlier), while the FDR is defined as \begin{equation}\label{eq:fdr_def_outlier_detection} \mathsf{FDR} = \E\left[\frac{|\{i\in\cR : H_{0,i}\textnormal{ is true}\}|}{\max\{1,|\cR|\}}\right],\end{equation} the expected proportion of false rejections.
elements.sent-2411-2161: To move towards the goal of controlling FWER or FDR, we will now consider a p-value based approach towards testing the $m$ null hypotheses.
elements.sent-2411-2162: Assuming as before that we have a pretrained score function $s : \cX\times\cY \to \mathbb{R}$, we will test each null hypothesis $H_{0,i}$ by comparing the score $S'_i=s(X'_i,Y'_i)$ against the calibration set scores $S_1,\dots,S_n$.
elements.sent-2411-2163: Concretely, we consider the conformal p-value for test point $i$ (recall Section~\ref{sec:conformal_as_perm ): \begin{equation}\label{eq:outlier-pvalue} p_i = \frac{1 + \sum_{j=1}^n \ind{S'_i \le S_j}}{n+1}.\end{equation}.}
elements.figure-2411-020: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/diagrams/p-value-bias-calibration-set.pdf} \caption{Visualization of conformal p-values for outlier detection. The two panels plot the conformal p-value for two realizations of the calibration set. The horizontal axis is the score of the test point, $s(X_i',Y_i')$. The vertical axis shows the p-value of the test point, $p_i$, as a piecewise function. The piecewise function depends on the realization of the calibration scores, plotted as the yellow points. The calibration scores on the left are smaller than average (due to chance), so the p-values assigned to a test point are smaller, and vice versa for the right panel. The dashed line shows the one minus the CDF of the conformal scores, which should be thought of as the oracle version of the conformal p-values---it would be the conformal p-value with an infinitely larger calibration set.} \label{fig:outlier-pvalues}
elements.sent-2411-2164: Identifying a single outlier is relatively simple---by labeling $i$ as an outlier if $p_i \leq \alpha$ (say, $\alpha = 0.05$), we are guaranteed to incur a Type I error rate of no more than $\alpha$, \begin{equation}\P(p_i \leq \alpha)\leq \alpha\textnormal{ for any $i\in[m]$ for which $H_{0,i}$ is true}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-2166: However, identifying multiple outliers out of the batch of $m$ data points is more challenging, due to dependencies between the p-values $p_i$ (which is induced by the shared calibration set, used to construct all the p-values, as was also the case in Section~\ref{sec:FWER_predictive_coverage above).}

elements.sent-2411-2174: The problem of controlling FDR in a multiple testing scenario is well understood if we have independent p-values.
elements.sent-2411-2175: For example, the well-known Benjamini--Hochberg algorithm is defined as \begin{equation}\label{eqn:define_BH_procedure}\cR = \left\{i : p_i \leq \frac{\alpha_{\textnormal{FDR}}\hat{k}}{m}\right\}\textnormal{ where }\hat{k} = \max\left\{k : \sum_{i=1}^m \ind{p_i\leq\frac{ \alpha_{\textnormal{FDR}} k}{ n}} \geq k\right\},\end{equation} where $\alpha_{\textnormal{FDR \in [0,1]$ is the target FDR level.}}

elements.sent-2411-2177: This leads to the main idea of this section: for the outlier detection problem, even though the conformal p-values $p_i$ defined above are not independent, we can still control the FDR using the Benjamini--Hochberg algorithm.

elements.sent-2411-2190: The implications of Theorem~\ref{thm:prds are twofold.}
elements.sent-2411-2191: At a theoretical level, this result reveals something about the structure of conformal p-values: the shared calibration set induces positive dependence among all p-values.
elements.sent-2411-2192: For example, if the calibration set happens to contain an unusually high fraction of data points $(X_i,Y_i)$ with a low score $S_i=s(X_i,Y_i)$, then all p-values $p_1,\dots,p_m$ will be more likely to be small.
elements.sent-2411-2193: Moreover, at a practical level, this result implies that using the Benjamini--Hochberg algorithm with the conformal p-values controls the FDR.
elements.sent-2411-2194: We record this fact in the following corollary.

elements.sent-2411-2196: In words, this means that conformal p-values can be used to detect outliers on a batch of test data points while retaining error control, making conformal p-values an attractive choice for statistical inference for outlier detection.

elements.sent-2411-2217: One important use of predictive models and prediction intervals is to identify promising examples for follow-up study.
elements.sent-2411-2218: For example, we might have a list of medical drug candidates and we wish to identify candidates that appear to be highly effective, in order to pursue follow-up studies on these candidates.
elements.sent-2411-2219: For problems of this flavor where we wish to focus on a set of ``interesting'' units, the marginal coverage property is insufficient---for the ``interesting'' units, the coverage from conformal prediction may be far less than the nominal rate.
elements.sent-2411-2220: For example, suppose that, for a test feature vector $X_{n+1}$, we output a prediction interval of the form $\cC(X_{n+1 ) = [\hf(X_{n+1 ) - \hat{q ,\hf(X_{n+1 ) + \hat{q ]$, as would be the case for split conformal prediction run with the residual score.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-2221: The marginal coverage guarantee for conformal prediction ensures that $\P(Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1 ))\geq 1-\alpha$, on average over all possible test points.}
elements.sent-2411-2222: But if this unit is selected for followup study only if some criterion $\hf(X_{n+1 ) \geq c$ is reached (i.e., only if the predicted output is sufficiently high), the probability of coverage conditional on the unit being selected for followup testing may be lower---that is, we may have \begin{equation}\P(Y_{n+1}\in\cC(X_{n+1})\mid \hf(X_{n+1}) \geq c) < 1-\alpha.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-2223: This is the problem of selective inference: a guarantee that holds marginally may no longer hold if we condition on the outcome of a (data dependent) selection rule.
elements.sent-2411-2224: We next explain how conformal prediction can be adapted to avoid undercoverage on selected units.
elements.sent-2411-2225: This discussion is related to the conditional coverage and Mondrian conformal prediction discussion in Chapter~\ref{chapter:conditional (we will return to this connection again below).}
elements.sent-2411-2226: Here, we will ask for coverage conditional on a point being identified for follow-up---that is, conditional on selection.
elements.sent-2411-2227: Consider a selection rule $\cI$ mapping a dataset $\cD\in(\cX\times\cY)^{n+1}$ to a selected subset of data points $\cI(\cD)\subseteq[n+1]$.
elements.sent-2411-2228: To give a concrete example, the map $\cI$ could select all points $i$ with $\hf(X_i)\geq c$ for some prefitted model $\hf$ and some predetermined threshold $c$, as above.
elements.sent-2411-2229: Or, as another example, the selection rule could depend in a more complex way on the entire dataset, by selecting the $k$ points with the largest predicted response $\hf(X_i)$ for some fixed $k$.
elements.sent-2411-2230: We assume $\cI$ is symmetric, in the following sense: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:symmetry_of_selection_rule}\sigma(i)\in \cI(\cD) \ \Longleftrightarrow \ i\in\cI(\cD_\sigma),\end{equation} for any dataset $\cD$, any permutation $\sigma$, and any index $i\in[n+1]$.
elements.sent-2411-2231: Essentially, this condition ensures that the ordering of the data points does not affect which data points are selected.
elements.sent-2411-2232: For example, if the dataset $\big((x,y),(x',y')\big)$ maps to the selection $\{1\}$ (meaning that $(x,y)$ is selected but $(x',y')$ is not selected), then if we permute to obtain a new dataset $\big((x',y'),(x,y)\big)$, this new dataset must map to $\{2\}$ (so that, again, $(x,y)$ is selected but $(x',y')$ is not selected).
elements.sent-2411-2233: With this selection rule defined, we now turn to the algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-2234: The prediction interval will take the following form: \begin{equation}\label{eq:selective_conformal_set} \cC(X_{n+1}) = \{y : S_{n+1}^y \le \hat{q}^y, n+1\in\cI(\cD^y_{n+1})\},\end{equation} where the notation $S_{n+1 ^y$ and $\cD^y_{n+1}$ is defined as in Chapter~\ref{chapter:conformal-exchangeability .}}

elements.sent-2411-2262: A common strategy in predictive modeling is model ensembling---that is, combining multiple predictive models to achieve better prediction accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-2263: This leads naturally to the question of how we might combine multiple prediction sets (rather than combining point predictions).
elements.sent-2411-2264: Methods for this question are of practical, as well as theoretical, interest.
elements.sent-2411-2265: Such a question is subtle, however, because the precision of our estimate will vary depending on how dependent the original prediction sets are: if we aggregate $K$ prediction sets that provide relatively independent information, the miscoverage rate should decrease substantially, while if we aggregate $K$ sets that essentially each repeat the same information, then we would not expect their aggregated version to be more accurate.
elements.sent-2411-2266: Indeed, we might even be concerned that aggregation in the presence of arbitrary dependence could potentially even inflate the miscoverage error.
elements.sent-2411-2267: To address this last concern, we will begin by showing that there is a simple worst-case bound ensuring that aggregation via majority vote will preserve the coverage guarantee up to a factor of two.
elements.sent-2411-2268: Next, we will outline a simple strategy that leads to more precise guarantees on coverage, but requires some additional data for calibration.
elements.sent-2411-2269: Lastly, we relate the question of aggregating prediction sets to the cross-conformal prediction method studied in Chapter~\ref{chapter:cv .}

elements.figure-2411-023: \includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth]{figures/diagrams/E_unif.pdf} \caption{\textbf{Illustration of the event $\mathcal{E}_{\textnormal{unif}}$}, which is defined in the proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:training-conditional-full-CP}. Each plot shows a different random draw of the histogram of the values $a(X_i)\in\{0,\dots,n-1\}$, for training points $i\in[n]$, where $n=50$. Since we have $n$ data points and $n$ values, we have 1 observation per value on average, but the random draws are quite noisy. However, setting $N=20$, a sliding window of length $n-N=30$ typically contains approximately $n-N$ many data points. For each draw of the data, we highlight two examples of the sliding window of indices $W_k$, at $k=0$ and $k=15$. For the left-hand plot, the event $\mathcal{E}_{\rm unif}$ holds---the window $W_k$ contains a sufficient fraction of the observed $a(X_i)$ values, for every $k\in\{0,\dots,n-1\}$. For the right-hand plot, the event $\mathcal{E}_{\rm unif}$ fails---in particular, the window $W_{0}$ contains too few of the $a(X_i)$ values.} \label{fig:E_unif_illustration}

elements.sent-2411-2433: The key concept is the conformal score function, which is a function $s(x,y)$ such that larger values indicate that the data point $(x,y)$ does not agree with (does not `conform' to) the trends observed in the training data.

elements.sent-2411-2669: This chapter begins Part~\ref{part:beyond-predictive-coverage , the last part of the book, where we move beyond conformal prediction and examine a range of different statistical problems through the lens of the distribution-free framework.}
elements.sent-2411-2670: This chapter will focus on the problem of regression: given training data $\{(X_i,Y_i)\}$ drawn from an unknown distribution $P$, we would like to estimate the regression function $\mu_P(x) = \E_P[Y\mid X=x]$.
elements.sent-2411-2671: Given an estimate $\hat{\mu}$ of this unknown function, can we provide a meaningful confidence interval around $\hat{\mu (x)$ that has distribution-free validity? In particular, we might hope to construct a confidence interval for $\mu_P(x)$, whose width is vanishing as the sample size $n$ increases.}
elements.sent-2411-2672: This type of result is standard in more classical settings, such as for parametric models, or even for nonparametric regression with smoothness assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-2673: In the distribution-free setting, however, our ability to achieve vanishing interval widths will vary widely in different contexts: we will show that constructing such intervals is possible when the covariate $X$ is discrete, but impossible when it is nonatomic.
elements.sent-2411-2674: This conclusion is qualitatively similar to the results obtained Chapter~\ref{chapter:conditional , where we showed that test-conditional coverage for predictive inference is straightforward in the case where $X$ is discrete but impossible when $X$ is nonatomic.}
elements.sent-2411-2675: This similarity is not coincidental; towards the end of this chapter, we will see that these two problems---inference for regression, and test-conditional predictive inference---are related on a fundamental level.
elements.sent-2411-2676: Throughout this chapter, we will assume the available data is given by $(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_n,Y_n)\iidsim P$ for some unknown distribution $P$ on $\cX\times\cY$, where $\cY\subseteq\R$.
elements.sent-2411-2677: Our goal is to provide distribution-free confidence intervals on the regression function, $\mu_P(x) = \E_P[Y \mid X=x]$, for all $x$.
elements.sent-2411-2678: More formally, let $\cC$ be trained on data $(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_n,Y_n)$, with $\cC(x)\subseteq\cY$.
elements.sent-2411-2679: We say that $\cC$ is a distribution-free confidence interval for regression at level $1-\alpha$ if \begin{equation}\label{eqn:DF_confidence_regression} \P(\mu_P(X_{n+1})\in\cC(X_{n+1}))\geq 1-\alpha\textnormal{ for any distribution $P$ on $\cX\times\cY$,}\end{equation} where the probability is calculated with respect to $(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )\iidsim P$.}}
elements.sent-2411-2680: Note that while we refer to $\cC$ as a `confidence interval', it may not necessarily return sets that are intervals---that is, $\cC(X_{n+1 )\subseteq\cY$ might be a region comprised of multiple disconnected intervals.}
elements.sent-2411-2681: A key question is whether~\eqref{eqn:DF_confidence_regression can be achieved while still producing a confidence interval with vanishing width.}
elements.sent-2411-2682: We phrase this question informally as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-2683: For i.i.d.\ data in $\cX\times\cY$, is there any $\cC$ that satisfies distribution-free validity~\eqref{eqn:DF_confidence_regression , and also satisfies $\E[\textnormal{Leb (\cC(X_{n+1 ))]\to0$ for `nice' distributions $P$?.}}}
elements.sent-2411-2684: Recall from our discussion at the beginning of this chapter that in classical settings, a confidence interval for $\mu_P(x)$ can have vanishing width as $n\to\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-2685: Our key question asks if it is possible to achieve the informativeness of these classical constructions while simultaneously achieving distribution-free validity.
elements.sent-2411-2686: This section shows that distribution-free inference for the regression function $\mu_P(x)$ is meaningful only in the setting where the response $Y$ is bounded.
elements.sent-2411-2687: In an unbounded case (say, $\cY=\R$), a distribution-free method cannot even return finite-length confidence intervals for $\mu_P(x)$, or indeed, even for the marginal mean of $Y$ (i.e., ignoring covariates $X$).
elements.sent-2411-2688: We will justify this claim via the following classical result:.
elements.sent-2411-2690: Suppose $\cC$ is a distribution-free confidence interval for the mean, i.e., $\cC$ satisfies \begin{equation}\P(\E_P[Y] \in \cC) \geq 1-\alpha \textnormal{ for any distribution $P$ on $\cY$ with finite mean},\end{equation} where the probability is calculated with respect to $Y_1,\dots,Y_n\iidsim P$.
elements.formula-2411-720: \begin{equation}\P(\E_P[Y] \in \cC) \geq 1-\alpha \textnormal{ for any distribution $P$ on $\cY$ with finite mean},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-2691: Then it must hold that \begin{equation}\P( y \in \cC) \geq 1-\alpha \textnormal{ for any distribution $P$ on $\cY$ with finite mean and for any $y\in\R$.}\end{equation} In particular, regardless of the distribution of the data, $\cC$ has infinite expected length (if $\alpha<1$): \begin{equation}\E[\textnormal{Leb}(\cC)]=\infty.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-721: \begin{equation}\P( y \in \cC) \geq 1-\alpha \textnormal{ for any distribution $P$ on $\cY$ with finite mean and for any $y\in\R$.}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-2692: In other words, if $\cY$ is unbounded, then distribution-free inference is impossible even for the marginal mean, $\E_P[Y]$.
elements.sent-2411-2693: Of course, the marginal mean $\E_P[Y]$ is a strictly easier target than the conditional mean (i.e., the regression function $\mu_P(x)$).
elements.sent-2411-2694: Thus, based on the results of this theorem, studying distribution-free inference for the regression function $\mu_P$ has the potential to be meaningful only for a bounded response space $\cY$.
elements.sent-2411-2695: This has implications for both positive and negative results.
elements.sent-2411-2696: For positive results, constructing methods that satisfy distribution-free validity as in~\eqref{eqn:DF_confidence_regression will inevitably require a bounded $\cY$.}
elements.sent-2411-2697: On the other hand, establishing hardness results (i.e., proving that distribution-free validity~\eqref{eqn:DF_confidence_regression leads inevitably to wide confidence intervals) is interesting only for a bounded $\cY$---if $\cY$ is unbounded, the theorem above already establishes the impossibility of distribution-free inference.}
elements.sent-2411-2698: Consequently, for the remainder of this chapter, we will restrict our attention to the setting of a bounded $\cY\subseteq\R$---for instance, $\cY=[0,1]$ or $\cY=\{0,1\}$.
elements.sent-2411-2699: Without loss of generality consider some $y\geq \E_P[Y]$.
elements.sent-2411-2702: First, since $\cY$ is unbounded, we can find some value \begin{equation}y'\in\cY\textnormal{ such that }y' \geq \frac{y - (1-\epsilon)\E_P[Y]}{\epsilon}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-2703: Now define a mixture distribution $P'$ on $\cY$ as \begin{equation}P' = (1-\epsilon')\cdot P + \epsilon'\cdot \delta_{y'},\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\epsilon' = \frac{y - \E_P[Y]}{y' - \E_P[Y]}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-2704: Note that, by construction, we have $\dtv(P,P')\leq\epsilon'\leq\epsilon$, and $\E_{P' [Y] = y$.}
elements.sent-2411-2705: The validity of $\cC$ as a confidence interval for the mean implies \begin{equation}\P_{(P')^n}(y\in\cC) = \P_{(P')^n}(\E_{P'}[Y]\in\cC) \geq 1-\alpha.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-2706: But we can also calculate \begin{equation}\P_{P^n}(y\in\cC) \geq \P_{(P')^n}(y\in\cC) -\dtv(P^n,(P')^n) \geq \P_{(P')^n}(y\in\cC) - n\epsilon' \geq 1-\alpha - n\epsilon.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-2707: Since $\epsilon>0$ can be taken to be arbitrarily small, this completes the proof of the first claim.
elements.sent-2411-2708: Finally, we calculate \begin{equation}\E_{P^n}[\textnormal{Leb}(\cC)] = \E_{P^n}\left[\int_{y\in\R}\ind{y\in\cC}\;dy\right]=\int_{y\in\R}\P_{P^n}(y\in\cC)\;dy\geq \int_{y\in\R} (1-\alpha)\;dy = \infty,\end{equation} where the second step holds by the Fubini--Tonelli theorem.
elements.sent-2411-2709: We are now ready to consider the question of inference on the regression function, $\mu_P(x) = \E_P[Y\mid X=x]$.
elements.sent-2411-2710: We first consider the discrete setting.

elements.subsection-2411-052: Comparing regression intervals and prediction intervals
elements.sent-2411-2734: To move towards the above aim, we begin with a surprising connection: any distribution-free confidence interval for $\mu_P(X_{n+1 )$ must be at least as wide as a distribution-free prediction interval for $Y_{n+1}$.}

elements.sent-2411-2800: With this construction in place, we now verify the bound on the difference in means.. Define \begin{equation}\mu_0 = \E_{P_0}[Y], \quad \mu_1 = \E_{P_1}[Y],\quad \mu_P = \E_P[Y] = \frac{\mu_0+\mu_1}{2}.\end{equation}.

Similarly, \begin{equation}\textnormal{Var}_{P_1}(Y) \leq (1-\mu_1)\big(\mu_1 - \textnormal{Med}_P\big) = (1-\mu_1)\big(\mu_P - \textnormal{Med}_P\big) + \frac{1}{2}(1-\mu_1)(\mu_1-\mu_0).\end{equation}.}

elements.section-2411-054: Relaxing the target for the continuous case
elements.sent-2411-2806: As we have now seen, in the setting where the feature $X$ has a nonatomic distribution, it is impossible to provide vanishing-width distribution-free inference on $\mu_P(X)$ in the original sense of~\eqref{eqn:DF_confidence_regression .}
elements.sent-2411-2807: But can we modify the definition of validity to enable a more informative distribution-free inference procedure? In this section, we will consider several possible relaxations.
elements.sent-2411-2808: We first consider a binning-based approach that effectively converts our question from a continuous problem to a discrete problem.
elements.sent-2411-2810: For each $k\in[K]$, we define a new target, \begin{equation}\mu_P(\cX_k) = \E[Y\mid X\in\cX_k],\end{equation} which measures the conditional expectation of $Y$ when $X$ lies in the bin $\cX_k$, under the unknown distribution $P$.
elements.sent-2411-2811: Defining $k(x)\in[K]$ as the index of the bin containing $x$, we can then relax the original notion of valid coverage~\eqref{eqn:DF_confidence_regression to the following: we aim to provide a confidence interval $\cC(\cX_k)$ for $\mu_P(\cX_k)$ satisfying \begin{equation}\label{eqn:DF_confidence_regression_binned}\P\big(\mu_P(\cX_{k(X_{n+1})}) \in \cC(\cX_{k(X_{n+1})})\big) \geq 1-\alpha.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-2812: That is, we replace the original goal (coverage of $\mu_P(X)$, the mean of $Y$ given $X$) with its binning-based relaxation (coverage of $\mu_P(\cX_{k(X) )$, the mean of $Y$ given only partial information about $X$---namely, the bin to which $X$ belongs).}
elements.sent-2411-2813: We now construct a distribution-free confidence interval for this relaxed target.
elements.sent-2411-2814: Let $P$ be a distribution on $\cX\times\cY$, where $\cY \subseteq [a,b]$, and let $\alpha\in[0,1]$.
elements.sent-2411-2815: Let $\cX = \cX_1\cup\dots\cup\cX_K$ be a fixed partition.. Let $(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_n,Y_n)\iidsim P$.
elements.sent-2411-2816: For each $k\in[K]$, define $n_k =\sum_{i=1 ^n\ind{X_i \in\cX_k}$, and let \begin{equation}\hat{\mu}(\cX_k) = \frac{1}{n_k}\sum_{i=1}^n Y_i \cdot\ind{X_i \in\cX_k}\end{equation} for each $k$ with $n_k\geq 1$, i.e., the mean response observed among all data points with $X_i \in\cX_k$.}
elements.sent-2411-2818: Then $\mu_P(\cX_k) = \mu_{\tilde{P (k)$, by construction, meaning that the relaxed inference target~\eqref{eqn:DF_confidence_regression_binned for $P$ is exactly equivalent to the original aim~\eqref{eqn:DF_confidence_regression for $\tilde{P}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-2819: The result then follows by applying Theorem~\ref{thm:DF_regression_discrete for the distribution $\tilde{P}$, which has a discrete covariate.}
elements.sent-2411-2820: We can observe a parallel between the ideas explored here for inference on $\mu_P$, and the results of Section~\ref{sec:test-conditional-binning , where we studied a binning-based relaxation for the problem of test-conditional predictive inference: in both cases, we circumvent an impossibility result (for continuous $X$) by relaxing the problem via binning (effectively moving to the setting of a discrete $X$).}
elements.sent-2411-2821: The connection between these two problems will be discussed further in Section~\ref{sec:regression_vs_conditional_prediction .}
elements.sent-2411-2822: The previous method, defined in Theorem~\ref{thm:DF_regression_binned , replaces the target $\mu_P(x)$ with its binned version, $\mu_P(\cX_k)$, in order to make the inference problem easier.}
elements.sent-2411-2823: We next consider a more general version of this approach.
elements.sent-2411-2824: Fix a function $H:\cX\times\cX\to\R_+$, with large values of $H(x,x')$ indicating that $x,x'\in\cX$ are similar, or lie nearby to each other.
elements.sent-2411-2825: As in Chapter~\ref{chapter:weighted-conformal (recall the localized conformal prediction method introduced in Section~\ref{sec:localized ), we refer to $H$ as a localization kernel, and can think of a Gaussian kernel, $H(x,x') = \exp\{-\|x-x'\|^2_2/2h^2\}$, as a canonical example.}}
elements.sent-2411-2826: Next, define $\tilde\mu_P(x)$ as an approximation to $\mu_P(x)$, \begin{equation}\label{eq:define_blurred_regression}\tilde\mu_P(x) = \frac{\E_P[\mu_P(X) \cdot H(x,X)]}{\E_P[H(x,X)]}\end{equation} (implicitly, we assume $\E_P[H(x,X)]>0$ for all $x\in\cX$).
elements.sent-2411-2827: In particular, the binned mean $\mu_P(\cX_k)$ can be viewed as a special case: by defining $H(x,x') = \sum_{k=1 ^K \ind{x,x'\in\cX_k}$ (that is, $H(x,x')=1$ whenever $x$ and $x'$ lie in the same bin), we obtain $\tilde\mu_P(x) = \mu_P(\cX_k)$ for any $x\in\cX_k$.}
elements.sent-2411-2828: Intuitively, if $x\mapsto \mu_P(x)$ is reasonably smooth, then we should expect $\tilde\mu_P(x)\approx \mu_P(x)$, as long as the kernel $H$ is reasonably strongly localized---for instance, a Gaussian kernel with a small bandwidth $h>0$.
elements.sent-2411-2829: We will now see that we can perform inference on $\tilde\mu_P(x)$ regardless of the properties of the underlying distribution.
elements.sent-2411-2830: Let $P$ be a distribution on $\cX\times\cY$, where $\cY\subseteq[a,b]$, and let $\alpha\in[0,1]$.
elements.sent-2411-2831: Let $H:\cX\times\cX\to[0,B]$ be a function satisfying $\E_P[H(x,X)]>0$ for all $x\in\cX$.. Let $(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_n,Y_n)\iidsim P$.
elements.sent-2411-2832: Let $U_1,\dots,U_n\iidsim \textnormal{Unif [0,1]$ be drawn independently of the data.}
elements.sent-2411-2833: For each $x\in\cX$, define $n(x) = \sum_{i=1 ^n \ind{U_i \leq \frac{H(x,X_i) {B }$, and let \begin{equation}\hat\mu(x) = \frac{1}{n(x)}\sum_{i=1}^n Y_i \cdot \ind{U_i \leq \frac{H(x,X_i)}{B}}\end{equation} if $n(x)\geq 1$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-2834: Define \begin{equation}\cC(x) =  \hat{\mu}(x)\pm (b-a)\sqrt{\frac{\log(2/\alpha)}{2n(x)}}, \textnormal{ if $n(x)\geq 1$}, [a,b], \textnormal{ if $n(x)=0$.}\end{equation} Then $\cC$ satisfies \begin{equation}\P(\tilde\mu_P(x)\in\cC(x))\geq 1-\alpha\end{equation} for every $x\in\cX$, where $\tilde\mu_P(x)$ is defined as in~\eqref{eq:define_blurred_regression , and where the probability is taken with respect to the draw of both the data points $(X_i,Y_i)$ and the auxiliary random variables $U_i$.}
elements.sent-2411-2835: The confidence interval $\cC$ defined in Theorem~\ref{thm:DF_regression_blurred uses auxiliary randomness coming from the uniform random variables $U_i$.}
elements.sent-2411-2836: This specific construction allows for a clean and simple finite-sample result, but alternative definitions that achieve finite-sample validity without randomization (and that may make more efficient use of the available data) are also possible; see the bibliographic notes at the end of this chapter for details.
elements.sent-2411-2837: Let $P_X$ and $P_{Y\mid X}$ denote the marginal distribution of $X$ and the conditional distribution of $Y\mid X$, respectively, under $P$.
elements.sent-2411-2838: Recalling the covariate shift setting studied in Section~\ref{sec:covariate-shift , define a distribution \begin{equation}Q = Q_X\times P_{Y\mid X},\end{equation} where $Q_X$ is the distribution on $\cX$ defined by the Radon--Nikodym derivative \begin{equation}\frac{dQ_X}{dP_X}(x',y) \propto H(x,x').\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-2839: Moreover, writing $Q_Y$ to denote the marginal of $Y$ under the joint distribution $Q$, we can calculate its mean as \begin{equation}\mu_{Q_Y} = \E_Q[Y] =\E_P\left[Y \cdot \frac{dQ}{dP}(X,Y)\right] =\E_P\left[Y \cdot \frac{H(x,X)}{\E_P[H(x,X)]}\right] =\E_P\left[ \mu_P(X) \cdot \frac{H(x,X)}{\E_P[H(x,X)]}\right] =\tilde\mu_P(x).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-2840: Next, let $\mathcal{I (x) = \{i\in[n]: U_i\leq \frac{H(x,X_i) {B}\}$, and note that $n(x) = |\mathcal{I (x)|$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-2841: Then the data points $\big((X_i,Y_i)\big)_{i\in\mathcal{I (x)}$ can be viewed as the output of rejection sampling, when run on available data sampled from the distribution $P$, and when the target distribution is given by $Q$.}
elements.sent-2411-2842: More specifically, conditioning on $n(x)$, these data points are distributed as $n(x)$ many i.i.d.\ draws from $Q$.
elements.sent-2411-2843: In particular, this means that the corresponding response values, $(Y_i)_{i\in \mathcal{I (x)}$, are distributed as $n(x)$ many i.i.d.\ draws from $Q_Y$.}
elements.sent-2411-2844: Therefore, conditional on $n(x)$ (and on the event $n(x)\geq 1$), the random variable $\hat\mu(x)$ is the sample mean of $n(x)$ many i.i.d.\ draws from $Q_Y$, which is a distribution supported on $[a,b]$.
elements.sent-2411-2845: By Hoeffding's inequality, therefore, $\cC(x)$ provides a confidence interval for $\mu_{Q_Y = \tilde\mu_P(x)$ at level $1-\alpha$.}
elements.sent-2411-2846: To close this section, we point to another direction for inference that may be more tractable than confidence intervals for the regression function.
elements.sent-2411-2847: Suppose we have computed an estimate $\hat\mu$ of the conditional mean function $\mu_P(x) = \E_P[Y\mid X=x]$, and would like to assess the accuracy of our estimate.
elements.sent-2411-2848: A different formulation of this is calibration, which quantifies how close the conditional mean $\E_P[Y\mid \hat\mu(X)]$ is to $\hat \mu(X)$.
elements.sent-2411-2849: Since the conditioning is now over a coarser event, we will see that this problem is sometimes statistically easier than inference on $\mu_P$.
elements.sent-2411-2850: We will discuss distribution-free results for calibration in Chapter~\ref{chapter:calibration .}
elements.sent-2411-2851: The results developed in this chapter bear strong resemblance to our findings for an earlier question: that of test-conditional coverage for predictive inference, in Chapter~\ref{chapter:conditional .}
elements.sent-2411-2852: In particular, for both problems, we have seen that the discrete case offers a straightforward solution, while the continuous case (or, more precisely, the case where $X$ is nonatomic) faces a fundamental hardness result that prohibits meaningful solutions.

elements.sent-2411-2854: In this section, we will examine the way in which these two problems are actually essentially equivalent.

elements.sent-2411-2876: Throughout this chapter, we have focused on the question of inference on $\mu_P$---and more specifically, a particular version of this question, where our aim is to construct a confidence interval $\cC(x)$ for $\mu_P(x)$.

In this section, we will examine this alternative formulation of the question of distribution-free inference, based on an estimator $\hat\mu$ for $\mu_P$.
elements.sent-2411-2878: To make this concrete, consider the following definition of distribution-free validity: for any distribution $P$, \begin{equation}\label{eqn:DF_validity_regression_L1}\P\left(\|\hat\mu - \mu_P\|_{L_1(P)} \leq \hat\epsilon\right)\geq 1-\delta,\end{equation} where the probability is taken with respect to the distribution of the training data, $(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_n,Y_n)\iidsim P$, and where $\hat\epsilon>0$ is a function of the training data intended to express our uncertainty around the estimated regression function $\hat\mu$.
elements.sent-2411-2879: At a high level, any solution for one of these inference problems can be leveraged to provide a solution to the other.
