elements.sent-2411-2932: We next consider calibration, a property that measures the reliability of a model $f(X)$ for $Y$.
elements.sent-2411-2933: We will restrict our attention to the setting where the response is binary, $Y\in\{0,1\}$, and so $f(X)$ is an estimate of the conditional probability of a positive label, $\P(Y=1\mid X)$.
elements.sent-2411-2934: Generally speaking, calibration requires that the estimate satisfies $f(X)\approx \P(Y=1\mid f(X))$.
elements.sent-2411-2935: For example, if we consider all data points for which $f(X)=20\%$ (i.e., the model estimates a 20\% probability of a positive label), then approximately $20\%$ of those cases should have $Y=1$, and so on.
elements.sent-2411-2936: In this chapter, we discuss distribution-free calibration guarantees, showing that they are possible in certain regimes but impossible in others.
elements.sent-2411-2937: This connects to previous chapters in two ways.
elements.sent-2411-2938: First, at a thematic level, calibration is a counterpart to conformal prediction, in that it is used to assess the quality of predictions from machine learning models and thus provide uncertainty quantification.
elements.sent-2411-2939: Indeed, at present, calibration approaches such as temperature scaling and isotonic regression are more widely used in practice than conformal prediction, raising the question of how these approaches compare.
elements.sent-2411-2940: Second, at a technical level, calibration is a different, interesting statistical problem, and it is worthwhile to ask what can be achieved with no assumptions other than access to i.i.d.. samples.
elements.sent-2411-2941: We have seen that marginally valid prediction intervals are possible distribution-free; in contrast, conditionally valid prediction intervals, and confidence intervals for the mean function, are generally not.
elements.sent-2411-2942: This leads to the question of whether calibration, which is related to these problems, is possible to achieve distribution-free.
elements.sent-2411-2943: We turn to this question next.
elements.sent-2411-2944: Consider a random variable $(X,Y)$ drawn from a distribution $P$ on $\cX \times \{0,1\}$.
elements.sent-2411-2945: Suppose we have a function $f : \cX \to [0,1]$ that is an estimate of the probability of a positive label, $Y=1$, based on the features $X$.
elements.sent-2411-2946: One basic notion of validity for $f$ is calibration, defined as follows.
elements.sent-2411-2947: This is an appealing basic property.
elements.sent-2411-2948: For instance, one example of a perfectly calibrated model is the oracle model that outputs the true conditional probabilities: $f(x) = \P(Y = 1 \mid X = x)$.
elements.sent-2411-2949: Note that many other models also satisfy perfect calibration, for example, the constant function $f(x) = \E[Y]$.
elements.sent-2411-2950: The latter example illustrates the fact that while calibration is a desirable property, it does not imply that $f$ is an accurate estimate of the true regression function for $Y\mid X$.
elements.sent-2411-2951: Notice that this definition is with respect to the distribution $P$, but in practical settings, we would only have access to a finite sample of data drawn from $P$.
elements.sent-2411-2952: As such, we wish to have procedures that use the available data to address the following two goals:.
elements.sent-2411-2953: Given a pretrained model $f$, we wish to check whether perfect calibration holds, and to quantify violations of calibration.
elements.sent-2411-2954: We wish to construct a function $f$, or modify a given pretrained function $f$, to satisfy or approximately satisfy perfect calibration.
elements.sent-2411-2955: The majority of this chapter will be aimed at these goals.
elements.sent-2411-2956: With respect to goal (b) above, we will focus on post-hoc calibration, a widely-used technique in applied machine learning.
elements.sent-2411-2957: We start with a pretrained model $f : \cX \to [0,1]$, and a sample of $n$ data points, $(X_1, Y_1),\dots,(X_n,Y_n)\iidsim P$ that were not used for model training.
elements.sent-2411-2958: As with split conformal prediction, we call this the calibration set.
elements.sent-2411-2959: Then, we seek to post-process the outputs of $f$ via a function $h : [0,1] \to [0,1]$ so that the composition $h \circ f$ (approximately) satisfies calibration.
elements.sent-2411-2960: Throughout this section, we will discuss algorithms that take as input a function $f$ and calibration data and output such an $h$.
elements.sent-2411-2961: To begin, we introduce three commonly used post-hoc calibration algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-2962: Each algorithm uses the observed data to select the function $h$ from some class of functions $\cH$, where the choice of this class varies across the three methods.
elements.figure-2411-037: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/diagrams/calibration.pdf} \caption{Visualization of three regressions with varying degrees of calibration. In each plot, the gray dotted line represents the true conditional probability $\P_P(Y=1\mid X=x)$, the dots represent observed values of $Y$, and the yellow line represents a fitted model $f(X)$. The left plot shows a poorly calibrated $f$. The middle plot shows a model $f(x)$ with that is an accurate estimate of $\P_P(Y=1\mid X=x)$ and consequently has good calibration. Finally, the right plot shows a binned regression with high error (i.e., $f(x)$ is not an accurate estimate of $\P_P(Y=1\mid X=x)$), but nonetheless good calibration: for each value $t$ that occurs in the output of $f(x)$, we can see that $\P_P(Y=1\mid f(X)=t)\approx t$.} \label{fig:calibration}
elements.sent-2411-2963: A natural strategy is to bin the values of $f(X_i)$ into $K$ bins, and then use $h$ to adjust the output value for each bin.
elements.sent-2411-2964: Concretely, let $[0,1]=B_1\cup\dots\cup B_K$ be a partition.
elements.sent-2411-2965: Let $n_k = \sum_{i=1 ^n \ind{f(X_i) \in B_k}$ be the number of calibration points in bin $k$, and for any $z \in [0,1]$, let $k(z)$ be the index of the bin containing $z$.}
elements.sent-2411-2966: Then, we define $h$ as \begin{equation}\label{eq:binning_h} \hat h(z) = \frac{1}{n_{k(z)}} \sum_{i=1}^n Y_i\cdot \ind{f(X_i) \in B_{k(z)}}\end{equation} whenever $n_{k(z) \geq 1$, and $\hat h(z) = 1/2$ otherwise.}
elements.sent-2411-2967: That is, for points with $f(x) \in B_k$, the function $\hat h \circ f$ returns the frequency of $Y=1$ for points in that bin based on the calibration data (or $1/2$ if there were no observations in that bin).
elements.sent-2411-2968: This is equivalent to minimizing the squared error of $h \circ f$ on the calibration data: \begin{equation}\label{eq:calibration_se_minimizer} \hat h = \arg \min_{h \in \cH} \sum_{i=1}^n \left([h \circ f](X_i) - Y_i\right)^2,\end{equation} where we choose the function class as $\cH = \cH_{\textnormal{bin }$, the set of functions that are constant within each bin $B_k$.}
elements.sent-2411-2969: This has the advantage that as the number of bins grows, the function class $\cH_{\textnormal{bin }$ is larger and more flexible.}
elements.sent-2411-2970: However, with a large number of bins, more data to is required to fit the function precisely.
elements.sent-2411-2971: Next, we consider isotonic regression, an alternative approach that avoids the need to specify bins, and is more commonly used than binning.
elements.sent-2411-2972: Here, we restrict the post-hoc calibration function to lie in $\cH=\cH_{\textnormal{iso }$, the set of all nondecreasing functions.}
elements.sent-2411-2973: Restricting $h$ to be nondecreasing is often natural, since the initial function $f$ is assumed to be a model where larger outputs correspond to higher confidence that $Y=1$.
elements.sent-2411-2974: We then fit $\hat h$ by minimizing the squared error on the calibration data as in~\eqref{eq:calibration_se_minimizer .}
elements.sent-2411-2975: While this approach avoids the specification of bins, it can only produce nondecreasing $h$.
elements.sent-2411-2976: As such, it is neither more nor less general than binning.
elements.sent-2411-2977: However, it requires less calibration data to achieve a stable fit, and it is more commonly used than binning.
elements.sent-2411-2978: We can impose even more structure by taking $\cH$ to be a parametric class.
elements.sent-2411-2979: One such approach is to take $h$ to be a logistic regression of $Y_i$ onto $f(X_i)$, which corresponds to the class of functions $\cH = \cH_{\textnormal{logistic = \{h_{\beta_0,\beta_1 : \beta_0,\beta_1\in\R\}$, where \begin{equation}h_{\beta_0,\beta_1}(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 \textnormal{logit}(z))}},\end{equation} with $\smash{\textnormal{logit (z) = \log(\frac{z {1-z )}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-2980: Notice that $\beta_1\approx 0$ encourages the output probabilities to be similar to each other, while larger (positive or negative) $\beta_1$ leads to more extreme probabilities that differ across the range of values of $f(X)$.
elements.sent-2411-2981: From a statistical perspective, this approach has only two parameters, so it requires less calibration data than the other two approaches.
elements.sent-2411-2982: However, the function class $\cH_{\textnormal{logistic }$ is less flexible, so the resulting fit may not be as well calibrated as other approaches when ample data are available.}
elements.sent-2411-2983: While Definition~\ref{def:perfect_calibration is the natural definition of perfect calibration, in practice we never expect this to hold exactly, and it is necessary to quantify the degree to which calibration is violated---goal (a) above.}
elements.sent-2411-2984: One widely-used measure of the extent to which a function $f$ is miscalibrated is given by the expected calibration error (ECE): \begin{equation}\textnormal{ECE}(f) = \E\Big[\Big|\E[Y \mid f(X)] - f(X)\Big|\Big].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-2985: Notice that this is defined with respect to a probability distribution $P$, although the notation suppresses this.
elements.sent-2411-2986: A closely related measure is the binned ECE: \begin{equation}\label{eq:bin_ece_hat} \textnormal{binECE}(f) = \sum_{k=1}^K\Big|\E[Y \mid f(X) \in B_k] - \E[f(X) \mid f(X) \in B_k] \Big| \cdot \P(f(X) \in B_k),\end{equation} where $[0,1]=B_1\cup\dots\cup B_K$ is some partition of the unit interval.
elements.sent-2411-2987: This quantity has a natural plug-in estimator, \begin{equation}\label{eqn:binECEhat} \widehat{\textnormal{binECE}}(f) = \sum_{k=1}^K\bigg| \frac{1}{n_k} \cdot \sum_{\substack{i\in[n] f(X_i)\in B_k}} \left(Y_i - f(X_i)\right)\bigg| \cdot \frac{n_k}{n},\end{equation} where $n_k = |\{i : f(X_i) \in B_k\}|$ is the number of data points observed in bin $k$.
elements.sent-2411-2988: This estimator is widely used in practice.
elements.sent-2411-2989: However, we will see that binned ECE can be arbitrarily far from ECE, which means that $\textnormal{binECE (f)$ (and its empirical estimate $\widehat{\textnormal{binECE (f)$) should not be viewed as a reliable proxy for $\textnormal{ECE (f)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-2990: An alternative relaxation of perfect calibration is the distance to calibration (dCE), which is the minimum $L_1$ distance between $f$ and a perfectly calibrated function: \begin{equation}\label{eq:dce_def} \textnormal{dCE}(f) = \inf_{\substack{g : \cX \to [0,1] \E[Y\mid g(X)] = g(X)}} \E[|g(X) - f(X)|].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-2991: A critical advantage of dCE (compared to ECE) is that it is possible to give distribution-free confidence bounds for dCE, which we will state formally soon.
elements.section-2411-058: Properties of ECE and binned ECE
elements.sent-2411-2992: We now consider the ECE in more detail.
elements.sent-2411-2993: In an empirical setting when we have access only to a finite amount of data, we will see that ECE is straightforward to work with if $f(X)$ takes only a finite number of values, but faces hardness results if $f(X)$ is continuously distributed.
elements.sent-2411-2994: This arises from a connection with the hardness of distribution-free regression, and we will make this connection explicit later on in this section.
elements.sent-2411-2995: These challenges make binned ECE an appealing alternative to ECE, but we will see that these two measures of miscalibration are fundamentally quite different.
elements.sent-2411-2996: A basic difficulty with the ECE is that it can change by a large amount with only small changes in the function $f$.
elements.sent-2411-2997: We will informally refer to this as the discontinuity of the ECE.
elements.sent-2411-2998: This is illustrated by the following example.
elements.sent-2411-2999: Consider the following distribution $P$ on $(X,Y)$: let $X\sim\textnormal{Unif [0,1]$, and let \begin{equation}\P(Y=1\mid X) =  0, X\in [0,0.25] \cup [0.75,1], 1, X\in(0.25,0.75).\end{equation} Then the constant function $f(x)= 1/2$ is perfectly calibrated.}
elements.sent-2411-3000: On the other hand, the function \begin{equation}f_\epsilon(x) = \frac{1-\epsilon}{2} + \epsilon x,\end{equation} which is arbitrarily close to $f$ when $\epsilon>0$ is small, is highly miscalibrated in terms of its ECE: since $f_\epsilon$ is injective, we have $\E[Y\mid f_\epsilon(X)] = \E[Y\mid X]$, and so \begin{equation}\textnormal{ECE}(f_\epsilon) = \E\left[ \left|\E[Y\mid f_\epsilon(X)] - f_\epsilon(X)\right|\right] = \E\left[\left| \ind{0.25 < X < 0.75} - \left(\frac{1-\epsilon}{2} + \epsilon X\right)\right|\right] = \frac{1}{2}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-3001: This discontinuity is central to the challenges inherent to estimating ECE empirically.
elements.sent-2411-3002: We can therefore consider binned ECE instead, since this discretized measure of calibration error is easier to estimate.
elements.sent-2411-3003: This raises the question of whether binned ECE is a reasonable proxy for ECE.
elements.sent-2411-3004: The following result tells us that binned ECE cannot be larger than ECE:.
elements.sent-2411-3005: For any distribution $P$ on $\cX\times\{0,1\}$, any function $f:\cX\to[0,1]$, and any partition $[0,1]=B_1\cup\dots\cup B_K$, \begin{equation}\textnormal{binECE}(f) \leq \textnormal{ECE}(f).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-3006: In fact, however, this inequality can be extremely loose.
elements.sent-2411-3007: For instance, returning to Example~\ref{example:discontinuity_ECE above, if we choose $K=2$ bins to form the partition $[0,1] = [0,0.5]\cup (0.5,1]$, we can verify that $\textnormal{binECE (f_\epsilon) = \frac{\epsilon {4}$, whereas the ECE is $1/2$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-3008: This illustrates an important point: estimating binned ECE, and observing a small error, does not necessarily imply that ECE is low.
elements.sent-2411-3009: The definition of binned ECE can then equivalently be expressed as \begin{equation}\textnormal{binECE}(f) = \sum_{k=1}^K\Big|\E[Y -f(X) \mid f(X) \in B_k] \Big| \cdot \P(f(X) \in B_k) = \E\Big[ \Big| \E\big[Y - f(X) \mid k(f(X))\big]\Big|\Big].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-3010: The result then follows by Jensen's inequality: \begin{equation}\E\Big[ \Big| \E\big[Y - f(X) \mid k(f(X))\big]\Big|\Big] \leq \E\Big[ \Big| \E\big[Y - f(X) \mid f(X)\big]\Big|\Big] = \textnormal{ECE}(f).\end{equation}.
elements.subsection-2411-061: Can ECE and binned ECE be estimated?
elements.sent-2411-3011: We now turn to the topic of estimating ECE and binned ECE.
elements.sent-2411-3012: More precisely, we are interested in providing upper bounds on these quantities---that is, we would like to use the available data to certify that ECE or binned ECE is low.
elements.sent-2411-3013: We will see that this is possible in general with binned ECE but not ECE.
elements.sent-2411-3014: For binned ECE, it is straightforward to construct a meaningful upper bound, using the estimator given in~\eqref{eqn:binECEhat .}
elements.sent-2411-3015: Fix any sample size $n$ and function $f:\cX\to[0,1]$, and let $[0,1] = B_1\cup\dots\cup B_K$ be a fixed partition.
elements.sent-2411-3017: Then for any distribution $P$ on $\cX\times \{0,1\}$, for any $\delta\in[0,1]$, \begin{equation}\P\left(\widehat{\textnormal{binECE}}(f) \geq \textnormal{binECE}(f) - \sqrt{\frac{2\log(1/\delta)}{n}}\right)\geq 1- \delta,\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\P\left(\widehat{\textnormal{binECE}}(f) \leq \textnormal{binECE}(f) + \sqrt{\frac{K}{n}} + \sqrt{\frac{2\log(1/\delta)}{n}}\right)\geq 1- \delta.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-3018: In particular, the first statement shows that $\widehat{\textnormal{binECE (f) + \sqrt{\frac{2\log(1/\delta) {n }$ provides a distribution-free valid upper confidence bound on the binned ECE---and thus we can view this as a way to certify that binned ECE is low, without any assumptions.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-3019: On the other hand, the second statement tells us that this upper confidence bound is fairly tight (as long as $K\ll n$), so this a meaningful way to check whether a function $f$ has low binned ECE.
elements.sent-2411-3020: Next, we show that the picture is substantially different for ECE.
elements.sent-2411-3021: Any upper confidence bound for ECE that has distribution-free validity can only return uninformative answers for a nonatomic $f(X)$.
elements.sent-2411-3022: Fix any sample size $n$ and function $f:\cX\to[0,1]$.
elements.sent-2411-3023: Let $\widehat{\textnormal{ECE (f)\in[0,1]$ be a function of the observed data $(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_n,Y_n)\in\cX\times\{0,1\}$), satisfying the following distribution-free validity property: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:ece_test_DF_valid} \P\left(\widehat{\textnormal{ECE}}(f) \geq \textnormal{ECE}(f)\right) \geq 1-\delta\textnormal{ for all $P$}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-3024: Then for any distribution $P$ on $\cX\times\{0,1\}$ for which $f(X)$ has a nonatomic distribution, \begin{equation}\P\left(\widehat{\textnormal{ECE}}(f) \geq \E[|Y-f(X)|] \right)\geq1-\delta.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-3025: In words, this means we cannot have a distribution-free upper confidence bound on ECE that shrinks to zero as the sample size grows, for any function $f$ where $f(X)$ is nonatomic.
elements.sent-2411-3026: Notice this result is very similar to Theorem~\ref{thm:regression_nonatomic_eps , which established the impossibility of providing a nontrivial distribution-free upper bound on the error in estimating $\E_P[Y\mid X]$ when $X$ is nonatomic.}
elements.sent-2411-3027: Here, we are instead interested in $\E_P[Y\mid f(X)]$, but if $f(X)$ is nonatomic then this is essentially the same question.
elements.sent-2411-3028: Then for each $k$, conditional on $n_k$ (and assuming $n_k\geq 1$), the quantity \begin{equation}\sum_{\substack{i\in[n] f(X_i)\in B_k}} (Y_i - f(X_i))\end{equation} is a sum of $n_k$ many i.i.d.\ terms, each with mean $\mu_k - \mu^f_k$ and variance $\leq 1$.
elements.sent-2411-3029: Consequently, a straightforward calculation verifies that \begin{equation}n_k |\mu_k - \mu^f_k| \leq \E\left[\left|\sum_{\substack{i\in[n] f(X_i)\in B_k}} (Y_i - f(X_i))\right| \ \bigg| \ n_k\right] \leq n_k|\mu_k - \mu^f_k| + \sqrt{n_k}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-3030:
Therefore, marginalizing over $n_k$, \begin{equation}\E\left[ \widehat{\textnormal{binECE}}(f) \right] = \E\left[\frac{1}{n}\sum_{k=1}^K\Bigg| \sum_{\substack{i\in[n] f(X_i)\in B_k}} \left(Y_i - f(X_i)\right)\Bigg|\right] \geq \E\left[\sum_{k=1}^K \frac{n_k}{n} |\mu_k - \mu^f_k| \right] = \sum_{k=1}^K p_k|\mu_k - \mu^f_k| = \textnormal{binECE}(f) ,\end{equation} since $\E[n_k] = np_k$, and similarly \begin{equation}\E\left[ \widehat{\textnormal{binECE}}(f) \right]\leq \E\left[\sum_{k=1}^K \frac{n_k}{n} |\mu_k - \mu^f_k| + \frac{\sqrt{n_k}}{n}\right] = \textnormal{binECE}(f) + \sum_{k=1}^K \frac{\E[\sqrt{n_k}]}{n}\end{equation}
\begin{equation}\leq \textnormal{binECE}(f) + \sum_{k=1}^K \frac{\sqrt{np_k}}{n}\leq \textnormal{binECE}(f) +\sqrt{\frac{K}{n}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-3031: Finally, we apply a bounded-differences argument: observe that resampling a single data point $(X_i,Y_i)$ can only change $\widehat{\textnormal{binECE (f)$ by at most $\pm \frac{2 {n}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-3032: By McDiarmid's inequality, we therefore have \begin{equation}\P\left( \widehat{\textnormal{binECE}}(f) \geq \E\left[\widehat{\textnormal{binECE}}(f) \right] - \sqrt{\frac{2\log(1/\delta)}{n}}\right)\geq 1-\delta\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\P\left( \widehat{\textnormal{binECE}}(f) \leq \E\left[\widehat{\textnormal{binECE}}(f) \right] + \sqrt{\frac{2\log(1/\delta)}{n}}\right)\geq 1-\delta,\end{equation} which completes the proof.
elements.sent-2411-3033: Fix any $M\geq 1$, let $(X^{(1) ,Y^{(1) ),\dots,(X^{(M) ,Y^{(M) )\iidsim P$, and let $\widehat{P _M$ be the empirical distribution.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-3035: On the event that $f(X^{(1) ),\dots,f(X^{(M) )$ are all distinct (which holds almost surely), we have \begin{equation}E_{\widehat{P}_M}[Y \mid f(X) = f(X^{(i)})] = Y^{(i)}\end{equation} for all $i\in[m]$, and consequently \begin{equation}\textnormal{ECE}_{\widehat{P}_M}(f) = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{i=1}^M |Y^{(i)} - f(X^{(i)})|\end{equation} where we use the subscript to denote that this is the ECE with respect to the distribution $\widehat{P _M$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-3036: Then by the distribution-free validity of our estimator of ECE~\eqref{eqn:ece_test_DF_valid , we have \begin{equation}\P\left(\widehat{\textnormal{ECE}}(f) \geq \frac{1}{M}\sum_{i=1}^M |Y^{(i)} - f(X^{(i)})| \ \middle| \ \widehat{P}_M\right)\geq 1-\delta,\end{equation} where $\widehat{\textnormal{ECE (f)$ is computed using a sample $(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_n,Y_n)\iidsim \widehat{P _M$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-3037: Defining $c_M = \E_P[|Y-f(X)|] - \frac{1 {2\sqrt{M }$, we can then write \begin{equation}\P\big( \widehat{\textnormal{ECE}}(f) > c_M \mid \widehat{P}_M\big) \geq 1-\delta - \ind{\frac{1}{M}\sum_{i=1}^M |Y^{(i)} - f(X^{(i)})| \leq c_M}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-3038: Next, by Lemma~\ref{lem:sample-resample (i.e., by bounding the difference between sampling with and without replacement), after marginalizing over $\widehat{P _M$, we have \begin{equation}\P\big( \widehat{\textnormal{ECE}}(f) > c_M\big) \geq 1-\delta - \P\left(\frac{1}{M}\sum_{i=1}^M |Y^{(i)} - f(X^{(i)})| \leq c_M\right) - \frac{n(n-1)}{2M},\end{equation} where now $\widehat{\textnormal{ECE (f)$ is computed using data $(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_n,Y_n)\iidsim P$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-3039: Since the terms $|Y^{(i) -f(X^{(i) )|\in[0,1]$ are i.i.d., the quantity $\frac{1 {M}\sum_{i=1 ^M |Y^{(i) - f(X^{(i) )|$ has mean $\E_P[|Y-f(X)|]$ and variance $\leq\frac{1 {4M}$, and therefore by Chebyshev's inequality, \begin{equation}\P\left(\frac{1}{M}\sum_{i=1}^M |Y^{(i)} - f(X^{(i)})| \leq c_M\right) \leq \frac{1}{2\sqrt{M}}.\end{equation}. Therefore, \begin{equation}\P\big( \widehat{\textnormal{ECE}}(f) > c_M\big) \geq 1-\delta - \frac{1}{2\sqrt{M}}- \frac{n(n-1)}{2M}.\end{equation}.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-3040: Finally, since $M$ can be taken to be arbitrarily large, and $\lim_{M\to\infty c_M = \E_P[|Y-f(X)|]$, this completes the proof.}
elements.subsection-2411-062: Can ECE be controlled with post-hoc calibration?
elements.sent-2411-3041: The result of Theorem~\ref{thm:ece_hardness_of_testing tells us that, given a trained model $f$, we cannot meaningfully estimate its ECE---unless $f$ returns outputs in a discrete space (i.e., $f(X)$ is effectively performing binning).}
elements.sent-2411-3042: In particular this means that a continuous function $f$, which may return a continuously-distributed output $f(X)$, generally cannot be certified to have low ECE.
elements.sent-2411-3043: But this does not exclude another possibility: instead of testing whether $\textnormal{ECE (f)$ is low, can we instead perform post-hoc calibration so that the resulting output is now guaranteed to have low ECE? We will now see that the same types of challenges arise for this alternative version of the question.}
elements.sent-2411-3044: If post-hoc calibration is performed via binning, as in~\eqref{eq:binning_h , then we can ensure low ECE since we are now in a discrete case.}
elements.sent-2411-3045: On the other hand, an injective post-hoc calibration (such as temperature scaling) cannot result in a guarantee of low ECE in the continuous setting.
elements.sent-2411-3046: We begin with a positive result showing distribution-free ECE is possible via the binning algorithm~\eqref{eq:binning_h .}
elements.sent-2411-3047: For each bin $B_k$, we compute the empirical mean of $Y$ when $f(X)$ takes values in $B_k$, and we use this as the value of $\hat h \circ f$.
elements.sent-2411-3048: The approach will result in approximate calibration, with the approximation quality improving with the number of points per bin (i.e., as the sample size grows).
elements.sent-2411-3049: Let $\hat h$ be the binning post-hoc adjustment as in~\eqref{eq:binning_h .}
elements.sent-2411-3050: Then \begin{equation}\E\left[\textnormal{ECE}(\hat h \circ f)\right] \le \sqrt{K / 2n},\end{equation} and moreover, for any $\delta\in[0,1]$, \begin{equation}\P\left(\textnormal{ECE}(\hat h \circ f)\leq \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\delta}}\cdot \sqrt{\frac{K}{n}}\right) \geq 1-\delta.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-3051: The bound in expectation follows from a similar calculation as in the proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:DF_regression_discrete_eps , and we omit the details.}
elements.sent-2411-3052: By construction, the binning procedure coarsens the information coming from $f$, since $\hat{h \circ f$ takes only finitely many values.}
elements.sent-2411-3053: Intuitively, this means that binning loses some of the information present in $f$.
elements.sent-2411-3054: To avoid this loss of information, we can instead ask whether we can use a post-hoc calibration procedure that returns an injective function $\hat{h _n$.}
elements.sent-2411-3055: However, we will now see that this goal is incompatible with distribution-free validity: if $f(X)$ is nonatomic, then requiring a distribution-free guarantee on ECE will inevitably prohibit the post-hoc calibration function $\hat{h _n$ from being injective.}
elements.sent-2411-3056: In particular, this implies that strategies such as temperature scaling are intrinsically unable to provide a distribution-free ECE guarantee.
elements.sent-2411-3057: Consider any post-hoc calibration procedure that satisfies a distribution-free guarantee on ECE, \begin{equation}\P_{P^n}\left(\textnormal{ECE}\big(\hat{h}_n\circ f\big)\leq \epsilon\right) \geq 1- \delta \textnormal{ \ for all $P$}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-3058: Then, for any distribution $P$ on $\cX\times\{0,1\}$ for which $f(X)$ has a nonatomic distribution, \begin{equation}\textnormal{If $\epsilon < \E_P[\textnormal{Var}_P(Y\mid X)]$ then }\P_{P^n}\left(\textnormal{$\hat{h}_n$ is an injective function}\right) \leq \delta.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-843: \begin{equation}\textnormal{If $\epsilon < \E_P[\textnormal{Var}_P(Y\mid X)]$ then }\P_{P^n}\left(\textnormal{$\hat{h}_n$ is an injective function}\right) \leq \delta.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-3059: Note that this bound on $\epsilon$ is not vanishing with $n$.
elements.sent-2411-3060: In other words, regardless of the sample size $n$, we cannot ensure that ECE will be very low, with any injective post-hoc calibration procedure.
elements.sent-2411-3061: The key idea of this proof is to connect the present aim, distribution-free calibration, with the problem of distribution-free regression as studied in Chapter~\ref{chapter:regression .}
elements.sent-2411-3062: In particular, a distribution-free guarantee on ECE implies that we are able to perform distribution-free inference on the regression of $Y$ onto the post-hoc calibrated predictor $\hat{h _n(f(X))$, i.e., inference on $\E[Y\mid \hat{h _n(f(X))]$---but from the results of Chapter~\ref{chapter:regression , we know that this is impossible in settings where $\hat{h _n(f(X))$ is nonatomic.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-3063: Let $\mu_{\tilde{P }$ be the regression function for this distribution, i.e., \begin{equation}\mu_{\tilde{P}}(f(X)) = \E_P[Y\mid f(X)]\end{equation} almost surely.}
elements.sent-2411-3065: Then $h(f(X))$ contains the same information as $f(X)$, and so \begin{equation}\mu_{\tilde{P}}(f(X)) = \E_P[Y\mid h(f(X))]\end{equation} almost surely.
elements.sent-2411-3066: In particular, \begin{equation}\textnormal{ECE}(h\circ f) = \E_P\left[\left| \E_P\left[Y\mid h(f(X))\right] - h(f(X))\right|\right] = \E_P\left[\left|\mu_{\tilde{P}}(f(X)) - h(f(X))\right|\right]= \|h - \mu_{\tilde{P}}\|_{L_1(\tilde{P})},\end{equation} where the $L_1(\tilde{P )$ norm follows the notation of Section~\ref{sec:regression__C_or_muhat .}}

elements.sent-2411-3073: We next turn to studying the properties of dCE.
elements.sent-2411-3074: As we will see in this section, unlike ECE, it is possible to estimate dCE empirically in a meaningful way.
elements.sent-2411-3075: Before considering the problem of estimation, we first compare dCE and ECE.
elements.sent-2411-3076: Our next result shows that dCE is always bounded by ECE---we can interpret this as saying that dCE is a strictly weaker notion of calibration than ECE.
elements.sent-2411-3077: For any distribution $P$ on $\cX\times\{0,1\}$ and any function $f: \cX \to [0,1]$, $\textnormal{dCE (f) \le \textnormal{ECE (f)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-3078: One such function $g$ is the true conditional mean, $g^*(x) = \E[Y \mid f(X) = f(x)]$.
elements.sent-2411-3079: With this choice, we have \begin{equation}\textnormal{dCE}(f) \leq \E[|g^*(X)-f(X)|] = \E\left[\left|\E[Y\mid f(X)] - f(X)\right|\right] = \textnormal{ECE}(f).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-3080: The inequality in this bound can sometimes be extremely loose, as in the following example.
elements.sent-2411-3081: Returning to the setting of Example~\ref{example:discontinuity_ECE , recall that the constant function $f(x) = 1/2$ is a perfectly calibrated, while $f_\epsilon(x) = \frac{1-\epsilon {2 + \epsilon x$ is a small perturbation with high ECE.}}}
elements.sent-2411-3082: On the other hand, for dCE, we have \begin{equation}\textnormal{dCE}(f_\epsilon) \leq \E[|f_\epsilon(X) - f(X)|] = \frac{\epsilon}{4}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-3083: In particular, this example highlights that, unlike ECE, the dCE measure of miscalibration is continuous: by definition, for any functions $f$ and $g$, we have \begin{equation}\textnormal{dCE}(f) \leq \textnormal{dCE}(g) + \E[|f(X) - g(X)|].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-3084: That is, dCE is $1$-Lipschitz with respect to the $L_1(P)$ norm on functions.
elements.sent-2411-3085: We now show that dCE can be estimated from data in a distribution-free way.
elements.sent-2411-3086: To define our estimator, we begin by fixing any $K\geq 1$.
elements.sent-2411-3087: Let \begin{equation}\widehat{\textnormal{dCE}}(f) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{k=1}^K \left|\sum_{\substack{i\in [n] f(X_i)\in B_k}} \left(Y_i - \frac{k}{K}\right)\right|,\end{equation} where the bins $B_1,\dots,B_K$ partition $[0,1]$ into equal-length intervals, \begin{equation}[0,1] = \left[0,\frac{1}{K}\right]\cup \left(\frac{1}{K},\frac{2}{K}\right] \cup \dots \cup \left(\frac{K-1}{K},1\right] =: B_1 \cup \dots \cup B_K.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-3088: Then for any distribution $P$ on $\cX\times\{0,1\}$, for any $\delta\in[0,1]$, \begin{equation}\P\left(\widehat{\textnormal{dCE}}(f) + \frac{1}{K} + \sqrt{\frac{2\log(1/\delta)}{n}} \geq \textnormal{dCE}(f) \right) \geq 1-\delta.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-3089: This means we have constructed an upper confidence bound on the dCE of $f$ with distribution-free validity.
elements.sent-2411-3090: That is, if we observe a small estimate $\widehat{\textnormal{dCE (f)$ (and if $K$ and $n$ are both reasonably large), then we can be confident that $\textnormal{dCE (f)$ is low.}}}
elements.sent-2411-3091: Moreover, unlike for ECE, this estimator can be meaningful (i.e., has the potential to return values close to zero) even when $f$ is continuous.
elements.sent-2411-3092: For example, if $f$ is perfectly calibrated (as in Definition~\ref{def:perfect_calibration ), a straightforward calculation shows that $\E[\widehat{\textnormal{dCE (f)] \leq \frac{1 {K +\sqrt{\frac{K {n }$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-3093: By choosing $K$ to satisfy $1\ll K \ll n$, we can therefore construct an estimator of dCE that will successfully certify that an accurate function $f$ has low dCE miscalibration error.
elements.sent-2411-3094: This is in contrast to ECE calibration---Theorem~\ref{thm:ece_hardness_of_testing shows that distribution-free certification of low ECE error is not possible, aside from the case where $f(X)$ is discrete.}
elements.sent-2411-3095: Next, let $\mu_k = \E[Y \mid f(X)\in B_k]$ be the true conditional probability for the $k$th bin, and define $g(x) = \sum_k \mu_k \ind{f(x)\in B_k}$, which is a perfectly calibrated function, by construction.
elements.sent-2411-3096: Following an identical argument as in the proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:binECEhat , we can verify that \begin{equation}\E[|g(X) - \tilde{g}(X)|] \leq \E\left[ \widehat{\textnormal{dCE}}(f)\right] \leq \E[|g(X) - \tilde{g}(X)|] + \sqrt{\frac{K}{n}}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-3097: Moreover, by construction, we have \begin{equation}|\tilde{g}(x) - f(x)| \leq \max_k \sup_{t\in B_k} \left| t -\frac{k}{K}\right|\leq \frac{1}{K},\end{equation} for all $x$, and therefore $\E[|\tilde{g (X)-f(X)|]\leq \frac{1 {K}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-3098: Since $g$ is perfectly calibrated, we therefore have \begin{equation}\textnormal{dCE}(f) \leq \E[|g(X)-f(X)|] \leq \E[|g(X)-\tilde{g}(X)|] + \E[|\tilde{g}(X)-f(X)|] \leq \E[\widehat{\textnormal{dCE}}(f)] + \frac{1}{K}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-3099: To complete the proof, we apply a bounded-differences argument, as in the proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:binECEhat : since resampling a single data point $(X_i,Y_i)$ can only change $\widehat{\textnormal{dECE (f)$ by at most $\pm \frac{2 {n}$, we therefore have \begin{equation}\P\left( \widehat{\textnormal{dECE}}(f) \geq \E\left[\widehat{\textnormal{dECE}}(f) \right] - \sqrt{\frac{2\log(1/\delta)}{n}}\right)\geq 1-\delta.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-3100: This section introduces the Venn--Abers predictor, a post-hoc calibration algorithm that leverages exchangeability in order to achieve its mathematical guarantee.
elements.sent-2411-3101: Unlike previous methods discussed in this chapter, the Venn--Abers predictor outputs an interval-valued prediction of $\P(Y=1 \mid f(X))$.
elements.sent-2411-3102: This interval contains a prediction that is calibrated, in a sense that will be made precise below.
elements.sent-2411-3103: The Venn--Abers predictor adapts post-hoc isotonic regression, as described in Section~\ref{subsec:post-hoc-calibration , by running two isotonic regressions---one on $\cD_{n+1 ^0$ and one on $\cD_{n+1 ^1$, where we recall the notation $\cD_{n+1 ^y = ((X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_n, Y_n), (X_{n+1 , y))$ for any hypothesized test point value $y$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-3104: The method then outputs an interval whose endpoints are given by the predictions at $x=X_{n+1}$ from these two regressions.
elements.sent-2411-3105: The Venn--Abers prediction algorithm is summarized below.. \begin{algbox [Venn--Abers Prediction].}
elements.sent-2411-3106: Input: Calibration data with binary labels $((X_1, Y_1), ..., (X_n, Y_n))$, a test point $X_{n+1}$, and a pre-trained model $f : \cX \to [0,1]$.
elements.sent-2411-3107: For each $y\in\{0,1\}$, perform isotonic regression on the augmented dataset $\cD_{n+1 ^y$, \begin{equation}\hat h^y = \argmin_{h \in \cH_{\textnormal{iso}}} \sum_{i=1}^n \left([h \circ f](X_i) - Y_i\right)^2 + \left([h \circ f](X_{n+1}) - y\right)^2,\end{equation} and return the fitted values, \begin{equation}\hat{p}^y = \big([\hat{h}^y\circ f](X_1),\dots,[\hat{h}^y\circ f](X_{n+1})\big).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-3109: The Venn--Abers predictor enjoys the following distribution-free guarantee:.
elements.sent-2411-3110: Let $(X_1,Y_1), \ldots, (X_{n+1 ,Y_{n+1 )\in\cX\times\{0,1\}$ be exchangeable, and let $f : \cX \to [0,1]$ be a pre-trained model.}}
elements.sent-2411-3111: Let $\hat{p ^0$ and $\hat{p ^1$ be defined as in Algorithm~\ref{alg:Venn--Abers .}}}
elements.sent-2411-3113: Informally, Theorem~\ref{thm:venn--abers is often interpreted as a guarantee on the interval-valued Venn--Abers prediction, $[\hat{p ^0_{n+1 , \hat{p ^1_{n+1 ]$, in the following sense: \begin{center There exists a random variable $W\in\left[\hat{p ^0_{n+1 , \hat{p ^1_{n+1}\right]$ that is perfectly calibrated, i.e., $\E[Y_{n+1}\mid W] = W$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-3114: If the interval is wide, it signifies high uncertainty in our probabilistic prediction.
elements.sent-2411-3115: The procedure's validity hinges on the fact that $Y_{n+1}$ is either equal to $0$ or $1$, and so the true dataset $\cD_{n+1 = \big((X_i,Y_i)\big)_{i\in[n+1]}$ is equal to either $\cD_{n+1 ^0$ or $\cD_{n+1 ^1$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-3116: When we guess $Y_{n+1}\in\{0,1\}$ correctly, the resulting isotonic regression trained on $\cD_{n+1}$ is perfectly calibrated, and therefore one of the endpoints of the resulting interval is perfectly calibrated.
elements.sent-2411-3117: Much like full conformal prediction, the validity of the Venn--Abers predictor follows by combining exchangeability with symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-3118: When we train the isotonic regression on all $n+1$ data points, $\cD_{n+1}$, the resulting output is a symmetric function of the data points, which preserves exchangeability of the probabilistic predictions.
elements.sent-2411-3119: The proof below makes all this precise.
elements.sent-2411-3120: By definition of the isotonic regression problem~\eqref{eq:calibration_se_minimizer (which depends symmetrically on the input data), the data points \begin{equation}\big(([\hat{h}\circ f](X_1),Y_1), \dots, ([\hat{h}\circ f](X_{n+1}),Y_{n+1})\big)\end{equation} are exchangeable.}
elements.sent-2411-3121: We will need to use a deterministic property of isotonic regression, which we state without proof: the fitted values of isotonic regression are piecewise constant, and the value within each bin is equal to the sample mean within the bin, i.e., \begin{equation}\label{eqn:Venn--Abers_iso_piecewise} \textnormal{For some partition $[n+1]=I_1\cup \dots \cup I_M$, \ $[\hat{h}\circ f](X_i) = \bar{Y}_{I_m}$ for all $m\in[M],i\in I_m$,}\end{equation} where $\bar{Y _{I_m = \frac{1 {|I_m|}\sum_{i\in I_m Y_i$ denotes the mean of the $Y$ values indexed by $i\in I_m$.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-866: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:Venn--Abers_iso_piecewise} \textnormal{For some partition $[n+1]=I_1\cup \dots \cup I_M$, \ $[\hat{h}\circ f](X_i) = \bar{Y}_{I_m}$ for all $m\in[M],i\in I_m$,}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-3123: We calculate \begin{equation}\E\left[\left(Y_{n+1} -\hat{p}^{Y_{n+1}}_{n+1} \right) \cdot g(\hat{p}^{Y_{n+1}}_{n+1})\right] =\E\left[\left(Y_{n+1} -[\hat{h}\circ f](X_{n+1})\right) \cdot g\big([\hat{h}\circ f](X_{n+1})\big)\right] =\frac{1}{n+1}\E\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n+1} \left(Y_i - [\hat{h}\circ f](X_i)\right)\cdot g\big([\hat{h}\circ f](X_i)\big)\right] =\frac{1}{n+1}\E\left[\sum_{m=1}^M \sum_{i\in I_m}\left(Y_i - [\hat{h}\circ f](X_i)\right) \cdot g\big([\hat{h}\circ f](X_i)\big)\right] =\frac{1}{n+1}\E\left[\sum_{m=1}^M \sum_{i\in I_m} \left(Y_i - \bar{Y}_{I_m}\right) \cdot g(\bar{Y}_{I_m})\right] =0,\end{equation} where the second step holds by exchangeability, the next-to-last step holds by~\eqref{eqn:Venn--Abers_iso_piecewise , and the last step holds since $\sum_{i\in I_m (Y_i-\bar{Y _{I_m ) = 0$ by definition of $\bar{Y _{I_m}$.}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-867: \begin{equation}\E\left[\left(Y_{n+1} -\hat{p}^{Y_{n+1}}_{n+1} \right) \cdot g(\hat{p}^{Y_{n+1}}_{n+1})\right] =\E\left[\left(Y_{n+1} -[\hat{h}\circ f](X_{n+1})\right) \cdot g\big([\hat{h}\circ f](X_{n+1})\big)\right] =\frac{1}{n+1}\E\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n+1} \left(Y_i - [\hat{h}\circ f](X_i)\right)\cdot g\big([\hat{h}\circ f](X_i)\big)\right] =\frac{1}{n+1}\E\left[\sum_{m=1}^M \sum_{i\in I_m}\left(Y_i - [\hat{h}\circ f](X_i)\right) \cdot g\big([\hat{h}\circ f](X_i)\big)\right] =\frac{1}{n+1}\E\left[\sum_{m=1}^M \sum_{i\in I_m} \left(Y_i - \bar{Y}_{I_m}\right) \cdot g(\bar{Y}_{I_m})\right] =0,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-3124: Since this holds for any function $g$, by definition of conditional expectation we have proved that $\E[Y_{n+1}\mid \hat{p ^{Y_{n+1 _{n+1 ] = \hat{p ^{Y_{n+1 _{n+1 $ almost surely, as desired.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-3125: Post-hoc calibration for machine-learning models goes back to \citet{platt1999probabilistic , which introduced a form of temperature scaling.}
elements.sent-2411-3126: Binning for post-hoc calibration goes back to~\cite{zadrozny2001obtaining .}
elements.sent-2411-3127: Isotonic regression for post-hoc calibration was introduced in~\cite{zadrozny2002transforming , leveraging the paired adjacent violators algorithm for isotonic regression~\cite{ayer1955empirical, barlow1972theory .}}
elements.sent-2411-3128: For more recent empirical studies of calibration in machine learning, see~\cite{guo2017calibration and~\cite{minderer2021revisiting .}}
elements.sent-2411-3129: Plotting empirical frequencies versus probabilities, known as reliability diagrams, goes back to at least~\cite{murphy1977reliability , and ECE is a numeric summary of the reliability diagram.}
elements.sent-2411-3130: The distance to calibration was introduced by~\citet{blasiok2023unifying , and Proposition~\ref{prop:dce_less_ece , as well as the fact that dCE can be estimated in a distribution-free setting is due to that work; the same paper also establishes connections between dCE and binned ECE.}}
elements.sent-2411-3131: Another important view on calibration, known as weighted calibration error~\citep{gopalan2022low , considers comparing the prediction error to a class of test functions \begin{equation}\sup_{g \in \mathcal G}\E\left[g(f(X))\left(\E[Y \mid f(X)] - f(X)\right)\right],\end{equation} for some class of functions $\mathcal G$.}
elements.sent-2411-3132: ECE and dCE can both be viewed as special cases of this; see also \citet{okoroafor2025near,rossellini2025can for an exploration of additional notions of calibration that lie in between ECE and dCE.}
elements.sent-2411-3133: Moreover, this view is closely related to the important topic of multicalibration~\citep{hebert2018multicalibration ---a stronger notion of calibration that requires validity across subgroups of the data.}
elements.sent-2411-3135: Venn--Abers predictors were introduced in~\citet{vovk2014vennabers , building on Venn predictors~\citet{vovk2003self .}}
elements.sent-2411-3136: The inductive version was introduced in~\citet{lambrou2015inductive and further elaborated in~\citet{nouretdinov2018inductive .}}
elements.sent-2411-3138: See also conformal predictive distributions~\citep{vovk2017nonparametric, vovk2018cross for a different approach to calibration-type guarantees leveraging exchangeability.}
elements.sent-2411-3139: Finally, we add some technical notes for several proofs appearing in this chapter.
elements.sent-2411-3140: Several proofs use McDiarmid's inequality to ensure concentration of estimators (namely, the estimators of dCE and of binned ECE); this concentration inequality is due to \citet{mcdiarmid1989method .}
elements.sent-2411-3141: In the proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:venn--abers we rely on properties of isotonic regression, as in~\eqref{eqn:Venn--Abers_iso_piecewise ; for background on isotonic regression, see \citet{barlow1972theory .}}}
elements.sent-2411-3142: In this chapter, we turn to a different type of inference problem: the question of conditional independence testing, where we would like to determine whether a feature $X$ and response $Y$ are independent after conditioning on an additional random variable $W$ (the `confounder', which is often high-dimensional in many practical applications).
elements.sent-2411-3143: Formally, we would like to test the hypothesis \begin{equation}\label{eqn:H0_conditional_indep}H_0 : \ X\indep Y \mid W,\end{equation} or equivalently, \begin{equation}H_0: \ P \in \cP_{X\indep Y\mid W},\end{equation} where $\cP_{X\indep Y\mid W}$ is the set of all joint distributions on $(X,Y,W)\in\cX\times\cY\times\cW$ for which it holds that $X\indep Y\mid W$.
elements.sent-2411-3144: Note that if we instead test $X\indep Y$, we are asking about marginal, rather than conditional, independence---e.g., testing whether the feature $X$ is associated with the response $Y$, but without accounting for the confounding effects of $W$.
elements.sent-2411-3145: The questions of testing independence or conditional independence are both intimately related to the same sorts of permutation-type arguments that arose in Chapter~\ref{chapter:exchangeability .}
elements.sent-2411-3146: As we will see shortly, in the distribution-free setting, testing marginal independence is typically straightforward, but testing conditional independence faces fundamental hardness results unless the confounder $W$ is discrete.
elements.sent-2411-3147: To begin, we tackle the easier problem of testing marginal independence.
elements.sent-2411-3148: We are given a random sample $(X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_n,Y_n)$ drawn i.i.d.\ from some distribution $P\in\cP_{(X,Y)}$, where $\cP_{(X,Y)}$ is the space of all distributions on $(X,Y)\in\cX\times\cY$.
elements.sent-2411-3149: The goal is to test the null hypothesis of independence: \begin{equation}\label{eq:null-marginal-independence} H_0 : \; X\indep Y,\end{equation} or equivalently, \begin{equation}H_0: \, P \in \cP_{X \indep Y},\end{equation} where $\cP_{X \indep Y}\subset \cP_{(X,Y)}$ is the space of product distributions on $\cX\times\cY$---i.e., the space of distributions $P$ on $(X,Y)$ for which it holds that $X\indep Y$.
elements.sent-2411-3150: We will consider any test statistic \begin{equation}T:(\cX\times\cY)^n\to\R\end{equation} that measures evidence against the null---for example, for the case of real-valued data ($\cX = \cY = \R$), we might choose \begin{equation}T\big((X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_n,Y_n)\big) = \left|\textnormal{Corr}\big((X_1,\dots,X_n),(Y_1,\dots,Y_n)\big)\right|,\end{equation} the absolute value of the sample correlation.
elements.sent-2411-3151: How can we use the value of $T$ to decide whether we have sufficient evidence to reject the null? Using a permutation test, it is straightforward to design tests of marginal independence with bounded Type I error under the null.
elements.sent-2411-3152: If $\cH_{X\indep Y}$ is in fact true, then permuting the $X_i$ while leaving the $Y_i$ fixed would not change the distribution of the data.
elements.sent-2411-3153: This yields a natural hypothesis test against the null of independence.
elements.sent-2411-3154: Before stating the test and the result, we first define some notation.
elements.sent-2411-3155: Write \begin{equation}\bfX = (X_1,\dots,X_n)\in\cX^n, \ \bfY = (Y_1,\dots,Y_n)\in\cY^n\end{equation} to denote the vectors of observations of $X$ and of $Y$, respectively, and also \begin{equation}\bfX_\sigma = (X_{\sigma(1)},\dots,X_{\sigma(n)})\end{equation} to denote the permuted vector of $X$ observations, for any $\sigma\in\cS_n$.
elements.sent-2411-3156: Moreover, for any test statistic $T:(\cX\times\cY)^n\to\R$, abusing notation we write $T(\bfX,\bfY)$ to denote $T((X_1,Y_1),\dots,(X_n,Y_n))$.
elements.sent-2411-3159: Then $\psi$ is a valid test of the null hypothesis $\cH_{X\indep Y}$, i.e., \begin{equation}\P_P\left( \psi(\bfX,\bfY) = 1 \right) \leq \alpha \text{ for all } P \in \cP_{X \indep Y}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-3160: Intuitively, the validity of this test arises from the fact that, under $\cH_{X \indep Y}$, we have \begin{equation}(\bfX_\sigma,\bfY) \eqd (\bfX,\bfY),\end{equation} for all permutations $\sigma \in \cS_n$---and consequently, the observed test statistic value $T(\bfX,\bfY)$ can be compared against permuted values $T(\bfX_\sigma,\bfY)$ to test the null.
elements.sent-2411-3161: In order to apply those results, we will need to condition on $\bfY$.
elements.sent-2411-3162: Under the null hypothesis of marginal independence~\eqref{eq:null-marginal-independence , the joint distribution $P$ can be written as a product, $P=P_X\times P_Y$.}
elements.sent-2411-3163: Therefore, even after conditioning on $\bfY$, it still holds that the feature values $X_1,\dots,X_n$ are i.i.d.\ draws from $P_X$.
elements.sent-2411-3164: In particular, this means that $X_1,\dots,X_n$ are exchangeable, after conditioning on $\bfY$.
elements.sent-2411-3165: Defining \begin{equation}p = \frac{\sum_{\sigma\inS_n}\ind{T(\bfX_\sigma,\bfY)\geq T(\bfX,\bfY)}}{n!},\end{equation} we therefore have $\P_P(p\leq \alpha \mid \bfY)\leq \alpha$, by Theorem~\ref{thm:perm-test (specifically, since we condition on $\bfY$, we are applying this theorem with the test statistic $T$ replaced by the map $\bfx \mapsto T(\bfx,\bfY)$).}
elements.sent-2411-3166: Finally, by definition, $\psi(\bfX,\bfY)=1$ if and only if $p\leq \alpha$.
elements.section-2411-062: Conditional independence with a discrete confounder
elements.figure-2411-038: \includegraphics[width=0.6\textwidth]{figures/diagrams/discrete-local-perm-test.pdf} \caption{Visualization of a dataset with a discrete confounder along with a local permutation of the covariate. Grayscale levels indicate numerical value. On the left, we show the dataset in its original ordering; note that $W$ only takes on three values, defining three subgroups. On the right, we show a locally permuted version of the dataset, where the values of $X$ are randomly permuted within each subgroup, as in the local permutation test from Section~\ref{sec:define_local_perm_test_discrete}.} \label{fig:local-perm-test-discrete}
elements.sent-2411-3167: Next, we study the problem of conditional independence testing, i.e., testing the null \eqref{eqn:H0_conditional_indep , in a special setting: we will consider the case that the confounder $W$ is a discrete random variable.}
elements.sent-2411-3168: In this context, the problem of conditional independence testing is solvable via an extension of the permutation test, which groups together data points according to their value of the confounder $W$, and then simply permutes the $(X,Y)$ data within groups.
elements.sent-2411-3169: This is sometimes called a local permutation test, because it only permutes `locally' among data points that share the exact same value of the confounder $W$.
elements.sent-2411-3170: The technical arguments used in these sections are roughly the same, and boil down to the fact that, when we have access to repeated measurements, it becomes possible to perform distribution-free inference.
elements.sent-2411-3171: For testing marginal (i.e., unconditional) independence, $X\indep Y$, we can simply permute the $X$ values to simulate a draw from the null distribution as in Section~\ref{sec:marginal-independence-testing above.}
elements.sent-2411-3172: When testing conditional independence, $X\indep Y \mid W$, for a discrete confounder $W$, we now see that we can use exactly the same strategy by partitioning the data into groups.
elements.sent-2411-3174: If we think of the distribution $P$ on $(X,Y,W)$ as describing the general population from which data points are sampled, for any specific value $w$ for the confounder we can consider the `subpopulation' of data points for which $W=w$.
elements.sent-2411-3175: Within this subpopulation, $X$ and $Y$ are independent---and so it is again valid to permute $X$ values in order to resample the data and test the null hypothesis.
elements.sent-2411-3177: Formally, we can define the local permutation test as follows.
elements.sent-2411-3178: We are given an i.i.d.\ sample of data $((X_i, Y_i, W_i) )_{i\in[n]}$ drawn from $P\in\cP_{X\times Y\times W}$, where $\cP_{X\times Y\times W}$ is the space of all distributions on $\cX\times\cY\times\cW$.
elements.sent-2411-3179: Let $\cS_n(\bfW) \subseteq \cS_n$ be the set of permutations of $\{1, \ldots, n\}$ that preserve the value of the vector of confounders $\bfW = (W_1, \ldots, W_n)$---i.e., \begin{equation}\label{eqn:permutations_discrete_confounder} \cS_n(\bfW) = \left\{ \sigma \in \cS_n : W_{\sigma(i)} = W_i \text{ for all } i \in \{1, \ldots, n\} \right\}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-881: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:permutations_discrete_confounder} \cS_n(\bfW) = \left\{ \sigma \in \cS_n : W_{\sigma(i)} = W_i  for all  i \in \{1, \ldots, n\} \right\}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-3180: Then, given some choice of test statistic $T:(\cX\times\cY\times\cW)^n\rightarrow\R$, the local permutation test is defined as \begin{equation}\label{eq:local-permtest} \psi(\bfX,\bfY,\bfW) = \ind{\frac{1}{|\cS_n(\bfW)|}\sum\limits_{\sigma\in\cS_n(\bfW)}\ind{T(\bfX_\sigma,\bfY,\bfW) \geq T(\bfX,\bfY,\bfW)} \leq \alpha}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-3181: Let $(X_1,Y_1,W_1),\dots,(X_n,Y_n,W_n)\iidsim P$, and let $\psi(\bfX,\bfY,\bfW)$ be defined as in~\eqref{eq:local-permtest .}
elements.sent-2411-3182: Then $\psi$ is a valid test of the null hypothesis $\cH_{X\indep Y\mid W}$, i.e., \begin{equation}\P_P\left( \psi(\bfX,\bfY,\bfW) = 1 \right) \leq \alpha \text{ for all } P \in \cP_{X \indep Y\mid W}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-3183: As before, the intuition is that under the null, the permuted data $(\bfX_\sigma,\bfY,\bfW)$ has the same distribution as the original data $(\bfX,\bfY,\bfW)$, for all permutations $\sigma \in \cS_n(\bfW)$---but this statement is not well defined since, of course, the set $\cS_n(\bfW)$ is itself random.
elements.sent-2411-3184: To be more precise, what we mean is that conditional on $\bfW$, for each $\sigma\in\cS_n(\bfW)$ it holds that $(\bfX_\sigma,\bfY)$ and $(\bfX,\bfY)$ are equal in (conditional) distribution.
elements.sent-2411-3185: We will formalize this intuition in our proof of the validity of the local permutation test, below.
elements.paragraph-2411-035: Will the local permutation test be powerful?
elements.sent-2411-3186: The power of the local permutation test will depend heavily on several factors.
elements.sent-2411-3187: First, we need to consider the number of `clashes'---i.e., data points $i\neq j\in[n]$ for which $W_i=W_j$, and therefore, feature values $X_i$ and $X_j$ may be permuted in our permutation test.
elements.sent-2411-3188: A large number of clashes will lead to a rich set of candidate permutations $\cS_n(\bfW)$ and, potentially, a powerful test.
elements.sent-2411-3189: At the other extreme, if each value $W_i$ is unique, then $\cS_n(\bfW)$ will be a singleton set (containing only the identity permutation), leading to $\psi(\bfX,\bfY,\bfW)=0$.
elements.sent-2411-3190: In general, if the sample size $n$ is sufficiently large so that many values of $W$ are observed multiple times in the dataset, then we have the potential for high power.
elements.sent-2411-3191: Second, we need to consider the choice of the test statistic $T$ as well.
elements.sent-2411-3192: As is the case for any test, if the test statistic cannot discriminate between the null and alternative, then the test will not be powerful.
elements.sent-2411-3193: We omit further discussion of this standard point.
elements.sent-2411-3194: While we have motivated this test by considering the case of a discrete confounder $W$, in fact the test can be implemented in any setting.
elements.sent-2411-3195: Of course, if the distribution of $W$ is continuous, all the $W_i$'s will be distinct almost surely and we will have no power.
elements.sent-2411-3196: But if the distribution of $W$ is, say, a mixture of discrete and continuous components, then we might still have a large number of clashes, and the local permutation test could then achieve high power.
elements.sent-2411-3197: As a result, while we have introduced this section by assuming that $W$ is discrete, in fact we can think of the local permutation test as an approach that can be applied for any confounder $W$, but that will have nontrivial power only if $W$ is discrete (or, more generally, has a mixture distribution that includes a substantial discrete component).
elements.subsection-2411-066: Proof of validity of the local permutation test
elements.sent-2411-3198: We now prove that the local permutation test is valid as a test of conditional independence, with no assumptions on the distribution or on the test statistic.
elements.sent-2411-3199: Then we have \begin{equation}\psi(\bfx,\bfy,\bfw) = \ind{\frac{1}{|\cS_n(\bfw)|}\sum\limits_{\sigma\in\cS_n(\bfw)}\ind{T(\bfx_\sigma,\bfy,\bfw) \geq T(\bfx,\bfy,\bfw)} \leq \alpha} = \ind{T(\bfx,\bfy,\bfw) > \hat{q}(\bfx,\bfy,\bfw)},\end{equation} where the first step is simply the definition of $\psi(\bfx,\bfy,\bfw)$, and the second step holds by definition of the quantile of a finite list (recall Definition~\ref{def:quantile ; this is also similar to the argument in the proof of Proposition~\ref{prop:conformal-via-pvalues ).}}
elements.sent-2411-3200: Moreover, for any $\sigma\in\cS_n(\bfw)$, we have an equality of sets, \begin{equation}\cS_n(\bfw) = \{\sigma\circ\sigma' : \sigma'\in\cS_n(\bfw)\},\end{equation} where $\circ$ denotes a composition of permutations; this holds specifically because $\cS_n(\bfw)$ is a subgroup, rather than an arbitrary subset, of $\cS_n$.
elements.sent-2411-3202: Therefore, \begin{equation}\label{eqn:psi_XYW_deterministic_step} \frac{1}{|\cS_n(\bfw)|}\sum_{\sigma\in\cS_n(\bfw)} \ind{\psi(\bfx_\sigma,\bfy,\bfw)=1} =\frac{1}{|\cS_n(\bfw)|}\sum_{\sigma\in\cS_n(\bfw)} \ind{T(\bfx_\sigma,\bfy,\bfw)>\hat{q}(\bfx,\bfy,\bfw)} =\frac{1}{|\cS_n(\bfw)|}\sum_{\sigma\in\cS_n(\bfw)} \ind{T(\bfx_\sigma,\bfy,\bfw)>\quantile\left( \big(T(\bfx_{\sigma'},\bfy,\bfw)\big)_{\sigma'\in\cS_n(\bfw)} ; 1-\alpha\right)} \leq \alpha,\end{equation} by standard properties of the quantile (see Fact~\ref{fact:conversion-quantiles-cdfs}\ref{fact:conversion-quantiles-cdfs_part3 ).}
elements.sent-2411-3203: Next, fix any distribution $P\in \cP_{X \indep Y \mid W}$.
elements.sent-2411-3204: We now claim that \begin{equation}\label{eqn:eqd_XY_W_localperm}(\bfX_\sigma,\bfY) \mid \bfW \ \eqd \ (\bfX,\bfY) \mid \bfW,\end{equation} for any $\sigma\in\cS_n(\bfW)$.
elements.sent-2411-3205: To see why, first note that conditional on $\bfW$, we have $(X_i,Y_i)\sim P_{X\mid W (\cdot\mid W_i)\times P_{Y\mid W (\cdot\mid W_i)$, since $X\indep Y\mid W$.}}

elements.sent-2411-3207: Similarly, for any $\sigma\in\cS_n(\bfW)$ (treating $\sigma$ as fixed once we have conditioned on $\bfW$), we have \begin{equation}(\bfX_\sigma,\bfY)\mid \bfW\sim \big(P_{X\mid W}(\cdot\mid W_{\sigma(1)})\times \dots \times P_{X\mid W}(\cdot\mid W_{\sigma(n)})\big) \times \big(P_{Y\mid W}(\cdot\mid W_1)\times \dots \times P_{Y\mid W}(\cdot\mid W_n)\big).\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-3208: But since $\sigma\in\cS_n(\bfW)$, we have $W_{\sigma(i) = W_i$ for all $i$---and therefore these two conditional distributions are the same.}
elements.sent-2411-3209: In particular, the equality~\eqref{eqn:eqd_XY_W_localperm implies that \begin{equation}\P_P(\psi(\bfX,\bfY,\bfW) = 1 \mid \bfW) = \P_P(\psi(\bfX_\sigma,\bfY,\bfW) = 1 \mid \bfW)\end{equation} holds for each $\sigma\in\cS_n(\bfW)$.}
elements.sent-2411-3210: Taking an average over $\sigma\in\cS_n(\bfW)$, we therefore have \begin{equation}\P_P(\psi(\bfX,\bfY,\bfW) = 1 \mid \bfW) = \frac{1}{|\cS_n(\bfW)|}\sum_{\sigma\in\cS_n(\bfW)}\P_P(\psi(\bfX_\sigma,\bfY,\bfW) = 1 \mid \bfW) =\E_P\left[ \frac{1}{|\cS_n(\bfW)|}\sum_{\sigma\in\cS_n(\bfW)} \ind{\psi(\bfX_\sigma,\bfY,\bfW) = 1} \ \middle| \ \bfW\right] \leq \alpha,\end{equation} where the last step holds by~\eqref{eqn:psi_XYW_deterministic_step .}
elements.sent-2411-3211: After marginalizing over $\bfW$, this completes the proof.
elements.section-2411-063: The hardness of conditional independence testing
elements.sent-2411-3212: The results of Section~\ref{sec:cond_indep_discrete_case above show that testing conditional independence is possible in the setting where the confounder $W$ is discrete (or, perhaps, is a mixture of a discrete and a continuous distribution).}
elements.sent-2411-3213: In this section, we turn to the more challenging setting where the distribution of $W$ is instead continuous, or more generally, nonatomic---recall Definition~\ref{def:nonatomic .}
elements.sent-2411-3214: We will see that in this setting, it is impossible to construct a distribution-free test with nontrivial power.
elements.sent-2411-3215: Next, we will turn to formalizing this hardness result.
elements.sent-2411-3216: We will use the notation \begin{equation}\psi: (\cX\times\cY\times\cW)^n \rightarrow \{0,1\}\end{equation} to represent a test function mapping a dataset of $n$ triples $((X_i,Y_i,W_i))_{i\in[n]}$ to the indicator of rejection.
elements.sent-2411-3217: That is, $\psi=0$ indicates a failure to reject the null hypothesis of conditional independence~\eqref{eqn:H0_conditional_indep , while $\psi=1$ indicates rejection.}
elements.sent-2411-3218: If the test $\psi$ has Type I error bounded by $\alpha$ for any distribution, i.e., \begin{equation}\P_P\big(\psi(\bfX,\bfY,\bfW)=1 \big)\leq \alpha\textnormal{ for all $P\in\cP_{X\indep Y \mid W}$,}\end{equation} then the power of the test is not better than random for any distribution $P$ such that $P_W$ is nonatomic, \begin{equation}\P_P\big(\psi(\bfX,\bfY,\bfW)=1 \big) \leq \alpha\textnormal{ for all $P\in\cP_{(X,Y,W)}$ with $P_W$ nonatomic.}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-3219: We will see that the proof of this hardness result will rely on the sample--resample technique introduced earlier, in Lemma~\ref{lem:sample-resample , where we approximate the process of sampling $n$ i.i.d.\ draws from $P$ by first drawing a larger list of $M$ samples from $P$, then drawing our training set by sampling uniformly from this list.}
elements.sent-2411-3220: Let $\widehat{P _M = \frac{1 {M}\sum_{i=1 ^M\delta_{(X^{(i) ,Y^{(i) ,W^{(i) )}$ be the empirical distribution of the sequence.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-3221: If $W^{(1) ,\dots,W^{(M)}$ are distinct, then under the distribution $\widehat{P _M$ on $(X,Y,W)$ it holds that $X$ and $Y$ are deterministic functions of $W$---and in particular it therefore trivially holds that $X\indep Y\mid W$ under the distribution $\widehat{P _M$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-3222: By distribution-free validity of the test $\psi$, we must therefore have \begin{equation}\P_{\widehat{P}_M}\big(\psi(\bfX,\bfY,\bfW)=1 \big) \leq \alpha\end{equation} as long as $W^{(1) ,\dots,W^{(M)}$ are distinct, where the probability is taken with respect to samples $((X_i,Y_i,W_i))_{i\in[n]}$ sampled i.i.d.\ from $\widehat{P _M$, or equivalently, sampled uniformly with replacement from the dataset $((X^{(i) ,Y^{(i) ,W^{(i) ))_{i\in[M]}$.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-3226: We have assumed that $P_W$ is nonatomic, and therefore $W^{(1) ,\dots,W^{(M)}$ are distinct almost surely.}
elements.sent-2411-3227: By the calculation above, then, \begin{equation}\P_{\widehat{P}_M}\big(\psi(\bfX,\bfY,\bfW)=1\mid \widehat{P}_M\big) \leq \alpha\end{equation} holds almost surely.
elements.sent-2411-3228: Now let $Q$ be the marginal distribution of $(X_1,Y_1,W_1),\dots,(X_n,Y_n,W_n)$ under the construction above.

elements.section-2411-064: Testing conditional independence under smoothness assumptions
elements.sent-2411-3232: The hardness result in Theorem~\ref{thm:hardness-conditional-independence-nonatomic establishes that a test $\psi$ cannot have nontrivial power for nonatomic $W$ if we require it to be valid against all distributions on $(X,Y,W)$ for which conditional independence holds.}
elements.sent-2411-3233: We can conclude from this that, if we would like to design a test $\psi$ that has nontrivial power to detect conditional dependence given a nonatomic confounder $W$, we need to relax our notion of validity---in other words, we need to require Type I error to be controlled against a more restricted set of possibilities under the null.
elements.sent-2411-3234: In this section, we examine one such possibility: placing a smoothness assumption on the conditional dependence between the variables.
elements.sent-2411-3235: For instance, if the space $\cW$ for the confounder $W$ is equipped with a norm $\|\cdot\|$, we can add the assumptions that the map \begin{equation}w \mapsto P_{X\mid W}(\cdot \mid w)\end{equation} is $L$-Lipschitz with respect to the Hellinger distance---that is, for any $w,w'\in\cW$, \begin{equation}\label{eqn:assume_XYW_Hellinger}\textnormal{d}_{\textnormal{H}}\big(P_{X\mid W}(\cdot \mid w),P_{X\mid W}(\cdot \mid w')\big) \leq L\|w-w'\|, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-3236: Here $\textnormal{d _{\textnormal{H }$ denotes the Hellinger distance: for two distributions $P_1,P_2$ that have densities $f_1,f_2$ with respect to a common base measure $\mu$, \begin{equation}\textnormal{d}_{\textnormal{H}}(P_1,P_2) = \left(\frac{1}{2}\int_{\cX} \left(\sqrt{f_1(x)}-\sqrt{f_2(x)}\right)^2\;d\mu(x)\right)^{1/2}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-3237: We now define a more restricted set of nulls, \begin{equation}\cP^L_{X\indep Y\mid W} = \{P\in\cP_{X\indep Y\mid W} : \textnormal{ condition~\eqref{eqn:assume_XYW_Hellinger} holds}\},\end{equation} i.e., all joint distributions $P$ on $(X,Y,W)$ for which $X\indep Y\mid W$ and for which the smoothness assumption~\eqref{eqn:assume_XYW_Hellinger holds for the conditional distribution $P_{X\mid W}$, with Lipschitz constant $L$.}
elements.sent-2411-3238: At a high level, this assumption allows for the continuous case to be approximately as easy as the discrete case.
elements.sent-2411-3239: In the case of discrete $W$, we are able to permute $X$ values within any subset of data points that all have the same value of $W$, as in~\eqref{eqn:permutations_discrete_confounder .}
elements.sent-2411-3240: In the case of continuous $W$, this is not a meaningful approach because no value of $W$ appears twice---but with a smoothness assumption in place, it again becomes possible to perform a test of conditional independence via a permutation test, because we can now permute $X$ values across data points that have similar values of $W$.
elements.sent-2411-3241: We can think of this as a binned version of the local permutation test studied in Section~\ref{sec:cond_indep_discrete_case .}
elements.figure-2411-039: \includegraphics[width=0.7\textwidth]{figures/diagrams/binned-local-perm-test.pdf} \caption{Visualization of a dataset with a continuous confounder along with a local permutation of the covariate under a smoothness assumption. The figure is similar to Figure~\ref{fig:local-perm-test-discrete}, but $W$ is continuous. To address this, we bin $W$ and permute within these bins. This is the type of permutation used for the test described Section~\ref{sec:conditional_indep_smoothness}.} \label{fig:binned-local-perm-test}
elements.sent-2411-3243: To formalize this, first construct a partition of $\cW$, given by \begin{equation}\cW = \cup_k \cW_k,\end{equation} where each bin $\cW_k$ contains only values of $W$ that are close together in the norm $\|\cdot\|$: we assume \begin{equation}\max_k \sup_{w,w'\in\cW_k} \|w - w'\|\leq h,\end{equation} for some finite $h$.
elements.sent-2411-3244: Finally, define \begin{equation}\label{eq:local-permtest-bin} \psi(\bfX,\bfY,\bfW) = \ind{\frac{1}{|\cS_n^{\textnormal{bin}}(\bfW)|}\sum\limits_{\sigma\in\cS_n^{\textnormal{bin}}(\bfW)}\ind{T(\bfX_\sigma,\bfY,\bfW) \geq T(\bfX,\bfY,\bfW)} \leq \alpha},\end{equation} which is analogous to the test $\psi(\bfX,\bfY,\bfW)$ defined in~\eqref{eq:local-permtest for the discrete case.}
elements.sent-2411-3245: Let $(X_1,Y_1,W_1),\dots,(X_n,Y_n,W_n)\iidsim P$, let $\cW = \cup_k \cW_k$ be any partition with $\max_k \sup_{w,w'\in\cW_k \|w - w'\|\leq h$, and let $\psi(\bfX,\bfY,\bfW)$ be defined as in~\eqref{eq:local-permtest-bin .}}
elements.sent-2411-3246: Then $\psi$ satisfies \begin{equation}\P_P\left( \psi(\bfX,\bfY,\bfW) = 1 \right) \leq \alpha + Lh\sqrt{2n}\text{ \ for all } P \in \cP_{X \indep Y\mid W}^L.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-909: \begin{equation}\P_P\left( \psi(\bfX,\bfY,\bfW) = 1 \right) \leq \alpha + Lh\sqrt{2n} \ for all  P \in \cP_{X \indep Y\mid W}^L.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-3247: In other words, by taking bins of diameter $h=\littleo(n^{-1/2 )$, we can ensure that the test $\psi$ is (nearly) a valid test of the null that $X \indep Y \mid W$, as long as we also assume the smoothness condition.}

elements.sent-2411-3249: The idea of the proof is to find a distribution $\tilde{P}$ that is similar to $P$, for which the test $\psi$ is exactly valid.

elements.formula-2411-912: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:dH_within_bin}\textnormal{d}_{\textnormal{H}}(P_{X\mid W}(\cdot\mid w),P^{(k)}_X)\leq Lh\textnormal{ for all $k$ and all $w\in\cW_k$,}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-3253: Next, we calculate a bound on the difference between sampling from $P$ and sampling from $\tilde{P}$.
elements.sent-2411-3254: By construction, the marginal distribution of $(Y,W)$ is the same under $P$ as under $\tilde{P}$, since in both cases it is equal to $\sum_k \pi_k P^{(k) _{(Y,W)}$.}
elements.sent-2411-3255: Therefore, the only difference between $P$ and $\tilde{P}$ arises from the differences in the conditional distribution of $X$: under $P$ this conditional distribution is given by \begin{equation}X\mid (Y,W) \sim P_{X\mid W}(\cdot \mid W),\end{equation} while under $\tilde{P}$, we have \begin{equation}X\mid (Y,W) \sim P^{(k(W))}_X,\end{equation} where $k(W)$ identifies the bin to which the confounder $W$ belongs---that is, for any $k$ and any $w\in\cW_k$, $k(w) = k$.
elements.sent-2411-3256: Therefore, \begin{equation}\textnormal{d}_{\textnormal{H}}^2(P,\tilde{P}) = \E_P\left[\textnormal{d}_{\textnormal{H}}^2\big(P_{X\mid W}(\cdot \mid W),P_X^{(k(W))}\big)\right] \leq (Lh)^2,\end{equation} where the first step holds by properties of the Hellinger distance, and the last step applies~\eqref{eqn:dH_within_bin .}
elements.sent-2411-3257: Since squared Hellinger distance is subadditive, we therefore have \begin{equation}\textnormal{d}^2_{\textnormal{H}}(P^n,\tilde{P}^n) \leq n(Lh)^2,\end{equation} and since total variation distance is bounded by the Hellinger distance up to a factor of $\sqrt{2}$, this implies \begin{equation}\dtv(P^n,\tilde{P}^n)\leq \sqrt{2} \textnormal{d}_{\textnormal{H}}(P^n,\tilde{P}^n) \leq Lh \sqrt{2n}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-3258: Therefore, \begin{equation}\P_P(\psi(\bfX,\bfY,\bfW)=1) \leq \P_{\tilde{P}}(\psi(\bfX,\bfY,\bfW)=1) + Lh\sqrt{2n},\end{equation} i.e., the probability of $\psi$ rejecting $H_0$ when applied to data $((X_i,Y_i,W_i))_{i\in[n]}$ sampled from $P$, can be bounded by the probability of rejection for data drawn from $\tilde{P}$.
elements.sent-2411-3259: To complete the proof, we need to bound $\P_{\tilde{P (\psi(\bfX,\bfY,\bfW)=1)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-3260: Indeed, we will see that it is simply bounded by $\alpha$, because of the construction of $\tilde{P}$.
elements.sent-2411-3261: Define random variable $K=k(W)$ and consider a new triple, \begin{equation}(X, (Y,W), K).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-3262: Note that, under the distribution $\tilde{P}$, by construction it holds that \begin{equation}\label{eqn:XYWK}X \indep (Y,W) \mid K.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-3263: Next we construct a test statistic $\tilde{T}$ on these new triples, \begin{equation}\tilde{T}: (\cX \times (\cY\times \cW) \times [K])^n \rightarrow \R.\end{equation}. defined by \begin{equation}\tilde{T}\Big(\big(X_1,(Y_1,W_1),K_1\big),\dots,\big(X_n,(Y_n,W_n),K_n\big)\Big) = T\big((X_1,Y_1,W_1),\dots,(X_n,Y_n,W_n)\big).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-3264: We can observe that running the local permutation test~\eqref{eq:local-permtest defined in Section~\ref{sec:cond_indep_discrete_case , with data $\big((X_i,(Y_i,W_i),K_i)\big)_{i\in[n]}$ and test statistic $\tilde{T}$, is exactly equivalent to our test $\psi(\bfX,\bfY,\bfW)$ as defined in~\eqref{eq:local-permtest-bin .}}}
elements.sent-2411-3265: But since the data $\big((X_i,(Y_i,W_i),K_i)\big)_{i\in[n]}$ satisfies the null hypothesis of conditional independence by~\eqref{eqn:XYWK , we can apply Theorem~\ref{thm:localperm-validity (with $(X,(Y,W),K)$ in place of $(X,Y,W)$, and with $\tilde{T}$ in place of $T$), to see that $\P_{\tilde{P (\psi(\bfX,\bfY,\bfW)=1)\leq \alpha$, as desired.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-3266: The problem of testing conditional independence is closely linked to the problem of variable selection in high-dimensional regression: given a high-dimensional feature vector $X=(X_1,\dots,X_d)$ and response $Y$, testing whether a particular feature $X_j$ should be included into the fitted model is often framed as a question of conditional independence, i.e., testing \begin{equation}H_{0,j}: \, X_j \indep Y \mid X_{-j},\end{equation} where $X_{-j =(X_1,\dots,X_{j-1 ,X_{j+1 ,\dots,X_d)$ denotes the remaining covariates once $X_j$ has been removed.}}}
elements.sent-2411-3267: Ideas related to the use of permutations or randomization within strata to test for conditional independence with a discrete confounder are widespread~\citep[e.g.,][]{birch1965detection, rosenbaum1984conditional , and the local permutation test presented in Section~\ref{sec:cond_indep_discrete_case (for the case of a discrete confounder $W$) is studied in detail in~\citet{canonne2018testing .}}}
elements.sent-2411-3268: For a continuous confounder, one can extend this idea to consider permutations that approximately preserve the values of the confounder~\citep[e.g.,][]{margaritis2005distribution, fukumizu2007kernel, doran2014permutation, sen2017model, kim2022local .}
elements.sent-2411-3269: The hardness of conditional independence testing in the continuous case was first established by \citet[Theorem 2]{shah2020hardness ; the result presented here, in Theorem~\ref{thm:hardness-conditional-independence-nonatomic , is due to~\citet[Theorem 1]{kim2022local .}}}
elements.sent-2411-3270: In Section~\ref{sec:conditional_indep_smoothness , we assumed a smoothness condition to enable testing conditional independence beyond the discrete case.}
elements.sent-2411-3271: Assuming a smoothness condition is one way to avoid the hardness result of Section~\ref{sec:cond_indep_hardness , but there are many alternative assumption-lean approaches in the literature as well.}
elements.sent-2411-3272: For example, \citet{azadkia2021simple,shi2024azadkia consider a conditional form of Chatterjee's correlation coefficient \citep{chatterjee2021new for asymptotically valid estimates of conditional dependence (and an asymptotically valid test of conditional independence) without such assumptions.}}
elements.sent-2411-3273: As another example, in the model-X framework \citep{candes2018panning , we assume that we have knowledge (or approximate knowledge) of the conditional distribution of $X\mid W$.}
elements.sent-2411-3274: Within this framework, testing conditional independence becomes possible, since we can use the (approximate) knowledge of the conditional distribution $P_{X\mid W}$ to resample $\bfX$ under the null hypothesis of conditional independence---see \citet{candes2018panning,berrett2020conditional .}
